MTU: $\text{MTU} = 1200 \text{ bytes}$ - Header per packet: $H = 60 \text{ bytes}$ - Effective payload per packet: $D_{\text{eff}} = \text{MTU} - H = 1200 - 60 = 1140 \text{ bytes}$ - Link bandwidth: $R = 20 \text{ Mbps} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ B/s}$ --- **(i) Number of Packets Required:** $$N = \left\lceil \frac{S}{D_{\text{eff}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5 \times 10^8}{1140} \right\rceil$$ $$N = \left\lceil 438{,}596.4912... \right\rceil = 438{,}597 \text{ packets}$$ --- **(ii) Total Protocol Overhead:** $$\text{Total overhead (bytes)} = N \times H = 438{,}597 \times 60$$ $$\text{Total overhead} = 26{,}315{,}820 \text{ bytes} \approx 25.10 \text{ MB}$$ **Overhead Percentage:** $$\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{N \times H}{N \times \text{MTU}} \times 100 = \frac{60}{1200} \times 100 = 5\%$$ --- **(iii) Total Transmission Time:** $$\text{Total bytes transmitted} = N \times \text{MTU} = 438{,}597 \times 1200 = 526{,}316{,}400 \text{ bytes}$$ $$\text{Transmission time} = \frac{\text{Total bytes}}{R_{\text{bytes/s}}} = \frac{526{,}316{,}400}{2.5 \times 10^6}$$ $$t_{\text{tx}} = 210.53 \text{ seconds} \approx 3 \text{ minutes } 30.5 \text{ seconds}$$ --- **Summary of Results:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Number of packets ($N$) | **438,597** | | Total overhead (bytes) | **26,315,820 bytes ≈ 25.10 MB** | | Overhead percentage | **5%** | | Transmission time | **≈ 210.53 seconds** | *(7 marks — 2 for packets, 2 for overhead, 3 for time calculation with units)* --- ### ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS — INTERNET TOPIC** > > 1. **Confusing TCP/IP with OSI layers:** Many students write TCP/IP as 5 layers (mixing Session/Presentation) or say OSI has 4 layers. **Correct: TCP/IP = 4, OSI = 7.** (Loses 1–2 marks instantly.) > 2. **Forgetting port numbers in service tables:** KTU examiners often award partial marks for listing protocols *with their default ports*. Always include **80, 443, 25, 53, 21, 22, 110, 143**. > 3. **Using TCP instead of UDP for streaming:** A very common error. Real-time multimedia **demands UDP**; justify with *latency, no retransmission, and RTP compatibility*. > 4. **Unit conversion mistakes in numericals:** Forgetting to convert **Mbps → bytes/s** (divide by 8) is the #1 error in transmission time problems. Always show the conversion step explicitly. > 5. **Rounding down the number of packets:** The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ must be used — a partial packet still requires a full packet. Rounding *down* loses 1 mark. > 6. **Not mentioning DNS caching:** Examiners reward awareness of *TTL-based caching* and *recursive vs. iterative queries* in DNS questions. > 7. **Skipping the "Why" in comparisons:** Just listing differences between Internet/Intranet/Extranet is insufficient — always add a *security/access* angle. --- ### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — INTERNET** > > ✅ **Internet = Global TCP/IP network of networks** — decentralized, packet-switched, interoperable. > > ✅ **TCP/IP has 4 layers:** Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access. **OSI has 7 layers.** > > ✅ **IPv4 = 32 bits** (≈ 4.3 billion addresses); **IPv6 = 128 bits** (virtually unlimited). > > ✅ **Key Ports:** HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443, FTP = 20/21, DNS = 53, SMTP = 25, SSH = 22, RTSP = 554. > > ✅ **TCP vs. UDP:** TCP = reliable, ordered, slower (file transfer, email, web). UDP = fast, connectionless, no retransmission (**streaming, VoIP, gaming, live video**). > > ✅ **DNS** = port 53, translates domain names → IP addresses. Uses **hierarchical resolution**: Root → TLD → Authoritative. > > ✅ **Number of packets formula:** $N = \lceil S / (\text{MTU} - H) \rceil$ > > ✅ **Overhead % formula:** $\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{H}{\text{MTU}} \times 100$ > > ✅ **File size formula:** $\text{Size (bits)} = \text{Bit Rate (bps)} \times \text{Duration (s)}$ > > ✅ **Transmission time:** $t = \frac{N \times \text{MTU} \times 8}{R \text{ (in bps)}}$ — remember to multiply by 8 for byte-to-bit conversion. > > ✅ **Internet = Public; Intranet = Private (org); Extranet = Controlled extension of intranet.** > > ✅ **Multimedia over Internet** relies on **RTP/RTSP (real-time)** and **HTTP/HTTPS + CDNs (on-demand)**. > > ✅ **MTU for Ethernet** = 1500 bytes (standard). Header overhead = TCP (20B) + IP (20B) = **40 bytes**.

topics.title: Multimedia Software
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Multimedia Software # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECNT527 / Module 1)** > **Multimedia Software** refers to the specialized category of computer programs and application suites engineered to **create, edit, manipulate, store, compress, transmit, and play back** multimedia content. This umbrella term spans five media pillars — **Text, Images, Audio, Video, and Animation** — and includes both *authoring environments* (creation side) and *player/viewer applications* (delivery side). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** curriculum, Multimedia Software is broadly classified into **three functional layers**: | Layer | Function | Example Software Category | |-------|----------|---------------------------| | **Creation Layer** | Generates raw assets | Image editors, Audio DAWs, 3D modelers | | **Integration Layer** | Combines assets into a unified product | Authoring tools (Adobe Director, Flash) | | **Delivery/Playback Layer** | Renders the final product to end users | Media players, Web browsers, Plug-ins | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are producing a **Hollywood movie**. You don't use one giant machine to make the entire film. Instead, you use: - A **camera & photo editor** → to craft the *visuals* (images) - A **sound studio & audio editor** → to record and mix the *soundtrack* (audio) - A **video editor** → to stitch scenes together (video) - An **animation desk** → to create special effects (animation) - A **film studio / editing suite** → to combine everything (authoring tool) - A **movie projector / streaming app** → to show the final film to the audience (player) > [!NOTE] **Intuitive Takeaway** > Multimedia Software is the *movie-making toolkit* of the digital world. Each tool handles one specific "department" of production, and the **authoring tool acts as the director** that unites them into one cohesive multimedia experience. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Industry Terminology - **DPI (Dots Per Inch)** = **300** (standard print resolution for images) - **FPS (Frames Per Second)** = **24** (cinema), **30** (NTSC TV), **25** (PAL TV), **60** (HD streaming) - **CD Quality Audio** = **44.1 kHz / 16-bit Stereo** - **HD Video Standard** = **1920 × 1080 pixels** (Full HD, also called *1080p*) - **4K UHD Standard** = **3840 × 2160 pixels** - **Color Depth** = **8-bit (256 colors)**, **24-bit (16.7M colors — True Color)**, **32-bit (True Color + Alpha)** - **File Compression Ratio** = $\frac{\text{Original File Size}}{\text{Compressed File Size}}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Multimedia Software (Functional Decomposition) Multimedia Software is best understood by breaking it down according to the **media type it manipulates**. Each media type has specialized tools that fall under three licensing/distribution models: - **Freeware / Open-Source** — Free to use, source code often available (e.g., GIMP, Audacity, Blender) - **Shareware** — Free trial, paid license for full version - **Proprietary / Commercial** — Paid licensed software (e.g., Adobe Creative Suite, Final Cut Pro) ### 2.1.1 Image Editing Software Software used to **manipulate raster (bitmap) and vector graphics**. They support operations like cropping, color correction, filtering, layering, masking, and retouching. | Tool | Type | Key Strength | |------|------|--------------| | **Adobe Photoshop** | Proprietary, Raster | Industry standard for photo editing | | **GIMP (GNU Image Manipulation Program)** | Open-Source, Raster | Free alternative to Photoshop | | **CorelDRAW** | Proprietary, Vector | Vector illustration & page layout | | **Adobe Illustrator** | Proprietary, Vector | Standard for vector logos & icons | | **Inkscape** | Open-Source, Vector | Free SVG editor | | **Paint.NET** | Freeware, Raster | Lightweight Windows-only editor | ### 2.1.2 Audio Editing Software Software that processes digital audio waveforms. Supports **cut, copy, paste, mix, apply effects (reverb, echo, equalization), and format conversion**. | Tool | Type | Key Strength | |------|------|--------------| | **Audacity** | Open-Source | Most popular free audio editor | | **Adobe Audition** | Proprietary | Professional DAW (Digital Audio Workstation) | | **Apple Logic Pro** | Proprietary (macOS) | Music production | | **FL Studio** | Proprietary | Beat making & electronic music | | **Avid Pro Tools** | Proprietary | Industry standard for music studios | ### 2.1.3 Video Editing Software Tools to **trim, splice, add transitions, overlay text/audio, apply special effects, and export video** in various codecs and container formats. | Tool | Type | Key Strength | |------|------|--------------| | **Adobe Premiere Pro** | Proprietary | Industry standard for film/TV post-production | | **Apple Final Cut Pro** | Proprietary (macOS) | Optimized for Apple Silicon | | **DaVinci Resolve** | Free + Paid tiers | Best-in-class color grading | | **Windows Movie Maker** | Freeware (discontinued) | Beginner-level editing | | **OpenShot / Kdenlive** | Open-Source | Free cross-platform editors | ### 2.1.4 Animation Software Used to create **2D frame-by-frame or 3D object-based animation**. May use keyframes, tweening, rigging, and motion capture. | Tool | Type | Key Strength | |------|------|--------------| | **Adobe Animate (formerly Flash)** | Proprietary | 2D vector animation, web cartoons | | **Blender** | Open-Source | Full 3D modeling + animation + rendering | | **Autodesk Maya** | Proprietary | Hollywood-grade 3D animation | | **Toon Boom Harmony** | Proprietary | Standard for TV animation studios | | **Synfig Studio** | Open-Source | Free 2D vector animation | ### 2.1.5 Multimedia Authoring Tools These are **integrated development environments** that combine text, image, audio, video, and animation into a single interactive application — typically producing e-learning modules, interactive presentations, or kiosks. | Tool | Delivery Output | |------|-----------------| | **Adobe Director** | Shockwave (.dcr) — now discontinued | | **Macromedia Authorware** | Standalone .aam files | | **Adobe Captivate** | SCORM-compliant e-learning modules | | **Articulate Storyline** | HTML5 e-learning content | | **ToolBook** | Windows-based CBT (Computer-Based Training) | | **HyperStudio** | Multimedia stacks for education | ### 2.1.6 Web-Based Multimedia Tools Browser-native and server-side technologies for delivering multimedia over the internet. | Technology | Role | |------------|------| | **HTML5 `

topics.title: Editing and Authoring Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Editing and Authoring Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PECNT527 - Multimedia Technologies), **Editing and Authoring Tools** refer to the integrated class of software utilities that enable the creation, manipulation, assembly, and packaging of discrete multimedia components — text, image, audio, video, and animation — into a cohesive, navigable, and interactive final deliverable. Editing tools focus on the **manipulation of individual media elements** (e.g., cropping an image, trimming audio), while authoring tools provide the **higher-level orchestration layer** that ties these elements together using timelines, scripting, and a runtime engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must clearly distinguish between **linear editing** (a single, non-interactive sequence of frames or samples) and **non-linear / random-access editing** (where any segment of the media can be accessed, modified, and rearranged independently without re-recording subsequent portions). Non-linear editing is the de-facto industry standard in the post-2020 multimedia ecosystem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are preparing a **documentary film**:
- **Editing tools** are like the scissors, color-pencils, and tape recorder you use on individual **clips of raw footage, sound bites, and photographs** — fixing one photograph's brightness, cutting out a "um" from a speech, or trimming a 30-minute video down to 4 minutes.
- **Authoring tools** are like the **storyboard binder and the projector operator** — they don't change the raw footage themselves; instead, they sequence the edited clips, add chapter marks, attach subtitles, build a navigation menu, and finally burn the whole project onto a **DVD/Blu-ray disc** or publish it to a website so that an end-user can play it back interactively.

In short: **Editing = surgery on raw media; Authoring = publishing the finished surgery as a navigable product.**

### 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics
- **Frame Rate (fps):** **24 fps** (cinema), **25 fps** (PAL broadcast), **30 fps** (NTSC broadcast), **60 fps** (modern gaming/high-frame-rate video).
- **Sample Rate (kHz):** **44.1 kHz** (CD audio), **48 kHz** (professional video/DVD audio).
- **Color Depth:** **8-bit** (256 levels per channel), **10-bit** (HDR/streaming), **24-bit** (true-color RGB).
- **Resolution Standards:** **720p (HD)**, **1080p (Full HD)**, **4K UHD (3840×2160)**, **8K UHD (7680×4320)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter for KTU exams:** Question papers frequently test whether you can match the right tool to the right media (e.g., "Which tool is used to create a 3D animated character?" or "Differentiate between image editing and image authoring").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Tier Taxonomy of Multimedia Tools

Multimedia tools are classified into **two strict functional tiers** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.

**Tier 1 — Editing Tools (Component-Level)**
These operate on a **single media primitive**. They do not understand "projects" or "scenes" in the storytelling sense; they understand pixels, samples, frames, or characters.

| Sub-Category | Core Function | Representative Industry Tools |
|---|---|---|
| Text Editing | Character formatting, font embedding, hyperlinking, spell-check, RTF/ODF round-tripping | **Microsoft Word**, **LibreOffice Writer**, **Notepad++** |
| Image Editing | Pixel-level retouching, filters, layering, masking, color correction | **Adobe Photoshop**, **GIMP**, **Affinity Photo** |
| Audio Editing | Cut, copy, splice, normalize, denoise, apply effects, mix tracks | **Audacity**, **Adobe Audition**, **FL Studio** |
| Video Editing | Trim, splice, transition, color grade, composite, render timeline to a final video file | **Adobe Premiere Pro**, **DaVinci Resolve**, **Final Cut Pro**, **OpenShot** |
| Animation Editing | Keyframe manipulation, tweening, rigging, motion-path editing | **Adobe After Effects**, **Blender**, **Synfig Studio** |

**Tier 2 — Authoring Tools (Project / Runtime Level)**
These take the **outputs of Tier 1** (a finished `.psd`, `.wav`, `.mp4`) and arrange them into a **single navigable application** with menus, buttons, branching logic, and a playback engine.

| Authoring Paradigm | Internal Architecture | Representative Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Timeline-Based (Icon/Flow-Based)** | Sequenced track of events; events trigger transitions and animations linearly | **Adobe Animate (Flash)**, **Toon Boom**, **Klystran Storyboard** |
| **Card / Page-Based** | Each "screen" of content is a discrete card; navigation is via jumps between cards | **HyperCard (historical)**, modern equivalents in **Articulate Storyline** |
| **Icon / Object-Based** | Multimedia elements are icons on a visual canvas; user defines event-action pairings visually | **Authorware**, **Lectora Inspire**, **H5P** |
| **Script / Code-Based** | Full programmatic control over a runtime engine via JavaScript, Lingo, or LUA | **Unity (with C#)**, **Unreal Engine (with Blueprints/C++)**, **HTML5/CSS3/JS** for web multimedia |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The current scheme explicitly highlights **object-based authoring** and **script-based authoring** as the two dominant modern paradigms, since the older **Macromedia Director (Lingo)** and **Flash (ActionScript)** ecosystems have been deprecated in favor of web standards.

### 2.2 The Multimedia Authoring Lifecycle (Why & How)

The "Why" behind each stage is to **progressively reduce entropy** — moving from chaotic raw assets to a deterministic, distributable runtime artifact.

1. **Requirement Analysis (Why):** Identify the target audience, deliverable platform (mobile, web, desktop, kiosk), and interactivity depth.
2. **Asset Acquisition (How):** Capture or generate raw text, image, audio, video, and 3D model assets using Tier 1 tools.
3. **Asset Editing (How):** Use Tier 1 tools to clean, format, and optimize each asset (e.g., re-sampling audio to 48 kHz, exporting video in H.264 codec).
4. **Project Authoring (How):** Import edited assets into a Tier 2 tool; define scenes, build navigation graph, attach interactivity logic.
5. **Testing (Why):** Validate the runtime on target hardware; check for broken links, missing fonts, codec issues.
6. **Packaging & Distribution (How):** Export the project as a self-contained executable, a web package (HTML5), or burn to optical media.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **quantitative relationships** and **standard metrics** that appear in KTU 2024 question papers for this topic. All formulas are mathematically verifiable and use the safe `\vert` notation for absolute values.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Boundary Conditions / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uncompressed Audio Size | $S_{audio} = R_{s} \times B \times C \times \frac{t}{8}$ | $R_{s}$ = Sample rate (Hz), $B$ = Bit depth (bits), $C$ = Channels, $t$ = duration (s), result in **Bytes** | Estimating storage before compression |
| Uncompressed Video Size | $S_{video} = W \times H \times B_{pp} \times F \times t$ | $W$ = Width (px), $H$ = Height (px), $B_{pp}$ = Bits per pixel, $F$ = Frame rate (fps), $t$ = duration (s) | Justifies why compression is mandatory |
| Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{\text{Uncompressed Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless, $CR \geq 1$ | Quantifies tool efficiency |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\vert V_{signal} \vert}{\vert V_{noise} \vert}\right)$ | Result in **decibels (dB)** | Audio editor quality benchmark ($\geq 60$ dB is consumer-grade) |
| Storage Capacity (Optical Disc) | $C_{disc} = \text{Track density} \times \text{Sector size} \times \text{Layers}$ | CD $\approx$ 700 MB, DVD $\approx$ 4.7 GB/side, Blu-ray $\approx$ 25 GB/layer | Authoring-to-disc capacity planning |
| Effective Project File Size | $S_{proj} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{asset_{i}} + S_{overhead}$ | $S_{overhead} \approx 2\text{–}10\%$ of total assets | Sizing the authoring project for backup / collaboration |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast Engineering:** Editors use **SNR** calculations to certify that an audio asset meets the **EBU R 128** loudness standard ($\,-\,23$ LUFS).
- **Game Development:** Authoring tools (Unity/Unreal) are used to author the runtime, while Maya/Blender are Tier 1 editing tools for 3D meshes.
- **E-Learning:** Rapid authoring tools like **Articulate Storyline** or **Adobe Captivate** package SCORM/xAPI packages that run inside any Learning Management System (LMS).
- **OTT Streaming:** Video editing tools (Premiere Pro) export **H.264/H.265 (HEVC)** files consumed by authoring pipelines that generate **HLS/DASH manifests** for adaptive bitrate streaming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Why Uncompressed Video Is Practically Impossible Without Editing Tools

The derivation below demonstrates, using a **Full HD 1080p** example, why an authoring pipeline must always include a video editing step to **transcode raw footage into a compressed codec** before distribution.

**Given (Step 1):**
- Resolution: $W = 1920$ pixels, $H = 1080$ pixels
- Color depth: $B_{pp} = 24$ bits per pixel (8 bits × 3 channels RGB)
- Frame rate: $F = 30$ fps (NTSC standard)
- Duration: $t = 60$ seconds (one minute of footage)

**Step 2 — Compute the size of a single uncompressed frame.**

$$
S_{frame} = W \times H \times B_{pp}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{frame} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \\
&= 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 \\
&= 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits per frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert bits to megabytes (divide by 8 bits/byte, then by $1024^2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{frame} &= \frac{49{,}766{,}400}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} \\
&\approx 5.93 \text{ MB per frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the per-second data rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{sec} &= S_{frame} \times F \\
&= 5.93 \text{ MB} \times 30 \\
&\approx 177.9 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 60-second uncompressed video size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{video} &= R_{sec} \times t \\
&= 177.9 \text{ MB/s} \times 60 \text{ s} \\
&\approx 10{,}674 \text{ MB} \\
&\approx 10.42 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with a typical consumer storage medium.**

A single-layer **DVD** holds approximately $4.7$ GB. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR_{required} &= \frac{S_{uncompressed}}{C_{DVD}} \\
&= \frac{10.42 \text{ GB}}{4.7 \text{ GB}} \\
&\approx 2.22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with practical streaming bandwidth.**

A typical broadband connection supports $5$ Mbps. The required rate in Mbps is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{Mbps} &= 177.9 \text{ MB/s} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} \\
&\approx 1423.2 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **285× greater** than typical broadband. Hence, a video editor must transcode the raw footage using a codec like **H.264** or **H.265** to bring the bitrate down by a factor of 50–200.

**Conclusion:** A 1-minute uncompressed 1080p clip is **$\approx 10.42$ GB**, demonstrating that **video editing tools are not optional** in any practical authoring workflow — they are mathematically necessary.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Tool for the Right Job

| Media Asset | Required Editing Step | Tool Category | Authoring Step | Tool Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spoken narration script | Spell-check, font selection | Text editor (Tier 1) | Embed as caption track | Script-based (Tier 2) |
| Product photograph | Background removal, color correction | Image editor (Tier 1) | Place inside an interactive hotspot | Object-based (Tier 2) |
| Background score (.wav) | Noise reduction, normalization | Audio editor (Tier 1) | Loop it across multiple scenes | Timeline-based (Tier 2) |
| Live-action interview | Cut, splice, add lower-third graphic | Video editor (Tier 1) | Link to a chapter menu button | Card-based (Tier 2) |
| 3D character model | Rigging, skinning, keyframe animation | Animation editor (Tier 1) | Trigger on user click via scripting | Script-based (Tier 2) |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Compression Ratio Calculation

Suppose a raw audio asset of duration $t = 120$ seconds is captured at $R_s = 44{,}100$ Hz, $B = 16$ bits, $C = 2$ channels. The MP3 encoder reduces it from $21.10$ MB to $2.11$ MB.

**Step 1 — Compute uncompressed size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{audio} &= 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 \times \frac{120}{8} \\
&= 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 \times 15 \\
&= 21{,}168{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 20.18 \text{ MiB}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using the $1$ MiB $= 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes convention, the file is $\approx 20.18$ MB.)

**Step 2 — Compute compression ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{S_{uncompressed}}{S_{compressed}} \\
&= \frac{20.18 \text{ MB}}{2.11 \text{ MB}} \\
&\approx 9.57
\end{aligned}
$$

The encoder achieves a **$9.57{:}1$ compression ratio**, typical of high-quality **MP3 192 kbps CBR** encoding.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note
All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `nRaw`, `nEdit`, `nAuthor`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting tags.

### 4.2 Diagram A — The Multimedia Authoring Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nReq["REQUIREMENT ANALYSIS"] --> nAcq["ASSET ACQUISITION"]
    nAcq --> nEdit["TIER 1 EDITING LAYER"]
    nEdit --> nText["TEXT EDITOR"]
    nEdit --> nImg["IMAGE EDITOR"]
    nEdit --> nAud["AUDIO EDITOR"]
    nEdit --> nVid["VIDEO EDITOR"]
    nEdit --> nAnim["ANIMATION EDITOR"]
    nText --> nAuthor["TIER 2 AUTHORING LAYER"]
    nImg --> nAuthor
    nAud --> nAuthor
    nVid --> nAuthor
    nAnim --> nAuthor
    nAuthor --> nTime["TIMELINE BASED"]
    nAuthor --> nCard["CARD BASED"]
    nAuthor --> nObj["OBJECT BASED"]
    nAuthor --> nScript["SCRIPT BASED"]
    nTime --> nTest["TESTING AND QA"]
    nCard --> nTest
    nObj --> nTest
    nScript --> nTest
    nTest --> nDist["PACKAGING AND DISTRIBUTION"]
    nDist --> nWeb["WEB HTML5"]
    nDist --> nDisc["OPTICAL DISC"]
    nDist --> nMob["MOBILE APP"]
    nDist --> nDesk["DESKTOP EXE"]
```

### 4.3 Diagram B — Authoring Paradigm Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nStart["CHOOSE AUTHORING TOOL"] --> nQ1{"INTERACTIVITY COMPLEX"}
    nQ1 -->|NO SIMPLE| nCard["CARD BASED E.G. ARTICULATE"]
    nQ1 -->|YES MEDIUM| nQ2{"CODE REQUIRED"}
    nQ2 -->|NO VISUAL| nObj["OBJECT BASED E.G. LECTORA"]
    nQ2 -->|YES PROGRAMMATIC| nQ3{"DELIVERABLE TARGET"}
    nQ3 -->|WEB| nScript1["SCRIPT BASED HTML5 JAVASCRIPT"]
    nQ3 -->|GAME OR 3D| nScript2["SCRIPT BASED UNITY OR UNREAL"]
    nQ3 -->|MOBILE| nScript3["SCRIPT BASED REACT NATIVE OR FLUTTER"]
```

### 4.4 Diagram C — The Editing Tool Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nIn["RAW MEDIA INPUT"] --> nIm["IMPORT MODULE"]
    nIm --> nBuf["MEMORY BUFFER"]
    nBuf --> nProc["PROCESSING PIPELINE"]
    nProc --> nFilter["FILTERS AND EFFECTS"]
    nProc --> nTransform["GEOMETRIC TRANSFORM"]
    nProc --> nCodec["CODEC ENCODER DECODER"]
    nFilter --> nOut["RENDER ENGINE"]
    nTransform --> nOut
    nCodec --> nOut
    nOut --> nExp["EXPORTED MEDIA FILE"]
    nBuf -. METADATA .-> nMeta["PROJECT METADATA STORE"]
```

### 4.5 Diagram D — Data Flow from Authoring to Final Deliverable

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nAsset["EDITED ASSETS TIER 1"] --> nProj["AUTHORING PROJECT FILE E.G. .APRX .STORY"]
    nProj --> nComp["AUTHORING COMPILER"]
    nComp --> nRun["RUNTIME ENGINE E.G. SCORM PLAYER OR WEBASSEMBLY"]
    nRun --> nUser["END USER INTERACTION"]
    nUser --> nEvent["EVENT TRIGGER"]
    nEvent --> nRun
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** The four diagrams above are not isolated. In the examination, a single 14-mark question may ask you to draw the **authoring pipeline (Diagram A)** *and* explain the **paradigm choice (Diagram B)** for a given scenario. Memorize the labels of each box in uppercase form.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Multimedia Authoring Tool" and list any two examples of object-based authoring tools.*

**Model Answer:**
A multimedia authoring tool is a software application that allows a developer to **assemble, integrate, and orchestrate pre-edited media assets** (text, image, audio, video, animation) into a single interactive runtime deliverable. The tool provides facilities for **navigation design, event handling, and final packaging** without requiring low-level code in many cases.

Two examples of **object-based** authoring tools are:
1. **Lectora Inspire** (desktop e-learning suite)
2. **Authorware** (legacy Macromedia/Adobe product)

(Acceptable alternatives: **H5P** for web-based object authoring, **Articulate Storyline 360** for hybrid paradigm.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between linear editing and non-linear editing in multimedia production.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Linear Editing | Non-Linear Editing |
|---|---|---|
| Access pattern | Sequential; must process from start to end | Random access; any segment can be edited independently |
| Media medium | Typically tape-based (VHS, Betacam) | File-based (digital files on disk/SSD) |
| Insertion cost | Inserting a new clip in the middle requires re-recording all subsequent content | Insertion is instantaneous; no re-recording needed |
| Modern usage | Largely obsolete in 2024 workflows | Industry standard (Premiere Pro, DaVinci Resolve) |
| Hardware | Two source decks + record deck | Single computer with software |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Scenario-Based

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

*You are appointed as a multimedia developer to create an **interactive e-learning module on "Renewable Energy Sources"** for Class 10 students. The module must include: (i) an introductory 2-minute animated video, (ii) clickable diagrams of solar, wind, and hydro systems, (iii) a quiz section with 5 multiple-choice questions, and (iv) a printable certificate upon completion.*

**(a)** Identify the **editing tools** you would use for each of the four media components, justifying your choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Select a suitable **authoring tool** (with paradigm justification) and describe the **step-by-step authoring workflow** you would follow to assemble the final SCORM-compliant package. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Editing Tools Selection**

- **Introductory 2-minute animated video → Adobe After Effects (Animation Editor, Tier 1)**
  Justification: After Effects is the industry standard for 2D motion graphics and character animation. It supports **shape layers, Puppet Tool rigging, and keyframe-based tweening**, all of which are required to animate a 2-minute explainer video with synchronized narration.

- **Clickable diagrams of solar, wind, and hydro systems → Adobe Illustrator + Adobe Photoshop (Image Editors, Tier 1)**
  Justification: Illustrator produces **vector diagrams** that scale without pixelation when the student zooms in; Photoshop is used to add **photorealistic textures** (e.g., solar panel cells, wind turbine blades) on raster layers.

- **Quiz section → Notepad++ or Visual Studio Code (Text Editor, Tier 1) for SCORM manifest, plus any audio recorder like Audacity for the "correct answer" ding.**
  Justification: SCORM manifests are **XML/IMS-based**; a plain text editor with syntax highlighting is required to author the `imsmanifest.xml`. Audacity is used to record short audio feedback cues.

- **Printable certificate → Adobe InDesign or Microsoft Word (Layout/Text Editor, Tier 1).**
  Justification: InDesign provides **CMYK color management and vector PDF export**, ensuring the certificate prints crisply on any standard A4 printer.

**(b) Authoring Tool Selection and Workflow**

- **Recommended Authoring Tool: Articulate Storyline 360 (Object-Based + Timeline Hybrid Paradigm).**
  Justification: Storyline is **SCORM 1.2 / 2004 / xAPI compliant** out-of-the-box, has built-in **variable tracking for the quiz score**, supports **triggers-and-actions object-based logic** (no code needed), and can generate a **printable certificate slide** as a fallback for the SCORM certificate.

- **Step-by-Step Authoring Workflow:**
  1. Create a **new Storyline project** sized at $1280 \times 720$ px (16:9 HD).
  2. **Scene 1 — Intro:** Import the After Effects video (exported as `.mp4` H.264, 1920×1080, 30 fps) and place it on a slide with auto-play.
  3. **Scene 2 — Diagrams:** Insert three image slides; convert each diagram into a **clickable hotspot** using Storyline's "hotspot" object type, linking to a layer containing detailed descriptions.
  4. **Scene 3 — Quiz:** Use Storyline's "Graded Question" bank to create 5 multiple-choice questions; set pass mark to 60%.
  5. **Scene 4 — Certificate:** Add a result slide that displays a personalized certificate with the learner's name (pulled from the LMS registration data via Storyline variables). Enable the **"Print" trigger**.
  6. **Test:** Run the Storyline preview; test the SCORM package by uploading to a SCORM Cloud sandbox; verify score reporting.
  7. **Publish:** Export as **SCORM 2004 4th Edition** package; deliver the `.zip` to the LMS administrator.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Theoretical

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **four major paradigms** of multimedia authoring with one example tool for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5-minute uncompressed stereo audio clip is recorded at a sample rate of 48 kHz and 16-bit depth. Calculate: (i) the uncompressed file size in MB, (ii) the data rate in kbps, and (iii) the compression ratio if the file is later encoded to AAC at 128 kbps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Major Authoring Paradigms**

1. **Timeline-Based (Icon-and-Flow):** Media elements and events are arranged on a horizontal timeline. The author places icons at specific time points; the runtime plays them in sequence. *Example: Adobe Animate (formerly Flash).*

2. **Card / Page-Based:** Each screen of content is a discrete card; the author defines jump links between cards. *Example: HyperCard (historical), modern equivalents in Articulate Rise.*

3. **Icon / Object-Based:** Media elements appear as draggable icons on a visual canvas; the author connects them with event-action arrows. *Example: Adobe Authorware, Lectora Inspire.*

4. **Script / Code-Based:** Full programmatic control via a scripting language binding a runtime engine. *Example: Unity (C#), HTML5 + JavaScript, Unreal Engine (C++/Blueprints).*

**(b) Numerical Calculation**

**Given:** $t = 5 \times 60 = 300$ s, $R_s = 48{,}000$ Hz, $B = 16$ bits, $C = 2$ channels (stereo).

**(i) Uncompressed file size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{audio} &= R_s \times B \times C \times \frac{t}{8} \\
&= 48{,}000 \times 16 \times 2 \times \frac{300}{8} \\
&= 48{,}000 \times 16 \times 2 \times 37.5 \\
&= 57{,}600{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 57.6 \text{ MB (decimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In mebibytes: $S_{audio} = \frac{57{,}600{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 54.93$ MiB.

**(ii) Data rate in kbps**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{kbps} &= R_s \times B \times C \div 1000 \\
&= 48{,}000 \times 16 \times 2 \div 1000 \\
&= 1{,}536 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Compression ratio for AAC at 128 kbps**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{R_{original}}{R_{compressed}} \\
&= \frac{1536 \text{ kbps}}{128 \text{ kbps}} \\
&= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

The compression ratio is **$12{:}1$**, which is consistent with high-quality **AAC-LC** encoding for stereo music.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse authoring with programming.** Many students write full HTML/JavaScript code in answer to "authoring tool" questions. The KTU rubric awards marks for **identifying the paradigm and justifying the choice**, not for writing code.
> 2. **Numerical answers must show ALL substitution steps.** A common mark-loss is omitting the unit conversion from **bits to bytes** (dividing by 8) before converting to MB. A 7-mark calculation question typically awards 1 mark for stating the formula, 3 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final answer, and 1 mark for unit + sanity check.
> 3. **Never use the term "Macromedia Flash" or "ActionScript" in 2024 scheme answers** without a deprecation disclaimer. KTU 2024 explicitly marks these as legacy. Mention **HTML5 + JavaScript** instead.
> 4. **"Authoring is the same as editing"** is a guaranteed 0-mark answer. Always state that **editing operates on a single media primitive, while authoring orchestrates multiple media primitives into a runtime project**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Editing Tools** = Component-level (Tier 1): Photoshop, Audacity, Premiere Pro, After Effects, Word.
- **Authoring Tools** = Project-level (Tier 2): Storyline, Unity, Animate, Lectora, HTML5 stack.
- **Four Authoring Paradigms:** Timeline, Card, Object, Script — remember one example tool for each.
- **Linear vs Non-Linear Editing:** Tape = linear; Disk = non-linear. Modern workflows are non-linear.
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $S_{audio} = R_s \times B \times C \times \frac{t}{8}$
  - $S_{video} = W \times H \times B_{pp} \times F \times t$
  - $CR = \frac{\text{Uncompressed}}{\text{Compressed}}$
  - $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\vert V_s \vert}{\vert V_n \vert}\right)$
- **Frame Rate Standards:** 24 / 25 / 30 / 60 fps — associate with cinema / PAL / NTSC / gaming.
- **Sample Rate Standards:** 44.1 kHz (CD), 48 kHz (video/DVD).
- **Optical Disc Capacities:** CD 700 MB, DVD 4.7 GB, Blu-ray 25 GB.
- **SCORM** = Sharable Content Object Reference Model; mandatory for e-learning authoring tool output.
- **Codecs to remember:** H.264 (video), H.265/HEVC (video), MP3 (audio), AAC (audio), PNG (image lossless), JPEG (image lossy).
- **Authoring vs Programming:** The runtime engine (SCORM player, web browser, Unity engine) executes the project; the author only configures the project file.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphics and Image Data Representation - Graphics/Image Data Types
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Graphics and Image Data Representation - Graphics/Image Data Types

# Graphics and Image Data Representation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of KTU's PECNT527 Multimedia Technologies syllabus, **Graphics and Image Data Representation** refers to the systematic encoding, storage, and rendering of two-dimensional visual information using mathematical and binary structures. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, this encompasses two primary families of visual data: **Raster (Bitmap) Graphics**, which represent images as a 2D grid of pixels, and **Vector Graphics**, which represent images as geometric primitives defined by mathematical equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Image data types are classified primarily by **bit-depth** (number of bits per pixel), which directly determines **color fidelity**, **storage requirements**, and **computational complexity** during rendering and compression.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two artists drawing the same portrait:

- **Raster Artist (Bitmap)**: Uses millions of tiny colored squares (mosaic tiles) to recreate the portrait. The more squares you give him, the closer to reality. But if you zoom in too much, you see the individual squares.

- **Vector Artist (Geometric)**: Instead of tiles, he writes down *instructions* like *"draw a circle of radius 3cm at coordinate (5,10), filled with red."* No matter how much you zoom in, the instructions always produce a crisp shape because they describe the *idea*, not the pixels.

**Pixel** = *Picture Element*, the smallest addressable dot of color on a screen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expected Value):** The industry reference resolution is **96 DPI** (Dots Per Inch) for web/screen media and **300 DPI** for professional print media. The full HD reference resolution is **1920 × 1080 = 2,073,600 pixels ≈ 2.07 Megapixels**.

### 1.3 Bit-Depth: The Core Dimension of Image Types

The most fundamental property distinguishing image data types is **bit-depth** — the number of binary bits used to represent each pixel. This single parameter dictates:

1. **Color Palette Size** = $2^n$ where $n$ = bit-depth.
2. **Storage per Pixel** = $n$ bits.
3. **Perceptual Smoothness** of gradients and tonal transitions.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"More Bits → More Colors → More Realism → More Memory"*. Trade-off is always at the heart of multimedia engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Graphics/Image Data Types

The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes image data types along three orthogonal axes:

1. **By Bit-Depth (Color Capacity)** — 1-bit, 8-bit, 16-bit, 24-bit, 32-bit.
2. **By Representation Model** — Raster (Bitmap) vs Vector (Geometric).
3. **By Color Model** — Grayscale, Indexed (Pseudo-color), RGB, CMYK, YCbCr, HSV.

### 2.2 Raster Image Types — Detailed Taxonomy

#### 2.2.1 1-Bit Image (Binary / Monochrome)

- Each pixel uses **1 bit** → 2 possible values: 0 (Black) or 1 (White).
- Palette size: $2^1 = 2$ colors.
- Commonly used for: **Barcodes, QR codes, fax documents, scanned text, line art**.
- File format: **BMP (1-bit mode), TIFF (Group 4 compression), PNG-1**.

#### 2.2.2 8-Bit Grayscale Image

- Each pixel uses **8 bits** → $2^8 = 256$ distinct shades of gray.
- The value **0** represents pure black and **255** represents pure white.
- Used in: **Medical imaging (X-rays, MRI preliminary scans), monochrome photography, scientific visualization**.
- Storage per pixel: exactly **1 byte**.

#### 2.2.3 8-Bit Color Image (Indexed Color / Pseudo-Color)

- Uses a **Color Look-Up Table (CLUT)** or **Palette**.
- Each pixel stores an 8-bit *index* (0–255) that points to a 24-bit RGB entry in the palette.
- **Critical KTU Point:** Display quality depends on the palette's suitability, not on bit-depth alone.
- File formats: **GIF (mandatory 8-bit indexed), PNG-8, BMP-8**.
- **Maximum 256 simultaneous colors** — ideal for logos, icons, web graphics with flat colors.

#### 2.2.4 16-Bit Color Image (High Color)

- Distribution: typically **5 bits Red + 6 bits Green + 5 bits Blue (RGB565)**.
- Green gets an extra bit because the human eye is most sensitive to green wavelengths.
- Total colors: $2^{16} = 65{,}536$.
- Used in: **Older display adapters, low-end LCD panels, embedded systems**.
- Limitation: **No alpha (transparency) channel** in standard RGB565.

#### 2.2.5 24-Bit Color Image (True Color)

- Distribution: **8 bits each for Red, Green, Blue** (RGB888).
- Total colors: $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \approx 16.7$ million colors.
- This exceeds the human eye's discriminable color space (~10 million colors).
- File formats: **JPEG, BMP-24, PNG-24, TIFF**.
- This is the **de-facto standard for photographic content** and is the reference baseline in KTU problems.

#### 2.2.6 32-Bit Color Image (True Color + Alpha)

- Adds an **8-bit Alpha Channel** for transparency representation.
- Channels: **Alpha (8) + Blue (8) + Green (8) + Red (8)** — stored as BGRA on little-endian systems.
- Alpha = 0 → fully transparent; Alpha = 255 → fully opaque.
- File formats: **PNG-32, TIFF-32, BMP-32, PSD**.

### 2.3 Vector Graphics Representation

Unlike raster, vector images store **drawing commands**, not pixel arrays. Common primitives:

| Primitive | Mathematical Form | Parameters Required |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Point | $(x, y)$ | 2 |
| Line Segment | Endpoints $(x_1,y_1), (x_2,y_2)$ | 4 |
| Circle | Center $(h,k)$, radius $r$ | 3 |
| Ellipse | Center $(h,k)$, axes $a,b$ | 4 |
| Polygon | Sequence of $n$ vertices | $2n$ |
| Bezier Curve | Control points $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$ | 8 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **File formats for vector graphics:** **SVG** (Scalable Vector Graphics — XML-based, web standard), **AI** (Adobe Illustrator), **EPS** (Encapsulated PostScript), **PDF** (can embed both), **CDR** (CorelDRAW).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Number of colors from bit-depth | $C = 2^n$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Bits per pixel | $b = \log_2(C)$ | bits/pixel |
| 3 | Raw file size (bits) | $S_{bits} = W \times H \times b$ | bits |
| 4 | Raw file size (bytes) | $S_{bytes} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8}$ | bytes |
| 5 | File size (KB) | $S_{KB} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024}$ | kilobytes |
| 6 | File size (MB) | $S_{MB} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}$ | megabytes |
| 7 | Aspect Ratio | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{W}{H}$ | ratio |
| 8 | Total display pixels | $N = W \times H$ | pixels |
| 9 | Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | dimensionless $\geq 1$ |
| 10 | Memory bandwidth | $\text{BW} = W \times H \times b \times f_{refresh}$ | bits/second |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** All pipe symbols inside cell expressions have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown parser breakage. In handwritten exams, write `|x|` normally.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Web Engineering**: 24-bit true-color is the universal baseline; PNG-8 used for icons.
- **Medical Imaging**: DICOM standard uses 12-bit or 16-bit grayscale for diagnostic accuracy.
- **Satellite Imaging**: 11–16 bit per band to capture subtle spectral differences.
- **Embedded Displays**: 16-bit RGB565 is preferred in microcontrollers (STM32, ESP32) to balance RAM and visual quality.
- **Print Engineering**: CMYK 32-bit color separation is mandatory for offset printing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of Core Image Data Formulas

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of File Size Formula

Consider an image of width $W$ and height $H$ pixels, with each pixel encoded using $b$ bits.

**Step 1 — Count total pixels.**

Each row contains $W$ pixels, and there are $H$ rows. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{pixels}} &= W \times H
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Multiplication gives total grid elements in a 2D rectangle.

**Step 2 — Multiply by bits per pixel.**

Since each pixel occupies exactly $b$ bits in memory, total bits required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{bits}} &= N_{\text{pixels}} \times b \\
                &= W \times H \times b
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Distributing the per-pixel cost over the entire grid.

**Step 3 — Convert bits to bytes.**

Because **1 byte = 8 bits**, divide by 8:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{W \times H \times b}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Byte is the fundamental addressing unit in computer memory.

**Step 4 — Convert bytes to kilobytes (KiB).**

Since **1 KB = 1024 bytes**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{KB}} = \frac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Scaling down the unit using the binary prefix.

**Step 5 — Convert to megabytes (MiB).**

Since **1 MB = 1024 KB**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{MB}} = \frac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Cascading unit conversion.

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Color Palette Size

For a pixel encoded with $n$ bits, each bit can independently take one of **2 states** (0 or 1). With $n$ independent bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} \\
  &= 2^n
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The multiplication principle of combinatorics — independent choices multiply.

#### 3.1.3 Reverse Derivation: Bit-Depth from Color Count

Given that an image must display $C$ unique colors, the minimum required bit-depth is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = \log_2(C)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Inverse exponential operation. If $C$ is not a power of 2, round **up** to the next integer bit-depth.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Style)

#### **Example 1: True-Color File Size**

**Problem:** Find the uncompressed storage required for a $1920 \times 1080$ image at 24-bit true color. Express in MB.

**Given:**
- $W = 1920$ pixels
- $H = 1080$ pixels
- $b = 24$ bits/pixel

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{bits}} &= 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \\
                &= 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 \\
                &= 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Multiply width × height to get total pixels (≈ 2.07 megapixels).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{bytes}} &= \frac{49{,}766{,}400}{8} \\
                 &= 6{,}220{,}800 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Convert bits to bytes by dividing by 8.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{MB}} &= \frac{6{,}220{,}800}{1024 \times 1024} \\
              &= \frac{6{,}220{,}800}{1{,}048{,}576} \\
              &\approx 5.93 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Convert bytes to megabytes by dividing by $1024^2$.

**Final Answer:** **$\approx 5.93$ MB** (uncompressed).

---

#### **Example 2: Bit-Depth from Color Count**

**Problem:** A scanner must capture $1{,}000{,}000$ distinct color shades. What minimum bit-depth is required?

**Given:** $C = 1{,}000{,}000$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \log_2(1{,}000{,}000) \\
  &= \frac{\log_{10}(1{,}000{,}000)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
  &= \frac{6}{0.30103} \\
  &\approx 19.93 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Apply logarithm change-of-base.

Since bit-depth must be an integer and we cannot fall short of $C$, round up:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\min} &= 20 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Rounding up ensures $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \geq 1{,}000{,}000$.

**Final Answer:** **20 bits per pixel**.

---

#### **Example 3: Compression Ratio Calculation**

**Problem:** A 24-bit, $1024 \times 768$ image compresses from **2.25 MB** to **300 KB**. Calculate compression ratio.

**Given:**
- Original size = $2.25$ MB $= 2{,}359{,}296$ bytes
- Compressed size = $300$ KB $= 307{,}200$ bytes

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= \frac{\text{Original}}{\text{Compressed}} \\
          &= \frac{2{,}359{,}296}{307{,}200} \\
          &\approx 7.68
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Compression ratio is unitless; always express as a ratio $\geq 1$.

**Final Answer:** **CR $\approx 7.68 : 1$** (commonly reported as "7.68× compression").

---

#### **Example 4: Bandwidth Estimation**

**Problem:** A $1920 \times 1080$, 24-bit display refreshes at 60 Hz. Find memory bandwidth in GB/s.

**Given:**
- $W \times H = 2{,}073{,}600$
- $b = 24$ bits
- $f = 60$ Hz

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{\text{bits/s}} &= 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 \times 60 \\
                          &= 2{,}985{,}984{,}000 \text{ bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Bits per frame × frames per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{\text{GB/s}} &= \frac{2{,}985{,}984{,}000}{8 \times 10^9} \\
                        &\approx 0.373 \text{ GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Convert to GB/s using decimal prefix ($10^9$) since GPU bandwidth is conventionally rated in decimal units.

**Final Answer:** **≈ 0.373 GB/s** ≈ **373 MB/s** (this matches HDMI 1.0 baseline).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Image Data Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Visual Data Representation] --> B[Raster Graphics]
    A --> C[Vector Graphics]
    B --> D[1-Bit Monochrome]
    B --> E[8-Bit Grayscale]
    B --> F[8-Bit Indexed Color]
    B --> G[16-Bit High Color]
    B --> H[24-Bit True Color]
    B --> I[32-Bit True Color Plus Alpha]
    C --> J[SVG XML Based]
    C --> K[AI Adobe Illustrator]
    C --> L[EPS PostScript]
    C --> M[PDF Portable Document]
    D --> D1[Barcode QR Code Fax Document]
    E --> E1[X-Ray MRI Document Scan]
    F --> F1[Logos Web Icons Sprites]
    G --> G1[Embedded LCD RGB565]
    H --> H1[JPEG PNG24 BMP24]
    I --> I1[PNG32 PSD UI Mockups]
    J --> J1[Web Icons Responsive Logos]
    K --> K1[Print Design Branding]
```

### 4.2 Raster Image Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawData[Raw Pixel Array W x H] --> bitDepth[Bit Depth n bits]
    bitDepth --> colorModel[Color Model Selection]
    colorModel --> rgbPath[RGB Model]
    colorModel --> grayPath[Grayscale Model]
    colorModel --> idxPath[Indexed CLUT Model]
    colorModel --> alphaPath[RGBA Model]
    rgbPath --> storage1[True Color 24 bits]
    grayPath --> storage2[Grayscale 8 bits]
    idxPath --> storage3[Indexed 8 bits]
    alphaPath --> storage4[True Color Plus Alpha 32 bits]
    storage1 --> compress[Optional Compression]
    storage2 --> compress
    storage3 --> compress
    storage4 --> compress
    compress --> output[File Format BMP PNG JPEG TIFF]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Correct Image Data Type

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Choose Image Data Type] --> q1{Need transparency?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Color fidelity critical?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Photographic content?}
    q2 -- Yes --> use32[Use 32 bit RGBA PNG32]
    q2 -- No --> use24alpha[Use 24 bit with matte channel]
    q3 -- Yes --> use24b[Use 24 bit True Color JPEG PNG24]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Less than 256 unique colors?}
    q4 -- Yes --> use8idx[Use 8 bit Indexed PNG8 GIF]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Monochrome only?}
    q5 -- Yes --> q6{Text or line art?}
    q6 -- Yes --> use1bit[Use 1 bit Monochrome TIFF G4]
    q6 -- No --> use8gray[Use 8 bit Grayscale PNG]
```

### 4.4 Bit Allocation Diagram for 16-bit RGB565

```mermaid
graph LR
    pixel[Pixel Word 16 bits] --> r5[R 5 bits 0 to 31]
    pixel --> g6[G 6 bits 0 to 63]
    pixel --> b5[B 5 bits 0 to 31]
    r5 --> note1[Red channel reduced]
    g6 --> note2[Green channel extra bit for human eye sensitivity]
    b5 --> note3[Blue channel reduced]
```

### 4.5 Channel Architecture of 32-bit RGBA

```mermaid
graph LR
    word[32 bit Pixel] --> chA[Alpha 8 bits]
    word --> chB[Blue 8 bits]
    word --> chG[Green 8 bits]
    word --> chR[Red 8 bits]
    chA --> descA[Transparency 0 transparent to 255 opaque]
    chB --> descB[Blue intensity]
    chG --> descG[Green intensity]
    chR --> descR[Red intensity]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**Differentiate between Raster Graphics and Vector Graphics. Give one example file format for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Raster Graphics | Vector Graphics |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Representation | Grid of pixels with color values | Mathematical geometric primitives |
| Resolution | Resolution-dependent (pixelation on zoom) | Resolution-independent (infinite zoom) |
| Best Use | Photographic images | Logos, fonts, technical drawings |
| Example Format | JPEG, PNG, BMP | SVG, AI, EPS |
| Storage Model | Per-pixel color data | Drawing commands/equations |

**Allocation:** 1 mark each for two valid differences + 1 mark for examples. ✓

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**An image uses 16 bits per pixel. Calculate the total number of distinct colors it can represent. Mention one disadvantage of this format compared to 24-bit true color.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2^{16} \\
  &= 65{,}536 \text{ colors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Disadvantage (1 mark):** Limited color palette causes **banding artifacts** in smooth gradients (e.g., sky, skin tones), and cannot support an alpha transparency channel in standard RGB565 mode. Photographs exhibit visible color stepping compared to 24-bit true color.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Image Data Types and Storage*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following image data types with their bit-depth, color capacity, and a typical use case: (i) 1-bit binary, (ii) 8-bit indexed color, (iii) 24-bit true color.

**b) [7 Marks]** A digital camera captures a $4000 \times 3000$ image at 24 bits per pixel. Calculate (i) total pixels, (ii) uncompressed file size in MB, (iii) memory bandwidth required to display this image at 30 fps refresh rate.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Image Data Types Explanation:**

**(i) 1-bit Binary Image:**
- **Bit-depth:** 1 bit per pixel.
- **Color capacity:** $2^1 = 2$ colors (black and white only).
- **Typical use case:** Fax transmission, QR codes, scanned text documents, old newspapers, and barcode labels.

**(ii) 8-bit Indexed Color Image:**
- **Bit-depth:** 8 bits per pixel (index).
- **Color capacity:** Up to $2^8 = 256$ simultaneous colors chosen from a larger palette via a CLUT.
- **Typical use case:** Web graphics (GIF format), logos with flat colors, animated sprites in 2D games.

**(iii) 24-bit True Color Image:**
- **Bit-depth:** 24 bits per pixel (8 bits each for R, G, B).
- **Color capacity:** $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \approx 16.7$ million colors.
- **Typical use case:** Digital photography, web banners, full-color print media, video frame buffers.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W = 4000$ pixels, $H = 3000$ pixels
- $b = 24$ bits/pixel
- $f_{refresh} = 30$ Hz

**(i) Total Pixels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= W \times H \\
  &= 4000 \times 3000 \\
  &= 12{,}000{,}000 \text{ pixels} \\
  &= 12 \text{ megapixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Uncompressed File Size in MB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{bits}} &= 12{,}000{,}000 \times 24 \\
                &= 288{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{MB}} &= \frac{288{,}000{,}000}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} \\
              &= \frac{288{,}000{,}000}{8{,}388{,}608} \\
              &\approx 34.33 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Memory Bandwidth at 30 fps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{\text{bits/s}} &= 12{,}000{,}000 \times 24 \times 30 \\
                          &= 8{,}640{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BW}_{\text{GB/s}} &= \frac{8{,}640{,}000{,}000}{8 \times 10^9} \\
                        &= 1.08 \text{ GB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **12 megapixels**, (ii) **≈ 34.33 MB**, (iii) **≈ 1.08 GB/s**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Color Models and Vector Graphics*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

**a) [7 Marks]** Compare the RGB, CMYK, and HSV color models. State one engineering application where each is most appropriate.

**b) [7 Marks]** A logo is designed as a vector graphic consisting of 50 line segments, 20 circles, and 5 polygons (each polygon has 6 vertices). Calculate the total number of numerical parameters required to store the logo if each parameter occupies 4 bytes. Express the answer in KB.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Color Model Comparison:**

| Property | RGB | CMYK | HSV |
|----------|-----|------|-----|
| Type | Additive (light-based) | Subtractive (pigment-based) | Perceptual (intuitive) |
| Channels | Red, Green, Blue (3) | Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black (4) | Hue, Saturation, Value (3) |
| Primary Use | Displays, scanners, cameras | Offset printing, press media | Color pickers, image editing |
| Application | LCD/OLED screens, web design | Magazine covers, brochures | Photoshop color selection, UI design |

**Engineering Application Justification (one each):**

- **RGB** → Used in **camera sensors and LCD panels** because displays emit light additively.
- **CMYK** → Used in **commercial offset printing presses** because inks absorb light subtractively.
- **HSV** → Used in **Adobe Photoshop eyedropper tool** because artists naturally think in terms of hue (color type) and saturation (vividness).

---

**(b) Vector Storage Calculation:**

**Given parameters per primitive:**

- **Line Segment:** 4 parameters (2 coordinates × 2 endpoints).
- **Circle:** 3 parameters (center h, k + radius r).
- **6-vertex Polygon:** $6 \times 2 = 12$ parameters.

**Total Parameter Count:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{params}} &= (50 \times 4) + (20 \times 3) + (5 \times 12) \\
                  &= 200 + 60 + 60 \\
                  &= 320 \text{ parameters}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step:* Multiply each primitive count by its parameter requirement, then sum.

**Storage in Bytes (4 bytes per parameter):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{bytes}} &= 320 \times 4 \\
                 &= 1280 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Storage in KB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{KB}} &= \frac{1280}{1024} \\
              &\approx 1.25 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **≈ 1.25 KB** — illustrating the storage efficiency of vector graphics over raster for the same content.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mistake is dividing by $10^6$ (decimal MB) instead of $1024 \times 1024$ (binary MiB) or vice versa. KTU expects **binary prefixes** ($1024$) for file-size problems unless explicitly stated.
> 2. **Forgetting the Palette Table:** When asked for 8-bit indexed color file size, students often forget the **CLUT overhead** (256 × 24 bits = 768 bytes). Unless the question explicitly excludes the palette, mention this.
> 3. **Rounding vs. Ceiling:** When computing bit-depth from a given color count, always **round UP** to the next integer — falling short means you cannot represent the required colors.
> 4. **RGB565 Confusion:** Students frequently write 5+5+5 = 15 bits. The correct distribution is **5+6+5 = 16 bits**, with extra bit allocated to green.
> 5. **Vector ≠ Smaller Always:** Vector files become *larger* than raster when the image has thousands of independent primitives (e.g., complex illustrations). Always state *appropriate use case*, not absolute superiority.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Graphics and Image Data Types**

- **Bit-Depth vs Colors:** $C = 2^n$; reverse formula $n = \lceil \log_2(C) \rceil$.
- **Standard Bit-Depths:** 1, 4, 8, 16, 24, 32 — each has well-defined use cases.
- **1-bit:** Black/white only — fax, QR codes.
- **8-bit Grayscale:** $256$ shades, 1 byte/pixel — medical, documents.
- **8-bit Indexed:** 256 colors via CLUT — GIF, PNG-8, web icons.
- **16-bit High Color:** RGB565 = 5+6+5 = **65,536 colors** — embedded displays.
- **24-bit True Color:** RGB888 = **16.7 million colors** — JPEG, PNG-24, photographic standard.
- **32-bit RGBA:** Adds **8-bit alpha** for transparency — PNG-32, UI mockups.
- **File Size Formula:** $S_{\text{MB}} = \dfrac{W \times H \times b}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}$.
- **Bandwidth Formula:** $\text{BW} = W \times H \times b \times f_{refresh}$.
- **Aspect Ratio:** $\text{AR} = W / H$ — standard ratios 4:3, 16:9, 21:9.
- **Raster vs Vector:**
   - Raster → pixel grid → resolution-dependent → JPEG, PNG, BMP, GIF, TIFF.
   - Vector → math primitives → resolution-independent → SVG, AI, EPS, PDF.
- **Color Models:** RGB (additive, displays), CMYK (subtractive, print), HSV (perceptual, editing).
- **Standard Resolutions:** VGA 640×480, HD 1280×720, FHD 1920×1080, 4K 3840×2160.
- **DPI Standards:** Screen **96 DPI**, Print **300 DPI**, Professional Photo **600 DPI**.
- **Compression Ratio:** $\text{CR} = \text{Original} / \text{Compressed}$, dimensionless $\geq 1$.
- **Human Eye Color Limit:** ~10 million distinguishable colors — so 24-bit is perceptually lossless.
- **YCbCr Model:** Used in **JPEG and video compression** — separates luminance (Y) from chrominance (Cb, Cr) for efficient compression exploiting human visual sensitivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Popular File Formats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Popular File Formats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multimedia File Format** is a standardized way of encoding and storing digital multimedia data (text, images, audio, video, and animations) within a computer file. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECNT527, file formats are categorized based on three orthogonal properties:

1. **Container Format** — Defines how the file's metadata, tracks (audio/video/subtitle), and synchronization information are packaged together (e.g., MP4, MKV, AVI).
2. **Codec (Coder-Decoder)** — The actual algorithm used to compress and decompress the raw media stream (e.g., H.264, AAC, MP3).
3. **Compression Scheme** — Whether the encoding preserves all data (**lossless**) or discards perceptually redundant data (**lossy**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):** A *container* is the **box**; a *codec* is the **language inside the box**. An `.mp4` file can contain H.264, H.265, or even MPEG-2 video depending on how it was encoded.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **packing a suitcase for international travel**:

- The **suitcase itself** = the **Container Format** (MP4, AVI, MKV). It dictates the shape, zippers, and compartments.
- The **items inside the suitcase** = the **Codec/Encoded Data** (H.264 video, AAC audio). The items themselves are the actual content.
- The **vacuum-seal bag inside the suitcase** = the **Compression Scheme**. You either fold clothes tightly and lose some shape (lossy) or use a perfect garment bag (lossless).
- The **tag on the suitcase** = the **File Header / Metadata** (duration, resolution, codec ID, bitrate).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU observation:** Students frequently confuse the terms "format" and "codec." If an examiner asks "Is MP4 a codec?", the correct answer is **No — MP4 is a container; H.264 (AVC) is the codec that commonly lives inside it.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

- **Bit Rate:** Measured in **bits per second (bps)**, commonly **kbps** or **Mbps**.
- **Sample Rate (Audio):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)** — e.g., **44.1 kHz** for CD-quality audio.
- **Color Depth:** Measured in **bits per pixel (bpp)** — typically **8, 16, 24, or 32 bpp**.
- **Resolution:** Measured in **pixels (width × height)** — e.g., **1920 × 1080 (Full HD)**.
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** A dimensionless quantity defined later in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Popular File Formats

### 2.1.1 Image File Formats

| Format | Compression | Color Depth | Best Use Case | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **JPEG / JFIF** | Lossy (DCT-based) | 24 bpp | Photographs, web images | Highest weightage — most asked |
| **PNG** | Lossless (DEFLATE) | 24/32 bpp | Logos, transparency, screenshots | Asked for transparency support |
| **GIF** | Lossless (LZW) | 8 bpp (256 colors) | Simple animations | Asked for animation capability |
| **BMP** | Uncompressed / RLE | 1/4/8/24/32 bpp | Windows native, raw pixel data | Asked for "uncompressed" property |
| **TIFF** | Lossless / Lossy | Up to 48 bpp | Professional print, archiving | Asked for "archival" quality |
| **SVG** | Vector / XML-based | N/A (vector) | Icons, scalable graphics | Vector vs raster distinction |
| **WebP** | Lossy & Lossless | 24 bpp | Modern web, smaller than JPEG | Asked as "modern web format" |
| **RAW** | Lossless (sensor data) | 12–14 bpp | Professional photography | Asked for "unprocessed sensor data" |

### 2.1.2 Audio File Formats

| Format | Compression | Sample Rate Support | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **WAV** | Uncompressed (PCM) | Up to 192 kHz | CD-quality standard |
| **MP3 (MPEG-1 Layer III)** | Lossy (Psychoacoustic) | 32–48 kHz | Most universal audio format |
| **AAC (Advanced Audio Coding)** | Lossy | 8–96 kHz | Used in iTunes, YouTube, MP4 |
| **FLAC** | Lossless | Up to 192 kHz | Archival audio |
| **OGG Vorbis** | Lossy (open-source) | Variable | Open-source alternative to MP3 |
| **WMA** | Lossy / Lossless | Variable | Microsoft proprietary |

### 2.1.3 Video File Formats

| Container | Common Video Codecs | Common Audio Codecs | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MP4 (.mp4)** | H.264, H.265 (HEVC), MPEG-4 | AAC, MP3 | De facto standard |
| **AVI (.avi)** | DivX, Xvid, MJPEG | MP3, PCM | Legacy Microsoft format |
| **MKV (.mkv)** | H.264, H.265, VP9 | AC-3, AAC, FLAC | Open-source, multiple tracks |
| **MOV (.mov)** | H.264, ProRes | AAC, ALAC | Apple QuickTime format |
| **WMV (.wmv)** | WMV, VC-1 | WMA | Windows Media |
| **FLV (.flv)** | VP6, H.264 | MP3, AAC | Flash Video (legacy streaming) |
| **WebM (.webm)** | VP8, VP9, AV1 | Vorbis, Opus | Modern open web video |

## 2.2 Compression Theory — The "Why" Behind File Formats

- **Lossless Compression** exploits statistical redundancy. The original data can be perfectly reconstructed. Examples: PNG, FLAC, ZIP.
- **Lossy Compression** exploits perceptual redundancy (Human Visual System / Human Auditory System limitations). It discards data that the human eye or ear cannot easily detect. Examples: JPEG, MP3, MP4 (H.264).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The JPEG standard uses the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** on 8×8 pixel blocks. The MP3 standard uses **Modified Discrete Cosine Transform (MDCT)** combined with a **psychoacoustic model** that removes frequencies masked by louder neighboring frequencies.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula required to solve KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic. Memorize these.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Compression Ratio | Dimensionless; $CR > 1$ means compression achieved |
| 2 | $S = \text{Bit Rate} \times T$ | File Size = Bitrate × Duration | $S$ in **bits**, $T$ in **seconds** |
| 3 | $\text{Bits Per Sample} = \log_2(L)$ | $L$ = number of quantization levels | For audio |
| 4 | $\text{Uncompressed Audio Size} = \text{SR} \times B \times C \times T$ | SR=Sample Rate (Hz), B=bits/sample, C=channels, T=duration (s) | Result in **bits** |
| 5 | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | Measured in **dB**; higher = better quality |
| 6 | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{mn}\sum_{i=0}^{m-1}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\!\left[I(i,j) - K(i,j)\right]^2$ | Mean Squared Error between original $I$ and compressed $K$ | Image quality metric |
| 7 | $\text{Savings \%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage space saved | Expressed in **%** |
| 8 | $\text{Storage (GB)} = \dfrac{\text{Resolution} \times \text{Depth} \times \text{Frames} \times T}{8 \times 10^9}$ | Raw uncompressed video size | **GB** for video storage problems |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Streaming Industry (Netflix, YouTube):** Uses H.264/H.265 inside MP4/WebM containers at variable bitrates (VBR) to adapt to network bandwidth.
- **Medical Imaging (DICOM):** A specialized container format that wraps TIFF/JPEG-encoded images with patient metadata — directly governed by file format standards.
- **Web Development:** Modern formats like **WebP** and **AV1** reduce bandwidth by 25–50% compared to legacy JPEG/MPEG-2.
- **Forensics & Archival:** Lossless formats (PNG, FLAC, TIFF) are mandatory because any data loss is unacceptable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Numerical questions on this topic are almost always one of three types: (i) file size calculation, (ii) compression ratio, (iii) PSNR comparison. Master the derivations below.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compression Ratio and Space Savings

**Problem Statement:** A raw image occupies **4096 KB**. After JPEG compression, the file size becomes **512 KB**. Find the compression ratio and the percentage space saved.

### Step 1 — Identify Original and Compressed Sizes
From the problem:

$$S_{\text{original}} = 4096 \text{ KB}$$

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = 512 \text{ KB}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Compression Ratio Formula

$$CR = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$$

$$CR = \frac{4096 \text{ KB}}{512 \text{ KB}}$$

$$CR = 8$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Space Savings Formula

$$\text{Savings \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \left(\frac{7}{8}\right) \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer

$$\boxed{CR = 8 \quad ; \quad \text{Savings} = 87.5\%}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uncompressed Audio File Size

**Problem Statement:** A song is recorded in stereo (2 channels) at a sample rate of **44.1 kHz** with **16 bits per sample**. The duration is **3 minutes 20 seconds**. Find the uncompressed (WAV) file size in MB.

### Step 1 — Convert Duration to Seconds

$$T = (3 \times 60) + 20 = 200 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Variables

$$\text{SR} = 44{,}100 \text{ Hz}, \quad B = 16 \text{ bits}, \quad C = 2, \quad T = 200 \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Uncompressed Audio Size Formula

$$S = \text{SR} \times B \times C \times T$$

$$S = 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 \times 200$$

$$S = 44{,}100 \times 6400$$

$$S = 282{,}240{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Megabytes

$$S = \frac{282{,}240{,}000}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} \approx 33.65 \text{ MB}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx 33.65 \text{ MB (uncompressed WAV)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Raw Uncompressed Video File Size

**Problem Statement:** A video has resolution **1280 × 720**, color depth **24 bits**, frame rate **30 fps**, and duration **10 seconds**. Find the raw uncompressed size in MB.

### Step 1 — Identify Variables

$$\text{Resolution} = 1280 \times 720 = 921{,}600 \text{ pixels/frame}$$

$$B = 24 \text{ bits/pixel}, \quad F = 30 \text{ fps}, \quad T = 10 \text{ s}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Total Pixels Across All Frames

$$P_{\text{total}} = 921{,}600 \times 30 \times 10 = 276{,}480{,}000 \text{ pixels}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Storage Formula

$$S = \frac{P_{\text{total}} \times B}{8 \times 10^6} \text{ MB}$$

$$S = \frac{276{,}480{,}000 \times 24}{8 \times 10^6}$$

$$S = \frac{6{,}635{,}520{,}000}{8{,}000{,}000}$$

$$S = 829.44 \text{ MB}$$

### Step 4 — Express Result and Justify Codec Need

$$\boxed{S_{\text{raw}} \approx 829.44 \text{ MB for just 10 seconds}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This single calculation **justifies why lossy codecs like H.264 exist.** Without compression, a 2-hour movie at this quality would need $\approx 600$ GB of storage — impractical for both Blu-ray (~50 GB) and streaming.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PSNR Calculation (Image Quality Metric)

**Problem Statement:** An original image $I$ and its compressed version $K$ both have a maximum pixel value of **255**. The Mean Squared Error (MSE) between them is computed as **18.5**. Find the PSNR in dB.

### Step 1 — Apply the PSNR Formula

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$$

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{18.5}\right)$$

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{65025}{18.5}\right)$$

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(3514.86)$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Logarithm

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \times 3.5458$$

$$\boxed{\text{PSNR} \approx 35.46 \text{ dB}}$$

**Quality Interpretation (KTU expects this knowledge):** A PSNR above **30 dB** is considered acceptable for lossy image/video compression. Values above **40 dB** indicate near-imperceptible distortion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multimedia File Format Processing Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **end-to-end functional flow** by which raw media becomes a compressed file format and is later decoded for playback.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[SOURCE MEDIA] --> B1[CAPTURE]
    B1 --> C1[RAW DATA BUFFER]
    C1 --> D1[PRE PROCESSING]
    D1 --> E1[ENCODE CODEC]
    E1 --> F1[CONTAINER MUX]
    F1 --> G1[OUTPUT FILE FORMAT]
    G1 --> H1[STORAGE OR NETWORK]
    H1 --> I1[CONTAINER DEMUX]
    I1 --> J1[DECODE CODEC]
    J1 --> K1[POST PROCESSING]
    K1 --> L1[PLAYBACK DEVICE]
    META[HEADER METADATA] --> F1
    META --> I1
    style A1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

**Block Descriptions (for KTU written answers):**

- **CAPTURE:** Sensor, microphone, or digitizer converts physical signals to digital samples.
- **RAW DATA BUFFER:** Uncompressed stream (PCM for audio, YUV for video, bitmap for images).
- **PRE-PROCESSING:** Noise filtering, deinterlacing, color space conversion.
- **ENCODE CODEC:** Applies DCT/FFT/wavelet transforms + quantization + entropy coding.
- **CONTAINER MUX:** Multiplexes audio, video, and subtitles into a single file with time stamps.
- **HEADER METADATA:** Resolution, bitrate, codec ID, duration, frame rate.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — File Format Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[MULTIMEDIA FILE FORMATS] --> B0[IMAGE]
    A0 --> C0[AUDIO]
    A0 --> D0[VIDEO]
    B0 --> B1[RASTER]
    B0 --> B2[VECTOR]
    B1 --> B3[LOSSLESS PNG BMP TIFF]
    B1 --> B4[LOSSY JPEG WebP]
    B2 --> B5[SVG EPS]
    C0 --> C1[UNCOMPRESSED WAV AIFF]
    C0 --> C2[LOSSY MP3 AAC OGG]
    C0 --> C3[LOSSLESS FLAC ALAC]
    D0 --> D1[CONTAINER LAYER MP4 MKV AVI MOV WebM]
    D0 --> D2[CODEC LAYER H264 H265 VP9 AV1]
    D0 --> D3[AUDIO LAYER AAC MP3 Opus]
    style A0 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style B0 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style C0 fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    style D0 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Lossy vs Lossless Compression Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[INPUT DATA] --> Q1{RECONSTRUCTION REQUIRED}
    Q1 -->|YES EXACT| Q2[LOSSLESS COMPRESSION]
    Q1 -->|NO APPROX OK| Q3[LOSSY COMPRESSION]
    Q2 --> Q4[PNG FLAC ZIP TIFF]
    Q3 --> Q5[JPEG MP3 MP4 H264]
    Q4 --> Q6[USED IN MEDICAL LEGAL ARCHIVAL]
    Q5 --> Q7[USED IN STREAMING WEB GAMING]
    style P1 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between lossy and lossless compression techniques. Give two examples of file formats for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Lossy Compression | Lossless Compression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data after decompression | Approximate, not identical to original | Exact replica of original |
| Compression ratio | High (e.g., 10:1 to 100:1) | Low to moderate (e.g., 2:1 to 3:1) |
| Underlying principle | Removes perceptually redundant data | Removes statistical redundancy |
| Examples | JPEG, MP3, MP4 (H.264) | PNG, FLAC, BMP, ZIP, TIFF |
| Use case | Web, streaming, consumer media | Medical imaging, archival, software |

**(1 mark per row — full 3 marks awarded for a clean table with examples.)**

---
