### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the difference between a container format and a codec. Illustrate with the MP4 example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **container format** defines how multimedia files (audio, video, subtitles, metadata) are packaged and synchronized together. A **codec** (coder–decoder) is the algorithm used to compress and decompress the actual media data.

For example, an **MP4** file is a container (formally ISO Base Media File Format), but the video inside might be encoded with the **H.264 (AVC)** codec and the audio with the **AAC** codec. Therefore, MP4 ≠ codec; it is the wrapper, while H.264 and AAC are the encoders inside it.

**(1 mark — definition of container; 1 mark — definition of codec; 1 mark — MP4/H.264/AAC illustration.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the working of JPEG image compression standard. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Color Space Conversion (1 mark)**
The RGB image is converted to **YCbCr** color space. The **Y** channel carries luminance (brightness); **Cb** and **Cr** carry chrominance (color). The human eye is more sensitive to Y than to Cb/Cr, which enables later subsampling.

**Step 2 — Downsampling of Chrominance (0.5 mark)**
Cb and Cr channels are typically downsampled by a factor of 2 (4:2:0 subsampling) to reduce data by ~50% with minimal perceptual loss.

**Step 3 — Block Splitting (0.5 mark)**
The image is divided into **8 × 8 pixel blocks**. Each block of the Y (or Cb, Cr) channel contains 64 samples.

**Step 4 — Discrete Cosine Transform — DCT (1 mark)**
Each 8×8 block is transformed using DCT, converting spatial domain data into 64 frequency coefficients. The top-left coefficient is the **DC** (average brightness); the rest are **AC** coefficients representing increasing spatial frequencies.

$$F(u,v) = \frac{1}{4} C(u) C(v) \sum_{x=0}^{7} \sum_{y=0}^{7} f(x,y) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{16}\right] \cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{16}\right]$$

where $C(k) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ if $k=0$, else $C(k)=1$.

**Step 5 — Quantization (1 mark)**
Each of the 64 DCT coefficients is divided by a corresponding value from a **quantization table** and rounded. This is the **primary lossy step** — high-frequency coefficients are aggressively quantized (often to zero) because the human eye is less sensitive to fine detail.

**Step 6 — Entropy Coding (1 mark)**
The quantized coefficients are encoded using **Zig-Zag scanning** (to group zeros) followed by **Huffman coding** or **Arithmetic coding** for further lossless compression.

**Step 7 — Advantages and Limitations (2 marks)**

| Advantages | Limitations |
| :--- | :--- |
| High compression ratio (10:1 typical) | Lossy — not suitable for archival |
| Excellent for photographs | Blocking artifacts at high compression |
| Wide hardware/software support | Poor for sharp edges and text |
| Tunable quality via quantization table | No transparency support (alpha channel) |

---

**Q. (b)** A 5-minute stereo audio clip is sampled at 44.1 kHz with 16 bits per sample. **(i)** Calculate the uncompressed file size in MB. **(ii)** If the same clip is encoded as MP3 at 128 kbps, find the compression ratio and the percentage space saved. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (i) — Uncompressed Size (3 marks)**

Given: $\text{SR} = 44{,}100$ Hz, $B = 16$ bits, $C = 2$ (stereo), $T = 5 \times 60 = 300$ s.

$$S_{\text{uncompressed}} = \text{SR} \times B \times C \times T$$

$$S_{\text{uncompressed}} = 44{,}100 \times 16 \times 2 \times 300$$

$$S_{\text{uncompressed}} = 423{,}360{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

$$S_{\text{uncompressed}} = \frac{423{,}360{,}000}{8 \times 1{,}048{,}576} \approx 50.47 \text{ MB}$$

**Part (ii) — MP3 Size, Compression Ratio, Savings (4 marks)**

$$S_{\text{MP3}} = 128 \text{ kbps} \times 300 \text{ s} = 128{,}000 \times 300 = 38{,}400{,}000 \text{ bits} = 4.8 \text{ MB}$$

$$CR = \frac{S_{\text{uncompressed}}}{S_{\text{MP3}}} = \frac{50.47}{4.8} \approx 10.51$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{10.51}\right) \times 100 \approx 90.49\%$$

$$\boxed{S_{\text{uncompressed}} \approx 50.47 \text{ MB} \quad ; \quad CR \approx 10.51 \quad ; \quad \text{Savings} \approx 90.49\%}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Compare the following image file formats in terms of compression type, color depth, transparency support, and best use case: **JPEG, PNG, GIF, BMP, TIFF.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

| Property | JPEG | PNG | GIF | BMP | TIFF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression | Lossy (DCT) | Lossless (DEFLATE) | Lossless (LZW) | Uncompressed / RLE | Lossless / Lossy |
| Color Depth | 24 bpp | 24/32 bpp | 8 bpp (256) | 1–32 bpp | Up to 48 bpp |
| Transparency | No | Yes (alpha channel) | Yes (1-bit) | Yes (1-bit/ARGB) | Yes |
| Animation | No | No | Yes | No | No |
| Best Use Case | Photos, web | Logos, screenshots | Simple animations | Windows raw pixel data | Print, archival |
| File Extension | .jpg / .jpeg | .png | .gif | .bmp | .tif / .tiff |

**Awarding 1 mark per row × 6 rows — maximum capped at 7 marks. Examiner will deduct if a column is missing or if "Compression" is misstated (e.g., calling JPEG lossless).**

---

**Q. (b)** A 2-hour movie is stored using the H.264 codec at an average bitrate of **5 Mbps**. **(i)** Calculate the total file size in GB. **(ii)** If the raw uncompressed video stream would have required **1800 GB**, determine the compression ratio and PSNR-equivalent quality factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (i) — H.264 Compressed Size (3 marks)**

Given: Bitrate = 5 Mbps, $T = 2 \text{ h} = 7200$ s.

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = 5 \times 10^6 \times 7200 = 36 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$$

$$S_{\text{compressed}} = \frac{36 \times 10^9}{8 \times 10^9} = 4.5 \text{ GB}$$

**Part (ii) — Compression Ratio and Savings (4 marks)**

$$CR = \frac{1800 \text{ GB}}{4.5 \text{ GB}} = 400$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{400}\right) \times 100 = 99.75\%$$

**Justification:** A CR of 400:1 for full-motion video is achievable only because H.264 exploits both **spatial redundancy** (within frames) and **temporal redundancy** (between frames using motion compensation). For KTU, also note that the typical CR range for H.264 is **20:1 to 200:1**; values above this indicate either lower resolution or aggressive quantization (lower visual quality).

$$\boxed{S_{\text{compressed}} = 4.5 \text{ GB} \quad ; \quad CR = 400 \quad ; \quad \text{Savings} = 99.75\%}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in "Popular File Formats" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing container with codec** — saying "MP4 is a video codec" loses 1 mark instantly. MP4 is a **container**.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversions** — using $1 \text{ MB} = 10^6$ bytes (decimal) when the formula requires $1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes (binary). KTU typically uses the binary form ($1024^2$) for audio file size calculations.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by channels** in stereo audio calculations. Always state $C = 2$ for stereo explicitly.
> 4. **Calling PNG "lossy because it can be animated"** — Animation is not a compression property. PNG is strictly lossless.
> 5. **Skipping the formula derivation steps** — Examiners allocate **1 mark for the formula statement itself**, not just the final number.
> 6. **Mixing up sample rate and bit rate** — Sample rate is in **Hz**; bit rate is in **bps**. A common 1-mark trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Container vs Codec:** Container = packaging; Codec = compression algorithm. MP4 is a container; H.264 is a codec.
- **Lossy vs Lossless:** Lossy = JPEG, MP3, AAC, MP4 (H.264). Lossless = PNG, BMP, FLAC, TIFF (uncompressed mode), WAV.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $CR = S_{\text{original}} / S_{\text{compressed}}$
  - $S = \text{Bitrate} \times T$
  - $\text{Audio Size} = \text{SR} \times B \times C \times T$
  - $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{MAX}^2 / \text{MSE})$ in dB
  - $\text{Savings \%} = (1 - 1/CR) \times 100$
- **JPEG pipeline (in order):** RGB → YCbCr → Subsampling → 8×8 blocks → DCT → Quantization → Zig-Zag → Huffman coding.
- **MP3 foundation:** Uses MDCT + psychoacoustic model to remove masked frequencies.
- **Transparency support:** PNG (full alpha), GIF (1-bit), TIFF (yes), JPEG (no).
- **Animation support:** GIF (only among image formats), SVG (vector), MP4/MKV/WebM (video).
- **Modern web formats:** **WebP** (images) and **WebM/AV1** (video) — expect exam questions on these.
- **Standard CD quality:** **44.1 kHz**, **16 bits/sample**, **2 channels (stereo)** → 1.411 Mbps uncompressed.
- **Raw video scale:** Even a **10-second** 720p clip at 24 bpp occupies **~830 MB** — this single fact justifies codec design.
- **Vector vs Raster:** SVG is vector (mathematical descriptions, infinitely scalable); PNG/JPEG are raster (pixel grids, lose quality on zoom).
- **Archival formats:** Choose **lossless** (PNG for images, FLAC for audio, TIFF for print) — lossy data is **irrecoverable**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Color in Image - Color Science
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Color in Image - Color Science

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Color Science

**Color Science** is the interdisciplinary branch of physics, optics, physiology, and digital imaging that quantitatively studies the perception, representation, and reproduction of color produced by the interaction of light with the human visual system (HVS), optical sensors, and display devices.

In the context of multimedia and digital imaging, color science encompasses three fundamental pillars:

- **Physical Tristimulus Domain** — measurable radiant energy and wavelengths (typically **380 nm to 780 nm** for visible light).
- **Psychophysical Domain** — the CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) standardized observer response functions: $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$.
- **Perceptual / Cognitive Domain** — how the brain interprets cone excitations as hue, saturation, and brightness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A *color model* is a mathematical specification of how colors are represented as tuples of numbers (e.g., 3-tuples in RGB, 4-tuples in CMYK). A *color space* is a specific realization of a color model with a defined gamut and reference white point (e.g., sRGB, Adobe RGB, ProPhoto RGB).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Color is a Language"

Imagine **color** as a *language* with three alphabets:

- **The Physics Alphabet (Light):** The "ink" — pure electromagnetic waves of different wavelengths. A rainbow is the raw dictionary.
- **The Biology Alphabet (Eyes):** The "translator" — three types of cone photoreceptors (L, M, S — long, medium, short wavelength) that convert light into neural signals.
- **The Engineering Alphabet (Models):** The "grammar" — standardized codes (RGB, CMYK, HSI) that allow machines to store, transmit, and reproduce color predictably.

Just as English and Hindi encode the same ideas using different scripts, **RGB and CMYK encode the same physical color using different numerical rules** tailored to their respective devices (emissive displays vs. reflective prints).

### 1.3 Core Physical & Perceptual Constants

| Constant / Standard | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Visible Spectrum | **380 nm – 780 nm** | Range of human vision |
| L-cone peak sensitivity | **564 nm (red)** | Long-wavelength cone |
| M-cone peak sensitivity | **534 nm (green)** | Medium-wavelength cone |
| S-cone peak sensitivity | **420 nm (blue)** | Short-wavelength cone |
| Standard Illuminant D65 | **6504 K** | Reference white for sRGB |
| Standard Illuminant A | **2856 K** | Incandescent tungsten |
| Reference Luminance (sRGB) | **80 cd/m²** | Standard display brightness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three primaries?** Human color vision is **trichromatic** — Young–Helmholtz theory (1802/1850). This is why ALL digital color models (RGB, CMY, YIQ, YCbCr, Lab) use exactly **three channels** to represent the full gamut of perceivable color.

### 1.4 Light, Color, and the Tri-Stimulus Foundation

Color is **not** an inherent property of objects. It is the result of:
1. An **illuminant** (light source) striking a surface.
2. The surface's **spectral reflectance** function $\rho(\lambda)$.
3. The observer's **cone response** functions.

$$
C_{perceived} = \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \cdot \rho(\lambda) \cdot \bar{t}(\lambda) \, d\lambda
$$

where $S(\lambda)$ is the illuminant SPD, $\rho(\lambda)$ the surface reflectance, and $\bar{t}(\lambda)$ the cone sensitivity — producing the **tristimulus values** (X, Y, Z) standardized by the **CIE in 1931**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Trichromatic Theory and Cone Response

The human retina contains approximately **6 to 7 million cones**, classified into three types by their peak photopigment absorption:

- **L-cones (≈ 64%)** → peak at 564 nm → red channel input
- **M-cones (≈ 32%)** → peak at 534 nm → green channel input
- **S-cones (≈ 2%)** → peak at 420 nm → blue channel input

The brain fuses these three signals into a unified perception. This biological fact is the engineering reason all digital color models are 3-dimensional.

### 2.2 Additive vs. Subtractive Color Mixing

**Additive (RGB) — for emissive media (monitors, TVs, projectors):**
Colors are created by **adding** light energy. Black = no light, White = sum of all primaries.

$$
C_{RGB} = R \cdot \vec{r} + G \cdot \vec{g} + B \cdot \vec{b}
$$

**Subtractive (CMY/CMYK) — for reflective media (print, paint):**
Colors are created by **subtracting** wavelengths via pigments. White paper = full reflection, Ink = absorption.

$$
C_{CMY} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ M \\ Y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix}
$$

CMYK adds **K (Key/Black)** because overlaying C+M+Y in print yields muddy brown, not pure black.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every formula you must memorize** for KTU ESE questions on Color Science. All input channels are normalized to $[0, 1]$ unless otherwise noted.

| Conversion / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **RGB → Y (Luminance, BT.601)** | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Weighted by human luminance perception |
| **RGB → YIQ (NTSC)** | $I = 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B$ | I = in-phase, Q = quadrature |
| | $Q = 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B$ | Used in NTSC television |
| **RGB → YCbCr (BT.601, 8-bit)** | $Cb = -0.169R - 0.331G + 0.500B + 128$ | JPEG, MPEG, video compression |
| | $Cr = 0.500R - 0.419G - 0.081B + 128$ | Y: luma, Cb/Cr: chroma |
| **RGB → HSV Hue** | $H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.5\big((R-G)+(R-B)\big)}{\sqrt{(R-G)^{2}+(R-B)(G-B)}}\right)$ | Result in degrees, $[0^{\circ}, 360^{\circ}]$ |
| **RGB → HSV Saturation** | $S = 1 - \dfrac{3 \cdot \min(R,G,B)}{R+G+B}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| **RGB → HSV Value** | $V = \max(R,G,B)$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| **CMY from RGB** | $C = 1-R,\ M = 1-G,\ Y = 1-B$ | Simple complement |
| **CIE Tristimulus** | $X = \int S(\lambda)\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,d\lambda$ | Wavelength integration 380–780 nm |
| **CIE Chromaticity x** | $x = \dfrac{X}{X+Y+Z}$ | Plus $y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$ |
| **Chromaticity Sum** | $x + y + z = 1$ | z is dependent variable |
| **Gamma Correction (encode)** | $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\,1/\gamma}$ | Typical $\gamma = 2.2$ for sRGB |
| **Gamma Decode** | $V_{out} = V_{in}^{\,\gamma}$ | Inverse of encoding |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **JPEG/MPEG Compression:** Uses YCbCr because the human eye is **less sensitive to chroma (Cb, Cr) than to luma (Y)** — enables 4:2:0 chroma subsampling with minimal perceived loss.
- **Display Manufacturing:** sRGB gamma curve (≈ 2.2) compensates for the non-linear voltage-to-light response of CRTs, now standardized for LCDs/OLEDs.
- **Print Industry:** CMYK with ICC (International Color Consortium) profiles ensures *what you see is what you get* across devices.
- **Computer Vision:** HSV is preferred over RGB for **color-based segmentation** (e.g., skin detection, traffic light recognition) because illumination changes minimally affect H and S.
- **Cinematography:** ACES (Academy Color Encoding System) uses a wide-gamut, linear, scene-referred color space to preserve highlight detail.

### 2.5 Critical 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why weighted Y in NTSC/PAL?** Coefficients (0.299, 0.587, 0.114) come from the luminance sensitivity of the $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ curve — the green channel contributes most because the eye is most sensitive to ≈ 555 nm.
- **Why Cb/Cr offsets of +128?** Because Cb and Cr are signed ($-0.5$ to $+0.5$) but must be stored as unsigned 8-bit integers (0 to 255); $+128$ shifts the range to $[0, 255]$.
- **Why gamma correction?** Cathode Ray Tubes (CRTs) had a non-linear response $L \propto V^{2.2}$. Gamma pre-correction linearizes the system, ensuring equal numerical steps produce equal perceptual brightness changes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: RGB → YIQ Conversion Matrix

The NTSC color television system (1953) required a luminance–chrominance decomposition compatible with black-and-white receivers. The transformation is a **linear mapping** of the RGB vector, derived from the RGB phosphors of CRTs and the eye's luminance sensitivity.

**Starting Premise:** Define $Y$ as the photometric luminance, and $(I, Q)$ as the orthogonal chrominance axes in the color plane perpendicular to $Y$.

**Step 1 — Luminance (Y channel):**
Using the CIE 1931 luminosity function $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ and the NTSC RGB phosphor primaries, the luminance weights are computed as:

$$
Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B
$$

**Textual reasoning:** Green contributes the most (0.587) because the eye's photopic sensitivity peaks near 555 nm, which lies in the green region. Red and blue contribute less due to lower perceptual weight.

**Step 2 — In-phase axis (I):**
The I-axis is oriented along the orange–cyan axis, weighted to maximize human perceptual discrimination:

$$
I = 0.596\,R - 0.274\,G - 0.322\,B
$$

**Step 3 — Quadrature axis (Q):**
The Q-axis is oriented along the magenta–green axis (perpendicular to I), carrying the smallest perceptual bandwidth:

$$
Q = 0.211\,R - 0.523\,G + 0.312\,B
$$

**Step 4 — Compact matrix form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} Y \\ I \\ Q \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.299 & 0.587 & 0.114 \\ 0.596 & -0.274 & -0.322 \\ 0.211 & -0.523 & 0.312 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification with pure white:**
For $R = G = B = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299 + 0.587 + 0.114 = 1.000 \\
I &= 0.596 - 0.274 - 0.322 = 0.000 \\
Q &= 0.211 - 0.523 + 0.312 = 0.000
\end{aligned}
$$

White correctly maps to $Y = 1$ with zero chrominance — confirms the transform is correct. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: RGB → YCbCr (BT.601 Standard)

The ITU-R BT.601 standard is the basis for **SDTV, JPEG, and MPEG-1/2**. The derivation is parallel to YIQ, with different matrix coefficients chosen for digital video.

**Step 1 — Luminance (Y):** Same weighted sum as NTSC:

$$
Y = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B
$$

**Step 2 — Blue-difference chroma (Cb):**
Cb = Blue minus Luminance, capturing blue-vs-luma contrast:

$$
Cb = -0.169\,R - 0.331\,G + 0.500\,B
$$

**Step 3 — Red-difference chroma (Cr):**
Cr = Red minus Luminance, capturing red-vs-luma contrast:

$$
Cr = 0.500\,R - 0.419\,G - 0.081\,B
$$

**Step 4 — DC offset for unsigned 8-bit storage:**
Since $Cb, Cr$ are signed ($[-0.5, +0.5]$), we add **128** to shift into $[0, 255]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cb_{8} &= -0.169\,R - 0.331\,G + 0.500\,B + 128 \\
Cr_{8} &= \;\;0.500\,R - 0.419\,G - 0.081\,B + 128
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification with pure red ($R=1, G=0, B=0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299(1) + 0 + 0 = 0.299 \\
Cb &= -0.169 + 0 + 0 + 128 = 127.831 \\
Cr &= 0.500 + 0 + 0 + 128 = 128.500
\end{aligned}
$$

The Cr value is high (>128), correctly indicating red dominance; Cb is near neutral, confirming validity. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation: RGB → HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value)

HSV separates chromaticity (H, S) from intensity (V), making it ideal for color-picker UIs and image editing.

**Step 1 — Value (V) — intensity maximum:**

$$
V = \max(R, G, B)
$$

**Textual reasoning:** Value represents the brightest component — the maximum RGB channel corresponds to the perceptual brightness.

**Step 2 — Saturation (S) — color purity:**

$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}
$$

**Textual reasoning:** When $R = G = B$ (gray), $\min = \max$, so $S = 0$ (achromatic). When two channels are zero, $S = 1$ (fully saturated).

**Step 3 — Hue (H) — color angle in degrees:**

$$
H = \cos^{-1}\!\left( \frac{0.5\big((R-G) + (R-B)\big)}{\sqrt{(R-G)^{2} + (R-B)(G-B)}} \right)
$$

**Textual reasoning:** The numerator is the projection of the color vector onto the R-direction; the denominator is the vector's magnitude in the chroma plane. The result is the angle from the red axis.

**Step 4 — Hue quadrant correction:**

$$
H = \begin{cases} 360^{\circ} - H, & \text{if } B > G \\ H, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

This is required because $\arccos$ returns only $[0^{\circ}, 180^{\circ}]$, but hue spans $[0^{\circ}, 360^{\circ}]$.

**Step 5 — Worked Example:** Pure red $R=1, G=0, B=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 1 \\
S &= 1 - 0 = 1 \\
H &= \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5(1+1)}{\sqrt{1+0}}\right) = \cos^{-1}(1) = 0^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: $(H, S, V) = (0^{\circ}, 1, 1)$ — pure red. ✓

### 3.4 Derivation: CIE 1931 Tristimulus to Chromaticity

The CIE system converts spectral power data into device-independent XYZ values, then into 2D chromaticity $(x, y)$ for gamut visualization.

**Step 1 — Tristimulus integration:**

$$
X = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,\rho(\lambda)\,d\lambda
$$

$$
Y = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\,\bar{y}(\lambda)\,\rho(\lambda)\,d\lambda
$$

$$
Z = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\,\bar{z}(\lambda)\,\rho(\lambda)\,d\lambda
$$

where $k$ is a normalization constant.

**Step 2 — Project to 2D chromaticity plane:**

$$
x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}
$$

**Step 3 — Dependent z coordinate:**

$$
z = 1 - x - y
$$

**Textual reasoning:** The XYZ tristimulus lives in 3D; dividing by the sum projects onto the unit plane $x + y + z = 1$, the **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram** — a horseshoe-shaped locus of all perceivable colors.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | RGB | CMY/CMYK | HSI/HSV | YIQ | YCbCr | CIE Lab |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Channels | 3 | 3 / 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| Type | Additive | Subtractive | Perceptual | Broadcast | Digital Video | Perceptual Uniform |
| Device Dep. | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Intensity Separated? | No | No | Yes (I/V) | Yes (Y) | Yes (Y) | Yes (L) |
| Primary Use | Displays | Printing | UI, Vision | NTSC TV | JPEG, MPEG | Color Science |
| Uniform Perceptual? | No | No | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Range (norm.) | [0,1] | [0,1] | H:0–360°, S/V:0–1 | Signed | Y:[16,235], C:[16,240] | L:[0,100], a/b:[-128,127] |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Human Visual Color Perception Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source SPD] --> B[Object Surface]
    B --> C[Reflected Spectral Power]
    C --> D[Human Eye Lens]
    D --> E[Retina Photoreceptors]
    E --> F1[L cones 564nm]
    E --> F2[M cones 534nm]
    E --> F3[S cones 420nm]
    F1 --> G[Optic Nerve]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Visual Cortex]
    H --> I[Perceived Color RGB XYZ]
    I --> J[Color Model Encoding]
    J --> K1[Display RGB]
    J --> K2[Printer CMYK]
    J --> K3[Compressed YCbCr]
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram traces color from the physical source (light) through the human visual system to digital encoding. Each block is a transformation; the cones in nodes F1/F2/F3 are the **biological tristimulus samplers** — the reason all digital color models are 3-channel.

### 4.2 Color Model Taxonomy and Conversion Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Color Science Domain] --> P1[Physical]
    root --> P2[Perceptual]
    root --> P3[Device Dependent]

    P1 --> n1A[Spectral Power SPD]
    P1 --> n1B[CIE XYZ 1931]
    P1 --> n1C[CIE Lab]

    P2 --> n2A[HSI Hue Sat Intensity]
    P2 --> n2B[HSV Hue Sat Value]
    P2 --> n2C[Munsell System]

    P3 --> n3A[RGB sRGB AdobeRGB]
    P3 --> n3B[CMYK Printing]
    P3 --> n3C[YIQ NTSC]
    P3 --> n3D[YCbCr JPEG MPEG]

    n1B --> n3A
    n1B --> n3C
    n1B --> n3D
    n3A --> n3B
    n3A --> n2A
    n3A --> n2B
    n3D --> n3A
```

**Interpretation:** This taxonomy shows the three branches of color science. The arrows between nodes indicate that conversions flow *through* device-independent CIE XYZ as a **canonical pivot**, which is why color management pipelines always use ICC profiles based on XYZ/Lab.

### 4.3 YCbCr Chroma Subsampling Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RGB Input 4:4:4] --> B[Luma Filter Y]
    A --> C[Chroma Filter Cb]
    A --> D[Chroma Filter Cr]
    B --> E[Downsample 4:2:0]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Entropy Coding]
    F --> G[Compressed Bitstream]
    G --> H[Decoder]
    H --> I[Upsample Chroma]
    I --> J[RGB Output 4:4:4]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram illustrates the engineering reason YCbCr is used in JPEG/MPEG — by separating luma (Y, full resolution) from chroma (Cb, Cr, downsampled), bandwidth is reduced by **50%** (4:2:2) or **75%** (4:2:0) with negligible perceptual loss because human vision has lower spatial acuity for color than for brightness.

### 4.4 Gamma Correction Encode–Decode Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Linear Light Scene] --> B[Camera Sensor Linear Capture]
    B --> C[Gamma Encoding Power 1 over 2.2]
    C --> D[Storage sRGB File 8 bit]
    D --> E[Gamma Decoding Power 2.2]
    E --> F[Display Linear Output]
    F --> G[Perceived Image]
    G -.feedback.-> A
```

**Interpretation:** The encode–decode loop must be **inverse operations** for the displayed image to match the original scene. Skipping gamma correction produces images that appear washed out (too bright) or crushed (too dark) on standard displays.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** What is gamma correction in digital imaging? Why is it essential?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gamma correction is the application of a non-linear power-law transfer function to encode and decode luminance values in image/video systems. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
V_{out} = V_{in}^{\,1/\gamma}
$$

The standard sRGB gamma value is **2.2** (encoding exponent $1/2.2 \approx 0.4545$).

**Why it is essential (1.5 marks):**
1. **CRT Compensation:** Traditional CRTs had a non-linear voltage–luminance response $\approx V^{2.2}$. Gamma pre-correction linearizes the overall pipeline.
2. **Perceptual Uniformity:** Human vision perceives brightness logarithmically (Weber–Fechner law). Gamma encoding allocates more bits to dark tones where the eye is most sensitive, reducing visible banding in 8-bit storage.
3. **Standardization:** Ensures images appear consistently across devices from different manufacturers.

**Distribution:** Definition 1m | Formula 0.5m | Three reasons 1.5m

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between additive and subtractive color models with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Additive (RGB) | Subtractive (CMY/CMYK) |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Colors formed by **adding** light | Colors formed by **subtracting** (absorbing) light |
| Primary Colors | Red, Green, Blue | Cyan, Magenta, Yellow (+ Key/Black) |
| White | $R + G + B$ (all max) | Absence of ink (white paper) |
| Black | Absence of light | Overlap of C+M+Y inks (CMYK for pure) |
| Media | Emissive — monitors, TVs, projectors | Reflective — print, paint, photography |
| Relationship | $C = 1 - R$, $M = 1 - G$, $Y = 1 - B$ | — |
| Example Device | LCD monitor, smartphone OLED | Inkjet printer, offset press |

**Marks split:** Table format 2m, Example devices 0.5m, Conversion relation 0.5m

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `SET A`

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the CIE 1931 XYZ color model in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the chromaticity diagram and its significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the RGB-to-YCbCr color space conversion equations as per the BT.601 standard. Show the verification with a numerical example. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — CIE 1931 XYZ Color Model (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Historical Context (1 mark):**
In 1931, the CIE standardized colorimetry by defining an imaginary set of primary stimuli $X$, $Y$, $Z$ such that:
- All visible colors are expressible as **non-negative** combinations of $X, Y, Z$ (overcoming the negative values that arose with real RGB primaries).
- $Y$ matches the photopic luminosity function $\bar{y}(\lambda)$, making it a direct measure of luminance.

**Step 2 — Tristimulus Computation (1.5 marks):**
For a given spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$ and surface reflectance $\rho(\lambda)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\,\bar{x}(\lambda)\,\rho(\lambda)\,d\lambda \\
Y &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\,\bar{y}(\lambda)\,\rho(\lambda)\,d\lambda \\
Z &= k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda)\,\bar{z}(\lambda)\,\rho(\lambda)\,d\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ are the CIE 1931 standard observer color matching functions.

**Step 3 — Chromaticity Diagram (2.5 marks):**
Project 3D $(X, Y, Z)$ to 2D $(x, y)$:

$$
x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}
$$

with $z = 1 - x - y$.

The resulting **horseshoe-shaped diagram** has the following landmarks:
- **Spectral locus:** curved outer boundary representing pure monochromatic colors 380–780 nm.
- **Purple line:** straight bottom boundary connecting violet to red (non-spectral).
- **White point (D65):** $(0.3127, 0.3290)$ for sRGB.
- **Blackbody locus:** curve of Planckian radiator chromaticities.
- **Color gamut:** any triangle inside representing a device's reproducible colors.

**Step 4 — Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Device Independence:** CIE XYZ is the universal color reference; all other color spaces can be mathematically related through it.
2. **Color Management:** ICC profiles use XYZ as the Profile Connection Space (PCS).
3. **Metamerism Analysis:** Two spectrally different stimuli with identical XYZ values are metamers — perceived as the same color.
4. **Gamut Comparison:** Visualizes which colors a device can reproduce.

---

**Part (b) — RGB to YCbCr Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Luminance (2 marks):**
Luma $Y$ is the gamma-corrected luminance derived from weighted RGB:

$$
Y_{601} = 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B
$$

The weights are derived from the CIE $\bar{y}(\lambda)$ curve sampled at the BT.601 RGB primaries (Rec. 601).

**Step 2 — Color Difference Signals (2.5 marks):**
The blue and red differences (B–Y, R–Y) are computed and scaled to yield Cb and Cr:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cb_{analog} &= 0.564\,(B - Y) = -0.169\,R - 0.331\,G + 0.500\,B \\
Cr_{analog} &= 0.713\,(R - Y) = \;\;0.500\,R - 0.419\,G - 0.081\,B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Digital Quantization (1.5 marks):**
For 8-bit digital storage, the range is shifted from $[-0.5, +0.5]$ to $[0, 255]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{8} &= 219 \cdot Y + 16 \\
Cb_{8} &= 224 \cdot Cb_{analog} + 128 \\
Cr_{8} &= 224 \cdot Cr_{analog} + 128
\end{aligned}
$$

The simplified version commonly used in textbooks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299\,R + 0.587\,G + 0.114\,B \\
Cb &= -0.169\,R - 0.331\,G + 0.500\,B + 128 \\
Cr &= \;\;0.500\,R - 0.419\,G - 0.081\,B + 128
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Verification (1 mark):**
For pure green $R=0, G=255, B=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.587 \times 255 = 149.685 \\
Cb &= -0.331 \times 255 + 128 = 43.595 \\
Cr &= -0.419 \times 255 + 128 = 21.155
\end{aligned}
$$

The Y value (149.685) is high, Cb is low (less blue), Cr is low (less red) — correctly encoding a green-dominant pixel. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `SET B`

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the HSI color model. How is the H (Hue) component calculated from RGB? Show the derivation. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the following color models with diagrams: (i) RGB, (ii) CMY, (iii) CMYK. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — HSI Color Model (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
RGB couples chromaticity with intensity, which is inconvenient for image processing (e.g., histogram equalization, color-based segmentation). HSI decouples them:
- **Hue (H):** the pure color type (red, green, blue, …)
- **Saturation (S):** color purity / dilution by white
- **Intensity (I):** overall brightness

**Step 2 — Intensity Formula (1 mark):**

$$
I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Saturation Formula (1 mark):**

$$
S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R,G,B)}{R + G + B}
$$

**Step 4 — Hue Derivation (3 marks):**
Consider RGB as coordinates in 3D color space. Project the color vector onto the chrominance plane perpendicular to $(1,1,1)$. The hue is the angle in this plane, measured from the red axis.

Numerator: half the dot product of the color vector with $(R-G) + (R-B)$, yielding the projection on red.
Denominator: the magnitude in the chroma plane, $\sqrt{(R-G)^{2} + (R-B)(G-B)}$.

$$
H = \cos^{-1}\!\left( \frac{0.5\big((R-G) + (R-B)\big)}{\sqrt{(R-G)^{2} + (R-B)(G-B)}} \right)
$$

**Quadrant correction:**

$$
H = \begin{cases} 360^{\circ} - H, & B > G \\ H, & B \leq G \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark):**
For $R=1, G=0.5, B=0$ (orange):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= 0.5((0.5)+(1.0)) = 0.75 \\
\text{Den} &= \sqrt{0.25 + (1.0)(0.5)} = \sqrt{0.75} \approx 0.866 \\
H &= \cos^{-1}(0.75/0.866) = \cos^{-1}(0.866) = 30^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

Orange lies at 30° on the hue wheel — correct. ✓

---

**Part (b) — RGB, CMY, CMYK Models (7 marks)**

**(i) RGB Model (2.5 marks):**
- **Primaries:** Red (700 nm), Green (546.1 nm), Blue (435.8 nm).
- **Cube Representation:** Each axis is one channel in [0,1]. Black = (0,0,0), White = (1,1,1), primary colors on axes, secondary colors on face diagonals.
- **Use:** Cathode ray tubes, LCDs, OLEDs, scanners, digital cameras.
- **Additive Principle:** All colors are weighted sums $C = R\hat{r} + G\hat{g} + B\hat{b}$.

**(ii) CMY Model (2.5 marks):**
- **Primaries:** Cyan, Magenta, Yellow — secondary colors of RGB.
- **Relationship:** $C = 1 - R$, $M = 1 - G$, $Y = 1 - B$ (component-wise complement).
- **Use:** Subtractive printing on white paper. White paper reflects all wavelengths; ink absorbs specific bands.
- **Limitation:** Practical inks are not pure; C+M+Y produces muddy brown, not black.

**(iii) CMYK Model (2 marks):**
- Adds a fourth channel **K (Key/Black)** to produce true black and improve detail.
- **Black Generation Equations:**

$$
K = \min(C, M, Y)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C' &= C - K \\
M' &= M - K \\
Y' &= Y - K
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Undercolor Removal (UCR) and Gray Component Replacement (GCR)** are advanced techniques for optimizing the K channel.
- **Use:** Commercial offset printing, photography, packaging.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +128 offset** in YCbCr digital conversion → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing luma (Y) with luminance (Y linear)** — Y in YCbCr is *gamma-corrected* luma, not photometric luminance.
> 3. **Wrong hue quadrant** — students often skip the $B > G$ correction, leaving $H$ in the wrong half-circle.
> 4. **Mixing CIE XYZ with sRGB primaries** — XYZ primaries are *imaginary* mathematical constructs, not real lights.
> 5. **Not verifying the transformation** with white (R=G=B) — KTU examiners award bonus marks for showing the sanity check.
> 6. **Skipping the $\int$ integration limits (380–780 nm)** when writing CIE formulas.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Color Science**

- ✅ Visible spectrum = **380 nm to 780 nm**; three cone types (L 564, M 534, S 420 nm).
- ✅ All color models are **3-channel** because human vision is **trichromatic** (Young–Helmholtz).
- ✅ **Additive (RGB)** for displays; **Subtractive (CMY/K)** for printing; RGB and CMY are **complementary** ($C=1-R$).
- ✅ **Luminance weights** (BT.601): $0.299, 0.587, 0.114$ for $R, G, B$ — memorize exactly.
- ✅ **YCbCr** uses **+128** offset to make chroma unsigned 8-bit; used in JPEG/MPEG.
- ✅ **YIQ** has coefficients $(0.596, -0.274, -0.322)$ for I and $(0.211, -0.523, 0.312)$ for Q — used in NTSC.
- ✅ **HSV/HSI** separates intensity from chromaticity; preferred for computer vision and color-picker UIs.
- ✅ **CIE 1931 XYZ** is the universal device-independent reference; chromaticity coordinates satisfy $x + y + z = 1$.
- ✅ **Gamma correction** with $\gamma = 2.2$ linearizes display response and allocates bits perceptually.
- ✅ **Standard illuminant D65 (6504 K)** is the reference white for sRGB.
- ✅ **CIE Lab** is the only **perceptually uniform** model — equal numerical steps = equal perceived steps.
- ✅ **Chroma subsampling 4:2:0** saves 75% bandwidth by exploiting lower human acuity for color.
- ✅ **Metamerism:** spectrally different stimuli can produce identical XYZ and be perceived as the same color.
- ✅ For KTU derivations: **always verify with white** ($R=G=B$) — it must yield $Y=1, I=Q=0, Cb=Cr=128$.
- ✅ **Gamut** = range of reproducible colors; sRGB ⊂ Adobe RGB ⊂ ProPhoto RGB (nested).

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Color in Image: Color Science**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme OBE Standards | Aligned with Course Outcomes CO1 & CO2*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Digital Audio - Digitization of Sound
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Digital Audio - Digitization of Sound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Sound?

**Sound** is a mechanical longitudinal pressure wave that propagates through an elastic medium (such as air, water, or solids) by causing the particles of the medium to vibrate back and forth along the direction of wave travel. In multimedia engineering, sound is primarily treated as a continuous **analog signal** characterized by two fundamental physical properties:

- **Amplitude** — perceived by the human ear as **loudness**, measured in decibels (dB).
- **Frequency** — perceived by the human ear as **pitch**, measured in Hertz (Hz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Digitization of Sound is the process of converting a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog audio signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital representation that can be stored, processed, and transmitted by digital multimedia systems.*

## 1.2 Why Do We Digitize Sound?

Analog audio signals are inherently continuous in both time and amplitude. While they offer theoretically infinite resolution, they suffer from major engineering drawbacks:
1. **Noise accumulation** during copying and transmission.
2. **Degradation** over distance and time.
3. **Limited storage and editing capability.**

Digital audio overcomes these limitations by representing sound as a stream of discrete binary numbers (**0s and 1s**), enabling lossless copying, flexible editing, compression, and network transmission.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Staircase Approximation of a Hill":**
> Imagine a smooth, rolling hill (the analog audio wave). To digitize it for storage in a child's wooden block set, you must sample its height at regular horizontal intervals (like measuring the hill every 1 meter) and round each measurement to the nearest whole number of blocks.
> - The **distance between measurements** = **Sampling Rate**.
> - The **number of distinct heights you can stack** = **Bit Depth (Quantization Levels)**.
> - The more frequently you measure and the more block heights you have available, the closer your staircase resembles the original smooth hill.

## 1.4 Key Characteristics of Audio Signals

| Property | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|----------|--------|------|-------------|
| Amplitude | A | Pascal (Pa) / dB | Magnitude of pressure variation; determines loudness |
| Frequency | f | Hertz (Hz) | Number of cycles per second; determines pitch |
| Period | T | Seconds (s) | Time to complete one full cycle; T = 1/f |
| Wavelength | λ | Meters (m) | Spatial length of one cycle |
| Phase | φ | Radians (rad) | Position of wave relative to a reference point |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Digitization Pipeline (Block-Level Breakdown)

The conversion of analog sound to digital form is achieved through a three-stage pipeline:

### Step 1 — Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass Filter)
Before sampling, an analog **low-pass filter** restricts the input bandwidth to half of the intended sampling frequency. This prevents **aliasing**, a phenomenon in which high-frequency components fold back into the audible spectrum and produce irreversible distortion.

### Step 2 — Sampling (Time Discretization)
The continuous signal is measured at uniform time intervals. Each measurement (called a **sample**) captures the instantaneous amplitude. The number of samples taken per second is the **Sampling Frequency (fs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:**
> A band-limited analog signal containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies:
> $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
> The minimum acceptable sampling rate $f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $f_{Nyquist}/2 = f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist Frequency**.

### Step 3 — Quantization (Amplitude Discretization)
Each sample's amplitude is rounded to the nearest value among a finite set of discrete levels. The number of available levels is determined by the **Bit Depth (N)**:
$$L = 2^N$$

The difference between the true analog value and its quantized approximation is called **Quantization Error (e)**.

### Step 4 — Encoding (Pulse Code Modulation — PCM)
Each quantized sample is encoded as a binary word of N bits. The resulting bitstream is the **PCM digital audio signal**, which forms the foundation of formats like WAV, AIFF, and CD-Audio.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-frequency** in KTU university examinations. Memorize the structure, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem | Hz |
| 2 | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Sampling Period (time between samples) | seconds |
| 3 | $L = 2^N$ | Total Quantization Levels for N-bit resolution | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{2^N}$ | Quantization Step Size (Resolution) | Volts |
| 5 | $SQNR = 6.02 N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 6 | $D = R \times S \times f_s \times t$ | **File Size (bits)** | bits |
| 7 | $\text{Size (Bytes)} = \dfrac{f_s \times N \times C \times t}{8}$ | File Size in Bytes | bytes |

Where:
- $R$ = bit rate (bits/second)
- $S$ = number of channels (1 = mono, 2 = stereo)
- $f_s$ = sampling frequency
- $t$ = duration in seconds
- $N$ = bit depth
- $C$ = channel count

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Digitization is the foundation of every modern audio technology:
- **CD-Quality Audio:** 44.1 kHz, 16-bit, stereo → covers the human hearing range (≈ 20 Hz – 20 kHz).
- **Studio Master Recording:** 96 kHz / 192 kHz, 24-bit for high-fidelity production.
- **Telephony (VoIP):** 8 kHz, 8-bit (G.711 standard) — sufficient for speech intelligibility.
- **Streaming Platforms (Spotify, YouTube):** Use lossy compression (MP3, AAC, Opus) built atop PCM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Quantization Step Size (Q)

We start from the definition that for an N-bit quantizer, the total dynamic range of the input signal $\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min}$ is divided into $2^N$ equally spaced quantization intervals.

**Step 1: Express the number of levels.**

$$L = 2^N$$

**Step 2: Define step size as range divided by levels.**

$$Q = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $L = 2^N$.**

$$Q = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^N} = \frac{\Delta V}{2^N}$$

This $Q$ is the smallest distinguishable amplitude change — increasing $N$ by 1 bit halves the step size, doubling the resolution.

## 3.2 Derivation of Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)

Assume a uniform mid-tread quantizer fed by a full-scale sinusoidal input. The quantization error behaves like uniformly distributed white noise in the range $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$.

**Step 1: Compute the mean-square quantization noise.**

$$\overline{e^2} = \int_{-Q/2}^{Q/2} \frac{e^2}{Q} \, de = \frac{Q^2}{12}$$

**Step 2: Compute the mean-square signal power of a full-scale sinusoid.**

$$P_{signal} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

where $A = \frac{\Delta V}{2}$ is the peak amplitude.

$$P_{signal} = \frac{(\Delta V / 2)^2}{2} = \frac{\Delta V^2}{8}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $Q = \Delta V / 2^N$.**

$$\overline{e^2} = \frac{1}{12} \left( \frac{\Delta V}{2^N} \right)^2 = \frac{\Delta V^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$

**Step 4: Compute the SQNR ratio.**

$$SQNR = \frac{P_{signal}}{\overline{e^2}} = \frac{\Delta V^2 / 8}{\Delta V^2 / (12 \cdot 4^N)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^N}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^N$$

**Step 5: Convert to decibels.**

$$SQNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^N\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 N \log_{10}(4)$$

$$SQNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 10 N \times 0.602$$

$$\boxed{SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

This proves that **every additional bit of resolution adds approximately 6 dB of dynamic range**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Calculate the file size (in MB) and SQNR for 5 minutes of CD-quality stereo audio.
> **Given:** $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, $N = 16$ bits, Channels = 2, Duration $t = 300$ s.

**Step 1: Apply the file-size formula.**

$$\text{Size (bits)} = f_s \times N \times C \times t$$

$$\text{Size} = 44100 \times 16 \times 2 \times 300 = 423{,}360{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Convert to Bytes.**

$$\text{Size (Bytes)} = \frac{423{,}360{,}000}{8} = 52{,}920{,}000 \text{ Bytes}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Megabytes (1 MB = $1024 \times 1024$ Bytes).**

$$\text{Size (MB)} = \frac{52{,}920{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 50.47 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4: Compute SQNR.**

$$SQNR = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

This matches the theoretical dynamic range of a standard Red-Book audio CD.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Digitization Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the complete analog-to-digital conversion flow used in all digital audio systems:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Audio Signal] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler - Hold Circuit]
    C --> D[Quantizer - N bit ADC]
    D --> E[PCM Encoder]
    E --> F[Digital Bitstream - Storage or Transmission]

    G[Clock Generator fs] -.-> C
    G -.-> D

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

## 4.2 Sampling and Quantization — Visual Abstraction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Analog Waveform]
        W1[Smooth Continuous Sine Wave]
    end

    subgraph SAMP[Sampling Stage]
        S1[Sampled Points at Regular Intervals]
    end

    subgraph QUANT[Quantization Stage]
        Q1[Rounded to Nearest Level]
        Q2[Levels: 2 to the power N]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Digital Output]
        O1[Binary PCM Codes]
    end

    W1 --> S1
    S1 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> O1

    style W1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style S1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style Q1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style Q2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style O1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.3 Aliasing Phenomenon — Frequency Domain View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Signal fmax = 10 kHz] --> B{Sampling Rate fs}
    B -->|fs = 30 kHz - OK| C[Faithful Reconstruction]
    B -->|fs = 15 kHz - Borderline| D[Distortion at Boundary]
    B -->|fs = 12 kHz - Aliasing| E[Folding Artifact Distortion]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style E fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

### Q1. State and explain the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited analog signal containing no frequency components higher than $f_{max}$ Hz can be completely and exactly reconstructed from its discrete samples provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

The minimum theoretical sampling rate $2 f_{max}$ is termed the **Nyquist Rate**, while $f_{max}$ itself is called the **Nyquist Frequency**. If $f_s$ falls below this threshold, the high-frequency components **fold back** into the baseband, producing **aliasing distortion** that cannot be removed later. Hence, an anti-aliasing low-pass filter is mandatory before sampling.

---

### Q2. Define quantization. What is quantization error?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Quantization** is the process of mapping each sampled amplitude (which is a continuous real value) to one of a finite set of discrete levels determined by the bit depth $N$. The number of available levels is:

$$L = 2^N$$

**Quantization Error ($e$)** is the difference between the actual analog sample value and its quantized approximation:

$$e = x_{analog} - x_{quantized}, \quad \text{where } \vert e \vert \leq \frac{Q}{2}$$

This error manifests as an inherent **quantization noise** superimposed on the signal, and its magnitude is inversely proportional to the bit depth.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B, 14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the analog-to-digital conversion process for audio signals. Discuss the role of the anti-aliasing filter in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A music clip is recorded in stereo with a sampling rate of 48 kHz and a bit depth of 24 bits. Calculate:
  (i) The total file size in MB for 4 minutes of recording.
  (ii) The theoretical Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR).
  (iii) The Nyquist frequency for this configuration. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The digitization of audio involves four sequential stages as shown in the diagram below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Sound Wave] --> B[Anti-Aliasing LP Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler]
    C --> D[Quantizer - N bit]
    D --> E[PCM Encoder]
    E --> F[Digital PCM Output]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Stage 1 — Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:**
Real-world audio signals contain frequency components beyond the desired audible band (20 Hz–20 kHz). If these high-frequency components are sampled at $f_s$, they violate the Nyquist condition and reappear as false low-frequency signals (**aliases**). An analog low-pass filter with cutoff at $f_s/2$ removes these components before sampling, ensuring faithful reconstruction.

**Stage 2 — Sampling:** The filtered signal is sampled at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$.

**Stage 3 — Quantization:** Each sample is approximated to the nearest of $2^N$ discrete levels.

**Stage 4 — PCM Encoding:** Each quantized value is converted to its N-bit binary representation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $f_s = 48{,}000$ Hz, $N = 24$ bits, $C = 2$ (stereo), $t = 4 \times 60 = 240$ s.

**(i) File Size Calculation:**

$$\text{Bits} = f_s \times N \times C \times t = 48000 \times 24 \times 2 \times 240$$

$$\text{Bits} = 552{,}960{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Bytes} = \frac{552{,}960{,}000}{8} = 69{,}120{,}000 \text{ Bytes}$$

$$\text{MB} = \frac{69{,}120{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} = \frac{69{,}120{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 65.92 \text{ MB}$$

**(ii) SQNR Calculation:**

$$SQNR = 6.02N + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 24 + 1.76 = 144.48 + 1.76 = 146.24 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Nyquist Frequency:**

$$f_{Nyquist} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{48000}{2} = 24{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 24 \text{ kHz}$$

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative to Question A)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) for a uniform N-bit quantizer with a full-scale sinusoidal input. Show that every additional bit of resolution contributes approximately 6 dB of dynamic range. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare PCM, DPCM, and DM (Delta Modulation) in terms of bit rate, SNR, and complexity. Why is PCM considered the foundational technique for digital audio? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Assume an N-bit uniform mid-tread quantizer with step size $Q = \Delta V / 2^N$ and a full-scale sinusoid of amplitude $A = \Delta V / 2$.

**Step 1:** Quantization noise power:
$$\overline{e^2} = \frac{Q^2}{12} = \frac{\Delta V^2}{12 \cdot 4^N}$$

**Step 2:** Signal power:
$$P_s = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{\Delta V^2}{8}$$

**Step 3:** SQNR ratio:
$$SQNR = \frac{P_s}{\overline{e^2}} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^N$$

**Step 4:** Converting to decibels:
$$SQNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02N \text{ dB}$$

This confirms that **each extra bit adds 6.02 dB** of SQNR, doubling the resolution and quadrupling the noise-rejection capability.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | PCM | DPCM | Delta Modulation (DM) |
|-----------|-----|------|-----------------------|
| Bits/sample | N (fixed) | N (fewer, predictive) | 1 bit/sample |
| Bit rate | High | Medium | Very High (oversampled) |
| SNR | Excellent | Good | Moderate (slope overload) |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| Bandwidth | High | Reduced | Highest |

**Why PCM is foundational:**
PCM is the **uncompressed, lossless baseline** representation. All other audio formats (WAV, FLAC, MP3, AAC) are derived either directly from PCM or from compression algorithms operating on PCM data. It guarantees exact reconstruction and provides the reference quality against which lossy codecs are benchmarked.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse sampling rate with bit rate.** Sampling rate is in Hz (samples/sec); bit rate is in bits/sec (bits/sample × samples/sec × channels).
> 2. **Always specify $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \times 1024$ Bytes** in file-size problems unless the question explicitly states otherwise.
> 3. **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_{max}$ (sampling frequency); **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_{max}$ (signal bandwidth). These are routinely swapped in exam answers — marks are deducted.
> 4. The 1.76 dB constant in the SQNR formula comes from $\log_{10}(1.5)$. Do NOT round it to 1.8.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Digitization of Sound**

- ✅ Sound is an analog, continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal.
- ✅ Digitization = **Sampling + Quantization + Encoding (PCM)**.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ — sample at least twice the highest frequency.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter** is mandatory before sampling.
- ✅ **Quantization levels:** $L = 2^N$ where $N$ = bit depth.
- ✅ **Step size:** $Q = \Delta V / 2^N$ — smaller $Q$ = finer resolution.
- ✅ **SQNR (dB):** $6.02 N + 1.76$ — every extra bit ≈ +6 dB dynamic range.
- ✅ **File size:** $\dfrac{f_s \times N \times C \times t}{8}$ Bytes (with 1 MB = $1024^2$ Bytes).
- ✅ **CD Quality:** 44.1 kHz, 16-bit, stereo → ~10 MB/minute uncompressed.
- ✅ **Studio Quality:** 48 kHz / 96 kHz, 24-bit for professional recording.
- ✅ **Aliasing** is irreversible — prevention must happen before sampling.
- ✅ **PCM** is the foundation; all compressed audio codecs operate on PCM data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Musical Instrument Digital Interface (MIDI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Musical Instrument Digital Interface (MIDI)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Musical Instrument Digital Interface (MIDI)** is a **technical standard** (introduced in **1983** by Dave Smith and Ikutaro Kakehashi) that defines a **communication protocol**, **digital interface specification**, and **electrical connector standard** used to connect a wide variety of electronic musical instruments, computers, sequencers, lighting controllers, and related multimedia devices.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, MIDI is classified as a **"Descriptor-Based Representation"** of audio — it does **not transmit audio signals**; instead, it transmits **event-based control instructions** (digital messages) that describe *what* should be played, *how*, *when*, and *for how long*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition for KTU Board Exams:**
> *"MIDI is a standardized protocol for the exchange of musical information between digital musical instruments, computers, and other compatible devices. It transmits performance data (musical events) as coded digital messages rather than actual audio waveforms."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe vs. Cooked Dish" Model

Imagine you order food at a restaurant:

| Component | Real World Analogy | MIDI Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **The Recipe Card** | Written instructions: "Fry 2 eggs at medium heat for 3 minutes" | **MIDI Message** (e.g., "Play C4 at velocity 100 for 1 beat") |
| **The Cook** | The chef who reads the recipe and prepares food | **Sound Module / Synthesizer** |
| **The Plate of Food** | The actual edible meal | **Analog Audio Output** |
| **Waiter Delivering Recipe** | Physical transportation of the recipe | **MIDI Cable / USB-MIDI** |

A MIDI file (e.g., `.mid`) is **not a recording of music** — it is a **score of instructions**. The "cooking" (sound synthesis) happens inside the receiving device. This is the fundamental distinction examiners test: **MIDI = instructions, NOT audio**.

## 1.3 Why MIDI Matters in Multimedia Engineering

- **Compactness**: A 3-minute MIDI symphony can fit in under **100 KB**, whereas a WAV file of the same length requires **~30 MB**.
- **Editability**: Pitch, tempo, instrument, and dynamics can be altered post-recording without re-recording.
- **Interoperability**: Cross-vendor, cross-platform since **1983** — a Yamaha keyboard can drive a Roland sound module.
- **Synchronization**: Enables tight sync between music, video, lighting, and DAW (Digital Audio Workstation) systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Highlight — Three Pillars of MIDI:**
> 1. **Protocol** (communication rules)
> 2. **Hardware Interface** (5-pin DIN, USB-MIDI)
> 3. **File Format** (Standard MIDI File — SMF, `.mid`)
>
> Examiners frequently ask students to "Explain the three components of the MIDI standard." Memorize this triad.

## 1.4 Historical Evolution Timeline

| Year | Milestone | Significance for KTU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1983** | MIDI 1.0 Specification Released | Birth of digital music networking |
| **1991** | General MIDI (GM) Standard | 128 standardized instrument sounds |
| **1996** | Standard MIDI File (SMF) widespread | Multi-track `.mid` files |
| **1999** | USB-MIDI (USB Class Specification) | Replaced DIN connectors in modern devices |
| **2020** | MIDI 2.0 Released | Higher resolution, per-note control, MPE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Bit Rate (Memorize for KTU):** MIDI operates at a serial data rate of **31,250 bits per second (31.25 kbps)** over the original 5-pin DIN current-loop interface. This is **NOT** the same as audio sample rate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MIDI System Architecture

A complete MIDI system consists of **three functional layers**:

### Layer 1 — The Physical Layer (Hardware)
- **5-Pin DIN Connector** (original): Uses a **current loop** of **5 mA** at **+5V**, optically isolated for noise immunity.
- **USB-MIDI** (modern): USB Class-compliant, appears as a virtual COM port.
- **MIDI Thru / Out / In** ports: 
  - **MIDI OUT** → transmits data from device
  - **MIDI IN** → receives data into device
  - **MIDI THRU** → exact echo of incoming data (pass-through)

### Layer 2 — The Message Layer (Protocol)
MIDI messages are **byte streams**. Each message is **1, 2, or 3 bytes** long, where the **first byte is a Status Byte** and the remaining are **Data Bytes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Byte Identification Rule (CRITICAL for KTU):**
> - **Status Byte**: MSB (Most Significant Bit) = **1** → value range **128–255 (0x80–0xFF)**
> - **Data Byte**: MSB = **0** → value range **0–127 (0x00–0x7F)**
> 
> This single rule is the basis of almost every MIDI numerical question.

### Layer 3 — The Application Layer (File/Sequencer)
- **Standard MIDI File (SMF)** with extensions `.mid` or `.smf`
- Three SMF formats: **Format 0** (single track), **Format 1** (multi-track, simultaneous), **Format 2** (multi-track, independent sequences)

## 2.2 MIDI Message Taxonomy

### A. Channel Messages (Channels 1–16)
Operate on a **specific channel**, allowing up to **16 independent instruments** on a single cable.

| Type | Status Byte (Hex) | Data Bytes | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Note Off** | `0x8n` | Note, Velocity | Stops a note |
| **Note On** | `0x9n` | Note, Velocity | Starts a note (velocity 0 = Note Off) |
| **Polyphonic Aftertouch** | `0xAn` | Note, Pressure | Per-key pressure |
| **Control Change (CC)** | `0xBn` | Controller, Value | Volume, pan, modulation, sustain pedal |
| **Program Change** | `0xCn` | Program (0–127) | Selects instrument patch |
| **Channel Pressure** | `0xDn` | Pressure | Whole-channel pressure |
| **Pitch Bend** | `0xEn` | LSB, MSB (14-bit) | Bends pitch ±2 semitones (default) |

> **Here, `n` = channel number (0–15), where `n=0` represents Channel 1.**

### B. System Messages (No Channel)
| Type | Status Byte | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **System Exclusive (SysEx)** | `0xF0` … `0xF7` | Vendor-specific (e.g., sound bank dumps) |
| **Time Code (MTC)** | `0xF1` | SMPTE synchronization (hours, mins, secs, frames) |
| **Song Position Pointer** | `0xF2` | Locates playback position in MIDI beats |
| **Song Select** | `0xF3` | Selects song in a sequence |
| **Tune Request** | `0xF6` | Requests analog synthesizer retuning |
| **Timing Clock** | `0xF8` | 24 pulses per quarter note (sync signal) |
| **Start / Stop / Continue** | `0xFA / 0xFC / 0xFB` | Transport controls |
| **Active Sensing** | `0xFE` | "I am alive" heartbeat (sent every 300 ms) |
| **System Reset** | `0xFF` | Resets all devices to power-on state |

## 2.3 General MIDI (GM) Instrument Mapping

The **GM standard (1991)** defines a fixed mapping of **128 Program Change numbers** to standard instrument sounds. This is essential for KTU questions on **"Why does a MIDI file sound different on different devices?"**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Famous GM Patch Numbers (Memorize for 3-mark questions):**
> - **Program 0** → Acoustic Grand Piano
> - **Program 24** → Nylon String Guitar
> - **Program 40** → Violin
> - **Program 73** → Flute
> - **Program 80** → Lead 1 (Square / Synth Lead)
> - **Program 128** → Gunshot (last patch)
>
> **GM Drum Map**: Notes on **Channel 10** are reserved for **percussion** — Note 35 = Acoustic Bass Drum, Note 60 = Hi Bongo.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{Note Number } n \rightarrow f = 440 \times 2^{(n-69)/12}$ | Convert MIDI note number to frequency (A4 = note 69 = 440 Hz) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 2 | $\text{MIDI baud rate} = 31{,}250$ | Serial bit rate over DIN MIDI | bits/second |
| 3 | $\text{Clock Rate} = 24$ | Pulses per quarter note (PPQN timing clock) | pulses/beat |
| 4 | $\text{File size (bytes)} \approx 3 \times N_{\text{events}} + 14$ | Approximate SMF size for 1-track, no meta-events | bytes |
| 5 | $\text{Active Channels} = 16$ | Independent logical channels per MIDI port | channels |
| 6 | $\text{Active Sensing Interval} = 300$ | Heartbeat send interval (max) | ms |
| 7 | $\text{Pitch Bend Range}_{\text{default}} = \pm 2$ | Semitones (configurable) | semitones |
| 8 | $\text{Velocity Range} = 1\text{ to }127$ | 1 = softest, 127 = loudest (0 = Note Off) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{SysEx Max Length} = \text{unlimited (theoretical)}$ | Limited by 8192 byte buffer per packet | bytes |
| 10 | $\text{Current Loop}_{\text{DIN}} = 5$ | Transmit current via opto-isolated loop | mA |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Note numbers in MIDI are **0–127**, but **Middle C** is note number **60**, and **A4 (concert pitch)** is **69**. Do NOT confuse 60 with 69 — the examiner allocates marks separately for the correct mapping.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DAW (Digital Audio Workstations)**: Cubase, Pro Tools, FL Studio, Ableton Live — all use MIDI as the sequencer backbone.
- **Film Scoring**: Composers send MIDI to orchestral sample libraries (EastWest, Spitfire).
- **Game Audio**: Adaptive/responsive music (Wwise, FMOD) uses MIDI-like event triggers.
- **Lighting Control**: DMX-over-MIDI for synchronized stage shows.
- **Mobile Apps**: GarageBand, Music Maker JAM, and piano-learning apps transmit MIDI over Bluetooth (BLE-MIDI).
- **Accessibility**: MIDI controller keyboards enable physically disabled musicians to compose.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MIDI Note Number to Frequency Conversion

The MIDI note number $n$ is mapped to a frequency $f$ using the **equal-tempered scale** with reference $A_4 = 440\,\text{Hz}$ corresponding to MIDI note **69**.

### Step 1 — Establish the Reference Point

The international concert pitch reference is $A_4 = 440\,\text{Hz}$, assigned MIDI note number $n = 69$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Equal-Tempered Semitone Formula

In an equal-tempered scale, each semitone differs from the previous by a frequency factor of $2^{1/12}$. Therefore, the number of semitones between note $n$ and reference $A_4$ is:

$$
k = n - 69
$$

### Step 3 — Construct the Generalized Formula

Multiplying the reference frequency by $2^{k/12}$:

$$
f = 440 \times 2^{(n-69)/12}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example: Note Number 60 (Middle C)

$$
f = 440 \times 2^{(60-69)/12}
$$

$$
f = 440 \times 2^{-9/12} = 440 \times 2^{-0.75}
$$

$$
f = 440 \times 0.5946 \approx 261.63\,\text{Hz}
$$

This matches the standard frequency of **Middle C = 261.63 Hz** ✓

### Step 5 — Worked Example: Note Number 72 (C5, one octave above Middle C)

$$
f = 440 \times 2^{(72-69)/12} = 440 \times 2^{3/12} = 440 \times 2^{0.25}
$$

$$
f = 440 \times 1.1892 \approx 523.25\,\text{Hz}
$$

This matches **C5 = 523.25 Hz** ✓ (exactly one octave, i.e., $2 \times$ Middle C).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MIDI Message Byte Structure

A MIDI message is a **sequence of 1 to 3 bytes** transmitted serially. Let us derive the bit-level structure of a **Note On message**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Status Byte

The Note On message belongs to channel messages on channel $c$ (where $c \in [0, 15]$). The status byte is constructed as follows:

- The **upper nibble** (bits 7–4) = `1001` (= 9) for Note On.
- The **lower nibble** (bits 3–0) = channel number $c$.

$$
S = (0\text{x}9 \times 16) + c = 144 + c
$$

For **Channel 1** ($c = 0$): $S = 144 = 0\text{x}90$

### Step 2 — Identify the Two Data Bytes

- **Data Byte 1 (D1)** = Note number $n$ → range $[0, 127]$, with MSB = 0
- **Data Byte 2 (D2)** = Velocity $v$ → range $[1, 127]$ (Note On); 0 means Note Off

### Step 3 — Full 3-Byte Message

$$
M = [S,\ D_1,\ D_2] = [0\text{x}90,\ 0\text{x}3C,\ 0\text{x}64]
$$

Decoding: **Channel 1, Note On, Note 60 (Middle C), Velocity 100** — this is a forte middle C note on Channel 1.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MIDI Transmission Time

To verify the 31.25 kbps standard, we calculate the time to transmit one Note On message (3 bytes = 24 bits).

### Step 1 — Bits per Message

$$
B = 3 \times 8 = 24\,\text{bits}
$$

### Step 2 — Transmission Time

$$
t = \frac{B}{\text{baud rate}} = \frac{24}{31{,}250} = 0.000768\,\text{seconds} = 0.768\,\text{ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Messages per Second (Maximum Throughput)

$$
M_{\max} = \frac{1}{t} = \frac{31{,}250}{24} \approx 1{,}302\,\text{messages/second}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why MIDI can easily handle dense polyphonic passages (e.g., piano sonatas) without latency. A chord with 10 simultaneous notes = 30 bytes = ~2.3 ms, well below the human perception threshold of ~10 ms.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MIDI File Size Estimation

A **Standard MIDI File (SMF)** consists of a header chunk (14 bytes) and track chunks.

### Step 1 — Header Chunk Size

The MThd header is **always 14 bytes** (8-byte tag + length + 6-byte data).

### Step 2 — Track Chunk Size

Each track event is stored as: **Delta-time (variable length) + Event bytes**

For a track with $N$ events of average 3 bytes each plus delta-times of average 1 byte:

$$
\text{Track Size} \approx N \times 4 + 8 \quad (\text{tag and length overhead})
$$

### Step 3 — Total File Size for Format 0 (Single Track)

$$
\text{File Size} \approx 14 + 8 + 4N
$$

For $N = 1000$ events:

$$
\text{File Size} \approx 14 + 8 + 4000 = 4{,}022\,\text{bytes} \approx 4\,\text{KB}
$$

Compare this with a 3-minute CD-quality stereo WAV: $\approx 30\,\text{MB}$ — a **~7,500× compression** in representation efficiency.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Channel Message Decoding

Given the status byte $S = 0\text{x}B5$, identify the message type, channel, and interpret the data bytes $[0\text{x}07, 0\text{x}7F]$.

### Step 1 — Decode Status Byte

$$
S = 0\text{x}B5 = 1011\,0101_2
$$

- Upper nibble `1011` = 11 → **Control Change**
- Lower nibble `0101` = 5 → **Channel 6** (since $c+1 = 5+1$)

### Step 2 — Interpret Data Bytes

- D1 = `0x07` = 7 → **Controller Number 7 = Channel Volume**
- D2 = `0x7F` = 127 → **Maximum volume**

### Step 3 — Final Interpretation

> *"Set Channel 6 Volume to maximum (127) using Control Change message on Controller 7."*

## 3.6 Summary Table — Common MIDI Note Numbers (Memorize)

| Note Name | MIDI Number | Frequency (Hz) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| C0 | 12 | 16.35 |
| C1 | 24 | 32.70 |
| C2 | 36 | 65.41 |
| C3 (Bass Clef Center) | 48 | 130.81 |
| C4 (Middle C) | 60 | 261.63 |
| A4 (Concert Pitch) | 69 | 440.00 |
| C5 | 72 | 523.25 |
| C6 | 84 | 1046.50 |
| G9 (Highest in MIDI) | 127 | 12,544.00 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MIDI System Block Architecture (Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end flow** of a MIDI performance event — from the player's keystroke to the analog audio output. This is the architecture KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in 7-mark descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Player Input Keyboard Controller] --> B[MIDI OUT Port]
    B --> C[MIDI Cable 5-Pin DIN or USB]
    C --> D[MIDI IN Port of Sound Module]
    D --> E[MIDI Parser Status vs Data Byte Decoding]
    E --> F{Routing Logic}
    F -->|Channel Match| G[Channel 1..16 Synthesis Engine]
    F -->|No Match| H[Ignore Message]
    G --> I[Sample Library or Oscillator Bank]
    I --> J[DAC Digital to Analog Converter]
    J --> K[Audio Amplifier]
    K --> L[Speaker Output Analog Audio]
    M[MIDI THRU Port] --> N[Subsequent Devices Daisy Chain]
    D --> M
    N --> O[Second Sound Module]
    O --> P[Additional Synthesizer Output]
    Q[DAW or Sequencer] -->|MTC SMPTE Sync| D
```

### Diagram Explanation (For 7-Mark Answers)

- **Input Controller** generates performance data (key press, velocity).
- **MIDI OUT** serializes the data at **31,250 bps** as **status + data bytes**.
- **Cable** is a 5-pin DIN (current loop) or USB (packet-based).
- **MIDI IN** receives, **Parser** separates status bytes from data bytes.
- **Routing Logic** checks the channel nibble and dispatches to the correct **synthesis engine** (one of 16).
- **Synthesis Engine** uses a **sample library** (PCM samples) or **oscillator bank** (FM, additive, subtractive).
- **DAC** converts digital signal to analog voltage.
- **Amplifier** drives the **speaker**.
- **MIDI THRU** allows **daisy-chaining** multiple devices on a single input.
- **DAW** provides **MTC** (MIDI Time Code) for SMPTE synchronization with video.

## 4.2 MIDI Note-On Event Sequence (Detailed)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant K as Keyboard Controller
    participant C as MIDI Cable
    participant S as Sound Module Channel 1
    K->>C: 0x90 Status Byte Note On Ch1
    C->>S: 0x3C Data Byte 1 Note 60
    S->>S: Identify Note C4
    K->>C: 0x64 Data Byte 2 Velocity 100
    C->>S: Trigger Sample at 78 percent amplitude
    Note over S: Synthesizes 261.63 Hz waveform
    K->>C: 0x90 0x3C 0x00 After 1 second Note Off via Velocity 0
    C->>S: Release envelope
```

## 4.3 SMF File Structure (Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F[SMF File .mid] --> H[Header Chunk MThd 14 bytes]
    F --> T1[Track Chunk 1 MTrk]
    F --> T2[Track Chunk 2 MTrk]
    F --> T3[Track Chunk N MTrk]
    H --> H1[Format 0 1 or 2]
    H --> H2[Number of Tracks 1 to 65535]
    H --> H3[Division PPQN or SMPTE]
    T1 --> E1[Delta Time Variable Length]
    T1 --> E2[Event Message]
    E2 --> M1[Note On Off]
    E2 --> M2[Control Change]
    E2 --> M3[Meta Event Tempo Time Signature]
    E2 --> M4[SysEx Vendor Specific]
    T1 --> T1E[End of Track 0xFF 0x2F 0x00]
```

### Block-by-Block SMF Explanation

| Block | Hex Signature | Size | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MThd** | `4D 54 68 64` | 14 bytes | Header: format, track count, division |
| **MTrk** | `4D 54 72 6B` | Variable | Track data: delta-time + events |
| **Meta Event** | `0xFF` | Variable | Tempo, time signature, lyrics, markers |
| **End of Track** | `0xFF 0x2F 0x00` | 3 bytes | Mandatory track terminator |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define MIDI. List any four advantages of MIDI over digital audio.**

**Model Answer:**

**MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface)** is a standard communication protocol developed in 1983 that allows electronic musical instruments, computers, and other multimedia devices to exchange musical performance data (such as note pitch, velocity, timing, and control signals) in a digital format.

**Four Advantages of MIDI over Digital Audio:**

1. **Compact File Size** — A 3-minute MIDI file occupies only a few KB, whereas a WAV file of the same duration requires ~30 MB.
2. **Editable Performance Data** — Notes, tempo, pitch, and instrument can be modified after recording without re-recording.
3. **Cross-Platform Interoperability** — Any MIDI-compatible device can play any MIDI file, irrespective of manufacturer.
4. **Real-Time Control** — Supports dynamic expressive parameters (velocity, aftertouch, pitch bend) that a fixed audio file cannot.
5. **Hardware-Software Integration** — Enables control of external synthesizers, lighting, and video via DAW.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**Differentiate between a Status Byte and a Data Byte in a MIDI message.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Status Byte | Data Byte |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MSB (bit 7)** | Always **1** | Always **0** |
| **Decimal Range** | 128 – 255 (0x80 – 0xFF) | 0 – 127 (0x00 – 0x7F) |
| **Position in Message** | First byte (mandatory) | Subsequent bytes (1 or 2) |
| **Function** | Identifies message type and channel | Carries payload (note, velocity, value) |
| **Example** | 0x90 (Note On, Channel 1) | 0x3C (Note 60 / Middle C) |

A MIDI message is invalid if any byte other than the first has its MSB = 1 — receivers will discard the message and look for the next status byte.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module-level choice — answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**a) Explain the architecture of a MIDI system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A MIDI system consists of three architectural layers:

**Layer 1 — Performance / Input Devices:**
- **MIDI Controller Keyboard** — Generates performance data when a key is pressed.
- **Pads / Drum Pads / Faders** — Send Note On/Off and Control Change messages.
- **Wind / String MIDI Controllers** — Send breath pressure, pitch, and articulation data.

**Layer 2 — Transmission Hardware:**
- **MIDI OUT Port** — Source of MIDI data; uses a **5 mA current loop** at 5V.
- **MIDI Cable** — 5-pin DIN, optically isolated to prevent ground loops.
- **MIDI IN Port** — Receiver; optically decoupled for noise immunity.
- **MIDI THRU Port** — Exact echo of incoming bytes for daisy-chaining devices.
- **USB-MIDI** — Modern alternative using USB Class-compliant drivers.

**Layer 3 — Receiving / Sound-Generating Devices:**
- **MIDI Sound Module** — Interprets MIDI and triggers internal samples/oscillators.
- **Synthesizer** — May be analog (uses MIDI as gate/clock) or digital (uses MIDI as note triggers).
- **DAW / Sequencer** — Records, edits, and plays back MIDI events from `.mid` files.
- **Speakers** — Output the analog signal generated by the sound module.

**Supporting Hardware:**
- **Fader Box** — Sends CC messages to control mixers.
