- **Sync Source** — Sends MTC (MIDI Time Code) for SMPTE synchronization with video.
- **Footswitch Controller** — Sends Program Change for live patch switching.

The communication flow is **asynchronous serial at 31,250 bps**, with messages of 1–3 bytes.

---

**b) Explain the following MIDI message types with examples: (i) Note On (ii) Control Change (iii) Program Change (iv) Pitch Bend. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Note On — Status 0x9n (3 bytes):**
- Tells the receiver to start a note.
- Example: `[0x90, 0x3C, 0x64]` → Channel 1, Note On, Middle C (60), Velocity 100.
- If velocity = 0, the receiver treats it as **Note Off** (running status optimization).

**(ii) Control Change — Status 0xBn (3 bytes):**
- Sends continuous controller (CC) data for modulation, volume, panning, sustain, etc.
- Example: `[0xB0, 0x07, 0x7F]` → Channel 1, CC#7 (Channel Volume) set to maximum 127.
- 128 possible controllers (0–127), each with values 0–127.

**(iii) Program Change — Status 0xCn (2 bytes):**
- Selects an instrument patch.
- Example: `[0xC0, 0x00]` → Channel 1 selects Program 0 (Acoustic Grand Piano in GM).
- 128 possible programs (0–127).

**(iv) Pitch Bend — Status 0xEn (3 bytes):**
- Continuous 14-bit pitch modulation (LSB + MSB).
- Default range: ±2 semitones.
- Example: `[0xE0, 0x00, 0x40]` → Channel 1, pitch bend centered (no bend).
- `[0xE0, 0x7F, 0x7F]` → maximum positive bend; `[0xE0, 0x00, 0x00]` → maximum negative bend.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> Students commonly lose marks by **forgetting the channel nibble (n)** in the status byte. Always write the status byte as `0x9n`, `0xBn`, `0xCn`, `0xEn` — NOT just `0x90`, `0xB0`. Additionally, do NOT confuse **velocity 0 (Note Off)** with **Note On with no sound**; velocity 0 explicitly means "release this note."

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**a) What is a Standard MIDI File (SMF)? Explain its structure with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Standard MIDI File (SMF)** is a file format (`.mid` or `.smf`) defined in 1988 that stores MIDI messages as a sequence of time-stamped events. It enables portable, cross-platform exchange of musical sequences.

**SMF Structure:**

**1. Header Chunk (MThd) — 14 bytes:**
- 4 bytes: ASCII tag "MThd" (`0x4D 0x54 0x68 0x64`).
- 4 bytes: Length (always 6).
- 2 bytes: Format (0 = single track, 1 = multi-track synchronous, 2 = multi-track independent).
- 2 bytes: Number of tracks (1 to 65,535).
- 2 bytes: Division (PPQN pulses per quarter note, or SMPTE-encoded negative value).

**2. Track Chunk (MTrk) — Variable size:**
- 4 bytes: ASCII tag "MTrk".
- 4 bytes: Length of track data.
- Sequence of **events**:
  - **Delta Time** (variable-length quantity, 1–4 bytes) — ticks since the previous event.
  - **Event Data** — MIDI message, meta event, or SysEx.

**3. Event Types:**
- **MIDI Events**: Note On/Off, CC, Program Change, etc.
- **Meta Events** (0xFF): Tempo (0x51), Time Signature (0x58), Lyrics (0x05), Markers (0x06), End of Track (0x2F).
- **System Exclusive (SysEx)**: Vendor-specific (0xF0 ... 0xF7).

**Three SMF Formats:**
- **Format 0**: Single multichannel track (simplest).
- **Format 1**: Multiple tracks played simultaneously (most common for songs).
- **Format 2**: Multiple independent sequences (rare; used for pattern libraries).

**Diagram (already provided in Section 4.3 of these notes) should accompany this answer.**

---

**b) Explain the General MIDI (GM) standard. Why is it important for cross-device compatibility? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**General MIDI (GM)** is a standardized extension to the MIDI 1.0 specification, ratified in **1991**, that ensures a MIDI file sounds the same (or at least similar) on any GM-compliant device, regardless of the manufacturer.

**Key Provisions of GM:**

**1. Minimum 128-Program Instrument Map:**
A fixed table assigns Program Change numbers 0–127 to specific instrument sounds.
- Example: Program 0 = Acoustic Grand Piano, Program 24 = Nylon Guitar, Program 73 = Flute, Program 127 = Gunshot.

**2. Minimum 24-Note Polyphony:**
Every GM device must be capable of playing at least 24 notes simultaneously.

**3. Standard Drum Map on Channel 10:**
Channel 10 is reserved exclusively for percussion. Note numbers map to specific percussion sounds:
- Note 35 = Acoustic Bass Drum
- Note 60 = Hi Bongo
- Note 81 = Open Triangle

**4. 16 Independent Channels:**
Maintains the 16-channel MIDI standard.

**5. Velocity Sensitivity:**
Velocity (1–127) must produce audible dynamic variation.

**Why GM Matters for Cross-Device Compatibility:**

- **Pre-GM (before 1991):** A Yamaha PSS-790 hearing a Program 25 would produce a Yamaha-specific patch — completely different from a Roland device's Program 25. Files were non-portable.
- **Post-GM (after 1991):** Any GM device, given the same Program Change, plays the **standardized instrument**. A file created on Device A sounds consistent on Device B.
- **Application Impact:** Karaoke files, game music, ringtone distribution, and online MIDI libraries became practical.

**Limitations of GM:**
- Only 128 instruments — not enough for orchestral libraries.
- No standardized effects (reverb, chorus) — varies by device.
- No standard for synthesizer parameters (filter cutoff, envelopes) — addressed by **GS (Roland)** and **XG (Yamaha)** extensions, and finally by **MIDI 2.0 (2020)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> For the SMF sub-question, students frequently **omit the byte-size of the header chunk** (14 bytes) and **forget the variable-length quantity (VLQ) encoding** of delta-times. Examiners specifically award marks for the **VLQ explanation** — memorize that delta-time is stored in 1–4 bytes with the MSB of each byte acting as a "continuation bit."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ MIDI = **Musical Instrument Digital Interface**, introduced in **1983**.
- ✅ MIDI transmits **performance instructions**, **NOT audio**.
- ✅ Standard baud rate: **31,250 bits/second**.
- ✅ Connector: **5-pin DIN** (with **5 mA current loop**) or **USB-MIDI**.
- ✅ **3 ports** per device: MIDI **IN**, **OUT**, **THRU**.
- ✅ **Status byte**: MSB = 1 (range 128–255). **Data byte**: MSB = 0 (range 0–127).
- ✅ **16 channels** (1–16) for independent instrument control.
- ✅ **Channel 10** = percussion (GM standard).
- ✅ **Middle C = MIDI note 60**; **A4 (440 Hz) = MIDI note 69**.
- ✅ Formula: $f = 440 \times 2^{(n-69)/12}$ Hz.
- ✅ **Velocity range**: 1–127 (0 = Note Off).
- ✅ **Pitch Bend**: 14-bit (LSB + MSB), default ±2 semitones.
- ✅ **General MIDI (GM)** = 128 standardized patches + Channel 10 drums.
- ✅ **SMF** = Standard MIDI File (`.mid`), with MThd (14 bytes) + MTrk chunks.
- ✅ **Format 0** (single track), **Format 1** (multi-track sync — most common), **Format 2** (multi-track independent).
- ✅ **Meta events**: Tempo (0xFF 0x51), Time Signature (0xFF 0x58), End of Track (0xFF 0x2F 0x00).
- ✅ **VLQ (Variable Length Quantity)**: 1–4 bytes for delta-times, with MSB = continuation flag.
- ✅ **MIDI Time Code (MTC)** = SMPTE synchronization for video sync.
- ✅ **Timing Clock** = 24 pulses per quarter note.
- ✅ **Active Sensing** = heartbeat every 300 ms; absence = connection error.
- ✅ **MIDI 2.0** (2020) introduces higher resolution, per-note controllers, and MPE (MIDI Polyphonic Expression).
- ✅ Typical MIDI file size: **a few KB per minute**; 3-min MIDI ~ 4 KB vs 30 MB WAV.
- ✅ MIDI is a **descriptor-based** representation in multimedia taxonomy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:**
> MIDI questions in KTU 2024 ESE are typically 3-mark (definition) or 14-mark (architecture + message types). Always:
> 1. Define MIDI in the first sentence.
> 2. State the year (1983) and baud rate (31,250 bps) for instant marker-friendliness.
> 3. Draw the **block diagram** with labeled arrows — examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for the diagram.
> 4. Tabulate message types with status bytes in hex.
> 5. Conclude with **real-world applications** (DAW, film scoring, games) to score the "Application" CO marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts in Digital Video
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concepts in Digital Video

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Digital Video** is a representation of moving visual information captured, stored, processed, transmitted, and displayed as a discrete-time sequence of digital images called **frames**. Each frame is a 2-D array of **pixels** (picture elements), where every pixel is encoded as a finite-precision binary number representing its luminance (brightness) and chrominance (color) components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"Digital video is a discrete representation of visual information obtained by sampling an analog video signal spatially (in two dimensions) and temporally (across time), and quantizing each sample into a finite number of bits."*

### 1.2 Three Pillars of Digital Video
Every digital video system is fully characterized by **three independent orthogonal parameters**:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Units | KTU Standard |
|---|-----------|--------|-------|--------------|
| 1 | Spatial Resolution | $W \times H$ | pixels per frame | **1920 × 1080 (Full HD)**, 3840 × 2160 (4K) |
| 2 | Temporal Resolution | $f$ | frames per second (fps) | **24, 25, 30, 50, 60 fps** |
| 3 | Color Resolution (Bit Depth) | $b$ | bits per pixel | **8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Flipbook**
> Think of digital video exactly like a **child's flipbook**. Each page is one **frame** of the video. The number of pages per second is the **frame rate** ($f$). The detail drawn on each page is the **spatial resolution** ($W \times H$). And the number of colored pencils used per drawing is the **bit depth** ($b$). The more pages you flip per second, the more detailed each page, and the more colors you have, the "smoother, sharper, and richer" the perceived motion becomes — but the bigger the book (file size) gets.

### 1.4 Analog vs. Digital Video

| Property | Analog Video | Digital Video |
|----------|--------------|---------------|
| Representation | Continuous waveform | Discrete samples (bits) |
| Noise susceptibility | High (cumulative) | Low (error correction possible) |
| Storage | Magnetic tape | File / SSD / Cloud |
| Editing | Linear, lossy | Non-linear, lossless possible |
| Bandwidth | Hz | Bits/second (bps) |
| Replication | Degrades | Identical copies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Statement:**
> The transition from analog → digital is governed by three operations: **Sampling** (spatial + temporal discretization), **Quantization** (amplitude discretization), and **Coding** (compression).

### 1.5 Human Visual System (HVS) Constraints

> [!NOTE]
> The design of every digital video system is bounded by the **Human Visual System (HVS)**:
> - **Flicker fusion threshold** ≈ **50–60 Hz** (above this, motion looks continuous)
> - **Static acuity** ≈ **1 arc-minute** (governs required pixel density — PPI)
> - **Chromatic acuity** ≈ 4× lower than luminance acuity (governs **chroma subsampling**)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Digital Video Frame

A single digital video frame is structured as a hierarchy:

1. **Frame** → entire still image
2. **Field** (in interlaced video) → half the lines (odd or even)
3. **Pixel** → smallest addressable point
4. **Component** (Y, Cb, Cr or R, G, B) → per-pixel channel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interlaced vs. Progressive — KTU Favourite Question**
> - **Interlaced ($i$)**: Odd lines drawn in one half-frame (field), even lines in the next. Halves bandwidth but causes **combing artifacts**. Example: 1080**i**.
> - **Progressive ($p$)**: All lines drawn sequentially in a single frame. Preferred for modern displays. Example: 1080**p**, 4K**p**.

### 2.2 Color Models Used in Digital Video

| Color Model | Components | Used In | Why |
|-------------|-----------|---------|-----|
| **RGB** | R, G, B | Cameras, displays, scanners | Native to sensors and screens |
| **YUV** | Y, U, V | Analog broadcast (PAL/NTSC) | Separates brightness from color |
| **YCbCr** | Y, Cb, Cr | **Digital video (ITU-R BT.601/709)** | Digital form of YUV; enables subsampling |
| **HSV/HSL** | Hue, Saturation, Value | Graphics, editing | Intuitive for humans |

The conversion from **RGB to YCbCr (ITU-R BT.601)** is:

$$Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$$

$$C_b = 0.564(B - Y)$$

$$C_r = 0.713(R - Y)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The coefficients 0.299, 0.587, 0.114 reflect the human eye's differing sensitivity to Red, Green, and Blue (Green dominates, hence the largest weight).

### 2.3 Chroma Subsampling (Critical KTU Topic)

Chroma subsampling exploits the fact that human eyes are **far more sensitive to luminance (Y) than chrominance (Cb, Cr)**. It reduces the number of chroma samples stored.

Notation: **J : a : b**
- **J** = horizontal sampling reference (usually 4)
- **a** = number of chroma samples in the **first** row of 4 luma samples
- **b** = number of chroma samples in the **second** row of 4 luma samples

| Format | Cb per 4 luma (row 1) | Cr per 4 luma (row 1) | Bits/pixel (8-bit) | Use Case |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|-------------------|----------|
| **4:4:4** | 4 | 4 | **24 bits** | Master / cinema |
| **4:2:2** | 2 | 2 | **16 bits** | Broadcast (ProRes 422) |
| **4:2:0** | 1 | 1 | **12 bits** | DVD, Blu-ray, streaming, **H.264** |
| **4:1:1** | 1 | 1 | **12 bits** | DV, NTSC DV |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All the formulas you need for Module 1 video calculations — memorize this table.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Pixels per frame | $N_{px} = W \times H$ | pixels |
| 2 | Bits per frame | $N_b = N_{px} \times c \times b$ | bits |
| 3 | **Video Bit Rate (Uncompressed)** | $R = W \cdot H \cdot f \cdot c \cdot b$ | **bits per second (bps)** |
| 4 | File Size (Uncompressed) | $S = R \cdot T$ | bits → bytes/8 |
| 5 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Uncompressed Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Bandwidth Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful data}}{\text{Total data}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Storage (in MB) | $S_{MB} = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{8 \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024}$ | MB |
| 8 | Storage (in GB) | $S_{GB} = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{8 \cdot 1024^3}$ | GB |

where:
- $W, H$ = width, height in pixels
- $f$ = frame rate (fps)
- $c$ = number of color components (= 3 for RGB, = 1.5 for 4:2:0 YCbCr)
- $b$ = bit depth per component
- $T$ = duration in seconds

### 2.5 Color Depth and Quantization

If a pixel uses **b bits per component** for 3 components (RGB), the number of distinct colors is:

$$N_{colors} = 2^{3b}$$

| Bit Depth $b$ | Colors per Pixel | KTU Standard |
|---------------|-----------------|--------------|
| 8 | 16,777,216 ($2^{24}$) | Standard HD, sRGB |
| 10 | 1,073,741,824 ($2^{30}$) | HDR, ProRes 422 HQ |
| 12 | 68,719,476,736 ($2^{36}$) | Cinema (ACES) |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are foundational for:
> - **Broadcast engineering**: designing MPEG-TS multiplexers and DVB pipelines.
> - **Storage engineering**: sizing SAN/NAS for 4K/8K post-production houses.
> - **Network engineering**: dimensioning CDN throughput for OTT streaming (Netflix, Hotstar).
> - **Codec design**: justifying H.264/HEVC/VVC quantization matrices and GOP structures.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Uncompressed Video Bit Rate

**Starting Principle.** A digital video is a temporal sequence of frames, each frame being a 2-D grid of pixels, each pixel having a finite set of bit-encoded color components.

**Step 1 — Count the pixels in one frame.**
Each frame has width $W$ pixels and height $H$ pixels. Therefore the total number of spatial samples per frame is simply the rectangular area:

$$N_{px} = W \times H \quad \text{(pixels per frame)}$$

**Step 2 — Count the samples per pixel.**
For an RGB signal, $c = 3$ (one sample per primary). For a 4:2:0 YCbCr signal, the effective sample count per 4 luma pixels is $4 + 1 + 1 = 6$ samples for 4 pixels, hence $c = 1.5$. Each sample is quantized to $b$ bits.

$$N_{b/pixel} = c \cdot b \quad \text{(bits per pixel)}$$

**Step 3 — Bits per single frame.**
Multiplying the number of pixels in a frame by the bits per pixel gives the data content of one still image:

$$N_{frame} = W \cdot H \cdot c \cdot b \quad \text{(bits per frame)}$$

**Step 4 — Bits per second (Rate).**
Since $f$ frames are presented every second, the per-frame count is scaled by $f$ to yield the streaming bit rate:

$$\boxed{R = W \cdot H \cdot f \cdot c \cdot b \quad \text{(bits per second, bps)}}$$

**Step 5 — Total File Size.**
Integrating the rate over a duration of $T$ seconds:

$$\boxed{S = R \cdot T = W \cdot H \cdot f \cdot c \cdot b \cdot T \quad \text{(bits)}}$$

To convert bits to bytes, divide by 8. To convert to megabytes (MB), divide by $8 \times 1024 \times 1024$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU 3-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** A 1080p (1920 × 1080) video is recorded at 30 fps with 8-bit RGB color. Calculate the **uncompressed bit rate** and the **file size for 10 minutes** of footage.

**Given:**
- $W = 1920$, $H = 1080$
- $f = 30 \text{ fps}$
- $c = 3$ (RGB)
- $b = 8 \text{ bits}$
- $T = 10 \text{ min} = 600 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Bit rate:**

$$R = 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 3 \times 8$$

$$R = 1920 \times 1080 \times 720$$

$$R = 1{,}492{,}992{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 1.493 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 2 — File size in bits:**

$$S_{bits} = R \cdot T = 1{,}492{,}992{,}000 \times 600 = 8.958 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — File size in GB:**

$$S_{GB} = \frac{8.958 \times 10^{11}}{8 \times 1024^3} = \frac{8.958 \times 10^{11}}{8.590 \times 10^{9}} \approx 104.3 \text{ GB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Only **10 minutes** of uncompressed 1080p RGB video consumes over **100 GB** of storage — this is precisely why compression (H.264, HEVC) is mandatory in real systems.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 (4:2:0 Subsampling)

> **Problem:** Same 1080p, 30 fps, 8-bit signal, but with **4:2:0 YCbCr** subsampling. Find the new bit rate and compare.

**Given:** $c = 1.5$ (effective samples per pixel for 4:2:0).

**Step 1 — Rate:**

$$R = 1920 \times 1080 \times 30 \times 1.5 \times 8$$

$$R = 7.464 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps} \approx 746.4 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison ratio:**

$$\frac{R_{4:2:0}}{R_{RGB}} = \frac{1.5}{3} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{50\% bandwidth saved}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **50% reduction** is achieved with **no perceptible visual loss** in typical viewing conditions — a perfect example of exploiting HVS characteristics.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 (Compression Ratio)

> **Problem:** The same 10-minute 4:2:0 video is encoded with H.264 to a final file size of 1.2 GB. Find the **compression ratio**.

**Step 1 — Uncompressed size (from Example 2, 10 min):**

$$S_{uncomp} = 7.464 \times 10^{8} \times 600 = 4.478 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits} = 52.15 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 2 — Compressed size:**

$$S_{comp} = 1.2 \text{ GB} = 1.2 \times 8 \times 1024^3 = 1.031 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio:**

$$CR = \frac{S_{uncomp}}{S_{comp}} = \frac{4.478 \times 10^{11}}{1.031 \times 10^{10}} \approx 43.4 : 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** H.264 has compressed the original by a factor of **≈ 43×** while remaining visually near-lossless.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix (4:2:0 vs RGB vs 4:2:2 at 1080p/30fps/8-bit)

| Subsampling | $c$ | Bit Rate (Mbps) | Size per min (MB) | Visual Quality |
|-------------|-----|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| RGB 4:4:4 | 3.0 | **1492.99** | **11{,}180** | Reference |
| YCbCr 4:2:2 | 2.0 | **995.33** | **7{,}453** | Excellent |
| YCbCr 4:2:0 | 1.5 | **746.50** | **5{,}590** | Very Good |
| YCbCr 4:1:1 | 1.5 | **746.50** | **5{,}590** | Acceptable |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Digital Video Pipeline (Capture → Display)

The following **Mermaid block diagram** depicts the complete journey of a digital video signal from physical light to the final display — a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark question pattern**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Light Source] --> B[Lens and Optical Filter]
    B --> C[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    C --> D[Analog Signal]
    D --> E[ADC - Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Color Filter Array - Bayer Pattern]
    F --> G[Demosaicing and Color Interpolation]
    G --> H[Color Space Conversion RGB to YCbCr]
    H --> I[Chroma Subsampling 4:2:0 or 4:2:2]
    I --> J[Compression Encoder H.264 or HEVC]
    J --> K[Bitstream Storage or Transmission]
    K --> L[Decoder at Receiver]
    L --> M[Decompression and YCbCr to RGB]
    M --> N[Display Device LCD or OLED]
    N --> O[Perceived Continuous Motion by HVS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When drawing this pipeline, **always label the conversions explicitly** — e.g., mention "RGB → YCbCr" at step H, and "YCbCr → RGB" at step M. Examiners award marks for these explicit transitions.

---

### 4.2 Chroma Subsampling Visual Block Diagram

The following block diagram explains **what happens to a 4×4 pixel block** in 4:4:4 vs 4:2:2 vs 4:2:0 — the KTU classic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[4x4 Pixel Block - 16 Luma Samples] --> Q1{4:4:4 - No subsampling}
    P --> Q2{4:2:2 - Horizontal 2:1 subsampling}
    P --> Q3{4:2:0 - Horizontal and Vertical 2:1}

    Q1 --> R1[Y: 16 samples, Cb: 16 samples, Cr: 16 samples]
    R1 --> S1[Total: 48 samples = 24 bits per pixel]

    Q2 --> R2[Y: 16 samples, Cb: 8 samples, Cr: 8 samples]
    R2 --> S2[Total: 32 samples = 16 bits per pixel]

    Q3 --> R3[Y: 16 samples, Cb: 4 samples, Cr: 4 samples]
    R3 --> S3[Total: 24 samples = 12 bits per pixel]
```

---

### 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of a Digital Video Stream

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Digital Video Stream] --> B[Sequence of GOPs]
    B --> C[GOP - Group of Pictures]
    C --> D1[I-Frame - Intra coded]
    C --> D2[P-Frame - Predictive]
    C --> D3[B-Frame - Bidirectional]

    D1 --> E[Slice]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    E --> F[Macroblock 16x16]
    F --> G[Block 8x8 or 4x4]
    G --> H[Pixel with Y, Cb, Cr]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This hierarchy (Sequence → GOP → Frame → Slice → Macroblock → Block → Pixel) is the structural skeleton of **H.264/AVC and HEVC** bitstreams — a frequent KTU Module 3 cross-link question.

---

### 4.4 Resolution Naming Convention (Industry Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SD 480p - 720x480] --> B[HD 720p - 1280x720]
    B --> C[Full HD 1080p - 1920x1080]
    C --> D[Quad HD 1440p - 2560x1440]
    D --> E[4K UHD 2160p - 3840x2160]
    E --> F[8K UHD 4320p - 7680x4320]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*With a neat diagram, explain the various steps involved in the digital video pipeline from capture to display.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The digital video pipeline is the sequence of operations that converts physical light into a viewable moving image:

1. **Capture:** Light from a scene passes through the lens and strikes the **image sensor** (CCD or CMOS), producing an analog electrical signal.
2. **Sampling & Quantization:** The **ADC** samples the analog signal spatially (into pixels) and temporally (into frames), then quantizes each sample to a finite bit-depth (typically 8 or 10 bits).
3. **Color Processing:** A **Bayer Color Filter Array** is demosaiced, and the resulting RGB triplet is **converted to YCbCr** (ITU-R BT.601) to separate luminance from chrominance.
4. **Chroma Subsampling:** 4:2:0 or 4:2:2 subsampling is applied to reduce data by exploiting the HVS's lower chromatic acuity.
5. **Compression:** A codec (H.264 / HEVC) encodes the YCbCr frames into a compressed bitstream (I, P, B frames).
6. **Storage/Transmission:** The bitstream is stored on media or transmitted over a network.
7. **Decode & Display:** The decoder reconstructs frames, converts back to RGB, and drives the display device (LCD/OLED), where the HVS perceives continuous motion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often skip the **YCbCr ↔ RGB** conversion arrows at both ends. This single omission loses **1 full mark**.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*Compare the 4:4:4, 4:2:2, and 4:2:0 chroma subsampling schemes with respect to bits per pixel and typical use cases.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Format | Cb per 4 luma (row 1 / row 2) | Cr per 4 luma (row 1 / row 2) | Bits/pixel (8-bit) | Typical Use |
|--------|------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 4:4:4 | 4 / 4 | 4 / 4 | 24 | Cinema master, high-end VFX |
| 4:2:2 | 2 / 2 | 2 / 2 | 16 | Broadcast ProRes 422, SDI |
| 4:2:0 | 2 / 0 | 2 / 0 | 12 | DVD, Blu-ray, web streaming, H.264/HEVC |

4:4:4 retains full chroma fidelity but doubles the data rate of 4:2:0. 4:2:2 is the broadcast compromise, halving chroma horizontally. 4:2:0 halves chroma in both directions, achieving the highest compression with acceptable visual loss for consumer use.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Pattern]

**Q.A.** *(a)* Define digital video. With a block diagram, explain the architecture of a digital video system and identify **five** key parameters that govern its quality.
*(b)* A standard-definition PAL-DVD stores video at **720 × 576** resolution, **25 fps**, with **4:2:0 subsampling** and **8-bit depth**. Calculate:
    (i) the **uncompressed bit rate**,
    (ii) the **uncompressed file size for 2 hours** of video,
    (iii) the **compression ratio** if the final encoded file size on the DVD is **4.7 GB**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Digital Video Definition & Architecture (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Digital video is the discrete binary representation of moving visual information obtained by spatially and temporally sampling a continuous visual scene, with each sample quantized to a finite number of bits and structured as a temporal sequence of still frames.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scene / Light] --> B[Camera Sensor]
    B --> C[Sampling and Quantization]
    C --> D[Color Space Conversion RGB to YCbCr]
    D --> E[Chroma Subsampling 4:2:0]
    E --> F[Compression Codec]
    F --> G[Storage or Network]
    G --> H[Decoder]
    H --> I[Display]
```

**Five Key Quality Parameters (2 marks):**
1. **Spatial resolution** $W \times H$ (pixels per frame)
2. **Temporal resolution / frame rate** $f$ (fps)
3. **Color depth / bit depth** $b$ (bits per sample)
4. **Chroma subsampling format** (4:4:4, 4:2:2, 4:2:0)
5. **Compression scheme & bit rate** (CBR, VBR, codec used)

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $W = 720$, $H = 576$, $f = 25$ fps, $c = 1.5$ (4:2:0), $b = 8$, $T = 2 \text{ h} = 7200 \text{ s}$, $S_{DVD} = 4.7 \text{ GB}$.

**(i) Uncompressed bit rate (2 marks):**

$$R = 720 \times 576 \times 25 \times 1.5 \times 8$$

$$R = 414{,}720 \times 25 \times 1.5 \times 8$$

$$R = 414{,}720 \times 300 = 1.244 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps} \approx 124.4 \text{ Mbps}$$

**(ii) Uncompressed file size for 2 hours (3 marks):**

$$S_{bits} = R \cdot T = 1.244 \times 10^{8} \times 7200 = 8.957 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits}$$

Converting to GB:

$$S_{GB} = \frac{8.957 \times 10^{11}}{8 \times 1024^3} = \frac{8.957 \times 10^{11}}{8.59 \times 10^{9}} \approx 104.3 \text{ GB}$$

**(iii) Compression Ratio (2 marks):**

$$S_{comp} = 4.7 \text{ GB} = 4.7 \times 8 \times 1024^3 = 4.038 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits}$$

$$CR = \frac{S_{uncomp}}{S_{comp}} = \frac{8.957 \times 10^{11}}{4.038 \times 10^{10}} \approx 22.18 : 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert 4:2:0's effective samples per pixel to $c = 1.5$ instead of $c = 3$. This **doubles** the bit rate spuriously and loses **2 marks**. Always derive $c$ from the chosen subsampling format.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**Q.B.** *(a)* Explain the **YCbCr color model** in detail. Derive the relationship between RGB and YCbCr components, and justify why digital video systems use YCbCr instead of RGB.
*(b)* A **4K UHD** video stream has resolution **3840 × 2160**, **60 fps**, **10-bit depth**, and uses **4:2:0 subsampling**. Calculate:
    (i) the **uncompressed bit rate** in **Gbps**,
    (ii) the **storage required for 90 minutes** in **TB**,
    (iii) the **minimum sustained network bandwidth** in **Mbps** to stream this video live without buffering.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) YCbCr Color Model (7 marks)**

YCbCr is the digital encoding of the analog YUV color space standardized by **ITU-R BT.601** for SD and **ITU-R BT.709** for HD. It **decouples luminance from chrominance**:

- **Y** — Luma (brightness) — weighted sum of RGB: $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$
- **Cb** — Blue-difference chroma: $C_b = 0.564(B - Y)$
- **Cr** — Red-difference chroma: $C_r = 0.713(R - Y)$

**Justification for YCbCr over RGB (4 marks):**

1. **HVS Exploitation:** The human eye has ~4× more sensitivity to luma than chroma, so chroma can be subsampled safely.
2. **Bandwidth Efficiency:** 4:2:0 YCbCr uses only **12 bits/pixel** vs RGB's **24 bits/pixel** — a **50% bandwidth saving**.
3. **Backward Compatibility:** Legacy black-and-white displays decode only the Y channel; chroma is ignored gracefully.
4. **Compression Friendliness:** Codecs (H.264, HEVC) operate on YCbCr internally, exploiting luma-chroma asymmetry via separate quantization tables.
5. **Standardization:** ITU-R BT.601/709/2020 are globally adopted broadcast standards.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation for 4K (7 marks)**

**Given:** $W = 3840$, $H = 2160$, $f = 60$ fps, $b = 10$ bits, $c = 1.5$ (4:2:0), $T = 90 \text{ min} = 5400 \text{ s}$.

**(i) Uncompressed bit rate (2 marks):**

$$R = 3840 \times 2160 \times 60 \times 1.5 \times 10$$

$$R = 8{,}294{,}400 \times 60 \times 15$$

$$R = 8{,}294{,}400 \times 900 = 7.465 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps} = 7.465 \text{ Gbps}$$

**(ii) Storage for 90 minutes (3 marks):**

$$S_{bits} = 7.465 \times 10^{9} \times 5400 = 4.031 \times 10^{13} \text{ bits}$$

Converting to TB:

$$S_{TB} = \frac{4.031 \times 10^{13}}{8 \times 1024^4} = \frac{4.031 \times 10^{13}}{8.796 \times 10^{12}} \approx 4.58 \text{ TB}$$

**(iii) Minimum Streaming Bandwidth (2 marks):**

For **live streaming without buffering**, the network must sustain the full uncompressed rate (in practical compressed streams, the same principle applies with the compressed rate):

$$R_{stream} = 7.465 \text{ Gbps} = 7465 \text{ Mbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a real codec (e.g., HEVC at 4K/60), practical compressed bit rates are **50–100 Mbps**, so the streaming bandwidth required is reduced by **~75×** due to compression.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Forgetting the factor **$c = 1.5$** for 4:2:0 — students who plug in $c = 3$ (RGB) get an inflated rate of **14.93 Gbps** and lose marks across all sub-parts. Also, watch the unit conversion: $1 \text{ TB} = 8 \times 1024^4$ bits, not $10^{12}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Concepts in Digital Video"**

- ✅ **Digital video** = discrete, sampled, quantized temporal sequence of 2-D image frames.
- ✅ Three governing parameters: **Resolution ($W \times H$)**, **Frame Rate ($f$)**, **Bit Depth ($b$)**.
- ✅ Master bit-rate formula: $\mathbf{R = W \cdot H \cdot f \cdot c \cdot b}$ (in bps).
- ✅ File size formula: $\mathbf{S = R \cdot T}$; always convert bits → bytes → KB → MB → GB → TB using **powers of 1024**.
- ✅ **RGB → YCbCr** conversion (ITU-R BT.601):
  - $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$
  - $C_b = 0.564(B - Y)$, $C_r = 0.713(R - Y)$
- ✅ **Chroma Subsampling** reduces bandwidth exploiting the HVS — remember the effective samples per pixel: 4:4:4 → $c=3$, 4:2:2 → $c=2$, 4:2:0 → $c=1.5$.
- ✅ Bits per pixel for 8-bit signals: 4:4:4 = **24**, 4:2:2 = **16**, 4:2:0 = **12**.
- ✅ **Interlaced ($i$) vs Progressive ($p$)** — modern video is progressive.
- ✅ **Number of colors** for $b$-bit RGB: $N = 2^{3b}$.
- ✅ **Compression Ratio** $CR = S_{uncompressed} / S_{compressed}$ — always > 1; H.264 typically achieves 40–100×, HEVC 100–300×.
- ✅ **Frame hierarchy:** Sequence → GOP → Frame → Slice → Macroblock (16×16) → Block (8×8) → Pixel.
- ✅ **HVS constants** to memorize: flicker fusion ~50–60 Hz, chromatic acuity ≈ ¼ of luma acuity.
- ✅ **Conversion watch:** $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$, $1 \text{ MB} = 1024^2 \text{ bytes}$, $1 \text{ GB} = 1024^3 \text{ bytes}$, $1 \text{ TB} = 1024^4 \text{ bytes}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lossless Compression Algorithms - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lossless Compression Algorithms - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lossless Compression** is a class of data compression algorithms that encode information such that the **original data can be perfectly reconstructed, bit-for-bit, from the compressed representation**. No information is discarded during the compression process. Mathematically, if $C$ is the compression function and $D$ is the decompression function, then $D(C(X)) = X$ for every input $X$.

**Information** in the context of multimedia refers to any digital payload — text, audio, image, or video — represented as a finite sequence of symbols drawn from a finite alphabet $\Sigma$. The fundamental goal of compression is to reduce the **expected code length per symbol** while preserving all semantic content.

## 1.2 Lossless vs. Lossy: The Two Philosophical Pillars

Multimedia compression techniques divide cleanly into two paradigms:

| Paradigm | Core Principle | Reconstruction | Typical Use Case |
|----------|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Lossless** | Preserves every bit | $D(C(X)) = X$ exactly | Text, executable code, medical imaging, archival storage |
| **Lossy** | Discards perceptually irrelevant bits | $D(C(X)) \approx X$ | Streaming video (H.264/HEVC), JPEG images, MP3 audio |

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Compression? — The Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> A single 1920×1080 **uncompressed** 24-bit RGB image consumes $1920 \times 1080 \times 3 \approx 6.22$ MB. A 90-minute uncompressed CD-quality audio file consumes roughly $750$ MB. An uncompressed 1080p video at 30 fps demands nearly $750$ GB/hour. Compression is not optional — it is the *enabling technology* of modern multimedia.

The three principal engineering drivers are:
- **Storage efficiency** — fitting more data on finite media
- **Bandwidth economy** — transmitting data over constrained channels
- **Latency reduction** — smaller payloads mean faster delivery

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Suitcase Packing Problem:** Imagine you must pack a wardrobe into a suitcase. *Lossy compression* is like leaving behind clothes you will probably never wear — faster, smaller, but you cannot recover them. *Lossless compression* is like a master folder who folds, tucks, and arranges every garment perfectly so nothing is lost, yet the suitcase is far smaller. The master folder achieves this by exploiting *patterns* (e.g., all socks together, rolled shirts) — exactly what statistical redundancy in data allows a compression algorithm to do.

## 1.5 Information-Theoretic Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entropy** $H(X)$ — defined by **Claude Shannon in 1948** — is the theoretical lower bound on the average number of bits required to encode a random variable $X$. For a discrete source emitting symbol $s_i$ with probability $p_i$, the self-information is $I(s_i) = -\log_2(p_i)$ bits, and the **Shannon Entropy** is the expected self-information:
>
> $$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i \text{ bits/symbol}$$

Every lossless codec operates by approximating this entropy bound; no lossless algorithm can compress below $H(X)$ without error.

## 1.6 Key Performance Metrics

- **Compression Ratio** $CR = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$
- **Space Saving** $SS = 1 - \frac{\text{Compressed Size}}{\text{Original Size}}$
- **Bits per Symbol** $B = \frac{\text{Total Compressed Bits}}{\text{Number of Source Symbols}}$
- **Coding Redundancy** $R = B - H(X)$ (must be $\geq 0$ for any lossless scheme)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sources of Data Redundancy

Compression — whether lossless or lossy — exists *only* because multimedia data is **redundant**. Three fundamental redundancy types are exploited:

1. **Coding Redundancy** — Fixed-length codes (e.g., ASCII = 8 bits/char) waste bits on frequent symbols and starve rare ones.
2. **Spatial / Temporal Redundancy** — Adjacent pixels in an image (spatial) or consecutive frames in video (temporal) are highly correlated.
3. **Psychovisual / Psychoacoustic Redundancy** — The human perceptual system cannot resolve certain high-frequency details (exploited by *lossy* methods).

> [!NOTE]
> Lossless algorithms exploit the *first* type rigorously, and the *second* type implicitly (through context modeling and prediction). The *third* type is the exclusive domain of lossy compression.

## 2.2 Two Philosophical Families of Lossless Compression

| Family | Core Idea | Classic Algorithms |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|
| **Statistical (Entropy-Based)** | Assign shorter codes to more probable symbols; approach Shannon entropy | Shannon-Fano, **Huffman**, Arithmetic, Golomb-Rice, ANS |
| **Dictionary-Based** | Replace repeated substrings with references to a single stored copy | **Lempel-Ziv (LZ77, LZ78)**, LZW, DEFLATE |

A **hybrid** (e.g., DEFLATE = LZ77 + Huffman) combines the strengths of both.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 2.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Self-Information | $I(s_i) = -\log_b p_i$ | bits if $b=2$ |
| 2 | Shannon Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol |
| 3 | Average Code Length | $\bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i$ | bits/symbol |
| 4 | Coding Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\%$ | percentage |
| 5 | Coding Redundancy | $R = \bar{L} - H(X) \geq 0$ | bits/symbol |
| 6 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{n_1}{n_2}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Space Saving | $SS = 1 - \frac{1}{CR}$ | fraction |
| 8 | Kraft Inequality (prefix codes) | $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | necessary & sufficient |
| 9 | Run-Length Encoded size | $n_2 = n_1 \cdot \frac{1 + \log_2(k)}{k}$ | for runs of avg. length $k$, alphabet size $\vert\Sigma\vert$ |
| 10 | Fano Inequality | $H(X) \leq \bar{L} < H(X) + 1$ | any instantaneous code |

> **Pitfall Note:** In the Kraft Inequality above, the symbol within the summation is **two raised to the power of negative code length**, not absolute value. Always express lengths as positive integers.

## 2.4 The Noiseless Coding Theorem (Shannon's First Theorem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Shannon's Source Coding Theorem (1948):** For a discrete memoryless source with entropy $H(X)$, it is possible to encode the source with an average length $\bar{L}$ such that $\bar{L} \geq H(X)$, and arbitrarily close to $H(X)$. Conversely, no lossless code can have $\bar{L} < H(X)$. Thus $H(X)$ is the **unbreakable speed limit** of lossless compression.

This single theorem is the *theological foundation* of every lossless codec.

## 2.5 Why Variable-Length Coding is the Heart of Lossless Compression

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If every symbol used the same number of bits (fixed-length), the average length would equal the symbol-length regardless of probability. Variable-length coding (VLC) breaks this ceiling: it spends **fewer bits on frequent symbols** and **more bits on rare symbols**, reducing the *expected* length $\bar{L}$ toward $H(X)$.

The non-negotiable engineering constraint is the **Prefix Property** — no codeword may be a prefix of another. This guarantees *instantaneous* (real-time) decodability.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PNG** image format — DEFLATE (LZ77 + Huffman)
- **ZIP / GZIP** archives — DEFLATE family
- **FLAC** lossless audio — Linear prediction + Rice coding
- **PDF** text streams — LZW
- **GIF** — LZW
- **BMP / TIFF** options — RLE
- **7-Zip** — LZMA (a sophisticated LZ77 variant + range coding)
- **Genomic data (FASTQ/FASTA)** — domain-specific lossless compressors
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Entropy is the Lower Bound on $\bar{L}$

We wish to prove that for any uniquely decodable code, the expected code length satisfies $\bar{L} \geq H(X)$. The proof hinges on a single algebraic identity — the **log-sum inequality**.

**Step 1 — Express the difference between $\bar{L}$ and $H(X)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} - H(X) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i l_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \bigl( l_i + \log_2 p_i \bigr) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 \bigl( 2^{l_i} \cdot p_i \bigr) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 \bigl( p_i \cdot 2^{l_i} \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* we are simply rewriting each term using the identity $l_i = \log_2 2^{l_i}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Log-Sum Inequality:**

For non-negative numbers $a_i, b_i$ with $\sum a_i \geq \sum b_i$, the log-sum inequality states:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \log_2 \frac{a_i}{b_i} \geq \bigl( \sum a_i \bigr) \log_2 \frac{\sum a_i}{\sum b_i}
$$

We choose $a_i = p_i$ and $b_i = 2^{-l_i}$, so that $\log_2(a_i/b_i) = \log_2(p_i \cdot 2^{l_i})$.

**Step 3 — Invoke the Kraft Inequality:**

For any prefix code, $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$. Therefore $\sum b_i \leq 1$, while $\sum a_i = 1$. The right-hand side of the log-sum inequality becomes:

$$
\bigl(\sum a_i\bigr) \log_2 \frac{\sum a_i}{\sum b_i} = 1 \cdot \log_2 \frac{1}{\sum b_i} \geq 1 \cdot \log_2 \frac{1}{1} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude:**

$$
\bar{L} - H(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 \frac{p_i}{2^{-l_i}} \geq 0
$$

Hence, $\bar{L} \geq H(X)$. Equality is achieved only when $p_i = 2^{-l_i}$ for every $i$, i.e., when symbol probabilities are exact negative powers of two. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing Entropy, $\bar{L}$, $\eta$, and $R$

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** A source emits four symbols $A, B, C, D$ with probabilities $p_A=0.5$, $p_B=0.25$, $p_C=0.125$, $p_D=0.125$. Compute $H(X)$, the average code length for a hypothetical assignment with $l_A=1, l_B=2, l_C=3, l_D=3$, and the efficiency.

**Step 1 — Compute self-informations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(A) &= -\log_2(0.5) = 1.000 \text{ bit} \\
I(B) &= -\log_2(0.25) = 2.000 \text{ bits} \\
I(C) &= -\log_2(0.125) = 3.000 \text{ bits} \\
I(D) &= -\log_2(0.125) = 3.000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) \\
&= 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 \\
&= 1.750 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute average code length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} &= 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) \\
&= 1.750 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute efficiency and redundancy:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1.750}{1.750} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

$$
R = 1.750 - 1.750 = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** The code matches the entropy bound exactly because each probability is an exact negative power of two. This is the *ideal* case — Huffman coding achieves this in such "clean" distributions.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Lossy-Looking-but-Lossless RLE Toy Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem.** A binary image row contains 200 consecutive white pixels (W) followed by 30 black (B) followed by 90 white. Original data uses 1 bit per pixel = 320 bits. Compute the size of the **Run-Length Encoded** bitstream if the encoder uses 8 bits for run count and 1 bit for the color.

**Step 1 — Identify runs:**

Three runs: $(W, 200), (B, 30), (W, 90)$.

**Step 2 — Compute encoded size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_2 &= 3 \text{ runs} \times (8 \text{ bits count} + 1 \text{ bit color}) \\
&= 3 \times 9 = 27 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute compression ratio and saving:**

$$
CR = \frac{320}{27} \approx 11.85
$$

$$
SS = 1 - \frac{1}{11.85} \approx 0.916 = 91.6\%
$$

**Interpretation:** RLE is spectacular for repetitive data but is *catastrophic* on noisy/random data, often producing an output *larger* than the input.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: When to Choose What

| Data Characteristic | Recommended Lossless Algorithm | Reasoning |
|---------------------|-------------------------------|-----------|
| Repetitive runs (bilevel images) | **RLE** | Trivial, ultra-fast |
| Skewed symbol probabilities | **Huffman / Arithmetic** | Approximates entropy |
| Repeated substrings (text, code) | **LZ77 / LZW** | Exploits dictionary patterns |
| Streaming real-time data | **Range / ANS coding** | Single-pass, competitive ratios |
| Numeric time-series | **Differential + Rice** | Predictable, low entropy residuals |
| Mixed workload | **DEFLATE (LZ77 + Huffman)** | Industry default; balanced |

## 3.5 Self-Assessment Numerical Drill

> [!TIP]
> **Self-check question:** A 4-symbol source has probabilities $(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1)$. Find $H(X)$ to 4 decimal places.  
> *Answer key:* $H(X) = 0.4 \log_2(1/0.4) + 0.3 \log_2(1/0.3) + 0.2 \log_2(1/0.2) + 0.1 \log_2(1/0.1) \approx 1.8464$ bits/symbol.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Lossless Compression Pipeline (High-Level Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Source X] --> B[Modeling Stage]
    B --> C[Probability Estimation]
    C --> D[Coding Stage]
    D --> E[Compressed Bitstream C]
    E --> F[Transmission or Storage]
    F --> G[Decoding Stage]
    G --> H[Inverse Modeling]
    H --> I[Reconstructed Source X-hat]
    I --> J{Verify X-hat equals X}
    J -- Yes --> K[Lossless Success]
    J -- No --> L[Algorithm Failure]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *modeling* stage captures statistical structure (probabilities, context, dictionary); the *coding* stage maps symbols to bits. The two-stage decomposition is universal — every practical lossless codec separates these concerns, even when they are tightly integrated.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Lossless Compression Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[Lossless Compression Algorithms]
    Root --> A1[Statistical / Entropy Coding]
    Root --> A2[Dictionary-Based Coding]
    Root --> A3[Run-Length Coding]
    Root --> A4[Hybrid Codecs]
    A1 --> B1[Shannon-Fano]
    A1 --> B2[Huffman Coding]
    A1 --> B3[Arithmetic Coding]
    A1 --> B4[Golomb and Rice Codes]
    A1 --> B5[Asymmetric Numeral Systems ANS]
    A2 --> C1[LZ77 Sliding Window]
    A2 --> C2[LZ78 Dictionary Builder]
    A2 --> C3[LZW Lempel Ziv Welch]
    A3 --> D1[Simple RLE]
    A3 --> D2[PackBits RLE]
    A4 --> E1[DEFLATE LZ77 plus Huffman]
    A4 --> E2[LZMA 7-Zip]
    A4 --> E3[Brotli Web Compression]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Where Lossless Sits in a Multimedia Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Raw Multimedia Data] --> L2[Pre-processing Sampling and Quantization]
    L2 --> L3{Compression Required}
    L3 -- Archival or Text --> L4[Lossless Compression]
    L3 -- Streaming or Distribution --> L5[Lossy Compression]
    L4 --> L6[Storage or Channel]
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7[Decompression at Receiver]
    L7 --> L8[Output Reproduction]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing a Lossless Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with input data] --> S2{Is data highly repetitive}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Use RLE first]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Does data have skewed symbol frequencies}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Use Huffman or Arithmetic]
    S4 -- No --> S6{Are there repeated substrings}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Use LZ77 or LZW]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Use BWT plus MTF plus Entropy coder]
    S3 --> S9[Evaluate compression ratio]
    S5 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10{Does CR exceed target}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Accept]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Tune parameters or switch codec]
```

## 4.5 Information-Theoretic Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Symbol Probability p sub i] --> K2[Self-Information I equals minus log p]
    K2 --> K3[Entropy H equals expected I]
    K3 --> K4[Shannon Source Coding Theorem]
    K4 --> K5[Average Length L-bar lower bound H]
    K4 --> K6[Efficiency eta equals H over L-bar]
    K3 --> K7[Kraft Inequality sum two to minus l sub i less or equal 1]
    K7 --> K5
    K5 --> K8[Coding Redundancy R equals L-bar minus H]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

### Q1. Differentiate between lossless and lossy compression techniques. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lossless | Lossy |
|--------|----------|-------|
| Reconstruction | Exact, bit-perfect | Approximate |
| Information discarded | None | Perceptually irrelevant bits |
| Compression ratio | Modest (typically 2:1 to 4:1) | High (10:1 to 100:1) |
| Typical use | Text, code, medical imaging | Streaming video, web images |
| **Example** | PNG, FLAC, ZIP (DEFLATE) | JPEG, MP3, H.264 |

*(1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark for reconstruction property, 1 mark for examples.)*

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

### Q2. Define Shannon entropy. State the Noiseless Source Coding Theorem.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Shannon entropy of a discrete source emitting symbols $s_i$ with probabilities $p_i$ is defined as:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

*(1 mark for the formula, 0.5 mark for stating units.)*

**Shannon's First Theorem (Noiseless Coding Theorem):** For a discrete memoryless source of entropy $H(X)$, there exists a prefix code whose average code length $\bar{L}$ satisfies $H(X) \leq \bar{L} < H(X) + 1$. *(1.5 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question Choice A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Define the terms *self-information*, *entropy*, *average code length*, *coding efficiency*, and *coding redundancy*. Establish the relationship between entropy and the average code length of any instantaneous code. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **Self-information** $I(s_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ bits — quantifies the "surprise" of symbol $s_i$.
- **Entropy** $H(X) = \mathbb{E}[I(s_i)] = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — average information per symbol.
- **Average code length** $\bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i$ where $l_i$ is the bit-length of the codeword for $s_i$.
- **Coding efficiency** $\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\%$.
- **Coding redundancy** $R = \bar{L} - H(X) \geq 0$.

**Relationship proof (4 marks):** For any instantaneous (prefix) code, the **Kraft Inequality** $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ holds. Applying the **log-sum inequality** to $a_i = p_i$ and $b_i = 2^{-l_i}$:

$$
\sum p_i \log_2 \frac{p_i}{2^{-l_i}} \geq \log_2 \frac{\sum p_i}{\sum 2^{-l_i}} \geq \log_2 \frac{1}{1} = 0
$$

Expanding the left-hand side:

$$
\sum p_i \log_2 p_i - \sum p_i (-l_i) = -H(X) + \bar{L} \geq 0
$$

Therefore $\bar{L} \geq H(X)$, proving that **entropy is the absolute lower bound on average code length for any lossless prefix code**.

---

**Q. (b)** A source emits five symbols $A, B, C, D, E$ with probabilities $0.35, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.05$. Calculate the entropy of the source. Suppose a code is designed with codeword lengths $2, 2, 3, 3, 4$ respectively. Compute the average code length, coding efficiency, and coding redundancy. Verify the Kraft inequality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Entropy (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -(0.35 \log_2 0.35 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.05 \log_2 0.05) \\
&= -(0.35(-1.5146) + 0.25(-2.0000) + 0.20(-2.3219) + 0.15(-2.7370) + 0.05(-4.3219)) \\
&= -(-0.5301 - 0.5000 - 0.4644 - 0.4105 - 0.2161) \\
&= 2.1211 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average code length (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{L} = 0.35(2) + 0.25(2) + 0.20(3) + 0.15(3) + 0.05(4) = 0.7 + 0.5 + 0.6 + 0.45 + 0.20 = 2.45 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency and redundancy (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{2.1211}{2.45} \times 100\% \approx 86.58\%
$$

$$
R = 2.45 - 2.1211 = 0.3289 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 4 — Kraft verification (1 mark):**

$$
\sum 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 + 0.0625 = 0.8125 \leq 1
$$

Kraft inequality is satisfied, so a valid prefix code exists with these lengths.

---

### 📌 Question Choice B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the general model of a lossless compression system. Discuss the roles of the *modeling* and *coding* stages. Identify which stage is responsible for the bulk of the intelligence in modern codecs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Source X] → [Modeler] → [Probabilities / Context] → [Coder] → [Bits] → [Channel]
                                                                              ↓
[Reconstructed X̂] ← [Decoder] ← [Bits] ← [Channel]
```

**Modeling stage (2.5 marks):** The modeler estimates the probability distribution of symbols (or, more generally, the conditional distribution given previous context). It captures *statistical structure* — frequencies, repeated substrings, predictive residuals. The output is a set of probabilities used by the coder. **Dictionary-based** methods (LZ77, LZW) and **context-modeling** methods (PPM) are advanced modelers.

**Coding stage (2 marks):** The coder maps symbols to bit strings. With a perfect probability model, *Arithmetic* or *ANS* coders achieve compression within one bit of entropy; *Huffman* achieves optimal discrete-length codes for the given symbol set.

**Bulk of intelligence (0.5 mark):** In modern codecs (e.g., Brotli, Zstd, LZMA), roughly **80–90% of the compression gain** comes from the *modeling* stage, not the entropy coder. A perfect entropy coder on a poor model gives poor compression; a good model with a weak coder still gives substantial gains.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the three fundamental types of data redundancy exploited in image compression. For each type, state whether it is exploited by lossless, lossy, or both categories of algorithms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Coding Redundancy (2.5 marks):** Different symbols in an image are assigned codes of equal length (e.g., 8 bits) even though their probabilities of occurrence differ greatly. Variable-length coding (Huffman, Arithmetic) replaces fixed-length codes with shorter codes for more probable symbols, exploiting this redundancy.
- **Exploited by:** BOTH lossless (Huffman in PNG) and lossy (Huffman in JPEG baseline).

**2. Spatial (Inter-pixel) Redundancy (2.5 marks):** Adjacent pixels in natural images are highly correlated — neighboring pixel values are usually close to each other. This correlation is also called *statistical redundancy*. Predictors (DPCM) and transform methods (DCT) exploit it.
- **Lossless exploitation:** Prediction (PNG filters, FLAC) followed by entropy coding of residuals.
- **Lossy exploitation:** Block transforms (DCT in JPEG, DWT in JPEG 2000).

**3. Psychovisual / Psychoacoustic Redundancy (2 marks):** The human visual and auditory systems cannot resolve all details of an image or sound; subtle high-frequency information can be discarded without perceived loss.
- **Exploited by:** LOSSY ALGORITHMS ONLY. Lossless algorithms *cannot* discard any information; doing so would violate the exact reconstruction requirement.

*(Conclusion: Lossless methods exploit types 1 and 2 only. Lossy methods exploit all three.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the base of the logarithm** — entropy demands $\log_2$, not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$. Wrong base ⇒ numerically wrong, but you may still earn the formula mark.
> 2. **Computing $H(X)$ with a wrong probability** — mixing up the given and computed probabilities. Always re-list $p_i$ before substituting.
> 3. **Confusing *average* and *total* code length** — the question asks for *average* unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 4. **Kraft inequality errors** — many students write $\sum 2^{l_i} \leq 1$ (sign flipped). The correct form is $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$.
> 5. **Omitting units** — entropy is in *bits/symbol*, not "bits" alone. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 6. **Equating lossless = perfect compression** — lossless means *recoverable*, not *optimal*. Compression is bounded below by $H(X)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Lossless Compression Introduction**

- ✅ **Lossless compression** guarantees $D(C(X)) = X$ — bit-perfect recovery.
- ✅ **Lossy compression** discards perceptually-irrelevant data — $D(C(X)) \approx X$.
- ✅ **Compression Ratio** $CR = n_1 / n_2$; **Space Saving** $SS = 1 - 1/CR$.
- ✅ **Self-information** $I(s_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ bits.
- ✅ **Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits/symbol — the unbeatable lower bound on $\bar{L}$.
- ✅ **Average code length** $\bar{L} = \sum p_i l_i$.
- ✅ **Coding efficiency** $\eta = (H(X) / \bar{L}) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Coding redundancy** $R = \bar{L} - H(X) \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Shannon's First Theorem:** $H(X) \leq \bar{L} < H(X) + 1$ for any prefix code.
- ✅ **Kraft Inequality:** $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ — necessary and sufficient for a prefix code to exist.
- ✅ **Three redundancies** — coding, spatial, psychovisual (only first two are lossless-eligible).
- ✅ **Two families** — statistical (entropy) and dictionary-based; DEFLATE is the classic hybrid.
- ✅ **Modeling vs. coding** — modern codec intelligence lives mostly in the modeling stage.
- ✅ **RLE** excels on repetitive data, fails on noisy data.
- ✅ **Fano's inequality** is the per-symbol companion to Shannon's theorem; useful for KTU derivations.
- ✅ Always state **units** (bits/symbol) and the **logarithm base** in entropy answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Information Theory
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Information Theory

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Information Theory?

**Information Theory** is the mathematical framework developed by **Claude E. Shannon** in his landmark 1948 paper *"A Mathematical Theory of Communication."* It quantitatively measures the amount of information contained in a message, the capacity of communication channels, and the fundamental limits of data compression and reliable transmission.

In the KTU 2024 Multimedia Technologies context, Information Theory provides the **theoretical foundation** for understanding how multimedia data (audio, image, video) can be efficiently encoded, transmitted, and decoded across noisy channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Information measure — Information content of a message, Average information (Entropy), Information rate, Markoff models, Channel capacity, Shannon's theorem on channel capacity, Bandwidth – S/N trade-off, Capacity of Gaussian channel, Shannon-Hartley theorem, Introduction to codes — Code efficiency, Redundancy.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (Formal Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Information (Surprisal)**
> The amount of information associated with a single event $x$ having probability $P(x)$ is defined as:
> $$I(x) = -\log_b P(x) = \log_b \left(\frac{1}{P(x)}\right)$$
> where $b$ is the base of the logarithm. When $b=2$, the unit is **bit**; when $b=e$, the unit is **nat**; when $b=10$, the unit is **Hartley** (or *dit*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Entropy (Average Information / Shannon Entropy)**
> The expected (average) self-information per symbol emitted by a discrete memoryless source $X$ with alphabet $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ and probability mass function $P(x_i)$ is:
> $$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_b P(x_i) \text{ bits/symbol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mutual Information**
> The amount of information that one random variable conveys about another. For two random variables $X$ and $Y$:
> $$I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Channel Capacity**
> The maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a noisy channel, defined as:
> $$C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y) \text{ bits/second}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are waiting for your **KTU exam result** on the university website:

- If the website says *"Your result will be published today"* — this message carries **low information** because it was highly expected (high probability).
- If the website says *"Earthquake damaged the examination branch — results delayed by 3 months"* — this carries **high information** because it was extremely unlikely (low probability).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Rule:** *The more surprising an event is, the more information it carries.*
> - Certain event ($P = 1$): $I(x) = 0$ bits (no surprise, no info)
> - Impossible event ($P = 0$): $I(x) = \infty$ bits
> - Coin flip ($P = 0.5$): $I(x) = 1$ bit (perfectly balanced surprise)

**Real-world multimedia example:** A black pixel in a dark video frame is *expected* (low information). A bright white pixel suddenly appearing in a dark scene is *surprising* (high information). Entropy tells us the **average** surprise per pixel — a quantity video codecs like H.264 exploit heavily.

## 1.4 Why Information Theory Matters for Multimedia

| Multimedia Application | Role of Information Theory |
|---|---|
| **Image Compression (JPEG)** | Lossless entropy coding (Huffman, Arithmetic) reaches entropy limit |
| **Video Compression (H.264/HEVC)** | Exploits statistical redundancy to reduce bit rate |
| **Streaming over noisy Wi-Fi** | Channel coding adds redundancy for error protection |
| **Audio (MP3, AAC)** | Perceptual entropy + psychoacoustic masking |
| **5G/6G Multimedia delivery** | Shannon capacity bound governs maximum achievable rate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Self-Information

The function $I(x) = -\log_b P(x)$ must satisfy four logical axioms for any meaningful information measure:

1. **Non-negativity:** $I(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$. Since $0 \leq P(x) \leq 1$, the negative log is always non-negative.
2. **Continuity:** $I(x)$ is a continuous function of $P(x)$. Small probability changes produce small information changes.
3. **Monotonicity:** As $P(x)$ decreases, $I(x)$ increases. Rarer events carry more information.
4. **Additivity for independent events:** If $x$ and $y$ are independent, $I(xy) = I(x) + I(y)$.

The **only** function satisfying all four axioms is the **logarithm** — this is the deep reason Shannon chose it.

## 2.2 Properties of Entropy

The Shannon entropy $H(X)$ has the following foundational properties:

- **Non-negativity:** $H(X) \geq 0$ for any distribution.
- **Maximum value:** For a source with $n$ symbols, $H(X) \leq \log_2 n$, with equality when all symbols are equiprobable (uniform distribution).
- **Minimum value:** $H(X) \geq 0$, with equality when the source is deterministic (one symbol has probability 1).
- **Symmetry:** Permuting the probabilities does not change the entropy.
- **Additivity for independent sources:** If $X$ and $Y$ are independent, $H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y)$.

## 2.3 Joint Entropy, Conditional Entropy, and Their Relationships

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint Entropy** — total uncertainty of a pair $(X,Y)$:
> $$H(X,Y) = -\sum_{i}\sum_{j} P(x_i, y_j) \log_2 P(x_i, y_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Entropy** — uncertainty of $X$ given $Y$ is known:
> $$H(X \mid Y) = -\sum_{j} P(y_j) \sum_{i} P(x_i \mid y_j) \log_2 P(x_i \mid y_j)$$

**Chain Rule of Entropy:**
$$H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y \mid X) = H(Y) + H(X \mid Y)$$

## 2.4 The Famous Venn Diagram of Information Measures

Using the information diagram (analogous to set-theoretic Venn diagrams):

$$I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$$

$$H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y \mid X)$$

$$I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$$

## 2.5 Source Information Rate

For a discrete source emitting $r_s$ symbols per second with entropy $H(X)$ bits per symbol:
$$R = r_s \cdot H(X) \text{ bits/second}$$

The **absolute maximum** source rate is $R_{\max} = r_s \log_2 M$ where $M$ is the alphabet size.

## 2.6 Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem (1948)**
> Given a discrete memoryless channel of capacity $C$ bits/second and a source generating information at rate $R$ bits/second:
> - If $R \leq C$, there exists a channel coding scheme that enables **reliable** (error-free) transmission.
> - If $R > C$, **reliable transmission is impossible** — errors are unavoidable regardless of coding.

## 2.7 Shannon–Hartley Theorem (Bandwidth–S/N Trade-off)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **continuous-time, band-limited, AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise) channel** with bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$:
> $$C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bits/second}$$

This is the single most important formula in digital communications. It states that **capacity can be increased by either increasing bandwidth $B$ or increasing $S/N$**, but with diminishing returns due to the logarithm.

If noise spectral density is $N_0$ (Watts/Hz), then $N = N_0 B$ and:
$$C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N_0 B}\right)$$

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-information | $I(x_i) = -\log_2 P(x_i)$ | bits |
| 2 | Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_i P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)$ | bits/symbol |
| 3 | Maximum entropy | $H_{\max} = \log_2 M$ | $M$ symbols, equiprobable |
| 4 | Joint entropy | $H(X,Y) = -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i,y_j)\log_2 P(x_i,y_j)$ | bits |
| 5 | Conditional entropy | $H(X \mid Y) = -\sum_j P(y_j) \sum_i P(x_i \mid y_j) \log_2 P(x_i \mid y_j)$ | bits |
| 6 | Chain rule | $H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y \mid X)$ | bits |
| 7 | Mutual information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | bits |
| 8 | Information rate | $R = r_s \cdot H(X)$ | bits/second |
| 9 | Shannon–Hartley | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bits/second, AWGN |
| 10 | Binary entropy (Bernoulli) | $H(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$ | bits |
| 11 | Channel capacity (DMC) | $C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y)$ | bits/use |
| 12 | Code efficiency | $\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L_{avg}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$ |
| 13 | Redundancy | $R_d = 1 - \eta$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** The three "golden equations" you must memorize cold for the exam are:
> 1. $H(X) = -\sum p \log_2 p$
> 2. $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$
> 3. $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$

## 2.9 Code Efficiency and Redundancy

For a source with entropy $H(X)$ encoded using symbols of average length $L_{avg}$:

$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} \quad \text{(Code Efficiency)}$$

$$R_d = 1 - \eta \quad \text{(Redundancy)}$$

**Real-world interpretation:** A Huffman code for English text yields $\eta \approx 0.99$ (only 1% redundancy remains), while a fixed-length ASCII code has $\eta \approx 0.4$ (60% redundancy).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solutions

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Entropy of a Binary Source

**Problem:** A binary source emits symbol '0' with probability $P(0) = 0.8$ and symbol '1' with $P(1) = 0.2$. Find the entropy of the source, the information rate if 1000 symbols are emitted per second, and the source redundancy.

### Step 1: Compute self-information of each symbol

Using the formula $I(x_i) = -\log_2 P(x_i)$:

For $x_1 = 0$:
$$I(0) = -\log_2(0.8)$$

$$\log_2(0.8) = \frac{\ln(0.8)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-0.2231}{0.6931} = -0.3219$$

$$I(0) = -(-0.3219) = 0.3219 \text{ bits}$$

For $x_2 = 1$:
$$I(1) = -\log_2(0.2) = -\frac{\ln(0.2)}{\ln(2)} = -\frac{-1.6094}{0.6931} = 2.3219 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the rarer symbol '1' carries **more information** (2.32 bits) than the common '0' (0.32 bits) — this validates the monotonicity property.

### Step 2: Compute the entropy

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{2} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)$$

$$H(X) = -\left[P(0)\log_2 P(0) + P(1)\log_2 P(1)\right]$$

$$H(X) = -[0.8 \times (-0.3219) + 0.2 \times (-2.3219)]$$

$$H(X) = -[-0.2575 + (-0.4644)]$$

$$H(X) = -[-0.7219] = 0.7219 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Cross-check using binary entropy function** $H_b(p)$:
$$H_b(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$$

$$H_b(0.2) = -0.2\log_2(0.2) - 0.8\log_2(0.8)$$

$$H_b(0.2) = -(0.2)(-2.3219) - (0.8)(-0.3219)$$

$$H_b(0.2) = 0.4644 + 0.2575 = 0.7219 \text{ bits/symbol} \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 3: Compute the information rate

$$R = r_s \cdot H(X)$$

With $r_s = 1000$ symbols/second:
$$R = 1000 \times 0.7219 = 721.9 \text{ bits/second}$$

### Step 4: Compute the redundancy

Assuming a fixed-length code of $L = 1$ bit/symbol (since binary):
$$L_{avg} = 1 \text{ bit/symbol}$$

$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} = \frac{0.7219}{1} = 0.7219 \text{ (or 72.19%)}$$

$$R_d = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.7219 = 0.2781 \text{ (or 27.81%)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** A Huffman code could compress this source to $L_{avg} = 0.7219$ bits/symbol on average, achieving $\eta = 100\%$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) Capacity

**Problem:** A Binary Symmetric Channel has transition probability $p = P(Y \neq X) = 0.1$. Find the channel capacity.

### Step 1: Set up the channel transition matrix

The BSC is described by:
$$P(Y=0 \mid X=0) = 1 - p = 0.9$$
$$P(Y=1 \mid X=0) = p = 0.1$$
$$P(Y=0 \mid X=1) = p = 0.1$$
$$P(Y=1 \mid X=1) = 1 - p = 0.9$$

### Step 2: Express mutual information

Let $P(X=0) = q$ and $P(X=1) = 1-q$. The mutual information:
$$I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$$

### Step 3: Compute $H(Y \mid X)$ for BSC

For BSC, regardless of input, the output has crossover probability $p$:
$$H(Y \mid X) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p) = H_b(p)$$

$$H(Y \mid X) = -0.1 \log_2(0.1) - 0.9 \log_2(0.9)$$

$$= -0.1 \times (-3.3219) - 0.9 \times (-0.1520)$$

$$= 0.3322 + 0.1368 = 0.4690 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4: Compute $H(Y)$

Output probabilities:
$$P(Y=0) = q(1-p) + (1-q)p = 0.9q + 0.1(1-q) = 0.8q + 0.1$$
$$P(Y=1) = 1 - P(Y=0) = 0.9 - 0.8q$$

$H(Y)$ is maximized when output is uniform, i.e., $P(Y=0) = P(Y=1) = 0.5$:
$$0.8q + 0.1 = 0.5 \implies q = 0.5$$

This means **uniform input distribution** maximizes $H(Y)$.

### Step 5: Maximum $H(Y)$ and capacity

$$H(Y)_{\max} = \log_2(2) = 1 \text{ bit}$$

$$C = \max_q I(X;Y) = H(Y)_{\max} - H(Y \mid X)$$

$$C = 1 - H_b(0.1) = 1 - 0.4690 = 0.5310 \text{ bits/channel use}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Shannon–Hartley Theorem Application

**Problem:** A telephone channel has bandwidth $B = 3.4$ kHz and $S/N = 30$ dB. Find the channel capacity.

### Step 1: Convert S/N from dB to linear scale

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$30 = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \implies \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) = 3$$

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^3 = 1000$$

### Step 2: Apply Shannon–Hartley

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$C = 3400 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) = 3400 \times \log_2(1001)$$

$$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\ln(1001)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{6.9088}{0.6931} = 9.9672 \text{ bits}$$

$$C = 3400 \times 9.9672 = 33,888.5 \text{ bits/s} \approx 33.9 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical significance:** Shannon's 1948 calculation showed that a 3.4 kHz telephone line could theoretically carry ~34 kbps — a stunning result at the time. This is the theoretical maximum; practical modems (V.90) reached 56 kbps using digital encoding tricks, but the underlying physics is constrained by Shannon.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Mutual Information from a Joint Distribution

**Problem:** Joint distribution $P(X,Y)$ is:
| | $Y=0$ | $Y=1$ |
|---|---|---|
| $X=0$ | 0.4 | 0.1 |
| $X=1$ | 0.1 | 0.4 |

Find $H(X)$, $H(Y)$, $H(X,Y)$, $H(X \mid Y)$, and $I(X;Y)$.

### Step 1: Marginals

$$P(X=0) = 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.5 \quad P(X=1) = 0.1 + 0.4 = 0.5$$
$$P(Y=0) = 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.5 \quad P(Y=1) = 0.1 + 0.4 = 0.5$$

### Step 2: Individual entropies

$$H(X) = -0.5 \log_2(0.5) - 0.5 \log_2(0.5) = 1 \text{ bit}$$
$$H(Y) = 1 \text{ bit (by symmetry)}$$

### Step 3: Joint entropy

$$H(X,Y) = -\sum_{i,j} P(x_i,y_j) \log_2 P(x_i,y_j)$$

$$= -[0.4\log_2 0.4 + 0.1\log_2 0.1 + 0.1\log_2 0.1 + 0.4\log_2 0.4]$$

$$\log_2 0.4 = -1.3219, \quad \log_2 0.1 = -3.3219$$

$$H(X,Y) = -[0.4(-1.3219) + 0.1(-3.3219) + 0.1(-3.3219) + 0.4(-1.3219)]$$

$$= -[-0.5288 - 0.3322 - 0.3322 - 0.5288]$$

$$= 1.7220 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4: Conditional entropy

Using $H(X \mid Y) = H(X,Y) - H(Y)$:
$$H(X \mid Y) = 1.7220 - 1 = 0.7220 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 5: Mutual information

$$I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) = 1 - 0.7220 = 0.2780 \text{ bits}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check using $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$:**
> $I(X;Y) = 1 + 1 - 1.7220 = 0.2780$ bits ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shannon's Communication System Model (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Information Source] --> encA[Source Encoder]
    encA --> chEncA[Channel Encoder]
    chEncA --> modA[Modulator]
    modA --> chA((Noisy Channel))
    chA --> demodA[Demodulator]
    demodA --> chDecA[Channel Decoder]
    chDecA --> srcDecA[Source Decoder]
    srcDecA --> destA[Destination / User]
    noiseA[Noise Source] -.-> chA

    classDef proc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class srcA,destA io
    class encA,chEncA,modA,demodA,chDecA,srcDecA,chDecA proc
```

**Block-by-block explanation (mapped to multimedia engineering):**

- **Information Source:** Audio, image, video data (e.g., camera sensor output).
- **Source Encoder:** Removes statistical redundancy (Huffman, Arithmetic coding, JPEG DCT).
- **Channel Encoder:** Adds controlled redundancy for error protection (Reed–Solomon, Turbo codes, LDPC).
- **Modulator:** Maps bits to analog waveforms for transmission (QAM, OFDM in 4G/5G).
- **Noisy Channel:** Physical medium (fiber, wireless, storage media).
- **Demodulator → Decoders:** Reverse the chain to reconstruct the original signal.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["H of X and Y Total Joint Entropy"]
        direction TB
        a1["H of X"]
        a2["H of Y given X"]
        a3["Mutual Information I of X and Y"]
    end
    note["Chain Rule: H(X,Y) equals H of X plus H of Y given X equals H of Y plus H of X given Y"]
    A --- note

    classDef hi fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef lo fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    class a1,a2 hi
    class a3 mid
    class note lo
```

## 4.3 Source-to-Destination Information Flow (with entropy taps)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Discrete Source X] -->|"symbols r_s per second"| C1[Channel]
    C1 -->|"output Y"| D1[Destination]
    S1 -.->|"H of X bits per symbol"| M1[Measure Average Info]
    C1 -.->|"Channel Capacity C bits per second"| M1
    M1 -.->|"Constraint R less than C for reliable comm"| D1

    classDef src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef chan fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef dst fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    class S1 src
    class C1 chan
    class D1 dst
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow $S_1 \to C_1 \to D_1$ represents physical transmission. The dotted lines show where **information measures** are evaluated: $H(X)$ is computed at the source, $C$ is a property of the channel, and Shannon's theorem demands $R \leq C$ for the destination to recover the source reliably.

## 4.4 Bandwidth vs. S/N Trade-off Visual Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis["Capacity C as function of B and S over N"] --> p1["Increase B holding S over N constant -> C grows linearly with B times log base 2 of one plus S over N"]
    axis --> p2["Increase S over N holding B constant -> C grows as B log base 2 of one plus S over N, diminishing returns"]
    axis --> p3["Limit as B grows with S fixed: C approaches S over N times log base 2 of e per unit bandwidth times B"]

    classDef base fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef p fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    class axis base
    class p1,p2,p3 p
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering insight from the diagram:** Doubling the bandwidth doubles capacity in the high-SNR regime, but in the power-limited regime, doubling bandwidth gives less than a doubling of capacity — engineers must trade bandwidth for power based on which is the constrained resource.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Define self-information and entropy of a discrete source. A source emits two symbols $x_1$ and $x_2$ with probabilities $0.7$ and $0.3$ respectively. Compute the entropy of the source.**

**Model Answer:**

**Self-Information** of an event $x_i$ with probability $P(x_i)$ is the logarithmic measure of surprise:
$$I(x_i) = -\log_2 P(x_i) \text{ bits}$$

**Entropy** of a discrete source $X$ is the expected value of self-information over all symbols:
$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i) \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Computation:**
$$H(X) = -[0.7 \log_2 0.7 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3]$$
$$= -[0.7 \times (-0.5146) + 0.3 \times (-1.7370)]$$
$$= -[-0.3602 - 0.5211] = 0.8813 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**State and explain the Shannon–Hartley theorem. Why is it called the Bandwidth–S/N trade-off?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon–Hartley Theorem:** For a continuous, band-limited AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$:
> $$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bits/second}$$

**Bandwidth–S/N trade-off** refers to the dual routes to increase capacity:
- **Increase $B$** (use more spectrum) — common in broadband, but spectrum is scarce and regulated.
- **Increase $S/N$** (transmit more power or reduce noise) — limited by hardware, battery, regulatory EM emission caps.

In practice, the engineer must choose an operating point on the $B$–$S/N$ plane that minimizes cost while meeting the required capacity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)
