### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Past Pattern]`

**(a) Derive the expression for entropy of a discrete memoryless source. Show that entropy is maximum when the source symbols are equiprobable. [7 marks]**

**(b) A source has an alphabet of 4 symbols $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with probabilities $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$. (i) Compute the entropy. (ii) Design a Huffman code and compute the average code length, code efficiency, and redundancy. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Derivation:**

For a discrete source with symbols $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ and probabilities $\{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\}$ where $\sum p_i = 1$:

The self-information of symbol $x_i$ is $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i$. The **average information per symbol** (entropy) is the expectation of $I(x_i)$:

$$H(X) = E[I(x_i)] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot I(x_i) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Maximum entropy proof** using Lagrange multipliers on $H(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ subject to $\sum p_i = 1$:

$$L = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i - \lambda \left(\sum_i p_i - 1\right)$$

Setting $\partial L / \partial p_i = 0$ for all $i$:

$$-\log_2 p_i - p_i \cdot \frac{1}{p_i \ln 2} - \lambda = 0$$

$$-\log_2 p_i - \frac{1}{\ln 2} - \lambda = 0$$

$$\log_2 p_i = -(\lambda + 1/\ln 2) = \text{constant for all } i$$

Therefore all $p_i$ are equal: $p_i = 1/n$. Substituting:

$$H_{\max} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \log_2 \frac{1}{n} = \log_2 n \text{ bits/symbol}$$

This is the upper bound: $H(X) \leq \log_2 n$ for any distribution.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Entropy computation:**

| Symbol | $p_i$ | $-\log_2 p_i$ | $p_i \log_2 p_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0.500 | 1.000 | 0.500 |
| B | 0.250 | 2.000 | 0.500 |
| C | 0.125 | 3.000 | 0.375 |
| D | 0.125 | 3.000 | 0.375 |

$$H(X) = 0.500 + 0.500 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.750 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**(ii) Huffman code construction:**

Merging the two lowest probabilities: combine C(0.125) and D(0.125) → node CD with weight 0.250.

Remaining nodes: A(0.500), B(0.250), CD(0.250). Merge the two lowest: B(0.250) + CD(0.250) = BCD(0.500).

Final merge: A(0.500) + BCD(0.500) = root.

**Huffman code assignment:**

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0.500 | 0 | 1 |
| B | 0.250 | 10 | 2 |
| C | 0.125 | 110 | 3 |
| D | 0.125 | 111 | 3 |

**Average code length:**
$$L_{avg} = \sum p_i l_i = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3)$$
$$= 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.750 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Code efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} = \frac{1.750}{1.750} = 1.00 \text{ (or 100%)}$$

**Redundancy:**
$$R_d = 1 - \eta = 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** The 100% efficiency happens because all symbol probabilities are exact powers of 1/2 — the source is "Huffman-optimal." For arbitrary probabilities, $\eta < 1$ slightly.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Past Pattern]`

**(a) State and prove Shannon–Hartley theorem. Discuss the Bandwidth–S/N trade-off for an AWGN channel. [7 marks]**

**(b) An AWGN channel has bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz and a received signal power of $10 \mu$W with noise spectral density $N_0 = 10^{-18}$ W/Hz. Compute (i) noise power, (ii) signal-to-noise ratio in dB, and (iii) channel capacity. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement (Shannon–Hartley Theorem):**
> The capacity of a continuous-time, band-limited AWGN channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is:
> $$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bits/second}$$

**Proof outline:**

Consider a bandlimited AWGN channel with input $X(t)$ and output $Y(t) = X(t) + N(t)$ where $N(t)$ is white Gaussian noise of PSD $N_0/2$. By the **sampling theorem**, the channel can be modeled as a discrete-time vector channel with $2B$ independent uses per second. The optimal input distribution is Gaussian, leading to:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N_0 B}\right) \text{ bits/second}$$

(Detailed proof uses the fact that mutual information for a fixed-variance Gaussian channel is maximized by Gaussian input, and the noise variance over the sampling window is $N_0 B = N$.)

**Bandwidth–S/N trade-off discussion:**

- **Case 1 — Power-limited regime** (small $S/N_0 B$): Using $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ for small $x$:
$$C \approx \frac{S}{N_0 \ln 2} \text{ bits/second}$$
  Capacity is **independent of $B$** in this regime — adding more bandwidth does not help.
- **Case 2 — Bandwidth-limited regime** (large $S/N_0 B$): Capacity grows linearly with $B$.
- **Practical implication:** Engineers choose operating point based on which resource is expensive (battery-powered IoT = power-limited; spectrum-licensed 5G = bandwidth-limited).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $B = 4000$ Hz, $S = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ W $= 10^{-5}$ W, $N_0 = 10^{-18}$ W/Hz.

**(i) Noise power:**
$$N = N_0 \cdot B = 10^{-18} \times 4000 = 4 \times 10^{-15} \text{ W} = 4 \text{ fW}$$

**(ii) Signal-to-noise ratio in dB:**
$$\frac{S}{N} = \frac{10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-15}} = 2.5 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^9) = 10[\log_{10}(2.5) + 9]$$
$$= 10[0.3979 + 9] = 93.979 \text{ dB} \approx 94 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Channel capacity:**
$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$
$$= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 2.5 \times 10^9)$$
$$\approx 4000 \times \log_2(2.5 \times 10^9)$$
$$= 4000 \times \frac{\ln(2.5 \times 10^9)}{\ln 2} = 4000 \times \frac{21.639}{0.6931}$$
$$= 4000 \times 31.221 = 124,884 \text{ bits/s} \approx 124.9 \text{ kbps}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks (verified from KTU valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the base of the logarithm** — KTU expects $\log_2$ by default (bits). Writing $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$ loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Sign errors in entropy formula** — The negative sign is often dropped. The correct form is $H(X) = -\sum p \log_2 p$, not $H(X) = \sum p \log_2 p$.
> 3. **Confusing self-information with entropy** — Self-info is per-symbol, entropy is the average. Examiners specifically test this distinction for 1–2 marks.
> 4. **S/N conversion errors** — dB value is $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ (not 20!) for power ratios. Voltage ratios use 20, but S/N in communications is a power ratio.
> 5. **Missing units in final answer** — Always write "bits/symbol" for entropy and "bits/second" for rate/capacity.
> 6. **Not justifying the "maximum entropy at uniform distribution"** — When the question says "show that entropy is maximum…", full Lagrange multiplier steps or a Jensen's inequality argument is required for full marks. A one-line "it is intuitive" gets only partial credit.
> 7. **In Shannon–Hartley, forgetting the "+1" inside the log** — Writing $C = B \log_2(S/N)$ instead of $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ is a classic 2-mark killer error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Basics of Information Theory**
>
> - **Self-Information:** $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p(x_i)$; rarer events → more information.
> - **Entropy:** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$; maximum value is $\log_2 M$ (uniform distribution).
> - **Joint & Conditional Entropy:** Chain rule $H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y \mid X)$.
> - **Mutual Information:** $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X) \geq 0$ always.
> - **Information Rate:** $R = r_s \cdot H(X)$ bits/second.
> - **Channel Capacity (Discrete Memoryless):** $C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y)$ bits/channel-use.
> - **BSC Capacity:** $C = 1 - H_b(p)$ where $H_b(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$.
> - **Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem:** Reliable communication possible iff $R \leq C$.
> - **Shannon–Hartley (AWGN):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$; this is the **fundamental limit** of digital communication.
> - **Bandwidth–S/N Trade-off:** Power-limited regime $C \approx S/(N_0 \ln 2)$ (independent of $B$); Bandwidth-limited regime $C \approx B \log_2(S/N)$.
> - **Code Efficiency:** $\eta = H(X) / L_{avg}$; Redundancy $R_d = 1 - \eta$.
> - **Huffman code is optimal** for symbol-by-symbol encoding when symbol probabilities are dyadic ($p_i = 2^{-k}$), $\eta = 1$ exactly.
> - **Unit conversions:** $1$ nat $= 1/\ln 2$ bits $\approx 1.4427$ bits; $1$ Hartley $= \log_2 10$ bits $\approx 3.3219$ bits.
> - **dB rule of thumb (power):** $S/N$ in dB $= 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$; each 3 dB ≈ factor of 2 in power; each 10 dB = factor of 10.
> - **Always** include units (bits, bits/symbol, bits/second, dB) in your final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Run-Length Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Run-Length Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Run-Length Coding (RLC)**, also known as **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)**, is a fundamental, simple, and *lossless* data compression technique defined under the KTU 2024 Multimedia Technologies syllabus. Formally, it is a coding scheme in which a *run* (a sequence of consecutive, identical data symbols) is represented as a single data value coupled with a *count*, rather than as the original run itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** "A simple form of data compression in which a run of identical data values is replaced by a single data value and a count."

The technique is particularly effective when the source data exhibits **high spatial redundancy**, meaning that the same symbol appears repeatedly in contiguous positions. Common multimedia applications include compression of **BMP, TIFF, and PCX** image files, as well as black-and-white facsimile (fax) transmission, where long runs of white or black pixels are highly common.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are writing a long grocery list and need to note down that you want to buy ten apples. Instead of writing:

> *Apple, Apple, Apple, Apple, Apple, Apple, Apple, Apple, Apple, Apple*

...you could simply write:

> *"10 Apples"*  (or)  *"Apple × 10"*

This is precisely how **Run-Length Coding** operates on raw digital data. The phrase *"10 Apples"* is the **(count, symbol) pair** that replaces the repetitive original string. The original information is preserved *exactly* — no data is lost — making RLE a **lossless compression** technique. The analogy makes it immediately clear why RLE is most effective on data with long, unbroken sequences and least effective on random, highly varied data (such as photographs with smooth gradients or cryptographic noise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property — Lossless Compression:** The original bitstream can be reconstructed *perfectly* and *bit-for-bit* from the RLE-encoded bitstream. This is a critical distinction from lossy techniques (e.g., JPEG, MP3) where some information is permanently discarded.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

In the KTU examination context, candidates are expected to compute and reason about the following standard compression metrics, all of which are highlighted in **bold** for emphasis:

- **Compression Ratio (CR)** — The ratio of the size of the original uncompressed data to the size of the compressed data. A higher CR indicates better compression.
- **Space Savings (SS)** — The percentage reduction in file size achieved by compression.
- **Redundancy (R)** — The fraction of the original data that was redundant (i.e., eliminated during compression).

These three metrics are mathematically interrelated and will be derived explicitly in Section 3 of these notes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" and "How"

Run-Length Coding transforms a 1-D (or 2-D) stream of input symbols into a stream of **(run-count, symbol)** pairs. The process can be broken down into a structured sequence of logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Sequential Scan:** The encoder reads the source symbol stream from left to right (or top-to-bottom in a raster-scanned image), examining one symbol at a time.
- **Step 2 — Run Detection:** It maintains a running count of how many times the *current* symbol has been repeated consecutively. The moment a *different* symbol is encountered, the current run terminates.
- **Step 3 — Pair Formation:** For the terminated run, the encoder emits a 2-tuple: **(count, symbol)**. The count is typically stored as a fixed-width byte (0–255) or a variable-length integer, depending on the standard.
- **Step 4 — Continuation:** The new symbol becomes the start of the *next* run, and the process returns to Step 1.
- **Step 5 — Termination Handling:** If the source stream ends mid-run, a final pair is emitted with the accumulated count.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoding Rule (Core Logic):** A run of `n` consecutive identical symbols `S` is encoded as a single pair `(n, S)`, reducing `n` symbols of input to exactly 2 symbols of output (the count and the value).

### 2.2 Decoding — The Reverse Operation

The decoder is the exact inverse of the encoder. It reads a sequence of `(count, symbol)` pairs and *expands* each pair by writing the symbol `count` times into the output buffer. Because no information about *which* symbol appeared or *how many* times it appeared is discarded, the decoding is guaranteed to reproduce the original stream perfectly.

### 2.3 Variants of Run-Length Coding

For complete KTU 2024 coverage, students must know that RLE is implemented in several practical variants:

- **1-D RLE (Basic RLE):** Operates along a single dimension (e.g., one scanline of an image at a time). This is the textbook baseline.
- **2-D RLE:** Encodes runs across two dimensions (rows and columns), exploiting rectangular regions of identical pixels. Used in **CCITT Group 4** fax compression.
- **Packbits RLE:** A widely used variant in TIFF and Apple Macintosh files, employing a flag byte to indicate whether the following bytes are literal data or run-length-encoded data.
- **Modified Huffman Code (MHC):** A RLE-based scheme standardised for **CCITT Group 3** fax transmission, which uses specially designed Huffman codes for run lengths of white and black pixels (typically 0–1728 pixels per scanline).
- **Word-Oriented RLE:** Operates on 16-bit or 32-bit words instead of bytes, useful in specific hardware accelerators.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

RLE is heavily used in:

- **Facsimile (Fax) Machines:** Where a typical page consists of long horizontal runs of white pixels. Modified Huffman coding (a RLE variant) is the ITU-T standard.
- **Bitmap Image Formats:** BMP (1-bit and 4-bit), TIFF, and PCX natively support RLE compression.
- **Video Codecs:** Modern codecs such as H.264 use run-length coding internally within their CAVLC (Context-Adaptive Variable-Length Coding) entropy stage to encode trailing zeros in transform coefficient blocks.
- **Data Transmission Protocols:** Modems and serial protocols sometimes use RLE to compress control sequences.
- **Simple Embedded Systems:** Because RLE requires minimal memory and trivial computational overhead, it is favoured in low-power microcontrollers.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarises every formula you need to solve Run-Length Coding problems in the KTU 2024 examination. Note the use of `\vert` to safely denote absolute values without breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{orig}}}{N_{\text{comp}}}$ | Ratio of original size to compressed size. A value of 2.0 means the compressed file is half the original. |
| 2 | **Space Savings (SS)** | $SS \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{comp}}}{N_{\text{orig}}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Percentage of original size eliminated. Always expressed as a percentage. |
| 3 | **Redundancy (R)** | $R \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{CR}$ | Fraction of the data stream that was redundant. Range: $0 \le R < 1$. |
| 4 | **Encoded Length** | $N_{\text{comp}} \;=\; 2 \times k$ (bytes, basic RLE) | Where $k$ is the number of runs in the source stream. |
| 5 | **Average Run Length** | $\bar{L} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{orig}}}{k}$ | Average number of symbols per run; a higher $\bar{L}$ yields better compression. |
| 6 | **RLE Efficiency Condition** | $CR > 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \bar{L} > 2$ | RLE is beneficial only when the average run length strictly exceeds **2** symbols. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever the average run length $\bar{L}$ is less than or equal to 2, RLE will *expand* the data rather than compress it. This is a frequently tested edge case in KTU questions.

### 2.6 Strengths and Limitations — Engineering Trade-offs

A balanced KTU answer must always discuss both sides. The trade-offs are summarised below.

| Strengths | Limitations |
|-----------|-------------|
| Extremely simple to implement (a few lines of code or hardware). | Ineffective on data with high entropy (e.g., photographs, encrypted data). |
| Lossless — preserves original information perfectly. | Worst-case expansion: a stream with no runs (e.g., `ABCDEF`) will double in size. |
| Very low computational overhead — ideal for embedded systems. | Not adaptive: cannot exploit probabilistic structure across symbols. |
| Excellent for binary, palette-based, and synthetic images. | Encoded length depends entirely on local symbol distribution. |
| Symmetric — encode and decode are nearly identical in complexity. | Cannot compress random, noisy, or stochastic data. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the exhaustive, KTU-valuation-ready worked derivations. Every algebraic transition and numerical evaluation is written out in full. **No step is skipped.**

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Encoding a Symbolic String

**Problem:** Encode the following source string using basic 1-D Run-Length Coding and compute the compression ratio:

> `Input: AAAAAABBBBCCCCCCCDD`

#### Step 1 — Identify the runs

We scan the string from left to right and group consecutive identical symbols:

| Run Index $i$ | Symbol $S_i$ | Count $n_i$ | Original Substring |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | A | 6 | `AAAAAA` |
| 2 | B | 4 | `BBBB` |
| 3 | C | 7 | `CCCCCCC` |
| 4 | D | 2 | `DD` |

#### Step 2 — Form the (count, symbol) pairs

The encoder emits one pair per run:

> Output: `(6,A) (4,B) (7,C) (2,D)`

#### Step 3 — Compute the encoded stream size

Since each pair contributes 1 byte for the count and 1 byte for the symbol (in the basic byte-oriented RLE model):

$$
N_{\text{comp}} \;=\; 2 \times k
$$

where $k = 4$ is the number of runs. Substituting:

$$
N_{\text{comp}} \;=\; 2 \times 4 \;=\; 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the original size

The original string contains:

$$
N_{\text{orig}} \;=\; 6 + 4 + 7 + 2 \;=\; 19 \text{ symbols}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute the Compression Ratio (CR)

$$
CR \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{orig}}}{N_{\text{comp}}} \;=\; \dfrac{19}{8} \;=\; 2.375
$$

The compression ratio is **2.375 : 1**.

#### Step 6 — Compute the Space Savings (SS)

$$
SS \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{comp}}}{N_{\text{orig}}}\right) \times 100\% \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{8}{19}\right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
SS \;=\; \left(\dfrac{19 - 8}{19}\right) \times 100\% \;=\; \left(\dfrac{11}{19}\right) \times 100\% \;\approx\; 57.89\%
$$

The space savings is approximately **57.89 %**.

#### Step 7 — Verify the Redundancy

$$
R \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{CR} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.375} \;=\; 1 - 0.4211 \;=\; 0.5789
$$

This matches the space savings (as a fraction), confirming the formulas are consistent. Approximately **57.89 %** of the original data was redundant.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — The Expansion Edge Case

**Problem:** Encode the string `ABABABAB` using basic RLE and analyse the result.

#### Step 1 — Identify runs

There are **no runs of length greater than 1**, since every symbol differs from its neighbour. Thus $k = 8$ runs, each of length 1.

#### Step 2 — Compute sizes

The original size is:

$$
N_{\text{orig}} \;=\; 8 \text{ symbols}
$$

The encoded size is:

$$
N_{\text{comp}} \;=\; 2 \times k \;=\; 2 \times 8 \;=\; 16 \text{ symbols}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Compression Ratio

$$
CR \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{orig}}}{N_{\text{comp}}} \;=\; \dfrac{8}{16} \;=\; 0.5
$$

The compression ratio is **0.5 : 1**, meaning the encoded file is *twice* the size of the original. **Space Savings** is therefore negative ($-100\%$), and **Redundancy** is computed as:

$$
R \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{0.5} \;=\; 1 - 2 \;=\; -1
$$

A negative redundancy confirms *expansion* rather than compression. This demonstrates the critical KTU 2024 takeaway: **RLE is unsuitable for high-entropy data.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance Summary

The following matrix consolidates the analytical results of both examples for quick KTU reference:

| Metric | Example 1 (Repeated) | Example 2 (Alternating) |
|---|---|---|
| Original string | `AAAAAABBBBCCCCCCCDD` | `ABABABAB` |
| Number of runs ($k$) | 4 | 8 |
| $N_{\text{orig}}$ | 19 | 8 |
| $N_{\text{comp}}$ | 8 | 16 |
| Compression Ratio (CR) | 2.375 : 1 | 0.5 : 1 |
| Space Savings (SS) | +57.89 % | $-100$ % |
| Redundancy ($R$) | 0.5789 | $-1.0$ |
| Average Run Length $\bar{L}$ | 4.75 | 1.0 |
| RLE Beneficial? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (expands) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — RLE Encoder Pipeline

The following diagram illustrates the block-level functional architecture of a 1-D Run-Length Encoding pipeline, as required by the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Input Symbol Stream] --> node2[Initialize Counter c = 1]
    node2 --> node3[Read Current Symbol S]
    node3 --> node4{Next Symbol equals S?}
    node4 -->|Yes| node5[Increment Counter c = c + 1]
    node5 --> node3
    node4 -->|No| node6[Emit Pair c comma S to Output Buffer]
    node6 --> node7{End of Stream?}
    node7 -->|No| node8[Reset Counter c = 1 and Set S = Next Symbol]
    node8 --> node3
    node7 -->|Yes| node9[Emit Final Pair and Terminate]
    node9 --> node10[Encoded RLE Bitstream]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — RLE Decoder Pipeline

The decoder is the exact inverse of the encoder. It expands each `(count, symbol)` pair by writing the symbol `count` consecutive times into the output stream.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Encoded RLE Bitstream] --> nodeB[Read Next Pair c comma S]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Initialize Loop Index i = 1]
    nodeC --> nodeD[i less than or equal to c?]
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Write Symbol S to Output Buffer]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Increment i = i + 1]
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeD -->|No| nodeG{More Pairs Available?}
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeB
    nodeG -->|No| nodeH[Decoded Original Stream]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — End-to-End Multimedia Compression System

The diagram below shows where Run-Length Coding fits inside a complete multimedia compression system. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU viva and short-answer questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Raw Image or Audio Source] --> pre[Preprocessing Module]
    pre --> trans[Transform Module DCT or Wavelet]
    trans --> quant[Quantisation Module]
    quant --> rle[Run Length Coding Module]
    rle --> huff[Huffman or Arithmetic Coding Module]
    huff --> out[Compressed Bitstream]
    out -.Transmission or Storage.-> store[(Compressed File)]
    store --> dec[Decoder Pipeline]
    dec --> ihuff[Inverse Huffman]
    ihuff --> irle[Inverse Run Length Coding]
    irle --> iquant[Inverse Quantisation]
    iquant --> itrans[Inverse Transform]
    itrans --> isrc[Reconstructed Source]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Insight:** RLE sits in the *entropy coding* stage of the compression pipeline, *after* transform and quantisation. In the case of JPEG, the DCT output is zig-zag-scanned to group long runs of zero coefficients, which RLE then encodes efficiently before Huffman coding further compacts the run-length symbols.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]

> **Q1.** Define **Run-Length Coding**. Mention any two image file formats that natively use RLE compression.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Run-Length Coding (RLC) is a simple **lossless** data compression technique in which a *run* of consecutive identical symbols is replaced by a single pair consisting of a **count** and the **symbol** value itself. For example, the input `XXXXX` is encoded as the pair `(5, X)`. This reduces storage space whenever the source data contains long repeated sequences.

Two image file formats that natively use RLE compression are:

1. **BMP (Bitmap)** — Microsoft's Windows bitmap format supports a byte-oriented RLE mode for 1-bit, 4-bit, and 8-bit indexed images.
2. **TIFF (Tagged Image File Format)** — Supports RLE compression via the Packbits algorithm, which is a flag-based variant of RLE defined in the TIFF 6.0 specification.

*(Other valid answers: PCX, TGA — full credit should be awarded for any two correct formats.)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** When is Run-Length Coding **ineffective**? Explain with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Run-Length Coding becomes ineffective when the source data has **low redundancy** — that is, when the input stream contains few or no long runs of identical symbols. A canonical failure case is the string `ABABABAB`, where every symbol differs from its immediate neighbour. The original is 8 bytes, but the RLE output `(1,A)(1,B)(1,A)(1,B)(1,A)(1,B)(1,A)(1,B)` is **16 bytes**, yielding a compression ratio of $0.5 : 1$ — the data is *expanded*, not compressed.

In general, RLE is ineffective when the **average run length** $\bar{L}$ is less than or equal to **2 symbols**, because each run requires 2 bytes (count + symbol) regardless of its length.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for Module 2, students must answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**. Each carries 14 marks, split into 7 + 7 across two sub-parts.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme, Module 2]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **basic Run-Length Encoding algorithm** with the help of a suitable example. Show the step-by-step encoding of the input string `WWWWWWWWBWWWWWWWWBBBWWWBWWWWW` and compute the **Compression Ratio** and **Space Savings** achieved.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Modified Huffman Code (MHC)** used in CCITT Group 3 facsimile transmission. How does it build upon the basic RLE algorithm? Mention the role of the **End-of-Line (EOL)** code and the **white/black** codebook separation.

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Algorithm Description (3 Marks):**

The basic 1-D RLE algorithm processes an input symbol stream sequentially. It maintains a **run counter** $c$ and a **current symbol** $S$. For every consecutive repetition of $S$, the counter is incremented. The moment a *different* symbol is encountered, the encoder emits the pair $(c, S)$ to the output buffer, resets $c = 1$, and updates $S$ to the new symbol. At the end of the stream, any remaining run is flushed as a final pair.

**Step-by-Step Encoding of `WWWWWWWWBWWWWWWWWBBBWWWBWWWWW` (4 Marks):**

We identify the runs in the input string:

| Run Index $i$ | Symbol $S_i$ | Count $n_i$ | Substring |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | W | 8 | `WWWWWWWW` |
| 2 | B | 1 | `B` |
| 3 | W | 8 | `WWWWWWWW` |
| 4 | B | 3 | `BBB` |
| 5 | W | 3 | `WWW` |
| 6 | B | 1 | `B` |
| 7 | W | 5 | `WWWWW` |

Total number of runs: $k = 7$.

**Original size** $N_{\text{orig}} = 8 + 1 + 8 + 3 + 3 + 1 + 5 = 29$ symbols.

**Compressed size** $N_{\text{comp}} = 2 \times k = 2 \times 7 = 14$ symbols.

**Compression Ratio:**

$$
CR \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{orig}}}{N_{\text{comp}}} \;=\; \dfrac{29}{14} \;\approx\; 2.071 : 1
$$

**Space Savings:**

$$
SS \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{14}{29}\right) \times 100\% \;=\; \left(\dfrac{15}{29}\right) \times 100\% \;\approx\; 51.72\%
$$

**Final Encoded Stream:** `(8,W)(1,B)(8,W)(3,B)(3,W)(1,B)(5,W)`

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Modified Huffman Code (MHC) — Overview (3 Marks):**

Modified Huffman Code is the ITU-T (formerly CCITT) standardised compression scheme for **Group 3 facsimile (fax) machines**. It is a **two-stage** coding scheme that builds directly upon basic RLE:

1. **Stage 1 — Run-Length Encoding:** The 1-D scanline of a fax page is encoded as a sequence of alternating **white** and **black** run lengths, exploiting the fact that a typical fax page is predominantly white. Each scanline is assumed to start with a white run (length 0 if it actually starts with black).
2. **Stage 2 — Huffman Coding:** Each run length (which can range from 0 to 1728 pixels per scanline) is assigned a **variable-length Huffman code**. Crucially, there are **two separate codebooks**: one for white runs and one for black runs, because the statistical distribution of white run lengths differs markedly from that of black run lengths.

**Run Length Codebooks and Make-Up/Terminating Codes (2 Marks):**

In MHC, run lengths from 0 to 1728 are encoded using:

- **Terminating codes** for runs of length 0 to 63.
- **Make-up codes** for runs of length 64 to 1728 (in multiples of 64), followed by a terminating code for the remainder.
- This hybrid design allows efficient encoding of both short runs (common) and long runs (rare but possible).

**End-of-Line (EOL) Code (2 Marks):**

The EOL code is a unique 12-bit pattern (`000000000001`) that:

- Marks the end of each scanline.
- Serves as a **resynchronisation marker** in case of transmission errors, allowing the decoder to recover by resynchronising to the next EOL.
- Precedes every page to establish initial synchronisation.

Thus, MHC is essentially **RLE + Huffman + EOL framing**, making it a practical, real-world descendant of the simple Run-Length Coding algorithm.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme, Module 2, Alternative Set]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **2-D Run-Length Coding**. How does it differ from basic 1-D RLE? Discuss its use in **CCITT Group 4** fax compression.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A 320 × 240 pixel grayscale image is stored using basic RLE. The image has only **16 unique gray levels**. After RLE encoding, the file size is **38,400 bytes**. Compute (i) the **original uncompressed size** assuming 1 byte per pixel, (ii) the **Compression Ratio**, and (iii) the **average number of bytes per encoded pair**. Comment on the efficiency.

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1-D vs 2-D RLE (3 Marks):**

Basic **1-D RLE** encodes runs only along a single dimension — typically one horizontal scanline at a time. Each run is described by its length and symbol value, and the encoding resets at every scanline boundary.

**2-D RLE**, in contrast, encodes runs across **two dimensions simultaneously**. Instead of treating each scanline independently, a 2-D RLE scheme tracks runs across the *current* line and the *reference* (previous) line, exploiting both horizontal and vertical repetitions. This is especially powerful for images containing rectangular regions of identical pixels, such as diagrams, text, and line art.

**2-D RLE in CCITT Group 4 (4 Marks):**

CCITT Group 4 fax compression uses a **Modified Modified READ (MMR)** algorithm, which is a sophisticated 2-D RLE scheme. MMR operates as follows:

- The first scanline is encoded using **Modified Huffman Code (1-D mode)** to establish a reference.
- Each subsequent scanline is encoded relative to the previous reference line using a set of **pass, horizontal, and vertical modes**:
  - **Pass mode:** A transition in the reference line is skipped because the current line has no transition in that region.
  - **Horizontal mode:** A run on the current line is encoded directly using MHC codewords.
  - **Vertical mode:** The position of a transition on the current line is encoded as a small offset ($\pm 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3$) from the corresponding transition on the reference line.
- Because the algorithm never *replaces* the reference line, error propagation is mitigated compared to differential coding.

MMR achieves typical compression ratios of **10:1 to 20:1** on text-heavy fax pages, far exceeding basic 1-D RLE.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**

- Image resolution: $320 \times 240$ pixels
- Bits per pixel: 8 (grayscale, 1 byte per pixel)
- Encoded file size: $N_{\text{comp}} = 38{,}400$ bytes
- Number of unique gray levels: 16

**(i) Original Uncompressed Size (2 Marks):**

$$
N_{\text{orig}} \;=\; 320 \times 240 \times 1 \text{ byte} \;=\; 76{,}800 \text{ bytes}
$$

**(ii) Compression Ratio (2 Marks):**

$$
CR \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{orig}}}{N_{\text{comp}}} \;=\; \dfrac{76{,}800}{38{,}400} \;=\; 2.0
$$

The compression ratio is **2 : 1**.

**(iii) Average Number of Bytes Per Encoded Pair (2 Marks):**

The number of encoded pairs (runs) is:

$$
k \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{comp}}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{38{,}400}{2} \;=\; 19{,}200 \text{ runs}
$$

The average run length is:

$$
\bar{L} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{orig}}}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{76{,}800}{19{,}200} \;=\; 4.0 \text{ pixels per run}
$$

So on average, each run is **4 pixels long** before being encoded as a 2-byte pair.

**Efficiency Comment (1 Mark):**

With only 16 unique gray levels and an average run length of 4, RLE achieves a **moderate 2:1** compression. While acceptable, this is far below the 5:1 to 10:1 ratios achievable by more sophisticated schemes like LZW or JPEG-LS on similar content. A better alternative would be a **palette-based entropy coder** that exploits the 16-symbol alphabet (4 bits per symbol) directly, avoiding the overhead of the 2-byte pair structure.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in RLE Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to count the count byte:** Many students compute $N_{\text{comp}} = k$ instead of $N_{\text{comp}} = 2k$. This single error cascades into wrong CR, wrong SS, and partial or zero credit.
> 2. **Ignoring the expansion edge case:** When a question gives you alternating data (e.g., `ABABABAB`), the expected answer is that RLE *expands* the data — not that it compresses it poorly. Failing to state this explicitly costs full marks.
> 3. **Confusing Modified Huffman with pure Huffman:** Modified Huffman is **RLE + Huffman + EOL framing**, not just plain Huffman coding. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Mixing up 1-D and 2-D RLE:** A common error is describing MMR (Group 4) when the question asks for basic RLE, or vice versa. Always read the question carefully.
> 5. **Not converting units:** Compression ratios are dimensionless, but Space Savings must be expressed as a *percentage* ($\times 100\%$). Marks are deducted if the unit is missing.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Run-Length Coding (RLC/RLE)** is a **lossless** compression technique that replaces a run of $n$ identical symbols with a single **(count, symbol)** pair.
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** $CR = N_{\text{orig}} / N_{\text{comp}}$. A value greater than 1 indicates successful compression.
- **Space Savings (SS):** $SS = (1 - N_{\text{comp}} / N_{\text{orig}}) \times 100\%$.
- **Redundancy (R):** $R = 1 - 1/CR$.
- **Encoded size** in basic byte-oriented RLE is $N_{\text{comp}} = 2k$, where $k$ is the number of runs.
- **RLE is effective** only when the average run length $\bar{L} > 2$.
- **RLE is ineffective** (and may *expand* data) on alternating, high-entropy, or random data.
- **Common applications:** BMP, TIFF, PCX image formats; CCITT Group 3 fax (via Modified Huffman); CCITT Group 4 fax (via MMR, a 2-D RLE); entropy stage of H.264/AVC.
- **Modified Huffman Code (MHC):** RLE + variable-length Huffman codes with separate white/black codebooks + EOL resynchronisation marker.
- **2-D RLE (MMR):** Exploits both horizontal and vertical runs using pass, horizontal, and vertical modes; used in Group 4 fax.
- **Key edge case:** Alternating data like `ABABAB` causes RLE to *double* the file size — a guaranteed exam question type.
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Always state your assumptions (byte-oriented pair, single scanline, etc.) before computing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable-Length Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variable-Length Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Variable-Length Coding (VLC)** is a statistical compression technique in which source symbols are assigned codewords whose lengths are **inversely proportional to the probability of occurrence** of the corresponding symbols. Frequently occurring symbols are encoded with shorter bit-strings, while rare symbols are encoded with longer bit-strings, thereby reducing the average number of bits per symbol required for representation.

Formally, a VLC system is defined by a coding function $C : \Sigma \rightarrow \{0,1\}^*$ that maps each symbol $s_i$ from a source alphabet $\Sigma = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ to a unique binary codeword $c_i$ such that the length of $c_i$, denoted $l_i$, satisfies:

$$l_i \leq l_j \quad \text{whenever} \quad P(s_i) \geq P(s_j)$$

where $P(s_i)$ is the probability of occurrence of symbol $s_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** VLC is classified as **lossless (entropy) coding** because the original data can be perfectly reconstructed from the encoded bitstream. It forms the foundation of modern multimedia codecs such as JPEG, MPEG, MP3, and H.264/AVC.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Shipping Container"

Imagine you are a logistics manager who must ship **1000 different items** from a warehouse. Some items (e.g., rubber bands, paper clips) are extremely common, while others (e.g., grand pianos, satellite dishes) are rare.

- If you use **the same fixed-size box** for every item, you will waste enormous space around the small items.
- A smarter approach is to use **small boxes for common items** and **large boxes for rare items**.

The total warehouse space used is dramatically reduced. **Variable-Length Coding applies this exact philosophy to digital data**: common symbols (like the letter 'E' in English text, or low-frequency DCT coefficients in an image) get short binary codes, and rare symbols get long ones.

## 1.3 Core Properties of an Optimal VLC

A properly designed VLC must satisfy three essential properties (collectively known as the **Kraft-McMillan conditions**):

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Uniquely Decodable** | No codeword is a prefix of another codeword (Prefix Property) |
| **Instantaneous** | A symbol can be decoded the moment its full codeword is received |
| **Optimal** | The average code length approaches the source entropy $H(X)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Content of a Symbol:** $I(s_i) = -\log_2 P(s_i)$ bits. This is the theoretical minimum number of bits needed to encode symbol $s_i$. VLC schemes attempt to match code lengths to this information content as closely as possible.

## 1.4 Why VLC Matters in Multimedia

Raw multimedia data (audio, image, video) contains massive statistical redundancy. By exploiting the **non-uniform probability distribution** of symbols, VLC achieves compression ratios of **40% to 70%** without any loss of information, making it indispensable for storage and transmission.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Information Theory Foundations

The mathematical edifice of VLC rests upon **Claude Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**. Three foundational quantities govern all entropy coding.

### 2.1.1 Self-Information (Surprisal)

The amount of information conveyed when symbol $s_i$ occurs:

$$I(s_i) = -\log_2 P(s_i) = \log_2 \left( \frac{1}{P(s_i)} \right) \quad \text{[bits]}$$

- A certain event ($P = 1$) carries **0 bits** of information.
- A rare event ($P \to 0$) carries **very high** information content.

### 2.1.2 Entropy (Average Information per Symbol)

The expected self-information over the entire source alphabet:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(s_i) \log_2 P(s_i) \quad \text{[bits/symbol]}$$

**Shannon's Source Coding Theorem (Fundamental Theorem):** The entropy $H(X)$ is the **theoretical lower bound** on the average code length of any lossless coding scheme. No coding technique can compress data to an average length below $H(X)$.

### 2.1.3 Average Code Length

For a specific coding scheme with codeword lengths $l_i$:

$$\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(s_i) \cdot l_i \quad \text{[bits/symbol]}$$

## 2.2 Performance Metrics

### 2.2.1 Coding Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.2.2 Redundancy

$$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} = \frac{\bar{L} - H(X)}{\bar{L}}$$

### 2.2.3 Kraft Inequality (Prefix Code Existence)

A prefix code with codeword lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ exists **if and only if**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

## 2.3 The Kraft-McMillan Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet** — Memorize this table for numerical problems.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Self-Information | $I(s_i) = -\log_2 P(s_i)$ | bits | Information per occurrence |
| Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum P(s_i) \log_2 P(s_i)$ | bits/symbol | Theoretical minimum length |
| Average Code Length | $\bar{L} = \sum P(s_i) \cdot l_i$ | bits/symbol | Actual scheme performance |
| Efficiency | $\eta = H(X) / \bar{L}$ | dimensionless (×100%) | Quality of code |
| Redundancy | $R = 1 - \eta$ | dimensionless | Wasted bits fraction |
| Kraft Inequality | $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | dimensionless | Prefix code feasibility |
| Variance of Length | $\sigma^2 = \sum P(s_i)(l_i - \bar{L})^2$ | bits$^2$ | Length consistency |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** When writing absolute values or conditions, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ (never the unescaped pipe `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind VLC — Three-Step Logic

1. **Observation:** Multimedia sources have highly skewed probability distributions (e.g., in English text, 'E' occurs ~13%, 'Z' ~0.07%).
2. **Insight:** Assigning equal-length codes (e.g., 8-bit ASCII) wastes bits on common symbols.
3. **Action:** Use **shorter codes for probable symbols** and **longer codes for improbable symbols** to reduce the weighted average $\bar{L}$ toward the entropy $H(X)$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **JPEG Image Compression:** Huffman coding is applied to quantized DCT coefficients.
- **MPEG Audio (MP3):** Huffman codes encode quantized MDCT spectral coefficients.
- **ZIP / GZIP Archives:** DEFLATE algorithm combines LZ77 + Huffman coding.
- **PNG Images:** DEFLATE stream with Huffman tables.
- **Wi-Fi (802.11):** VLC is used in error-control coding layer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Huffman Coding Procedure — Worked Example

Huffman coding (1952) is the **optimal** prefix code construction algorithm for a known probability distribution.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A source emits **6 symbols** with the following probabilities. Construct the Huffman code, compute the average code length, entropy, and efficiency.

| Symbol | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | $F$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Probability $P(s_i)$ | **0.35** | **0.20** | **0.15** | **0.12** | **0.10** | **0.08** |

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Initial Sort (Descending Order)

Arrange symbols in descending order of probability:

$$P(A) = 0.35 \geq P(B) = 0.20 \geq P(C) = 0.15 \geq P(D) = 0.12 \geq P(E) = 0.10 \geq P(F) = 0.08$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — First Merge (Combine Two Rarest)

Combine $E$ (0.10) and $F$ (0.08) into a composite node $N_1$:

$$P(N_1) = P(E) + P(F) = 0.10 + 0.08 = 0.18$$

Updated list:

| Symbol | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $N_1 \{E,F\}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Probability | 0.35 | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.12 | **0.18** |

Re-sort descending: $A(0.35), N_1(0.18), B(0.20), C(0.15), D(0.12)$.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Second Merge

Combine the two lowest: $C$ (0.15) and $D$ (0.12) into $N_2$:

$$P(N_2) = P(C) + P(D) = 0.15 + 0.12 = 0.27$$

Updated list (after re-sort): $A(0.35), N_2(0.27), B(0.20), N_1(0.18)$.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Third Merge

Combine the two lowest: $B$ (0.20) and $N_1$ (0.18) into $N_3$:

$$P(N_3) = P(B) + P(N_1) = 0.20 + 0.18 = 0.38$$

Updated list: $A(0.35), N_3(0.38), N_2(0.27)$. Re-sorted: $N_3(0.38), A(0.35), N_2(0.27)$.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Fourth Merge

Combine $A$ (0.35) and $N_2$ (0.27) into $N_4$:

$$P(N_4) = P(A) + P(N_2) = 0.35 + 0.27 = 0.62$$

Updated list: $N_4(0.62), N_3(0.38)$.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Final Merge (Root)

$$P(\text{Root}) = P(N_4) + P(N_3) = 0.62 + 0.38 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Assign Binary Codes (Tree Traversal)

Convention: **left edge = 0**, **right edge = 1**.

| Symbol | Path from Root | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Left → Left | **00** | 2 |
| $C$ | Left → Right → Left | **010** | 3 |
| $D$ | Left → Right → Right | **011** | 3 |
| $B$ | Right → Left | **10** | 2 |
| $E$ | Right → Right → Left | **110** | 3 |
| $F$ | Right → Right → Right | **111** | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Prefix Property Verified:** No codeword is a prefix of any other. For example, `00` is a complete codeword; the next bit decides uniquely between `010` and `011`.

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Compute Average Code Length $\bar{L}$

$$\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} P(s_i) \cdot l_i$$

$$\bar{L} = (0.35)(2) + (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.12)(3) + (0.10)(3) + (0.08)(3)$$

Computing each term individually:

$$\bar{L} = 0.70 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.36 + 0.30 + 0.24 = 2.45 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

### 3.1.10 Step 9 — Compute Entropy $H(X)$

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{6} P(s_i) \log_2 P(s_i)$$

Computing each $\log_2$ value with high precision:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_2(0.35) &= -1.5146 \\
\log_2(0.20) &= -2.3219 \\
\log_2(0.15) &= -2.7370 \\
\log_2(0.12) &= -3.0589 \\
\log_2(0.10) &= -3.3219 \\
\log_2(0.08) &= -3.6439
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\bigl[ (0.35)(-1.5146) + (0.20)(-2.3219) + (0.15)(-2.7370) \\
&\quad + (0.12)(-3.0589) + (0.10)(-3.3219) + (0.08)(-3.6439) \bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

Evaluating the products:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\bigl[ -0.5301 + (-0.4644) + (-0.4106) \\
&\quad + (-0.3671) + (-0.3322) + (-0.2915) \bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

Summing the negatives:

$$H(X) = -(-2.3959) = 2.3959 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

### 3.1.11 Step 10 — Compute Efficiency $\eta$

$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.3959}{2.45} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = 0.9779 \times 100\% = 97.79\%$$

### 3.1.12 Step 11 — Compute Redundancy $R$

$$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9779 = 0.0221 = 2.21\%$$

### 3.1.13 Step 12 — Verify Kraft Inequality

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3}$$

$$= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1.00 \leq 1 \checkmark$$

The prefix code is **feasible** and **saturates** the Kraft bound (a hallmark of optimal Huffman codes).

## 3.2 Shannon-Fano Coding — Companion Method

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort symbols in descending order of probability.
2. Divide the list into two groups such that the **sum of probabilities** in each group is as close to **0.5** as possible.
3. Assign `0` to the upper group and `1` to the lower group.
4. Recursively apply steps 2–3 to each sub-group until each symbol has a unique code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shannon-Fano vs. Huffman:** Huffman coding always produces an **optimal** code (minimum $\bar{L}$), while Shannon-Fano is a **suboptimal** top-down method. Huffman is bottom-up and is the de facto standard in modern codecs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of VLC Schemes

| Scheme | Year | Optimality | Complexity | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon-Fano | 1948 | Suboptimal | $O(n \log n)$ | Educational use |
| Huffman | 1952 | Optimal (symbol-wise) | $O(n \log n)$ | JPEG, MP3, MPEG |
| Run-Length | 1960s | Pattern-specific | $O(n)$ | Fax, BMP |
| Arithmetic | 1976 | Near-optimal (fractional bits) | $O(n)$ | JPEG2000, H.264 |
| LZW | 1984 | Dictionary-based | $O(n)$ | GIF, TIFF, Unix compress |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Huffman Code Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **Huffman tree** constructed in Section 3.1, with codeword labels on each leaf and probability values on each node.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Root: 1.00]
    n4[N4: 0.62]
    n3[N3: 0.38]
    n2[N2: 0.27]
    leafA[Sym A: 0.35]
    n1[N1: 0.18]
    leafB[Sym B: 0.20]
    leafC[Sym C: 0.15]
    leafD[Sym D: 0.12]
    leafE[Sym E: 0.10]
    leafF[Sym F: 0.08]

    root --- n4
    root --- n3
    n4 --- leafA
    n4 --- n2
    n2 --- leafC
    n2 --- leafD
    n3 --- leafB
    n3 --- n1
    n1 --- leafE
    n1 --- leafF
```

**Codeword Path Interpretation** (Left = 0, Right = 1):

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[00 = A] --> B[Length 2]
    C[10 = B] --> D[Length 2]
    E[010 = C] --> F[Length 3]
    G[011 = D] --> H[Length 3]
    I[110 = E] --> J[Length 3]
    K[111 = F] --> L[Length 3]
```

## 4.2 VLC Encoder / Decoder — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram shows the **complete data flow** of a generic VLC-based multimedia compression system.

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Input Source: Image / Audio / Video]
    qtz[Quantizer]
    stat[Probability Estimator]
    huff[Optimal Code Generator: Huffman Tree]
    codebook[Codebook / Table Storage]
    enc[Variable Length Encoder]
    chan[Transmission Channel or Storage]
    dec[Variable Length Decoder]
    deq[Dequantizer]
    out[Reconstructed Output]

    src --> qtz
    qtz --> stat
    stat --> huff
    huff --> codebook
    codebook --> enc
    qtz --> enc
    enc --> chan
    chan --> dec
    codebook --> dec
    dec --> deq
    deq --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Functional Block Rationale:**
> - **Quantizer:** Reduces the dynamic range of source values, creating many zeros and small numbers that improve VLC efficiency.
> - **Probability Estimator:** Calculates $P(s_i)$ for the symbol stream (often approximated by a static model).
> - **Code Generator:** Constructs the optimal prefix tree.
> - **Codebook Storage:** The codebook itself is transmitted as side information (or pre-shared between encoder and decoder).
> - **Decoder:** Uses the codebook to perform instantaneous, lossless reverse mapping.

## 4.3 Huffman Merge Process — Stepwise Reduction

The following flowchart traces the bottom-up merging logic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Step 1: Sort 6 symbols descending]
    s2[Step 2: Merge E and F to form N1 with prob 0.18]
    s3[Step 3: Merge C and D to form N2 with prob 0.27]
    s4[Step 4: Merge B and N1 to form N3 with prob 0.38]
    s5[Step 5: Merge A and N2 to form N4 with prob 0.62]
    s6[Step 6: Merge N4 and N3 to form Root with prob 1.00]
    s7[Step 7: Traverse tree and assign binary codes]
    s8[Step 8: Verify Kraft inequality and compute metrics]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Variable-Length Coding. Why is it preferred over Fixed-Length Coding in multimedia compression?**

**Model Answer:**

Variable-Length Coding (VLC) is a statistical compression technique that assigns shorter codewords to frequently occurring symbols and longer codewords to rarely occurring symbols, such that the average code length approaches the source entropy $H(X)$.

VLC is preferred over Fixed-Length Coding (FLC) because:

1. **Multimedia sources are highly redundant** — symbol probabilities are non-uniform (e.g., pixel values in natural images cluster around the mean).
2. **FLC wastes bits** — assigning 8 bits to every symbol ignores the probability distribution, resulting in $\bar{L} = \log_2 n$ regardless of symbol frequency.
3. **VLC exploits redundancy** — by matching code length to $-\log_2 P(s_i)$, VLC pushes $\bar{L}$ toward the theoretical minimum $H(X)$, achieving 30–70% bit savings.
4. **Decodability** — when constructed as a prefix code (e.g., Huffman), VLC remains uniquely and instantaneously decodable.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State and explain Shannon's Source Coding Theorem. What is the significance of entropy $H(X)$ in VLC?**

**Model Answer:**

**Shannon's Source Coding Theorem:** For a discrete memoryless source $X$ with entropy $H(X)$, the average code length $\bar{L}$ of any uniquely decodable code satisfies:

$$H(X) \leq \bar{L} < H(X) + 1$$

**Significance of Entropy $H(X)$:**

- $H(X)$ represents the **average information content per symbol** in bits.
- It is the **theoretical lower bound** on the average number of bits required to encode the source losslessly.
- $H(X)$ serves as the **benchmark** for evaluating any VLC scheme: the closer $\bar{L}$ is to $H(X)$, the more efficient the code (i.e., higher $\eta$).
- For English text, $H \approx 1.5$ bits/letter, meaning a well-designed VLC can compress ASCII (8 bits/letter) by a factor of ~5.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A source emits 6 symbols with the following probabilities: $P(A) = 0.35, P(B) = 0.20, P(C) = 0.15, P(D) = 0.12, P(E) = 0.10, P(F) = 0.08$.**

**(a)** Construct the Huffman code for the source. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the average code length $\bar{L}$, entropy $H(X)$, coding efficiency $\eta$, and redundancy $R$. Verify the Kraft inequality. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Huffman Tree Construction (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** List symbols in descending order of probability.

| Symbol | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | $F$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P$ | 0.35 | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.12 | 0.10 | 0.08 |

**Step 2:** Merge two lowest: $E (0.10) + F (0.08) = N_1 (0.18)$.

**Step 3:** Merge two lowest: $C (0.15) + D (0.12) = N_2 (0.27)$.

**Step 4:** Merge two lowest: $B (0.20) + N_1 (0.18) = N_3 (0.38)$.

**Step 5:** Merge two lowest: $A (0.35) + N_2 (0.27) = N_4 (0.62)$.

**Step 6:** Merge: $N_3 (0.38) + N_4 (0.62) = \text{Root} (1.00)$.

**Step 7:** Assign codes (Left=0, Right=1):

| Symbol | Code | Length |
|---|---|---|
| A | 00 | 2 |
| B | 10 | 2 |
| C | 010 | 3 |
| D | 011 | 3 |
| E | 110 | 3 |
| F | 111 | 3 |

---

#### Part (b) — Performance Metrics (7 Marks)

**Average Code Length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} &= \sum P_i \cdot l_i \\
&= (0.35)(2) + (0.20)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.12)(3) + (0.10)(3) + (0.08)(3) \\
&= 0.70 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.36 + 0.30 + 0.24 \\
\bar{L} &= 2.45 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Entropy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum P_i \log_2 P_i \\
&= -[(0.35)(-1.5146) + (0.20)(-2.3219) + (0.15)(-2.7370) \\
&\quad + (0.12)(-3.0589) + (0.10)(-3.3219) + (0.08)(-3.6439)] \\
H(X) &= 2.3959 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.3959}{2.45} \times 100\% = 97.79\%$$

**Redundancy:**

$$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9779 = 0.0221 = 2.21\%$$

**Kraft Verification:**

$$\sum 2^{-l_i} = 2(2^{-2}) + 4(2^{-3}) = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \leq 1 \checkmark$$

---

### Question B — Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A discrete memoryless source has 5 symbols with probabilities $P(s_1) = 0.40, P(s_2) = 0.25, P(s_3) = 0.15, P(s_4) = 0.12, P(s_5) = 0.08$.**

**(a)** Apply Shannon-Fano coding and derive the codewords. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the average code length, entropy, efficiency, and redundancy. Comment on the optimality of the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Shannon-Fano Procedure (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sort descending:**

| Symbol | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $s_4$ | $s_5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P$ | 0.40 | 0.25 | 0.15 | 0.12 | 0.08 |

**Step 2 — First Partition** (target = 0.50):

- Group 1: $\{s_1\}$ with sum $= 0.40$
- Group 2: $\{s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5\}$ with sum $= 0.60$

(The split closest to 0.5 each gives $\{s_1\}$ vs the rest.)

Assign: Group 1 → `0`, Group 2 → `1`

**Step 3 — Recurse on Group 2** $\{s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5\}$:

- Sub-group 2a: $\{s_2\}$ with sum $= 0.25$
- Sub-group 2b: $\{s_3, s_4, s_5\}$ with sum $= 0.35$

Assign: 2a → `0`, 2b → `1`. So $s_2$ gets `10`.

**Step 4 — Recurse on Sub-group 2b** $\{s_3, s_4, s_5\}$:

- 2b-i: $\{s_3\}$ with sum $= 0.15$
- 2b-ii: $\{s_4, s_5\}$ with sum $= 0.20$

Assign: 2b-i → `0`, 2b-ii → `1`. So $s_3$ gets `110`.

**Step 5 — Recurse on Sub-group 2b-ii** $\{s_4, s_5\}$:

- $s_4$ gets `0` → final code `1110`
- $s_5$ gets `1` → final code `1111`

**Final Shannon-Fano Codes:**

| Symbol | Code | Length |
|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 1 |
| $s_2$ | 10 | 2 |
| $s_3$ | 110 | 3 |
| $s_4$ | 1110 | 4 |
| $s_5$ | 1111 | 4 |

---

#### Part (b) — Performance Analysis (7 Marks)

**Average Code Length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} &= (0.40)(1) + (0.25)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.12)(4) + (0.08)(4) \\
&= 0.40 + 0.50 + 0.45 + 0.48 + 0.32 \\
\bar{L} &= 2.15 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Entropy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -[(0.40)\log_2(0.40) + (0.25)\log_2(0.25) + (0.15)\log_2(0.15) \\
&\quad + (0.12)\log_2(0.12) + (0.08)\log_2(0.08)] \\
&= -[(0.40)(-1.3219) + (0.25)(-2.0000) + (0.15)(-2.7370) \\
&\quad + (0.12)(-3.0589) + (0.08)(-3.6439)] \\
H(X) &= -[-0.5288 - 0.5000 - 0.4106 - 0.3671 - 0.2915] \\
H(X) &= 2.0980 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{2.0980}{2.15} \times 100\% = 97.58\%$$

**Redundancy:**

$$R = 1 - 0.9758 = 0.0242 = 2.42\%$$

**Comment on Optimality:** Although the Shannon-Fano code yields high efficiency (~97.58%), it is **not provably optimal**. The first split decision $\{s_1\}$ vs rest is somewhat arbitrary; a Huffman code on the same distribution typically achieves $\bar{L} = 2.12$ bits/symbol with efficiency $\eta = 98.96\%$. Huffman is the guaranteed-optimal bottom-up alternative.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to re-sort after each merge** in Huffman coding — partial marks loss.
> 2. **Confusing efficiency with redundancy** — $\eta$ and $R$ are complementary; never both can be >100%.
> 3. **Using natural log instead of $\log_2$** — entropy and code lengths must be in **bits**, not nats.
> 4. **Forgetting to verify Kraft inequality** — examiners award marks for this sanity check.
> 5. **In Shannon-Fano, choosing unbalanced splits** — always aim for sums closest to 0.5 in each partition.
> 6. **Not stating units** — $\bar{L}$ and $H(X)$ are in bits/symbol; write the unit explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Variable-Length Coding**

- **VLC Definition:** Codeword length inversely proportional to symbol probability.
- **Entropy $H(X)$:** Theoretical lower bound; $H(X) = -\sum P_i \log_2 P_i$ bits/symbol.
- **Shannon's Theorem:** $H(X) \leq \bar{L} < H(X) + 1$.
- **Huffman Algorithm:** Bottom-up merge of two lowest probabilities; always re-sort after every merge; left=0, right=1.
- **Shannon-Fano Algorithm:** Top-down recursive bisection; sums in each partition should be as close to 0.5 as possible.
- **Kraft Inequality:** $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ — necessary and sufficient for a prefix code.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = H(X)/\bar{L} \times 100\%$.
- **Redundancy:** $R = 1 - \eta$.
- **Huffman vs Shannon-Fano:** Huffman is **optimal**; Shannon-Fano is suboptimal but simpler.
- **Prefix Property:** No codeword is a prefix of another → ensures instantaneous, unambiguous decoding.
- **Common codecs using VLC:** JPEG (Huffman on DCT), MP3 (Huffman on MDCT), JPEG2000 (Arithmetic), PNG/GZIP (Huffman via DEFLATE).
- **Unit Discipline:** Always use $\log_2$ (base 2) for **bits**; never use $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$ in VLC calculations.
- **Variance Formula:** $\sigma^2 = \sum P_i (l_i - \bar{L})^2$ — measure of code length consistency.
- **Key Insight:** VLC exploits **statistical redundancy**, not perceptual redundancy; it is **lossless**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dictionary-Based Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dictionary-Based Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Dictionary-Based Coding** is a family of lossless data compression algorithms that achieve compression by replacing groups of consecutive symbols (strings or phrases) with compact codes that index into a dynamically constructed or pre-populated **dictionary** of such strings. Unlike statistical methods (e.g., Huffman, Shannon-Fano) that encode single symbols based on probability distributions, dictionary-based coders exploit **repeated substrings** within the source stream by storing previously seen patterns and referencing them with shorter pointers.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Library Analogy:**
> Imagine you are transcribing the entire *Harry Potter* series by hand, but instead of writing out every word like *"the boy who lived"* each time it appears, you write it once on a separate **reference card** and label it **#001**. Every subsequent occurrence, you simply write *"#001"*. This is exactly what dictionary-based coding does — it builds a **reference catalog (dictionary)** of frequently seen phrases and replaces them with short **index tokens**.

Think of it this way:
- **Encoder side** → Maintains a dictionary of strings, reads input, checks if the next string exists in the dictionary, and outputs its index.
- **Decoder side** → Receives these indices, rebuilds the same dictionary (deterministically) and outputs the corresponding strings.

### 1.2 Key Sub-Families of Dictionary-Based Coding

| Algorithm | Year | Dictionary Strategy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LZ77** | 1977 | Sliding Window | gzip, PNG, ZIP |
| **LZ78** | 1978 | Explicit Tree Dictionary | compress (.Z), Unix |
| **LZW** | 1984 | Fixed-Initial + Adaptive | GIF, TIFF, PDF, V.42 modems |

### 1.3 Core Operational Symbols

- **Symbol Alphabet ($\Sigma$)** — Set of all source symbols $\{a, b, c, \ldots\}$.
- **Dictionary ($D$)** — Collection of indexed strings $D = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_N\}$.
- **Codeword** — The index $i$ pointing to dictionary entry $d_i$.
- **Window Size ($W$)** — Number of past symbols the encoder can reference (LZ77).
- **Lookahead Buffer Size ($L$)** — Number of upcoming symbols examined (LZ77).
- **Compression Ratio (CR)** — Ratio of original size to compressed size.

### 1.4 Why Dictionary-Based Coding Matters for Multimedia

> [!NOTE]
> Multimedia files (images, audio, video) contain massive amounts of **repeated pixel patterns, audio waveforms, and frame redundancies**. Dictionary-based coding captures these repetitions efficiently. For example, a sky-blue region in a photograph contains thousands of identical RGB triples — a dictionary coder collapses these into short references, yielding compression ratios unattainable by Huffman alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 LZ77 — The Sliding Window Algorithm

LZ77 (Lempel–Ziv, 1977) uses a **sliding window** divided into two parts:

1. **Search Buffer** (already-encoded symbols, of size $W$).
2. **Lookahead Buffer** (symbols yet to be encoded, of size $L$).

The encoder searches the search buffer for the longest match of the lookahead buffer. It then outputs a **triple** $(o, l, c)$ where:
- $o$ = **Offset** (distance back to the match start).
- $l$ = **Length** of the match.
- $c$ = **Next symbol** following the match.

### 2.2 LZ78 — Explicit Dictionary Tree

LZ78 abandons the sliding window and instead maintains an **explicit dictionary**. The encoder parses input into substrings such that each substring is the **longest match** not already in the dictionary. It outputs a pair $(i, c)$ where:
- $i$ = Index of the longest matching prefix in the dictionary.
- $c$ = Next unmatched symbol.
- The new string (concatenation) is added to the dictionary.

### 2.3 LZW — Lempel–Ziv–Welch

LZW (Welch, 1984) refines LZ78 by:
- **Pre-initializing** the dictionary with all single-character strings.
- **Eliminating the transmission of the next character** $c$ — only dictionary indices are output.
- The encoder outputs index $i$ representing a multi-character string.

### 2.4 LZW Encoding Algorithm (Pseudocode Steps)

1. Initialize dictionary $D$ with all $256$ ASCII (or $0$–$255$) single-character entries.
2. Set string $w = \emptyset$ (empty).
3. Read next input character $K$.
4. If $wK$ exists in $D$, set $w \leftarrow wK$.
5. Else:
   - Output code for $w$.
   - Add $wK$ to $D$ with next available index.
   - Set $w \leftarrow K$.
6. Repeat from step 3 until end-of-input.
7. Output code for remaining $w$.

### 2.5 LZW Decoding Algorithm

1. Initialize dictionary $D$ with single-character entries.
2. Read first code, output its string, set $w = $ that string.
3. Read next code $K$.
4. If $K$ is in $D$: entry $= D[K]$, output entry.
5. Else: entry $= w + w[0]$ (the "KwKwK case").
6. Add $w + $ entry$[0]$ to $D$.
7. Set $w = $ entry; repeat.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exam:**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| LZ77 Code Tuple | $(o, l, c)$ | Offset, Length, Next Char |
| LZ78 Code Pair | $(i, c)$ | Index, Next Char |
| LZW Code | $i$ | Single index output |
| Match Length Bound | $1 \le l \le L$ | Where $L$ = lookahead buffer size |
| Offset Bound | $1 \le o \le W$ | Where $W$ = search buffer size |
| Bits per Triple | $\lceil \log_2 W \rceil + \lceil \log_2 L \rceil + \lceil \log_2 \vert\Sigma\vert \rceil$ | LZ77 bits per code |
| Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Bits}}{\text{Compressed Bits}}$ | Higher = better |
| Dictionary Index Size | $\lceil \log_2 \vert D \vert \rceil$ bits | Bits needed to represent each index |
| Space Savings | $SS = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Compressed}}{\text{Original}}$ | Normalized savings |
| LZW Initial Dict Size | $\vert\Sigma\vert$ entries | For ASCII, typically $256$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always express bit-widths using $\log_2$ of buffer/dictionary sizes. KTU examiners award partial marks for correct unit annotation.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GIF/TIFF Images** → LZW (historically patented; now expired).
- **PNG Images** → LZ77 variant (DEFLATE).
- **ZIP Archives** → LZ77 + Huffman hybrid.
- **PDF Files** → FlateDecode (LZ77 + Huffman).
- **Modem Standards (V.42bis)** → LZW variant for dial-up.
- **Unix `.Z` files** → LZW (compress utility).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: LZW Encoding

**Input String:** `ABABABA`

**Initial Dictionary (ASCII-like):**

$$
D = \{ 0\!:\!A,\ 1\!:\!B,\ 2\!:\!C,\ \ldots\},\quad \text{size} = 256
$$

We will track variables $w$ (current string), $K$ (next input char), and dictionary updates.

#### Step-by-Step Trace Table

| Step | Read $K$ | $w$ before | $wK$ in $D$? | Action | Output | Add to $D$ | New $w$ |
|------|----------|------------|--------------|--------|--------|------------|---------|
| 1 | A | "" | Yes (single A) | Extend | — | — | A |
| 2 | B | A | No (AB not in D) | Output A, add AB | 0 (A) | 256: AB | B |
| 3 | A | B | No (BA not in D) | Output B, add BA | 1 (B) | 257: BA | A |
| 4 | B | A | Yes | Extend | — | — | AB |
| 5 | A | AB | No (ABA not in D) | Output AB, add ABA | 256 (AB) | 258: ABA | A |
| 6 | B | A | Yes | Extend | — | — | AB |
| 7 | A | AB | Yes (AB exists) | Extend | — | — | ABA |
| 8 | EOF | ABA | — | Output ABA | 258 (ABA) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoded Output Sequence:** $\{0,\ 1,\ 256,\ 258\}$ — only **4 codes** for a **7-character** input. Each code needs $\lceil \log_2 259 \rceil = 9$ bits, totaling $4 \times 9 = 36$ bits vs. $7 \times 8 = 56$ bits uncompressed.

#### Compression Ratio Calculation

$$
CR = \frac{\text{Original Bits}}{\text{Compressed Bits}} = \frac{56}{36} \approx 1.556
$$

$$
SS = 1 - \frac{36}{56} \approx 0.357\ (35.7\%\ \text{savings})
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: LZW Decoding

**Received Code Stream:** $\{0,\ 1,\ 256,\ 258\}$

| Step | Read Code | $w$ (prev) | $K$ in $D$? | Output | Add to $D$ | New $w$ |
|------|-----------|-------------|-------------|--------|------------|---------|
| 1 | 0 (A) | — | Yes | A | — | A |
| 2 | 1 (B) | A | Yes | B | 256: A+B=A | B |
| 3 | 256 (AB) | B | Yes | AB | 257: B+A=BA | AB |
| 4 | 258 (ABA) | AB | Yes | ABA | 258: AB+A=ABA | ABA |

**Decoded Output:** `ABABABA` ✓ (Lossless reconstruction confirmed)

### 3.3 Derivation: Bit-Width Formula for LZ77

Let us derive the number of bits per LZ77 triple $(o, l, c)$:

**Given:**
- Search buffer size $= W$ symbols.
- Lookahead buffer size $= L$ symbols.
- Alphabet cardinality $= \vert\Sigma\vert$.

**Number of distinct offset values** $= W$ (offsets $1$ to $W$).
**Number of distinct length values** $= L$ (lengths $1$ to $L$).
**Number of distinct literal characters** $= \vert\Sigma\vert$.

Each must be encoded using its minimum binary length:

$$
\text{bits}(o) = \lceil \log_2 W \rceil
$$

$$
\text{bits}(l) = \lceil \log_2 L \rceil
$$

$$
\text{bits}(c) = \lceil \log_2 \vert\Sigma\vert \rceil
$$

**Total bits per LZ77 triple:**

$$
B_{\text{triple}} = \lceil \log_2 W \rceil + \lceil \log_2 L \rceil + \lceil \log_2 \vert\Sigma\vert \rceil
$$

**Bits to encode $N$ triples (no literals):**

$$
B_{\text{total}} = N \cdot B_{\text{triple}}
$$

### 3.4 Numerical Problem: LZ77 Bit Calculation

**Problem:** Source alphabet $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ ($\vert\Sigma\vert = 2$), $W = 8$, $L = 4$, encoded sequence requires $12$ triples.

**Step 1 — Compute each bit-width:**

$$
\text{bits}(o) = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3
$$

$$
\text{bits}(l) = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2
$$

$$
\text{bits}(c) = \lceil \log_2 2 \rceil = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Total bits per triple:**

$$
B_{\text{triple}} = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Total compressed bits:**

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 12 \times 6 = 72\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compared to uncompressed:**

If uncompressed uses $\lceil \log_2 2 \rceil = 1$ bit per symbol and $N_{\text{sym}} = 80$ symbols:

$$
B_{\text{uncomp}} = 80 \times 1 = 80\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Compression ratio:**

$$
CR = \frac{80}{72} \approx 1.11
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even modest LZ77 setups can outperform naïve encoding. In practice, longer match lengths dramatically improve compression because multiple source symbols are replaced by a single triple.

### 3.5 Derivation: LZ78 Dictionary Growth

Starting from empty, after processing $N$ input characters producing $N_c$ code pairs:

$$
\vert D \vert = \vert\Sigma\vert + N_c
$$

Where $\vert\Sigma\vert$ is the initial ASCII entries (LZW) or zero (pure LZ78). Each new dictionary entry increases the index's bit-width by $1$ whenever $\vert D \vert$ crosses a power of two.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 LZ77 Sliding Window Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Stream] --> B[Sliding Window Buffer]
    B --> C[Search Buffer]
    B --> D[Lookahead Buffer]
    C --> E[Longest Match Search]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Match Found?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Emit Triple offset, length, char]
    F -- No --> H[Emit Literal Symbol]
    G --> I[Shift Window Right]
    H --> I
    I --> B
    I --> J[Compressed Output]
```

### 4.2 LZW Encoder Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Character K] --> B[w + K exists in Dictionary D?]
    B -- Yes --> C[Update w = w + K]
    B -- No --> D[Output Code for w]
    D --> E[Add w + K to Dictionary D]
    E --> F[Set w = K]
    C --> G[Read Next Character]
    F --> G
    G --> A
    G --> H{End of Input?}
    H -- No --> A
    H -- Yes --> I[Output Code for w]
    I --> J[Compressed Stream]
```

### 4.3 LZW Decoder Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressed Stream] --> B[Read First Code]
    B --> C[Output Dictionary Entry]
    C --> D[Set w = Entry]
    D --> E[Read Next Code K]
    E --> F{K in Dictionary?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Output Entry K]
    F -- No --> H[Output w + First char of w]
    G --> I[Add w + First char of new entry to Dictionary]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Set w = new entry]
    J --> E
    E --> K{More Codes?}
    K -- Yes --> F
    K -- No --> L[Reconstructed Output]
```

### 4.4 Dictionary-Based Coding vs. Statistical Coding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lossless Compression Techniques] --> B[Statistical Coding]
    A --> C[Dictionary-Based Coding]
    B --> B1[Huffman Coding]
    B --> B2[Shannon-Fano]
    B --> B3[Arithmetic Coding]
    C --> C1[LZ77 Sliding Window]
    C --> C2[LZ78 Tree Dictionary]
    C --> C3[LZW Enhanced LZ78]
```

### 4.5 LZ77 Encoding Trace Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WB[Sliding Window W=8]
        SB[Search Buffer: already encoded past symbols]
        LB[Lookahead Buffer: next symbols to encode]
    end
    SB --> MS[Match Search: longest matching substring]
    LB --> MS
    MS --> OUT[Emit Triple: offset, length, next char]
    OUT --> SHT[Shift Window Forward by l+1]
    SHT --> WB
```

### 4.6 Memory Architecture of LZW Dictionary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Dictionary D] --> D0[Index 0: A]
    D --> D1[Index 1: B]
    D --> D2[Index 2: C]
    D --> D255[Index 255: ...]
    D --> D256[Index 256: AB]
    D --> D257[Index 257: BA]
    D --> D258[Index 258: ABA]
    D --> D259[Index 259: dynamically added strings]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Differentiate between **statistical coding** and **dictionary-based coding** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Statistical Coding | Dictionary-Based Coding |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit encoded** | Individual symbols | Groups of symbols / phrases |
| **Basis** | Symbol probability distribution | Repeated substrings in source |
| **Dictionary** | Probability table (static or adaptive) | Explicit string catalog |
| **Examples** | Huffman, Shannon-Fano, Arithmetic | LZ77, LZ78, LZW |
| **Best for** | Skewed distributions | Repeated patterns / long runs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Award 3 marks if all 4–5 rows are correctly contrasted with at least one example per family.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> What is the role of the **sliding window** in the LZ77 algorithm? Why is the window size $W$ a critical parameter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The sliding window in LZ77 is divided into a **search buffer** (size $W$) and a **lookahead buffer** (size $L$). The encoder searches the search buffer for the longest prefix match of the lookahead buffer. The window size $W$ is critical because:
1. **Larger $W$** → better match opportunities → higher compression, but more memory.
2. **Smaller $W$** → fewer bits for offset encoding → faster, but reduced compression.
3. $W$ directly determines the **bit-width** for the offset field: $\lceil \log_2 W \rceil$ bits.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **LZW encoding algorithm** with a neat flowchart. Illustrate the encoding process for the input string **`BABAABAAA`** using an initially empty dictionary. Show the dictionary built and the compressed output codes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With an example, explain the **KwKwK case** that arises during **LZW decoding**. How is it resolved? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) LZW Encoding for `BABAABAAA`** (7 marks)

**Initial Dictionary:** Indices $0$–$255$ for all ASCII characters (we use only `A` and `B` here):

$$
D_0 = \{ 0\!:\!A,\ 1\!:\!B,\ 2\!:\!C,\ \ldots \}
$$

**Step-by-step Trace:**

| Step | Read $K$ | $w$ before | $wK$ in $D$? | Output | Add to $D$ | New $w$ |
|------|----------|------------|--------------|--------|------------|---------|
| 1 | B | "" | Yes | — | — | B |
| 2 | A | B | No | 1 (B) | 256: BA | A |
| 3 | B | A | No | 0 (A) | 257: AB | B |
| 4 | A | B | Yes | — | — | BA |
| 5 | A | BA | No | 256 (BA) | 258: BAA | A |
| 6 | A | A | Yes | — | — | AA |
| 7 | A | AA | No | 257 (AB) ❌ *(Correction: 257=AB; here w=AA, wK=AAA)* | 259: AAA | A |
| 8 | A | A | Yes | — | — | AA |
| 9 | EOF | AA | — | 259 (AAA) | — | — |

**Compressed Output Stream:** $\{1,\ 0,\ 256,\ 257,\ 259\}$ — **5 codes** for **9 input characters**.

**(b) The KwKwK Case (7 marks)**

The **KwKwK case** occurs in LZW decoding when the received code $K$ refers to a dictionary entry that **has not yet been added to the decoder's dictionary**. This typically happens when:
- Encoder adds entry $w + K[0]$ to its dictionary at index $K$, but
- The decoder has not yet processed code $K$ (because $K$ references a newly formed entry from current iteration).

**Classic Example — Encoding `ABABABA`:**

During encoding, the encoder outputs code `258` (which was just created as entry `ABA`). The decoder, upon reading code `258`, finds that entry `258` is **not yet in its dictionary**. It resolves this as:

$$
\text{Entry}(258) = w + w[0]
$$

Where $w$ is the previous decoded string and $w[0]$ is its first character.

In our trace: when decoder reads `258` after $w = AB$, it computes:
$$
\text{Entry} = AB + A = ABA
$$
This matches what the encoder intended, and the dictionary is updated correctly with $w + \text{entry}[0] = AB + A = ABA$ at index $258$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Works:** The encoder only creates a new entry $w + K[0]$ when $wK$ is not in the dictionary, meaning $w$ is a proper prefix of the next output. Thus $w + w[0]$ equals the unresolved entry — guaranteeing correct decoding.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **LZ77 algorithm** in detail. Explain the encoding process with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an LZ77 system with search buffer $W = 16$, lookahead buffer $L = 8$, and source alphabet $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$:
- Calculate the **number of bits** required to encode a triple $(o, l, c)$. **(3 marks)**
- If the encoded stream produces **20 triples**, compute the **compressed size in bits**. **(2 marks)**
- Compute the **compression ratio** assuming the original uncompressed stream was **160 bits**. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) LZ77 Algorithm Description (7 marks):**

LZ77 uses a sliding window consisting of two parts: a **search buffer** of size $W$ containing recently encoded symbols, and a **lookahead buffer** of size $L$ containing upcoming symbols.
