**Encoding Steps:**
1. Position the sliding window at the start of input.
2. Search the search buffer for the **longest match** of the prefix of the lookahead buffer.
3. If a match of length $l \ge 1$ exists at offset $o$, output triple $(o, l, c)$ where $c$ is the next unmatched symbol.
4. If no match, output $(0, 0, c)$ — a literal.
5. Shift window forward by $l + 1$ symbols.
6. Repeat until input exhausted.

**Decoding** mirrors encoding: read triple, copy $l$ symbols from position (current $-o$), append $c$, advance.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Compute bit-width for each field:**

$$
\text{bits}(o) = \lceil \log_2 W \rceil = \lceil \log_2 16 \rceil = 4
$$

$$
\text{bits}(l) = \lceil \log_2 L \rceil = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3
$$

$$
\text{bits}(c) = \lceil \log_2 \vert\Sigma\vert \rceil = \lceil \log_2 2 \rceil = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Bits per triple:**

$$
B_{\text{triple}} = 4 + 3 + 1 = 8\ \text{bits/triple}
$$

**Step 3 — Total compressed bits for 20 triples:**

$$
B_{\text{compressed}} = 20 \times 8 = 160\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compression Ratio:**

$$
CR = \frac{B_{\text{original}}}{B_{\text{compressed}}} = \frac{160}{160} = 1.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** With these buffer sizes, compression is neutral. To achieve $CR > 1$, we need longer average match lengths. Real LZ77 achieves CR 2–4 because most triples encode multiple source symbols.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting initial dictionary entries** — LZW starts with all 256 ASCII entries; pure LZ78 starts empty. Marks deducted if distinction is missed.
> 2. **Confusing LZ77 vs LZ78 output format** — LZ77 emits triples $(o, l, c)$; LZ78 emits pairs $(i, c)$; LZW emits single indices $i$. Mixing these is a frequent error.
> 3. **Ignoring the KwKwK case** — In LZW decoding, students often forget the special handling when the code refers to a not-yet-built dictionary entry. This guarantees lossless reconstruction.
> 4. **Bit-width formula errors** — Use $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$, **not** $\log_{10}$ or natural log. KTU examiners are strict on this.
> 5. **Forgetting to output the final code** — In LZW encoding, the last $w$ must be flushed; missing this loses 1–2 marks in trace problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Dictionary-based coding exploits **repeated substrings** by replacing them with short dictionary references.
- ✅ **LZ77** uses a **sliding window** with search + lookahead buffers; outputs **triples** $(o, l, c)$.
- ✅ **LZ78** uses an **explicit tree dictionary**; outputs **pairs** $(i, c)$.
- ✅ **LZW** is an enhanced LZ78: pre-initialized dictionary (256 ASCII), outputs **single indices**.
- ✅ Bit-width for LZ77 offset = $\lceil \log_2 W \rceil$, length = $\lceil \log_2 L \rceil$, char = $\lceil \log_2 \vert\Sigma\vert \rceil$.
- ✅ LZW encoding logic: extend $w$ while $wK \in D$; otherwise output code for $w$ and add $wK$ to $D$.
- ✅ LZW decoding logic: must handle the **KwKwK case** when received code refers to an unbuilt entry — resolve as $w + w[0]$.
- ✅ Applications: **GIF/TIFF/PDF** (LZW), **PNG/ZIP** (LZ77 via DEFLATE), **Unix compress** (LZW).
- ✅ Compression ratio $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original}}{\text{Compressed}}$; higher = better; space savings $SS = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Compressed}}{\text{Original}}$.
- ✅ Dictionary size grows adaptively; bit-width per code increases as $\vert D \vert$ crosses powers of 2.
- ✅ LZ77 advantages: bounded memory, sliding naturally; LZ78/LZW advantages: unbounded dictionary, often better compression.
- ✅ Lossless: reconstruction is **exact**, bit-for-bit — critical for text, executable files, and medical imaging.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arithmetic Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arithmetic Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Arithmetic Coding** is an advanced **entropy-based lossless data compression** technique that represents an entire source message as a single real number (a fractional value) lying within the unit interval **[0, 1)**. Unlike symbol-by-symbol coding schemes (such as Huffman Coding), arithmetic coding encodes the *entire sequence* into one floating-point number whose precision determines the compression efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PECNT527 Module 2):** Arithmetic Coding is positioned as a superior alternative to Huffman Coding for sources where symbol probabilities are *not* exact negative powers of one-half ($\neq 2^{-k}$). It achieves compression performance theoretically approaching the **Shannon entropy limit**.

Mathematically, if a source emits a string $S = s_1 s_2 s_3 \ldots s_n$ with probability $P(S)$, the arithmetic coder maps $S$ to a unique sub-interval of length $P(S)$ inside $[0, 1)$. The encoded output is any single tag value $T$ chosen from this sub-interval.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Number-Line Treasure Hunt"

Imagine the number line between **0** and **1** as a chocolate bar divided into unequal slices based on how often each letter appears in your text:

- The letter **E** (most frequent in English) gets a **fat slice**
- The letter **Q** (rare) gets a **thin slice**
- A blank space gets another slice, and so on...

To encode the word **"HELLO"**, you don't write down letters. Instead, you perform a **successive "zoom-in"** action:

1. Start by pointing somewhere inside E's slice (which is inside the full bar).
2. Zoom *into* that slice — it too is divided into sub-slices for H, E, L, L, O proportionally.
3. Point to the sub-slice for the first letter H.
4. Zoom *into* H's region, find E's sub-region, and so on.
5. After processing all 5 letters, you have a tiny interval. The **encoded message is a single decimal number** (like 0.06752) that lies inside that final tiny interval.

The decoder reverses the zoom: it finds which slice contains the tag number, outputs that letter, then re-zooms.

## 1.3 Why Arithmetic Coding Matters (KTU Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with Huffman Coding:**
> - **Huffman Coding**: Produces *integer bits per symbol* — wastes bits when probabilities are non-uniform.
> - **Arithmetic Coding**: Achieves *fractional bits per symbol*, asymptotically optimal.
> - **Example**: Symbol probabilities $P(A) = 0.99$, $P(B) = 0.01$.
>   - Huffman: Both symbols ≥ 1 bit each → wastes ~99% of capacity.
>   - Arithmetic: Theoretical length $\approx 0.08$ bits/symbol for $A$ → near-entropy performance.

The KTU syllabus treats arithmetic coding as the **gold-standard reference** against which other entropy coders are benchmarked in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The Interval Narrowing Algorithm

The arithmetic coder maintains a current interval $[\text{low}, \text{high})$ throughout the encoding process. For each input symbol $s_i$:

- The interval is partitioned into sub-intervals proportional to symbol probabilities.
- The sub-interval corresponding to $s_i$ becomes the new interval.

### Logic Steps

1. **Initialize** $\text{low} = 0$, $\text{high} = 1$. (Initial interval is the entire unit range.)
2. **Build a cumulative frequency model**:
   - Compute cumulative probabilities $F(x)$ for every alphabet symbol $x$ (sorted by probability).
3. **For each symbol** $s_i$ in the message:
   - Compute the current range: $\text{range} = \text{high} - \text{low}$.
   - Update: $\text{low} \leftarrow \text{low} + \text{range} \times F_{\text{low}}(s_i)$.
   - Update: $\text{high} \leftarrow \text{low}_{\text{old}} + \text{range} \times F_{\text{high}}(s_i)$.
4. **Terminate**: After the last symbol, output *any* tag value $T \in [\text{low}, \text{high})$.

## 2.2 Decoding (Reverse Process)

The decoder receives the tag value $T$ and the probability model. It iteratively:

1. Finds the symbol $s_i$ whose interval contains $T$ (i.e., $F_{\text{low}}(s_i) \leq (T - \text{low})/\text{range} < F_{\text{high}}(s_i)$).
2. Outputs $s_i$ and updates $[\text{low}, \text{high})$ exactly as in encoding.
3. Repeats until the special **end-of-message (EOF)** symbol is decoded.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield and must be memorized for KTU ESE.**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Probability of symbol $x$** | $P(x)$ | Likelihood of symbol $x$ in the source |
| **Cumulative probability (low)** | $F_{\text{low}}(x) = \sum_{y < x} P(y)$ | Lower edge of $x$'s interval |
| **Cumulative probability (high)** | $F_{\text{high}}(x) = F_{\text{low}}(x) + P(x)$ | Upper edge of $x$'s interval |
| **Current interval range** | $\text{range} = \text{high} - \text{low}$ | Width before processing next symbol |
| **New low** | $\text{low}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{low}}(s_i)$ | Updated lower bound |
| **New high** | $\text{high}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{high}}(s_i)$ | Updated upper bound |
| **Encoded tag value** | $T \in [\text{low}_{\text{final}}, \text{high}_{\text{final}})$ | Final single-number output |
| **Bits required** | $-\log_2 P(S)$ | Theoretical codeword length |
| **Shannon Entropy** | $H = -\sum_x P(x) \log_2 P(x)$ bits/symbol | Theoretical compression limit |
| **Code Efficiency** | $\eta = H / L_{\text{avg}}$ | Ratio of entropy to average length |

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert x \vert$ in your written exams (NOT the pipe symbol `|` inside table cells) to denote absolute value or interval width — this preserves markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Arithmetic coding (often in its practical variants: **Range Coding**, **CABAC** in H.264/HEVC, **ANS** in Zstandard/LZMA) is the entropy backbone of:

- **JPEG 2000 image compression** (MQ-coder, an arithmetic variant).
- **H.265/HEVC and AV1 video codecs** for residual and motion vector encoding.
- **PDF document compression** (every PDF uses arithmetic/LZW streams).
- **Genomic data compression** (DNA sequences have highly skewed symbol probabilities — ideal for arithmetic coding).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example — Encoding the String "abc"

### Source Model Setup

Consider a source with alphabet $\{a, b, c\}$ and known probabilities:

| Symbol | Probability $P(x)$ | Cumulative Low $F_{\text{low}}$ | Cumulative High $F_{\text{high}}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **a** | **0.6** | 0.0 | 0.6 |
| **b** | **0.3** | 0.6 | 0.9 |
| **c** | **0.1** | 0.9 | 1.0 |

**Verify**: $0.6 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 1.0$ ✓ — probability axioms satisfied.

We will encode the string $S = \text{"abc"}$.

### Initialization

$$\text{low}_0 = 0.0$$

$$\text{high}_0 = 1.0$$

$$\text{range}_0 = 1.0 - 0.0 = 1.0$$

### Step 1 — Process Symbol 'a'

Symbol 'a' occupies the interval $[0.0, 0.6)$.

$$\text{low}_1 = \text{low}_0 + \text{range}_0 \cdot F_{\text{low}}(a) = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.0 = 0.0$$

$$\text{high}_1 = \text{low}_0 + \text{range}_0 \cdot F_{\text{high}}(a) = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.6 = 0.6$$

$$\text{range}_1 = 0.6 - 0.0 = 0.6$$

**Current interval:** $[0.0, 0.6)$

### Step 2 — Process Symbol 'b'

Symbol 'b' occupies $[0.6, 0.9)$ within the unit probability space. We map this proportionally into the current interval $[0.0, 0.6)$.

$$\text{low}_2 = \text{low}_1 + \text{range}_1 \cdot F_{\text{low}}(b) = 0.0 + 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36$$

$$\text{high}_2 = \text{low}_1 + \text{range}_1 \cdot F_{\text{high}}(b) = 0.0 + 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$$

$$\text{range}_2 = 0.54 - 0.36 = 0.18$$

**Current interval:** $[0.36, 0.54)$

### Step 3 — Process Symbol 'c'

Symbol 'c' occupies $[0.9, 1.0)$ within the unit probability space. We map this proportionally into the current interval $[0.36, 0.54)$.

$$\text{low}_3 = \text{low}_2 + \text{range}_2 \cdot F_{\text{low}}(c) = 0.36 + 0.18 \times 0.9 = 0.36 + 0.162 = 0.522$$

$$\text{high}_3 = \text{low}_2 + \text{range}_2 \cdot F_{\text{high}}(c) = 0.36 + 0.18 \times 1.0 = 0.36 + 0.18 = 0.54$$

$$\text{range}_3 = 0.54 - 0.522 = 0.018$$

**Final interval:** $[0.522, 0.54)$

### Step 4 — Choose the Encoded Tag

Any number in $[0.522, 0.54)$ is valid. We choose the convenient midpoint:

$$T = 0.531$$

**Verification:** $0.522 \leq 0.531 < 0.54$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Bits required**: The minimum number of bits $n$ needed is the smallest integer such that $2^{-n} \leq \text{range}_3 = 0.018$.
> $2^{-n} \leq 0.018 \Rightarrow n \geq \log_2(1/0.018) \approx 5.79$. So $n = 6$ bits suffice. Compare this with naive fixed-length coding: 2 bits per symbol × 3 symbols = **6 bits total** — the same! However, for longer strings or skewed probabilities, arithmetic coding vastly outperforms.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example — Decoding "abc" from Tag $T = 0.531$

The decoder starts with $[\text{low}, \text{high}) = [0, 1)$ and $T = 0.531$.

### Decode Symbol 1

Compute normalized offset within current range:

$$\text{offset} = \frac{T - \text{low}}{\text{range}} = \frac{0.531 - 0.0}{1.0} = 0.531$$

Find which symbol's $[F_{\text{low}}, F_{\text{high}})$ contains 0.531:
- $a$: $[0.0, 0.6)$ — **Contains 0.531** ✓ → Output **a**.

Update interval same as encoder:
$$\text{low} = 0.0, \quad \text{high} = 0.6, \quad \text{range} = 0.6$$

### Decode Symbol 2

$$\text{offset} = \frac{0.531 - 0.0}{0.6} = 0.885$$

Find symbol containing 0.885:
- $a$: $[0.0, 0.6)$ — No
- $b$: $[0.6, 0.9)$ — **Contains 0.885** ✓ → Output **b**.

Update interval:
$$\text{low} = 0.0 + 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.36$$
$$\text{high} = 0.0 + 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.54$$
$$\text{range} = 0.18$$

### Decode Symbol 3

$$\text{offset} = \frac{0.531 - 0.36}{0.18} = \frac{0.171}{0.18} = 0.95$$

Find symbol containing 0.95:
- $a$: $[0.0, 0.6)$ — No
- $b$: $[0.6, 0.9)$ — No
- $c$: $[0.9, 1.0)$ — **Contains 0.95** ✓ → Output **c**.

**Decoded message: "abc"** ✓ — Perfect round-trip!

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Input Symbol | low | high | range | After Update Interval |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Init | — | 0.000 | 1.000 | 1.000 | $[0.000, 1.000)$ |
| 1 | **a** | 0.000 | 0.600 | 0.600 | $[0.000, 0.600)$ |
| 2 | **b** | 0.360 | 0.540 | 0.180 | $[0.360, 0.540)$ |
| 3 | **c** | 0.522 | 0.540 | 0.018 | $[0.522, 0.540)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The interval width after $n$ symbols equals the probability of that sequence, $P(s_1 s_2 \ldots s_n)$. For our example, $P(\text{abc}) = 0.6 \times 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.018$ — exactly matching the final range. This is the foundational invariant of arithmetic coding.

## 3.4 Handling the Underflow Problem (KTU Theoretical Note)

In practical implementation, working with infinite-precision reals is impossible. Two critical issues arise:

- **Underflow**: The range $[0, 1)$ is fixed; as it shrinks, fixed-point integer arithmetic is used (e.g., 32-bit integers).
- **Renormalization (Bit-Stuffing / Bit-Shifting)**: When the leading bits of $\text{low}$ and $\text{high}$ become identical, those bits are *emitted* and both bounds are shifted left by 1 bit — effectively zooming the interval back to fill $[0, 1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exam questions often test the **shannon entropy comparison** and **expected code length**. Always write: Average arithmetic code length $L_{\text{avg}} = -\sum_x P(x) \log_2 P(x) = H$, meaning arithmetic coding is **entropy-optimal** in the limit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Arithmetic Coding System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete data flow between the encoder and decoder subsystems, mapping all functional interactions and the intermediate data products.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Symbol Stream S] --> B[Probability Model Estimator]
    B --> C[Cumulative Probability Table F_low F_high]
    C --> D[Arithmetic Encoder Core]
    D --> E[Bit Stream Generator]
    E --> F[Compressed Bit Stream]
    
    F --> G[Bit Stream Reader]
    G --> H[Arithmetic Decoder Core]
    H --> I[Symbol Recovery Block]
    C --> H
    I --> J[Reconstructed Symbol Stream S_hat]
    
    D --> K[Interval Tracker low high range]
    H --> K
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style J fill:#e8f5e9
    style D fill:#ffe0e0
    style H fill:#ffe0e0
```

## 4.2 Interval Narrowing Process — Visual Schematic

The diagram below depicts the *narrowing of the working interval* as each symbol of the string "abc" is processed. Notice how the interval length equals the cumulative product of symbol probabilities.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Step 0: Interval [0.000, 1.000) Range = 1.000"]:::init
    P1["Step 1: After 'a' Interval [0.000, 0.600) Range = 0.600"]:::step1
    P2["Step 2: After 'b' Interval [0.360, 0.540) Range = 0.180"]:::step2
    P3["Step 3: After 'c' Interval [0.522, 0.540) Range = 0.018"]:::step3
    P4["Final Tag Value T = 0.531"]:::final
    
    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    
    classDef init fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef final fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Encoder–Decoder Symmetric Architecture

The encoder and decoder share an *identical* internal state machine. The only difference is the direction of information flow: the encoder pushes symbols through state updates, while the decoder pulls symbols out from a single tag value.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Encoder
        E1[Read symbol s_i] --> E2[Compute low_new high_new]
        E2 --> E3[Update Interval Tracker]
        E3 --> E4{More symbols?}
        E4 -->|Yes| E1
        E4 -->|No| E5[Emit Tag Value T]
    end
    
    subgraph Shared_State[Shared Model]
        M1[Probability Table P]
        M2[Cumulative Table F]
        M3[Interval State low high]
    end
    
    subgraph Decoder
        D1[Read Tag T] --> D2[Find symbol whose range contains T]
        D2 --> D3[Update Interval State]
        D3 --> D4[Emit symbol s_i]
        D4 --> D5{End of message?}
        D5 -->|No| D2
        D5 -->|Yes| D6[Reconstructed String]
    end
    
    E5 --> D1
    M1 --> E2
    M2 --> E2
    M3 --> E3
    M1 --> D2
    M2 --> D2
    M3 --> D3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The decoder does **not** need the full encoder history — only the *probability model* (transmitted once as a side-channel or fixed by spec) and the *single tag value* $T$. This is why arithmetic coding achieves superior compression for *longer* messages: the model overhead is amortized over many symbols.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Arithmetic Coding. Mention any two advantages it has over Huffman Coding.**

**Model Answer:**
Arithmetic Coding is a lossless data compression technique that encodes an entire message into a single fractional number $T \in [0, 1)$, lying in a sub-interval whose length equals the probability of the source message.

**Two advantages over Huffman Coding:**
1. **Achieves entropy limit**: Arithmetic coding produces fractional bits per symbol, theoretically matching Shannon entropy $H$, whereas Huffman coding is constrained to integer codeword lengths and cannot go below 1 bit/symbol for any symbol.
2. **No symbol-by-symbol restriction**: Huffman requires a code tree built from symbol probabilities; arithmetic coding handles non-integer, highly skewed probabilities (e.g., 0.99 / 0.01) gracefully without code-tree imbalance.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the role of cumulative probability in arithmetic coding.**

**Model Answer:**
Cumulative probabilities define the **boundaries of each symbol's sub-interval** within the unit range $[0, 1)$. For a symbol $x$:

$$F_{\text{low}}(x) = \sum_{y < x} P(y), \quad F_{\text{high}}(x) = F_{\text{low}}(x) + P(x)$$

These bounds are used during encoding to compute the **narrowed interval** at every step via:

$$\text{low}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{low}}(s_i)$$

$$\text{high}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{high}}(s_i)$$

During decoding, they help the decoder identify which symbol's sub-interval contains the tag value $T$, thereby reconstructing the message.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Arithmetic Coding algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A source emits symbols $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with probabilities $P(a) = 0.4$, $P(b) = 0.3$, $P(c) = 0.2$, $P(d) = 0.1$. Using Arithmetic Coding, encode the string **"bad"**. Show all interval updates and find the final tag value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. Compute cumulative probabilities $F_{\text{low}}(x)$ for all symbols $x$.
2. Initialize $\text{low} = 0$, $\text{high} = 1$.
3. For each symbol $s_i$ in the message:
   - $\text{range} = \text{high} - \text{low}$
   - $\text{low} \leftarrow \text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{low}}(s_i)$
   - $\text{high} \leftarrow \text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{high}}(s_i)$
4. Emit a tag value $T$ from the final interval $[\text{low}, \text{high})$.

**Limitations of Arithmetic Coding:**
1. **Computational complexity**: Requires floating-point multiplications per symbol — slower than Huffman for short messages.
2. **Patent issues (historical)**: IBM and other entities held patents until the early 2000s, limiting widespread adoption until expiry.
3. **Precision/underflow concerns**: Practical implementations need integer arithmetic and renormalization, adding implementation complexity.
4. **Not adaptive by default**: Requires an accurate probability model; mismatches degrade performance.
5. **Single point of failure**: A single bit error in the tag value can corrupt the entire decoded message (no resynchronization).

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Build Cumulative Table**

| Symbol | $P(x)$ | $F_{\text{low}}(x)$ | $F_{\text{high}}(x)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| a | 0.4 | 0.0 | 0.4 |
| b | 0.3 | 0.4 | 0.7 |
| c | 0.2 | 0.7 | 0.9 |
| d | 0.1 | 0.9 | 1.0 |

**Step 2: Initialize**

$$\text{low}_0 = 0.0, \quad \text{high}_0 = 1.0, \quad \text{range}_0 = 1.0$$

**Step 3: Process 'b' (first symbol)**

$$\text{low}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.4 = 0.4$$

$$\text{high}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.7 = 0.7$$

$$\text{range}_1 = 0.3, \quad \text{interval} = [0.4, 0.7)$$

**Step 4: Process 'a' (second symbol)**

$$\text{low}_2 = 0.4 + 0.3 \times 0.0 = 0.4$$

$$\text{high}_2 = 0.4 + 0.3 \times 0.4 = 0.4 + 0.12 = 0.52$$

$$\text{range}_2 = 0.12, \quad \text{interval} = [0.4, 0.52)$$

**Step 5: Process 'd' (third symbol)**

$$\text{low}_3 = 0.4 + 0.12 \times 0.9 = 0.4 + 0.108 = 0.508$$

$$\text{high}_3 = 0.4 + 0.12 \times 1.0 = 0.4 + 0.12 = 0.52$$

$$\text{range}_3 = 0.52 - 0.508 = 0.012, \quad \text{interval} = [0.508, 0.52)$$

**Step 6: Select the Tag Value**

Choose the midpoint of the final interval:

$$T = \frac{0.508 + 0.52}{2} = 0.514$$

**Verification:**
- $P(\text{"bad"}) = 0.3 \times 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.012$ ✓ — matches $\text{range}_3$.
- $T = 0.514$ satisfies $0.508 \leq 0.514 < 0.52$ ✓.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Arithmetic Coding** and **Huffman Coding** in detail. Under what conditions do they yield identical performance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a binary source with $P(0) = 0.75$ and $P(1) = 0.25$. Encode the sequence **"001"** using arithmetic coding and determine the minimum number of bits required for transmission. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Huffman Coding | Arithmetic Coding |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Output unit** | Discrete code per symbol | Single tag value for whole message |
| **Code length** | Integer bits per symbol | Fractional bits per symbol |
| **Optimality** | Optimal for symbol-by-symbol coding | Asymptotically optimal (entropy-bound) |
| **Probabilities** | Efficient for $P(x) = 2^{-k}$ | Efficient for *any* probability distribution |
| **Implementation** | Simple — code tree | Complex — interval arithmetic + renormalization |
| **Adaptivity** | Hard to make adaptive | Naturally adaptive (model can be updated) |
| **Patent status** | Unrestricted | Historically patented (now expired) |
| **Use cases** | Deflate (ZIP), JPEG baseline | H.264, H.265, AV1, JPEG 2000, Bzip2 |

**Conditions for Identical Performance:**

Huffman and Arithmetic Coding yield **identical average code lengths** when **all symbol probabilities are exact negative powers of 1/2** (i.e., $P(x) = 2^{-k}$ for some integer $k$). Under this condition, Huffman's integer codeword lengths are already optimal and match the entropy. Beyond this, arithmetic coding strictly dominates.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Build Cumulative Table for binary source**

| Symbol | $P(x)$ | $F_{\text{low}}(x)$ | $F_{\text{high}}(x)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0.75 | 0.00 | 0.75 |
| 1 | 0.25 | 0.75 | 1.00 |

**Step 2: Initialize**

$$\text{low}_0 = 0.0, \quad \text{high}_0 = 1.0, \quad \text{range}_0 = 1.0$$

**Step 3: Process first '0'**

$$\text{low}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.0 = 0.0$$

$$\text{high}_1 = 0.0 + 1.0 \times 0.75 = 0.75$$

$$\text{range}_1 = 0.75, \quad \text{interval} = [0.0, 0.75)$$

**Step 4: Process second '0'**

$$\text{low}_2 = 0.0 + 0.75 \times 0.0 = 0.0$$

$$\text{high}_2 = 0.0 + 0.75 \times 0.75 = 0.5625$$

$$\text{range}_2 = 0.5625, \quad \text{interval} = [0.0, 0.5625)$$

**Step 5: Process '1'**

$$\text{low}_3 = 0.0 + 0.5625 \times 0.75 = 0.421875$$

$$\text{high}_3 = 0.0 + 0.5625 \times 1.0 = 0.5625$$

$$\text{range}_3 = 0.5625 - 0.421875 = 0.140625, \quad \text{interval} = [0.421875, 0.5625)$$

**Step 6: Select the Tag Value**

$$T = \frac{0.421875 + 0.5625}{2} = 0.4921875$$

**Step 7: Compute Minimum Bits Required**

We need the smallest integer $n$ such that $2^{-n} \leq \text{range}_3 = 0.140625$:

$$2^{-n} \leq 0.140625 \Rightarrow n \geq \log_2\left(\frac{1}{0.140625}\right) \approx 2.83$$

$$\therefore n = 3 \text{ bits minimum}$$

**Cross-check with Shannon Entropy:**

$$H = -[0.75 \log_2 0.75 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25] = 0.8113 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

For 3 symbols: $L_{\text{theoretical}} = 3 \times 0.8113 = 2.4339$ bits. The minimum integer bound of 3 bits is consistent.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong interval formula**: Many students write $\text{low} + \text{range} \cdot P(x)$ instead of $\text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{low}}(x)$. Always use **cumulative low/high** — not the raw probability.
> 2. **Misplaced $T$ value**: Tag value must satisfy $\text{low}_{\text{final}} \leq T < \text{high}_{\text{final}}$. Choosing $T = \text{high}_{\text{final}}$ is technically outside the half-open interval.
> 3. **Forgetting cumulative probability ordering**: Sort symbols consistently (alphabetically or by probability) — inconsistent ordering between encoder and decoder causes decoding failure.
> 4. **Skipping step-by-step work**: Examiners award marks per interval update. Showing *all* intermediate intervals and ranges is mandatory for full 7-mark sub-parts.
> 5. **Ignoring the verification step**: Always verify $P(S) = \text{range}_{\text{final}}$. This demonstrates conceptual mastery and earns bonus goodwill marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Arithmetic Coding:**

- ⭐ Arithmetic Coding maps an **entire message** to a **single number** in $[0, 1)$.
- ⭐ Uses **cumulative probabilities** $F_{\text{low}}(x)$ and $F_{\text{high}}(x)$ — *not* raw probabilities.
- ⭐ Interval narrowing formula: $\text{low}_{\text{new}} = \text{low} + \text{range} \cdot F_{\text{low}}(s_i)$; same pattern for $\text{high}$.
- ⭐ Final range = product of symbol probabilities = $P(S)$.
- ⭐ Any tag value $T$ inside the final interval is a valid codeword.
- ⭐ Bits required = smallest $n$ such that $2^{-n} \leq \text{range}_{\text{final}}$.
- ⭐ Achieves **Shannon entropy limit** — optimal in the asymptotic sense.
- ⭐ Strictly outperforms Huffman for non-power-of-2 probabilities.
- ⭐ Identical to Huffman only when $P(x) = 2^{-k}$.
- ⭐ Decoder needs only the tag value + the probability model — no symbol-by-symbol stream.
- ⭐ Final range length invariant: $\text{width} = \prod_i P(s_i)$.
- ⭐ Practical forms: **Range Coding**, **CABAC**, **MQ-coder** — all are industry derivatives.
- ⭐ Applications: H.264/HEVC/AV1 video, JPEG 2000 images, PDF, Bzip2, 7-Zip.
- ⭐ Examiner's pet: Always show **interval table + bit-count computation** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lossless Image Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lossless Image Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Lossless Image Compression** is a class of data compression algorithms that allows the exact original image data to be perfectly reconstructed from the compressed data, with **no loss of information**. The reconstructed image is mathematically identical (bit-for-bit) to the source image. This is in strict contrast to lossy compression (e.g., JPEG), where some pixel data is permanently discarded to achieve higher compression ratios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The 2024 scheme mandates study of *Information Theory Basics, Entropy, Shannon-Fano, Huffman Coding, Arithmetic Coding, Dictionary-based (LZW), and Run-Length Encoding (RLE)*. All these are lossless techniques.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine you have a grocery list written in full sentences: *"I need to buy one apple, one apple, one apple, one banana, one banana, and one carrot."* That is 21 words for 6 items. A lossless compression is like rewriting it as: *"3 apples, 2 bananas, 1 carrot."* This second list is **shorter**, yet if your friend reads it, they can write back the **exact original sentence** word-for-word without any error. Nothing is lost — only redundancy is removed. Lossless image compression does the same: it finds statistical patterns (repeated pixel values, frequent color codes) and rewrites them using fewer bits, while keeping the ability to restore the **exact** original file.

### 1.3 Where Lossless Compression is Mandatory
- Medical imaging (X-rays, MRI, CT scans) — diagnostic accuracy cannot tolerate any pixel error.
- Satellite imagery for scientific measurement.
- Technical drawings, blueprints, and CAD files.
- Archival storage of master copies of photographs.
- Source code and text file compression (ZIP, PNG, GIF).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Mathematical Guarantee:** For any lossless scheme, the average codeword length $L_{avg} \geq H(X)$, where $H(X)$ is the **entropy** of the source. Compression is impossible below the entropy limit.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used
- **Compression Ratio (CR)** $= \dfrac{n_1}{n_2}$ (original size / compressed size).
- **Compression Factor (CF)** $= n_1 - n_2$ (bits saved).
- **Coding Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} \times 100\%$.
- **Redundancy** $R = 1 - \eta$.
- **Bits per Pixel (bpp)** $= \dfrac{n_2}{M \times N}$ for an $M \times N$ image.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Information Theory Foundations
The mathematical bedrock of lossless compression is **Shannon's Information Theory** (1948). Every image pixel is treated as a random event drawn from a probability distribution.

- **Self-Information** $I(x_i)$: The amount of surprise carried by a single event $x_i$ with probability $P(x_i)$.
  - A rare event carries more information than a common one.
- **Entropy** $H(X)$: The **average** amount of information per symbol — the theoretical lower bound on bits needed.
- **Source Coding Theorem (Shannon's First Theorem)**: It is possible to encode a source in $H(X)$ bits per symbol on average, and **no fewer** bits per symbol are possible losslessly.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Technique

**A. Run-Length Encoding (RLE)**
- **Why:** Many images (especially binary ones like fax, simple icons) have long runs of identical pixel values.
- **How:** Replace `xxxxxxx` with the count `7` and value `x`. Encoded as `<count, value>` pairs.
- **Best for:** High-redundancy images, binary images (BMP, PCX format).

**B. Shannon-Fano Coding**
- **Why:** Frequent symbols should get short codes; rare symbols get long codes (variable-length coding).
- **How:** Recursively split the symbol set into two halves of *near-equal* total probability. Assign `0` to top half, `1` to bottom half.
- **Limitation:** Not always optimal; sub-optimal split selection reduces efficiency.

**C. Huffman Coding**
- **Why:** Guarantees an *optimal* prefix-free code given a known probability distribution.
- **How:** Build a binary tree bottom-up using a greedy priority queue (min-heap) of frequencies. The two lowest-probability nodes are merged repeatedly.
- **Key property — Prefix-free:** No code is a prefix of another, so decoding is **unambiguous**.

**D. Arithmetic Coding**
- **Why:** Huffman assigns whole bits per symbol, which is wasteful when one symbol has probability close to 0.5. Arithmetic coding encodes the *entire message* as a single number in $[0, 1)$.
- **How:** Maintain a sub-interval that shrinks based on cumulative symbol probabilities. The final interval uniquely identifies the message.

**E. Dictionary-Based Coding (LZ77 / LZW)**
- **Why:** Image data has repeated *patterns* (not just repeated bytes). A dictionary captures these patterns.
- **How:** LZW builds a string table at encode time, replacing repeated strings with single dictionary indices.
- **Used in:** GIF, TIFF, PNG (via DEFLATE = LZ77 + Huffman).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-Information | $I(x_i) = -\log_2 P(x_i)$ | Information in bits per occurrence of symbol $x_i$ |
| 2 | Entropy (Average Info) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)$ | Lower bound on average bits/symbol; units: bits/symbol |
| 3 | Average Codeword Length | $L_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \cdot l_i$ | $l_i$ = length of codeword for $x_i$ |
| 4 | Coding Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage; $0\% \leq \eta \leq 100\%$ |
| 5 | Redundancy | $R = 1 - \eta$ | Fractional waste in the code |
| 6 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2}$ | Original bits / Compressed bits (ratio, not %) |
| 7 | Bits per Pixel | $bpp = \dfrac{\text{total bits}}{M \cdot N}$ | For an $M \times N$ image |
| 8 | Shannon Limit (Noiseless) | $L_{avg} \geq H(X)$ | Lossless impossible below entropy |
| 9 | Number of bits for $N$ symbols | $N_{bits} = N \times L_{avg}$ | Total bits for an image with $N$ symbols |
| 10 | Kraft-McMillan Inequality | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | Necessary condition for any prefix code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always convert $\log$ to base **2** when the question says "in bits". Use $\log_{10}$ only when explicitly asked for "in digits" or "in hartleys". One natural unit of info = $\log_2 e$ bits.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Lossless coding is the **backbone of every web page, photo archive, and document storage pipeline**. PNG (web images), FLAC (audio), ZIP (general data), and PDF (document text) all rely on the lossless techniques covered in this module. In telemedicine, a single miscoded pixel in a tumor scan can change a diagnosis — therefore lossless is **non-negotiable** in medical PACS systems. In space telemetry (ISRO, NASA), lossless compression preserves raw sensor data before ground-side analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Entropy & Coding Efficiency

**Problem (KTU Style):** A grayscale image has 4 grey levels with the following probabilities from a 4-symbol alphabet:

$$
P(A) = 0.4, \quad P(B) = 0.3, \quad P(C) = 0.2, \quad P(D) = 0.1
$$

Compute: (i) entropy of the source, (ii) the minimum possible average codeword length, and (iii) a hypothetical efficiency check.

**Step 1 — Self-Information of each symbol**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(A) &= -\log_2(0.4) = 1.3219 \text{ bits} \\
I(B) &= -\log_2(0.3) = 1.7370 \text{ bits} \\
I(C) &= -\log_2(0.2) = 2.3219 \text{ bits} \\
I(D) &= -\log_2(0.1) = 3.3219 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Entropy (average information)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{4} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i) \\
     &= -(0.4 \cdot \log_2 0.4 + 0.3 \cdot \log_2 0.3 + 0.2 \cdot \log_2 0.2 + 0.1 \cdot \log_2 0.1) \\
     &= -(0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) + 0.3 \cdot (-1.7370) + 0.2 \cdot (-2.3219) + 0.1 \cdot (-3.3219)) \\
     &= -(-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322) \\
     &= 1.8465 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum average codeword length (Shannon limit)**

$$
L_{avg}^{\min} = H(X) = 1.8465 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A fixed-length code for 4 symbols needs $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits per symbol. Since $1.8465 < 2$, **variable-length coding can do better** — this is exactly the motivation for Huffman.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Constructing a Huffman Code

**Problem:** Build a Huffman code for the source above. Then compute the actual $L_{avg}$ and coding efficiency $\eta$.

**Step 1 — List symbols with their probabilities (sorted descending)**

| Symbol | Probability |
|:---:|:---:|
| A | 0.4 |
| B | 0.3 |
| C | 0.2 |
| D | 0.1 |

**Step 2 — Merge the two lowest probabilities repeatedly**

- **Iteration 1:** Merge C (0.2) and D (0.1) → new node **CD = 0.3**. Pool: {A:0.4, B:0.3, CD:0.3}.
- **Iteration 2:** Merge B (0.3) and CD (0.3) → new node **BCD = 0.6**. Pool: {A:0.4, BCD:0.6}.
- **Iteration 3:** Merge A (0.4) and BCD (0.6) → **root = 1.0**.

**Step 3 — Assign 0/1 to branches and read codewords**

Going top-down, assign 0 to left, 1 to right (or vice versa — both are valid):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow 0 \quad (l_A = 1) \\
B &\rightarrow 10 \quad (l_B = 2) \\
C &\rightarrow 110 \quad (l_C = 3) \\
D &\rightarrow 111 \quad (l_D = 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Kraft-McMillan Inequality**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} = 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.0
$$

Since the sum equals **1.0**, the code is a *complete* prefix code. ✔

**Step 5 — Compute Average Codeword Length**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= \sum P(x_i) \cdot l_i \\
        &= 0.4(1) + 0.3(2) + 0.2(3) + 0.1(3) \\
        &= 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.3 \\
        &= 1.9 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Coding Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.8465}{1.9} \times 100\% = 97.18\%
$$

**Step 7 — Redundancy**

$$
R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9718 = 0.0282 \;(2.82\%)
$$

**Step 8 — Bits for a 100-symbol block**

$$
N_{bits} = 100 \times 1.9 = 190 \text{ bits (Huffman) vs. } 200 \text{ bits (fixed-length)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Note that $L_{avg} = 1.9$ but $H(X) = 1.8465$. The "loss" of $0.0535$ bits/symbol is unavoidable here because no integer codeword length can exactly match the fractional ideal. This is the **coding inefficiency gap** KTU examiners love to test.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Run-Length Encoding (RLE)

**Problem:** Encode the following binary row of an image using RLE:  
`BBBBBBBWWWWBBBBBBBBBWW`

**Step 1 — Scan and count consecutive runs**

- `B` runs 7 times → `(7, B)`
- `W` runs 4 times → `(4, W)`
- `B` runs 9 times → `(9, B)`
- `W` runs 2 times → `(2, W)`

**Step 2 — Output as sequence of (count, symbol) pairs**

$$
\text{Encoded: } \{(7, B), (4, W), (9, B), (2, W)\}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare bit counts**

Assuming each original symbol needs 1 bit: original length = $7 + 4 + 9 + 2 = 22$ bits.  
RLE needs: 4 pairs × 8 bits = 32 bits. **No gain here** because the runs are short. RLE is efficient only when run length > 2 or so.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world RLE** stores counts in fixed-size fields (e.g., 1 byte count + 1 byte value = 2 bytes per pair). A common variant uses a *flag byte* to indicate literal vs. run, e.g., the PackBits algorithm in TIFF.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Arithmetic Coding (Conceptual)

**Problem:** Encode the message `BACA` using arithmetic coding with symbol probabilities $P(A)=0.4, P(B)=0.3, P(C)=0.2, P(D)=0.1$.

**Cumulative ranges (for arithmetic coding):**

| Symbol | Range (low, high) |
|:---:|:---:|
| A | [0.00, 0.40) |
| B | [0.40, 0.70) |
| C | [0.70, 0.90) |
| D | [0.90, 1.00) |

**Encoding the message `BACA` step by step:**

Start interval = $[0, 1)$.

- **Read 'B':** new interval = $[0 + 1 \times 0.40, \; 0 + 1 \times 0.70) = [0.40, 0.70)$
- **Read 'A':** new interval = $[0.40 + 0.30 \times 0.00, \; 0.40 + 0.30 \times 0.40) = [0.40, 0.52)$
- **Read 'C':** new interval = $[0.40 + 0.12 \times 0.70, \; 0.40 + 0.12 \times 0.90) = [0.484, 0.508)$
- **Read 'A':** new interval = $[0.484 + 0.024 \times 0.00, \; 0.484 + 0.024 \times 0.40) = [0.484, 0.4936)$

**Final tag:** any number in $[0.484, 0.4936)$ represents `BACA`. E.g., **0.49**. This single fraction encodes 4 symbols — the decoder reverses the process using the same probability ranges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The decoder must know the symbol probabilities (transmitted as a model header) to reverse the process. The output is typically a fixed-precision binary fraction, e.g., 0.49 → binary ≈ 32 bits. For long messages, this approaches entropy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Lossless Compression Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Original Image Pixels"]
    model["Probability / Dictionary Model"]
    enc["Entropy Encoder (Huffman / Arithmetic / LZW)"]
    comp["Compressed Bitstream"]
    dec["Entropy Decoder"]
    rmodel["Probability / Dictionary Reconstructor"]
    rec["Reconstructed Image (Identical to Original)"]

    src --> model
    src --> enc
    model --> enc
    enc --> comp
    comp --> dec
    dec --> rmodel
    rmodel --> rec
    dec --> rec
```

### 4.2 Huffman Tree Construction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Sorted symbol list with frequencies"]
    init["Build priority queue (min-heap) of leaf nodes"]
    check{"Queue has only one node?"}
    merge["Extract two smallest nodes, merge into parent with sum of frequencies"]
    pushback["Insert parent back into queue"]
    done["Tree complete: assign 0 to left, 1 to right edges"]
    codes["Output codewords by traversing root to leaves"]

    start --> init --> check
    check -- "No" --> merge --> pushback --> check
    check -- "Yes" --> done --> codes
```

### 4.3 LZW Encoding State Machine (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s0["Initialize dictionary with all 256 single-byte strings (0-255)"]
    s1["Set w = empty string"]
    s2["Read next symbol k from input stream"]
    s3{"Is w+k in dictionary?"}
    s4["Set w = w+k (extend current string)"]
    s5["Output dictionary code for w"]
    s6["Add w+k to dictionary as new entry"]
    s7["Set w = k"]
    s8{"More input symbols?"}
    s9["Output code for remaining w"]

    s0 --> s1 --> s2 --> s3
    s3 -- "Yes" --> s4 --> s2
    s3 -- "No"  --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8
    s8 -- "Yes" --> s2
    s8 -- "No"  --> s9
```

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Lossless Techniques (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lossless["Lossless Image Compression Techniques"]
    rle["Run-Length Encoding (RLE)"]
    shannonFano["Shannon-Fano Coding"]
    huffman["Huffman Coding"]
    arith["Arithmetic Coding"]
    lzw["LZW Dictionary Coding"]

    lossless --> rle
    lossless --> shannonFano
    lossless --> huffman
    lossless --> arith
    lossless --> lzw

    rle -- "Best for binary images, fax" --> note1["Replaces repeated runs with count-value pairs"]
    shannonFano -- "Top-down splits" --> note2["Sub-optimal; rarely used alone"]
    huffman -- "Optimal prefix code" --> note3["Used in PNG, JPEG, ZIP, MP3"]
    arith -- "Whole-message to a fraction" --> note4["Used in JBIG, H.264 CABAC"]
    lzw -- "Dictionary of patterns" --> note5["Used in GIF, TIFF, PDF"]
```

### 4.5 Decoding Logic for Huffman (Sequential Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a["Start decoding: bit pointer at position 0"]
    b["Traverse Huffman tree from root"]
    c{"Read next bit from stream"}
    d{"Bit = 0?"}
    e["Go to left child node"]
    f["Go to right child node"]
    g{"Is current node a leaf?"}
    h["Output the symbol at this leaf"]
    i["Reset pointer to root"]
    j{"More bits remaining?"}
    k["Decoding complete"]

    a --> b --> c --> d
    d -- "Yes" --> e
    d -- "No"  --> f
    e --> g
    f --> g
    g -- "Yes" --> h --> i --> j
    g -- "No"  --> b
    j -- "Yes" --> c
    j -- "No"  --> k
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define **entropy** in the context of image compression. Why is it considered the theoretical lower bound on the average number of bits per symbol?

**Model Answer:**
Entropy $H(X)$ is the average amount of self-information per symbol of a discrete memoryless source, defined as:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i) \;\; \text{bits/symbol}
$$

It is the theoretical **lower bound** because Shannon's Source Coding Theorem (Noiseless Coding Theorem) proves that any lossless encoding must satisfy $L_{avg} \geq H(X)$. You cannot compress a source losslessly below its entropy without losing information, since entropy measures the irreducible information content of the source. **(3 marks)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **lossless** and **lossy** image compression. Give two real-world applications where lossless compression is mandatory.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Lossless | Lossy |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Reconstruction | Identical to original | Approximate |
| Compression ratio | Lower (typically 2:1 to 3:1) | Higher (10:1 to 100:1) |
| Algorithms | Huffman, Arithmetic, LZW, RLE, PNG | JPEG, JPEG2000 lossy mode, MP3 |
| Information lost | None | Permanent, irreversible |

**Mandatory lossless applications:** (1) Medical imaging (X-rays, MRI, CT scans) where diagnostic accuracy is critical; (2) Archival storage of master images and technical drawings. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *(a)* Explain the **Huffman coding algorithm** with a clear step-by-step procedure and the construction of the binary tree. *(7 marks)*  
> *(b)* A source emits 5 symbols $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ with probabilities $\{0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10\}$. Construct the Huffman code, compute the **average codeword length**, **entropy**, **coding efficiency**, and **redundancy**. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**(a) Huffman Algorithm Steps (7 marks):**

1. **List symbols** with their probabilities in descending order.
2. **Create a leaf node** for each symbol, with weight = probability.
3. **Insert all leaf nodes** into a min-priority queue (min-heap).
4. **Repeat until one node remains:** Extract the two nodes of smallest weight, create a new internal node whose weight is the sum, and insert the new node back into the queue.
5. **The single remaining node is the root** of the Huffman tree.
6. **Assign 0 and 1** to the two branches of every internal node (convention: 0 = left, 1 = right).
7. **Read off codewords** by traversing from root to each leaf — the path of 0s and 1s is the code.

**Key properties:** (i) Prefix-free — no code is a prefix of another, so decoding is unambiguous; (ii) Optimal — minimum possible $L_{avg}$ for the given probabilities; (iii) Satisfies Kraft-McMillan inequality $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sort symbols by probability:**

| Symbol | Probability |
|:---:|:---:|
| A | 0.30 |
| B | 0.25 |
| C | 0.20 |
| D | 0.15 |
| E | 0.10 |

**Step 2 — Huffman tree construction (merges in order):**

- **Merge 1:** D (0.15) + E (0.10) → DE = 0.25. Pool: {A:0.30, B:0.25, C:0.20, DE:0.25}.
- **Merge 2:** B (0.25) + C (0.20) → BC = 0.45. Pool: {A:0.30, DE:0.25, BC:0.45}.
- **Merge 3:** A (0.30) + DE (0.25) → ADE = 0.55. Pool: {ADE:0.55, BC:0.45}.
- **Merge 4:** ADE (0.55) + BC (0.45) → **root = 1.00**.

**Step 3 — Codeword assignment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow 00 \quad (l_A = 2) \\
B &\rightarrow 10 \quad (l_B = 2) \\
C &\rightarrow 11 \quad (l_C = 2) \\
D &\rightarrow 010 \quad (l_D = 3) \\
E &\rightarrow 011 \quad (l_E = 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Kraft inequality:**

$$
2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.0 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Average Codeword Length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= 0.30(2) + 0.25(2) + 0.20(2) + 0.15(3) + 0.10(3) \\
        &= 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.30 \\
        &= 2.25 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Entropy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -[0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10] \\
     &= -[0.30(-1.7370) + 0.25(-2.0000) + 0.20(-2.3219) + 0.15(-2.7370) + 0.10(-3.3219)] \\
     &= -[-0.5211 - 0.5000 - 0.4644 - 0.4105 - 0.3322] \\
     &= 2.2282 \text{ bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Coding Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.2282}{2.25} \times 100\% = 99.03\%
$$

**Step 8 — Redundancy:**

$$
R = 1 - 0.9903 = 0.0097 \;(0.97\%)
$$

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **general model of a lossless image compression system**. Describe the roles of the **encoder** and **decoder**. *(7 marks)*  
> *(b)* Explain **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)** and **LZW Dictionary-based coding** with suitable examples. Compare their suitability for different image types. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**(a) General Lossless Compression Model (7 marks):**

The compression system consists of two complementary processes: **encoding** and **decoding**.

- **Encoder (Compressor):** Takes the original image as input, removes redundancy using a model + entropy coder, and outputs a compressed bitstream.
- **Model:** Estimates the probability distribution (statistical model) or builds a dictionary (LZ-style model) of the source.
- **Entropy Coder:** Assigns short codes to frequent events using the model — e.g., Huffman or Arithmetic.
- **Compressed Bitstream:** The reduced-size output stored or transmitted.
- **Decoder (Decompressor):** Reverses the process using the same model reconstruction. Since no information is destroyed, output equals input exactly.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original Image"]
    B["Modeller (Probability / Dictionary)"]
    C["Encoder (Huffman / Arithmetic / LZW)"]
    D["Compressed Bitstream"]
    E["Decoder"]
    F["Reconstructed Model"]
    G["Reconstructed Image (Identical)"]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    E --> G
```

**(b) RLE and LZW (7 marks):**

**Run-Length Encoding (RLE):**
- Replaces consecutive identical symbols (runs) with a pair (count, value).
- **Example:** `WWWWBBBAAARRR` → `(4,W), (3,B), (3,A), (3,R)`.
- **Best suited for:** Binary images (fax, scanned text), images with large uniform regions, icons, simple graphics.
- **Poor for:** Natural photographs (noisy, no long runs — may even expand file size).

**Lempel-Ziv-Welch (LZW) Coding:**
- Builds a dictionary of strings seen so far; replaces future occurrences with dictionary indices.
- **Example:** Encoding `ABABABABC` from alphabet {A, B, C}:
  - Dictionary initially has 256 entries (ASCII).
  - After processing: codes 256 = `AB`, 257 = `ABA`, 258 = `ABAB`, 259 = `ABC` may be added.
  - Output is a stream of dictionary codes (typically 9–12 bits each).
- **Best suited for:** Images with repeated patterns — photographs, GIF, TIFF.
- **Poor for:** Highly random data, encrypted data.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | RLE | LZW |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Mechanism | Replaces runs of identical values | Replaces repeated strings/patterns |
| Compression ratio | Best on runs > 3 | Best on patterned data |
| Image type | Binary, line art | Photographic, mixed |
| Used in | BMP, PCX, fax | GIF, TIFF, PDF, PNG (via LZ77) |
| Computational cost | Very low | Moderate (dictionary lookup) |
| Adaptivity | None | Adaptive — learns on the fly |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $\log$ to base 2.** Marks lost silently when $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$ is used but the question asks for bits. Always state: *"Using $\log_2$ since we measure in bits."*
> 2. **Not verifying the Kraft-McMillan inequality** after constructing a Huffman code. KTU examiners often award 1 mark for this verification.
> 3. **Swapping the roles of efficiency and redundancy.** Coding **efficiency** $\eta$ is high (good), redundancy $R$ is low (good). Do not invert them.
> 4. **Drawing Huffman trees without showing the merge order.** Each merge step is a step, and KTU allots marks per step. Show the sorted pool after every merge.
> 5. **Confusing RLE with LZW.** RLE replaces *repeated identical symbols*; LZW replaces *repeated strings* (which can be different). This is a classic 2-mark question.
> 6. **Missing the 0.5 + 0.5 split convention** in Shannon-Fano. Top half = 0, bottom half = 1 (or vice versa, but be consistent).
> 7. **Forgetting the "lossless" guarantee in the final answer.** A common Part-A trap asks "can $L_{avg}$ be less than $H(X)$ losslessly?" — answer: **no**, by Shannon's theorem.
> 8. **Arithmetic coding examples:** Students often forget to scale the interval by the *width* of the previous range, leading to wrong intervals. Always use: `new_low = old_low + range × cum_low`, `new_high = old_low + range × cum_high`.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Lossless compression** guarantees **exact** bit-for-bit reconstruction; lossy does not.
- **Information content** of symbol $x_i$ is $I(x_i) = -\log_2 P(x_i)$ — rare events carry more info.
- **Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum P(x_i) \log_2 P(x_i)$ is the **absolute lower bound** on average bits per symbol for lossless coding (Shannon's Source Coding Theorem).
- **Average codeword length** $L_{avg} = \sum P(x_i) \cdot l_i$ must satisfy $L_{avg} \geq H(X)$.
- **Coding efficiency** $\eta = H(X)/L_{avg}$; **redundancy** $R = 1 - \eta$.
- **Huffman coding** uses a **bottom-up greedy** approach with a min-heap; produces an **optimal prefix code** given known probabilities.
- **Huffman tree construction steps:** list probabilities → merge two smallest → repeat → assign 0/1 → read off codes.
- **Always verify** with **Kraft-McMillan inequality**: $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ (equality for complete codes).
- **Shannon-Fano** is **top-down** (recursive probability halving); **not always optimal**; mostly of historical interest, rarely used in practice.
- **Arithmetic coding** encodes the **entire message** as a single number in $[0, 1)$ — superior to Huffman when symbol probabilities are very skewed (close to 0.5 / 0.5 splits).
- **LZW (dictionary-based)** builds a string table during encoding; outputs dictionary indices; used in **GIF, TIFF, PDF** (modern PNG uses LZ77 + Huffman = DEFLATE).
- **RLE** replaces runs of identical symbols with (count, value) pairs; best for **binary** and line-art images.
- **Compression ratio** $CR = n_1/n_2$ where $n_1$ = original bits, $n_2$ = compressed bits.
- **Bits per pixel** $bpp = \text{total bits} / (M \times N)$ — used in KTU problems to compute file size.
- **Prefix-free property** is essential for unambiguous decoding of variable-length codes.
- **LZW decoder** does not need the dictionary transmitted — it rebuilds it from the code stream itself.
- **Lossless applications:** medical imaging, satellite imagery, technical drawings, archival, source code, text/PDF/ZIP.
- **Lossy applications** (for contrast only): JPEG photos, video streaming, MP3 audio — these are **not** covered in this module.
- **Order of compression performance** (typical): Arithmetic ≥ Huffman > Shannon-Fano; LZW depends on data redundancy; RLE is highly data-dependent.
- **KTU 2024 favorite sub-topics:** (1) Entropy calculation from a probability table, (2) Huffman tree construction from 5–6 symbols, (3) LZW encoding trace, (4) RLE encode/decode, (5) Coding efficiency comparison.
- **Key mnemonic — "HALF"**: **H**uffman = **A**rithmetic = **L**empel-Ziv = **F**ano (all lossless, all entropy-related).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In a 14-mark Huffman problem, allocate ~3 marks each to: probability sorting, tree merges, code assignment, $L_{avg}$ calculation, and $H(X)$ + $\eta$ + $R$ calculation. Missing the **efficiency and redundancy** sub-part is where most students lose 2–3 easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lossy Compression Algorithms - Distortion Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Lossy Compression Algorithms - Distortion Measures # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Lossy Compression Algorithms**, a **Distortion Measure** is a mathematically rigorous, quantitative metric (or fidelity criterion) used to quantify the numerical difference, error, or perceptual dissimilarity between the **original (uncompressed) signal** $x$ and its **reconstructed (decompressed) approximation** $\hat{x}$ after the lossy compression–decompression pipeline. It is formally represented as a function $d(x, \hat{x})$ whose output is a non-negative scalar or vector representing the cost of losing information. According to **Shannon's Rate-Distortion Theory (1948)**, the entire field of lossy compression is mathematically formulated as the constrained optimization problem: $$ \min_{q(\hat{x}\vert x)} I(X;\hat{X}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbb{E}[d(X,\hat{X})] \leq D $$ where: - $I(X;\hat{X})$ is the **mutual information** between source and reconstruction. - $q(\hat{x}\vert x)$ is the conditional probability distribution of the decoder/encoder mapping. - $D$ is the **maximum permissible distortion budget**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** > Distortion measures are the **mathematical backbone** of any lossy compressor. Without them, we cannot quantify *how much* information we lost, nor can we mathematically justify the design choice between competing codecs (e.g., JPEG vs. JPEG 2000, MP3 vs. AAC). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Photocopy Machine" Analogy** > > Imagine you photocopy a high-resolution color photograph using a low-quality machine. The output looks "similar" but not identical — colors are slightly washed out, fine textures are blurred, and edges are jagged. > > - The **photocopier settings** (dpi, toner level) determine *how aggressively* it compresses/throws away data. > - The **distortion measure** is the ruler you use to measure *how bad the damage is*. Is it the average pixel error? The worst pixel error? Or does it look bad to the human eye? > > Just like a tailor cannot alter a dress without knowing the customer's tolerance for imperfection, a compression engineer cannot design a lossy codec without a formal **distortion measure** to define "good enough". --- ## 1.3 The Three Philosophical Classes of Distortion Distortion measures in multimedia are grouped into three categories: | Class | Definition | Example Metrics | Typical Application | |:------|:-----------|:----------------|:--------------------| | **Sample-Based (Objective)** | Mathematical norms computed sample-by-sample on raw pixel/audio values. | MSE, MAE, PSNR, SNR | Image, Audio, Video codec benchmarking | | **Signal-Based (Transform Domain)** | Distortion measured in a perceptually-meaningful transformed domain (DCT, wavelet). | LMSE, Perceptual Weighting | JPEG, JPEG 2000 | | **Perceptual / Subjective** | Distortion modeled on Human Visual System (HVS) or Human Auditory System (HAS). | JND, MOS, SSIM, MS-SSIM | End-user QoE for streaming | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The course emphasizes **Sample-Based and Signal-Based** distortion measures mathematically, while introducing **Perceptual measures** conceptually. Both definition and formula are mandatory. --- ## 1.4 Why Distortion Measures Matter — The Rate-Distortion Trade-Off The fundamental law governing lossy compression is the **Rate-Distortion (R-D) Trade-Off**: $$ \boxed{R \uparrow \;\; \Longleftrightarrow \;\; D \downarrow} $$ - **Higher bit-rate $R$** ⇒ smaller distortion (better fidelity, larger file size). - **Lower bit-rate $R$** ⇒ larger distortion (worse fidelity, smaller file size). A distortion measure allows the system designer to **operationalize** this trade-off: "What is the minimum number of bits/sec required so that the perceptual quality does not drop below an acceptable threshold?" # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Rate-Distortion Function $R(D)$ For a memoryless source with variance $\sigma^2$, the **Rate-Distortion function** $R(D)$ defines the theoretical lower bound on the bit-rate required to achieve an average distortion $\leq D$. ### 2.1.1 For a Gaussian Source (Most Important Case) For a continuous-amplitude, memoryless Gaussian source with variance $\sigma^2$ and squared-error distortion: $$ R(D) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} \log_2 \left(\dfrac{\sigma^2}{D}\right) & \text{if } 0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2 \\[2mm] 0 & \text{if } D > \sigma^2 \end{cases} $$ **Interpretation:** - $D_{\max} = \sigma^2$: If we allow distortion equal to the signal variance, we need **zero bits** (the best estimate is just the mean). - As $D \to 0$, $R(D) \to \infty$: Lossless compression of a continuous source requires infinite bits — proving that **lossy compression is mathematically inevitable** for continuous media. ### 2.1.2 For a Uniform Source For a uniform source on $[-a, a]$ with squared-error distortion: $$ R(D) = \begin{cases} \log_2\left(\dfrac{a^2}{D}\right) - \log_2(3) & 0 \leq D \leq a^2/3 \\[2mm] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ --- ## 2.2 The Five Standard Objective Distortion Measures (Detailed) ### 2.2.1 Average Difference (AD) $$ AD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{x}_i) $$ - Measures **bias** (signed error). - Weak metric — large positive and negative errors can cancel. ### 2.2.2 Mean Absolute Difference (MAD) / $L_1$ Norm $$ MAD = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert $$ - Robust to outliers compared to MSE. - Used in motion estimation (block matching). ### 2.2.3 Mean Squared Error (MSE) / $L_2$ Norm — **The Workhorse** $$ MSE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 $$ - **Smooth, differentiable** — excellent for optimization. - Penalizes large errors quadratically. - Units: square of the original signal's units. ### 2.2.4 Normalized Mean Squared Error (NMSE) $$ NMSE = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2} $$ - **Scale-invariant** — useful when comparing signals of different magnitudes. - Range: $[0, \infty)$. ### 2.2.5 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) & Peak SNR (PSNR) $$ SNR = 10 \log_{10} \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2} \;\; \text{dB} $$ $$ PSNR = 20 \log_{10} \frac{MAX_x}{\sqrt{MSE}} \;\; \text{dB} $$ where $MAX_x$ is the peak signal value (e.g., 255 for 8-bit images). ### 2.2.6 Maximum Absolute Distortion $$ D_{\max} = \max_{1 \leq i \leq N} \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert $$ - Worst-case fidelity — critical in medical imaging. ### 2.2.7 Laplacian Mean Squared Error (LMSE) $$ LMSE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla^2 (x_i - \hat{x}_i) $$ Used in transform-domain perceptual coding. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MANDATORY KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE** — memorize every row for the ESE. | # | Distortion Metric | Formula | Unit | Range | Primary Use | |:-:|:------------------|:--------|:----:|:-----:|:------------| | 1 | Average Difference | $AD = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)$ | Same as signal | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Bias detection | | 2 | Mean Absolute Diff | $MAD = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert$ | Same as signal | $[0, \infty)$ | Motion estimation | | 3 | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | Square of signal | $[0, \infty)$ | Universal codec benchmark | | 4 | Normalized MSE | $NMSE = \frac{\sum (x_i-\hat{x}_i)^2}{\sum x_i^2}$ | Dimensionless | $[0, \infty)$ | Cross-signal comparison | | 5 | SNR | $SNR = 10\log_{10}\frac{\sum x_i^2}{\sum (x_i-\hat{x}_i)^2}$ | dB | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | Power-limited systems | | 6 | **PSNR** | $PSNR = 20\log_{10}\frac{MAX_x}{\sqrt{MSE}}$ | **dB** | $[0, \infty)$ | **Image/Video standard** | | 7 | Max Absolute Distortion | $D_{\max} = \max \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert$ | Same as signal | $[0, \infty)$ | Medical imaging | | 8 | Rate-Distortion (Gaussian) | $R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2 / D)$ | bits/sample | $[0, \infty)$ | Theoretical bound | | 9 | Shannon Lower Bound | $H(X) \leq R(D) \leq H(X) + \log_2(\dots)$ | bits/sample | — | Information-theoretic limit | | 10 | Perceptual (SSIM) | $SSIM(x,\hat{x}) = l \cdot c \cdot s$ | Dimensionless | $[-1, 1]$ | Modern perceptual codec | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Image Codecs (JPEG, JPEG 2000, WebP, AVIF):** Use **PSNR** and **SSIM** to evaluate compression efficiency against bit-rate. - **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC, Opus):** Use **Perceptual distortion measures** based on psychoacoustic masking. - **Video Streaming (Netflix, YouTube):** Use **per-title encoding** optimizing an R-D curve parameterized by VMAF (Video Multi-Method Assessment Fusion). - **Medical Imaging (DICOM):** Use **Maximum Absolute Distortion** since diagnosis cannot tolerate any pixel exceeding tolerance. - **Deep Learning Compression (Autoencoders, VAEs):** Use **MSE** or **perceptual loss** combined with KL-divergence as the distortion+rate objective (the famous **ELBO**): $$ \mathcal{L} = \underbrace{D_{KL}(q_\phi(z\vert x) \,\|\, p(z))}_{\text{Rate}} \;+\; \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q}[-\log p_\theta(x\vert z)]}_{\text{Distortion}} $$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Squared Error to PSNR (Board-Exam Style) This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 2 examinations. **Step 1: Start with the definition of MSE** We define the Mean Squared Error for a vector of $N$ samples as the average of the squared differences: $$ MSE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 $$ **Step 2: Recognize the Root Mean Squared Error** To bring the units back to those of the original signal, we take the square root: $$ RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} $$ This is geometrically the **Euclidean ($L_2$) distance** between $x$ and $\hat{x}$ divided by $\sqrt{N}$. **Step 3: Define the Peak Signal Power** For an $n$-bit signal (e.g., 8-bit image), the peak value is: $$ MAX_x = 2^n - 1 $$ For 8-bit, $MAX_x = 255$. For 16-bit, $MAX_x = 65535$. **Step 4: Construct the Ratio** The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** is defined as the ratio of peak signal power to noise power: $$ SNR_{peak} = \frac{MAX_x^2}{MSE} $$ We use $MAX_x^2$ because **power is proportional to amplitude squared**. **Step 5: Apply the Decibel Scale** A ratio expressed in decibels uses $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios. Substituting: $$ PSNR = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{MAX_x^2}{MSE} \right) $$ **Step 6: Algebraic Simplification** Using the identity $\log(a^2) = 2 \log(a)$: $$ \begin{aligned} PSNR &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_x^2}{MSE}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}(MAX_x^2) - 10 \log_{10}(MSE) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}(MAX_x) - 10 \log_{10}(MSE) \end{aligned} $$ Since $MSE = (\sqrt{MSE})^2$: $$ \boxed{\,PSNR = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_x}{\sqrt{MSE}}\right) \;\; \text{dB}\,} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Rate-Distortion Function for a Gaussian Source This derivation links distortion measures directly to Shannon's information theory — a frequent **14-mark** KTU question. **Step 1: Setup** Consider a memoryless Gaussian source $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. We want the minimum rate $R$ such that $\mathbb{E}[(X - \hat{X})^2] \leq D$. **Step 2: Apply Shannon's Lower Bound** The rate-distortion function is bounded below by the **differential entropy** of the source minus the differential entropy of the distortion: $$ R(D) \geq h(X) - h(\text{noise}) $$ **Step 3: Compute the Differential Entropy of a Gaussian** For $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$: $$ h(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2 (2\pi e \sigma^2) \;\; \text{bits/sample} $$ **Step 4: Bound the Distortion Entropy** The noise $N = X - \hat{X}$ is constrained to have variance $\leq D$. The Gaussian maximizes entropy for a given variance, so: $$ h(N) \leq \frac{1}{2} \log_2 (2\pi e D) $$ **Step 5: Combine** $$ \begin{aligned} R(D) &\geq \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e D) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\sigma^2}{D}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Achievability** By reverse-water-filling and quantization theorems (Berger, 1971), this bound is **tight** for the Gaussian source. Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,R(D) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2\!\left(\frac{\sigma^2}{D}\right) \;\; \text{bits/sample, for } 0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2\,} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — PSNR Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (KTU Board-Style):** > > Consider a 4×4 grayscale image block with 8-bit intensity (so $MAX_x = 255$). The original and reconstructed blocks are: > > Original $x$: > > $$\begin{pmatrix} 120 & 200 & 90 & 50 \\ 30 & 80 & 240 & 150 \\ 200 & 100 & 75 & 60 \\ 90 & 110 & 180 & 220 \end{pmatrix}$$ > > Reconstructed $\hat{x}$: > > $$\begin{pmatrix} 122 & 198 & 89 & 52 \\ 31 & 78 & 238 & 151 \\ 199 & 102 & 73 & 61 \\ 88 & 112 & 179 & 218 \end{pmatrix}$$ > > Compute (a) MSE, (b) PSNR. **Step 1: Compute the error matrix $e = x - \hat{x}$** $$ e = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 & 1 & -2 \\ -1 & 2 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & -2 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 2: Compute the squared error matrix $e^2$** $$ e^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 4 & 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 4 & 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 4 & 1 \\ 4 & 4 & 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 3: Sum the squared errors** $$ \sum e_i^2 = (4+4+1+4) + (1+4+4+1) + (1+4+4+1) + (4+4+1+4) = 13 + 10 + 10 + 13 = 46 $$ **Step 4: Compute MSE** With $N = 16$: $$ MSE = \frac{46}{16} = 2.875 $$ **Step 5: Compute PSNR** $$ \begin{aligned} PSNR &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255}{\sqrt{2.875}}\right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255}{1.6956}\right) \\ &= 20 \log_{10}(150.39) \\ &= 20 \times 2.1773 \\ &= 43.55 \;\; \text{dB} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A PSNR of 43.55 dB indicates **excellent** fidelity — visually indistinguishable from the original in most practical cases (threshold for perceptual transparency is typically ~40–50 dB). --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Rate-Distortion > A Gaussian source has variance $\sigma^2 = 16$. Find the minimum bit-rate $R$ required to achieve an average distortion $D = 4$. Using $R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2 / D)$: $$ R(4) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{16}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(4) = \frac{1}{2}(2) = 1 \;\; \text{bit/sample} $$ **Interpretation:** To compress this Gaussian source so that the MSE is at most 4, the theoretical minimum rate is **1 bit/sample**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Lossy Compression Pipeline with Distortion Measurement ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC["ORIGINAL DOMAIN"] A["Original Signal x_n
N samples
8-bit PCM / Image / Audio"] end subgraph CMP["COMPRESSION STAGE"] B["Transform
DCT / DWT / KLT"] C["Quantizer
Q step Delta
Source of ALL distortion"] D["Entropy Coder
Huffman / Arithmetic"] E["Bitstream

Approximation x_hat_n"] H["Inverse Transform"] end subgraph QA["QUALITY ASSESSMENT"] I["Reconstructed x_hat"] J["Distortion Measure d x, x_hat
MSE, PSNR, SSIM"] K["Quality Metric Q dB
Bit-Rate R bps"] L["R-D Operating Point
on Curve R of D"] end A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I A -. "original reference" .-> J I --> J --> K --> L style C fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px style J fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **Quantizer** (highlighted red) is the *only* irreversible block in the lossy pipeline. This is where distortion $D$ is introduced. - The **Distortion Measure** (blue) takes both the original and reconstructed signals and computes the quality. - The **R-D operating point** (green) is the final scorecard. --- ## 4.2 The Rate-Distortion Trade-Off Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RAX[" "] direction LR AX1["Distortion D"] AX2["D_max = sigma squared - zero bits needed"] AX3["D decreases"] AX4["D -> 0 - R approaches infinity"] end P1["Point A

The slope at any operating point gives the *rate of return* in distortion reduction per additional bit. --- ## 4.3 Taxonomy of Distortion Measures ```mermaid flowchart TB DM["Distortion Measures d x, x_hat"] DM --> OBJ["Objective Measures"] DM --> PER["Perceptual Measures"] OBJ --> SAM["Sample Domain"] OBJ --> TRN["Transform Domain"] SAM --> S1["Average Difference AD"] SAM --> S2["Mean Absolute Diff MAD"] SAM --> S3["Mean Squared Error MSE"] SAM --> S4["Normalized MSE NMSE"] SAM --> S5["SNR dB"] SAM --> S6["PSNR dB"] SAM --> S7["Maximum Absolute"] TRN --> T1["Laplacian MSE LMSE"] TRN --> T2["Frequency Weighted MSE"] TRN --> T3["Perceptual Weighting"] PER --> P1["SSIM Structural Similarity"] PER --> P2["MS-SSIM Multi-Scale"] PER --> P3["VMAF Video Multi-Method"] PER --> P4["MOS Mean Opinion Score"] PER --> P5["JND Just Noticeable Difference"] style DM fill:#ffff99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style OBJ fill:#99ccff style PER fill:#ff99cc ``` --- ## 4.4 PSNR vs SSIM — The Modern Trade-off ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Signal x
Ground Truth"] B["Reconstructed x_hat
Approximation"] A --> C["MSE = 1 over N sum x - x_hat squared"] A --> D["Luminance l x, x_hat"] A --> E["Contrast c x, x_hat"] A --> F["Structure s x, x_hat"] B --> C B --> D B --> E B --> F C --> G["PSNR dB
Pure math metric

Perceptual metric
