KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "distortion measure" in the context of lossy compression. Why is it necessary to have a formal distortion measure?** **Model Answer:** A distortion measure $d(x, \hat{x})$ is a mathematical function that quantifies the dissimilarity between the original signal $x$ and its reconstructed version $\hat{x}$ after lossy compression and decompression. It is necessary because: 1. **Quantification** — provides an objective, reproducible metric to evaluate codec performance. 2. **Optimization** — enables mathematical minimization of error during encoder design. 3. **Trade-off formalization** — anchors the Rate-Distortion function $R(D)$. 4. **Standardization** — allows fair comparison between competing algorithms (e.g., JPEG vs. WebP). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between objective and perceptual distortion measures. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Objective Measure | Perceptual Measure | |:-------|:------------------|:-------------------| | Basis | Pure mathematical norm | Human Visual/Auditory System | | Computation | Automated, deterministic | Requires HVS/HAS modeling or user study | | Speed | Very fast (pixel arithmetic) | Slower, more complex | | Example | **PSNR**, MSE, SNR | **SSIM**, MOS, VMAF, JND | **Example of objective:** $\text{PSNR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_x}{\sqrt{MSE}}\right)$. **Example of perceptual:** $\text{SSIM}(x, \hat{x}) = \frac{(2\mu_x\mu_{\hat{x}} + C_1)(2\sigma_{x\hat{x}} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_{\hat{x}}^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_{\hat{x}}^2 + C_2)}$. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) starting from the Mean Squared Error. Explain each step clearly. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Gaussian source has zero mean and variance $\sigma^2 = 64$. Compute the minimum bit-rate required (in bits/sample) if the maximum allowed distortion is $D = 4$. Comment on the significance of the result. #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1: Definition of MSE** $$ MSE = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 $$ This represents the average squared pixel-wise (or sample-wise) error between the original and reconstructed signals. **Step 2: Introduce Peak Signal Power** For an $n$-bit signal, the maximum possible amplitude is $MAX_x = 2^n - 1$. The corresponding peak **signal power** is proportional to $MAX_x^2$. **Step 3: Define Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Power Form)** A power ratio in decibels: $$ SNR = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) $$ **Step 4: Substitute Peak Power and MSE** $$ PSNR = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_x^2}{MSE}\right) $$ **Step 5: Simplify Using Logarithm Identity** $$ \boxed{\,PSNR = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_x}{\sqrt{MSE}}\right) \;\; \text{dB}\,} $$ For an 8-bit image: $MAX_x = 255$, giving a maximum theoretical PSNR of $\approx 48.13$ dB when $MSE = 1$. #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** $\sigma^2 = 64$, $D = 4$. **Step 1: Recall the Rate-Distortion Function for Gaussian Source** $$ R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\sigma^2}{D}\right) $$ **Step 2: Substitute the Values** $$ R(4) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{64}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(16) = \frac{1}{2}(4) = 2 \;\; \text{bits/sample} $$ **Step 3: Comment on Significance** - The theoretical minimum rate is **2 bits/sample**. - Any practical codec (e.g., a Lloyd–Max quantizer) cannot beat this lower bound. - If $D$ were halved to 2, the rate would increase to $\frac{1}{2}\log_2(32) = 2.5$ bits/sample — illustrating the **logarithmic trade-off**: a 2× reduction in distortion costs only 0.5 additional bits/sample, while halving again to $D=1$ costs 3 bits/sample. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Rate-Distortion function $R(D)$ for a Gaussian source. State and prove the Shannon lower bound for $R(D)$. **(b) [7 Marks]** The original image $x = [120, 130, 125, 140]^T$ is reconstructed as $\hat{x} = [118, 132, 122, 142]^T$. Compute the (i) Mean Absolute Difference (MAD), (ii) Mean Squared Error (MSE), and (iii) PSNR for an 8-bit representation. #### Model Solution for (a) The Rate-Distortion function $R(D)$ represents the minimum number of bits per symbol required to encode a source such that the average distortion does not exceed $D$. For a memoryless Gaussian source $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with squared-error distortion: $$ R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\sigma^2}{D}\right) \quad \text{for } 0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2 $$ **Proof of Shannon Lower Bound:** Consider the joint distribution $p(x,\hat{x}) = p(x)\,p(\hat{x}\vert x)$. By the chain rule for mutual information: $$ I(X;\hat{X}) = h(X) - h(X\vert \hat{X}) $$ Since distortion $D \geq \mathbb{E}[(X-\hat{X})^2]$, the conditional differential entropy is bounded by the entropy of a Gaussian with variance $D$: $$ h(X\vert \hat{X}) \leq \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e D) $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} R(D) &\geq h(X) - \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e D) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e D) \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\sigma^2}{D}\right) \end{aligned} $$ The bound is achievable via vector quantization or trellis-coded quantization, completing the proof. #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1: Compute the Error Vector** $$ e = x - \hat{x} = [120-118,\; 130-132,\; 125-122,\; 140-142] = [2,\; -2,\; 3,\; -2] $$ **Step 2: Compute MAD** $$ MAD = \frac{1}{4}(\vert 2\vert + \vert -2\vert + \vert 3\vert + \vert -2\vert) = \frac{1}{4}(2+2+3+2) = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25 $$ **Step 3: Compute MSE** $$ MSE = \frac{1}{4}(2^2 + (-2)^2 + 3^2 + (-2)^2) = \frac{1}{4}(4 + 4 + 9 + 4) = \frac{21}{4} = 5.25 $$ **Step 4: Compute PSNR** For 8-bit image, $MAX_x = 255$: $$ \begin{aligned} PSNR &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255}{\sqrt{5.25}}\right) \\ &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255}{2.2913}\right) \\ &= 20\log_{10}(111.29) \\ &= 20 \times 2.0463 \\ &= 40.93 \;\; \text{dB} \end{aligned} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in PSNR's logarithm:** Many students write $10\log_{10}(MAX_x^2 / MSE)$ but then incorrectly compute $\log_{10}(MAX_x^2/MSE)$ instead of $2\log_{10}(MAX_x) - \log_{10}(MSE)$. Always double-check. > 2. **Using $n$ instead of $2^n - 1$:** For 8-bit, $MAX_x = 255$, not 256 or 8. Marks lost for every instance. > 3. **Confusing SNR and PSNR:** SNR uses actual signal power; PSNR uses peak signal power. Examiners deduct 1 mark. > 4. **Ignoring units:** PSNR is in **dB**. Always write "dB" in the final answer. > 5. **R-D function domain:** Always verify $D \leq \sigma^2$ before plugging into $\frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$. If $D > \sigma^2$, the answer is $R(D) = 0$. > 6. **Forgetting the half in R(D):** $\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$ is **wrong**; the correct formula has a $\frac{1}{2}$ multiplier. > 7. **Per-question marks:** ESE Module 2 Part B expects two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Writing only one combined answer will lose marks for missing structure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Lossy Compression: Distortion Measures** - [x] **Distortion** = mathematical cost of losing information in a lossy codec. - [x] **MSE** = $\frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ — squared-error norm. - [x] **PSNR** = $20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{MAX_x}{\sqrt{MSE}}\right)$ — the industry-standard dB metric for image/video. - [x] **SNR** = $10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sum x_i^2}{\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}\right)$ — actual signal power vs. noise power. - [x] **MAD** = $L_1$ norm, robust to outliers; used in motion estimation. - [x] **NMSE** = scale-invariant MSE — dimensionless ratio. - [x] **Rate-Distortion Function (Gaussian)** = $R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2 / D)$, valid for $0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2$. - [x] **Shannon Lower Bound** = $R(D) \geq h(X) - h(\text{noise}) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$. - [x] **$D_{\max} = \sigma^2$**, **$D_{\min} = 0$** for continuous sources; $D_{\min} > 0$ for finite-alphabet sources. - [x] **PSNR ≥ 40 dB** generally considered perceptually transparent for images. - [x] **Objective measures** (MSE, PSNR, SNR) are simple and fast but ignore HVS. - [x] **Perceptual measures** (SSIM, MS-SSIM, VMAF, MOS) model human perception. - [x] **Quantizer** is the *only* irreversible block — it is the sole source of distortion. - [x] **R-D Curve** is monotonically decreasing and convex. - [x] **Real-world applications** include JPEG (PSNR), Netflix streaming (VMAF), medical imaging (max distortion), and VAEs (MSE + KL). > **End of Notes — Module 2: Distortion Measures**

topics.title: The Rate-Distortion Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Rate-Distortion Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rate-Distortion Theory** is a fundamental mathematical framework in information theory, originally conceived by **Claude Shannon**, that addresses the central problem of **lossy data compression**. It precisely quantifies the fundamental trade-off between two competing quantities:

- The **rate (R)** — measured in **bits per source sample** (or **bits/symbol**), representing the information preserved in the compressed representation.
- The **distortion (D)** — a non-negative real number measuring the cost (loss, error, or fidelity penalty) incurred when the original source is reconstructed from the compressed form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"The Rate-Distortion Function R(D) is defined as the infimum of the mutual information rate I(X; X̂) between the source output X and its reconstructed output X̂, taken over all conditional probability distributions p(x̂ | x) such that the expected distortion E[d(X, X̂)] does not exceed D."*

Formally:

$$R(D) = \min_{p(\hat{x} \mid x) : E[d(X,\hat{X})] \leq D} \frac{1}{n} I(X^n ; \hat{X}^n)$$

Where:
- $X^n = (X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n)$ is the source block of length $n$.
- $\hat{X}^n$ is the reconstructed block.
- $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a per-symbol distortion measure.
- $I(\cdot;\cdot)$ is Shannon's mutual information.

## 1.2 The Source–Coder–Channel–Decoder Model

A lossy compression system consists of an **encoder** $f_E$ and a **decoder** $f_D$ that together act as a mapping from the source alphabet to the reconstruction alphabet. The combined encoder–decoder pair forms a **test channel** whose transition probability $p(\hat{x}\mid x)$ is the central design variable.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Travel Backpack Problem"**
> 
> Imagine you are packing for a vacation with a **tiny backpack (small R)**. You can only carry a *limited amount of clothing* (rate). Naturally, some items get left behind, which causes a *discomfort* (distortion D). 
> 
> - If you allow yourself a **larger backpack (↑ R)**, you carry more clothes, so discomfort goes **down (↓ D)**.
> - If you allow **more discomfort (↑ D)**, you can use a **smaller backpack (↓ R)**.
> 
> The curve that traces the *minimum backpack size* for a *given allowed discomfort* is exactly the **Rate-Distortion Function R(D)**. It is the ultimate packing-efficiency frontier imposed by information theory.

## 1.4 Distortion Measures — The Two Canonical Choices

| Distortion Measure | Formula | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Hamming Distortion** | $d(x,\hat{x}) = 0$ if $x=\hat{x}$, else $1$ | Binary sources, symbol-error counting |
| **Squared-Error Distortion** | $d(x,\hat{x}) = (x - \hat{x})^2$ | Continuous sources, MSE-based codecs |
| **Absolute-Error Distortion** | $d(x,\hat{x}) = \vert x - \hat{x} \vert$ | Robust estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A distortion measure is called a **single-letter (or memoryless) distortion measure** when $d(x^n, \hat{x}^n) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d(x_i, \hat{x}_i)$. Almost all KTU problems use this form.

## 1.5 Why Rate-Distortion Theory Matters in Multimedia

In **multimedia technologies**, every image (JPEG), audio (MP3), and video (H.264, H.265, AV1) codec is an **engineering approximation** of the rate-distortion optimum. The theory gives the **fundamental performance bound** — the lowest bit-rate at which a given fidelity can theoretically be achieved for a given source.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Quantities

### 2.1.1 Information Rate R
The rate is the **average number of bits per source symbol** that must be transmitted or stored:

$$R = \frac{1}{n} H(\hat{X}^n) \;\;\text{or}\;\; R = \frac{1}{n} I(X^n;\hat{X}^n)$$

For a stationary source, the per-letter form is:

$$R = I(X;\hat{X}) \quad \text{(bits/source symbol)}$$

### 2.1.2 Average Distortion D
The expected per-symbol loss between the original and reconstructed signals:

$$D = E\bigl[d(X,\hat{X})\bigr] = \sum_{x}\sum_{\hat{x}} p(x)\,p(\hat{x}\mid x)\,d(x,\hat{x})$$

## 2.2 Operational vs. Information-Theoretic Definitions

- **Operational rate-distortion function** $R^*(D)$: The infimum of rates achievable by *actual* $(2^{nR}, n)$-rate-distortion codes (encoders + decoders) with average distortion $\leq D$.
- **Information-theoretic rate-distortion function** $R(D)$: The infimum of mutual information $I(X;\hat{X})$ over all test channels satisfying the distortion constraint.

> Shannon's **Source Coding Theorem (Lossy Version)** guarantees: $R^*(D) = R(D)$ — i.e., the operational and information-theoretic definitions are *equivalent* in the limit $n \to \infty$.

## 2.3 Deriving the Rate-Distortion Function — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Define the test channel.** Model the encoder–decoder pair as a conditional distribution $p(\hat{x}\mid x)$ from source to reconstruction.

**Step 2 — Set up the constrained optimization.** The goal is to find the channel that retains *maximum relevant information* about $X$ for a given distortion budget $D$.

**Step 3 — Use the calculus of constrained optimization** (Lagrange multipliers). Introduce a parameter $s \geq 0$:

$$J\bigl[p(\hat{x}\mid x)\bigr] = I(X;\hat{X}) + s\,E\bigl[d(X,\hat{X})\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Take the variation.** Using $I(X;\hat{X}) = H(\hat{X}) - H(\hat{X}\mid X)$ and the variational principle, one obtains the **Shannon necessary condition**:

$$p(\hat{x}\mid x) = \frac{p(\hat{x})\,e^{s\,d(x,\hat{x})}}{K(x,s)}$$

where $K(x,s) = \sum_{\hat{x}} p(\hat{x})\,e^{s\,d(x,\hat{x})}$ is a normalization (partition-function-like) term.

**Step 5 — The parameter $s$ acts as the "slope"** of the R(D) curve. The relation $s = -R'(D)$ holds, so $s$ is the *negative derivative* of $R(D)$.

**Step 6 — Solve the resulting self-consistent equations** for the specific source distribution and distortion measure.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rate-Distortion Function** | $R(D) = \min_{p(\hat{x}\mid x)} I(X;\hat{X})$ s.t. $E[d]\le D$ | bits/symbol |
| 2 | **Distortion-Rate Function** | $D(R) = \min_{p(\hat{x}\mid x)} E[d(X,\hat{X})]$ s.t. $I(X;\hat{X})\le R$ | Inverse of R(D) |
| 3 | **Average Distortion** | $D = \sum_{x,\hat{x}} p(x)p(\hat{x}\mid x)\,d(x,\hat{x})$ | Single-letter |
| 4 | **Mutual Information** | $I(X;\hat{X}) = H(X) - H(X\mid\hat{X})$ | Symmetric, non-negative |
| 5 | **Binary Source, Hamming Distortion — Shannon Lower Bound** | $R(D) \ge H_b(D) - H_b(D)$ form ⇒ $R(D) = H_b(p) - H_b(D)$ for $0 \le D \le \min(p,1-p)$ | $H_b$ is binary entropy |
| 6 | **Binary Entropy Function** | $H_b(p) = -p\log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$ | In bits |
| 7 | **Gaussian Source, Squared Error** | $R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\bigl(\sigma^2/D\bigr)$ for $0 \le D \le \sigma^2$ | Optimal test channel is Gaussian |
| 8 | **Maximum Tolerable Distortion** | $D_{\max} = \min_{\hat{x}} E\bigl[d(X,\hat{x})\bigr]$ | Reconstruction that ignores input |
| 9 | **Rate at Zero Distortion** | $R(0) = H(X)$ for **discrete** sources (lossless limit) | Equals source entropy |
| 10 | **Convexity** | $R(D)$ is **convex** and **non-increasing** | Critical property for proofs |
| 11 | **Shannon Necessary Condition** | $p(\hat{x}\mid x) = \dfrac{p(\hat{x})\,e^{s\,d(x,\hat{x})}}{K(x,s)}$ | Optimum test channel |
| 12 | **Lagrangian Slope Relation** | $s = -R'(D)$ | $s \ge 0$ parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Gaussian $R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$ formula is the **theoretical backbone of transform coding** (DCT in JPEG, MDCT in MP3) and **predictive coding**. It tells the codec designer exactly how many bits to allocate per coefficient.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Image / Video Codecs (JPEG, H.264, HEVC, AV1, VVC):** Each codec block (transform, quantizer, entropy coder) is engineered to operate as close as possible to the **R(D) curve** of the residual signal.
- **Streaming & ABR (Adaptive Bit-Rate):** Operators select the working point on the $R(D)$ curve that matches network bandwidth.
- **Perceptual Audio Coding (MP3, AAC, Opus):** Uses **perceptual distortion weights** instead of MSE, leading to a perceptual rate-distortion function $R_p(D)$.
- **Channel Capacity Analogy:** $R(D)$ plays for **sources** the same role that **channel capacity $C$** plays for **channels** — both are fundamental information-theoretic limits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Rate-Distortion Function for a Binary Source with Hamming Distortion

**Setup:** A memoryless binary source with $X \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$, alphabet $\{0,1\}$, and the **Hamming distortion measure**:

$$d(x,\hat{x}) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x = \hat{x} \\ 1 & \text{if } x \neq \hat{x} \end{cases}$$

**Goal:** Find $R(D)$ for $0 \leq D \leq \min(p, 1-p)$.

---

### Step 1 — Express the average distortion
The probability that reconstruction is wrong is exactly $D$ (by definition of Hamming distortion):

$$D = P(X \neq \hat{X}) = p(\hat{X} = 1 \mid X = 0)\,P(X=0) + p(\hat{X} = 0 \mid X = 1)\,P(X=1)$$

Let $D_0 = p(\hat{X} = 1 \mid X = 0)$ and $D_1 = p(\hat{X} = 0 \mid X = 1)$. Then:

$$D = (1-p)\,D_0 + p\,D_1$$

### Step 2 — Compute the joint and marginal distributions
The joint probability:

$$p(X=x,\hat{X}=\hat{x}) = p(x)\,p(\hat{x}\mid x)$$

The output marginal:

$$P(\hat{X}=0) = (1-p)(1-D_0) + p\,D_1$$
$$P(\hat{X}=1) = (1-p)D_0 + p(1-D_1)$$

### Step 3 — Write the mutual information
$$I(X;\hat{X}) = H(\hat{X}) - H(\hat{X}\mid X)$$

Conditional entropy of the reconstruction:

$$H(\hat{X}\mid X) = (1-p)\,H_b(D_0) + p\,H_b(D_1)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Shannon Lower Bound (SLB)
For a binary source with Hamming distortion, the Shannon Lower Bound is:

$$R(D) \geq H(X) - H(D)$$

The lower bound is achieved when the test channel is **symmetric**, i.e., $D_0 = D_1 = D$. This is verified by:

$$\frac{\partial I(X;\hat{X})}{\partial D_0} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial I(X;\hat{X})}{\partial D_1} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; D_0 = D_1 = D$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the SLB
With $D_0 = D_1 = D$ and $H(\hat{X}\mid X) = H_b(D)$, we get:

$$I(X;\hat{X}) = H(\hat{X}) - H_b(D)$$

For the symmetric channel, $H(\hat{X}) = H_b\!\bigl(p(1-D) + (1-p)D\bigr) = H_b(p*D + (1-p)*D) = H_b(D)$ when the crossover probabilities balance. Expanding the binary entropy difference:

$$H(X) - H_b(D) = H_b(p) - H_b(D)$$

### Step 6 — Final result
$$\boxed{\,R(D) = H_b(p) - H_b(D), \quad 0 \leq D \leq \min(p, 1-p)\,}$$

**Sanity checks:**
- At $D = 0$: $R(0) = H_b(p) - 0 = H_b(p) = H(X)$ ✓ (lossless limit)
- At $D = \min(p,1-p)$: $R(D_{\max}) = H_b(p) - H_b(p) = 0$ ✓ (constant reconstruction)
- $R(D)$ is convex and non-increasing ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Rate-Distortion Function for a Gaussian Source with Squared-Error Distortion

**Setup:** $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, MSE distortion $d(x,\hat{x}) = (x-\hat{x})^2$.

**Goal:** Find $R(D)$ for $0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2$.

---

### Step 1 — Write the constrained optimization
$$R(D) = \min_{p(\hat{x}\mid x) : E[(X-\hat{X})^2]\leq D} I(X;\hat{X})$$

### Step 2 — Introduce the Lagrange multiplier
$$\mathcal{L} = I(X;\hat{X}) + s\,E[(X-\hat{X})^2]$$

where $s \geq 0$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Shannon variational condition
For a continuous source, the optimal test channel takes the form:

$$p(\hat{x}\mid x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \beta}}\, \exp\!\left[-\frac{(\hat{x} - \alpha x)^2}{2\beta}\right]$$

with $\alpha, \beta$ to be determined.

### Step 4 — Compute the resulting distortion
Since $E[\hat{X}\mid X=x] = \alpha x$:

$$E[(X-\hat{X})^2 \mid X=x] = (1-\alpha)^2 x^2 + \beta$$

Averaging over $X$:

$$D = (1-\alpha)^2 \sigma^2 + \beta$$

### Step 5 — Compute the resulting rate
The conditional entropy is:

$$H(\hat{X}\mid X) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(2\pi e \beta)$$

The output variance is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{X}) = \alpha^2 \sigma^2 + \beta$$

So:

$$H(\hat{X}) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\bigl(2\pi e(\alpha^2 \sigma^2 + \beta)\bigr)$$

Therefore:

$$I(X;\hat{X}) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\alpha^2 \sigma^2 + \beta}{\beta}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{\alpha^2 \sigma^2}{\beta}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Minimize w.r.t. $\alpha$ and $\beta$ subject to the distortion constraint
Substitute $\beta = D - (1-\alpha)^2 \sigma^2$ and differentiate. The minimum occurs at $\alpha = 1$ (no bias in the test channel) and $\beta = D$. This yields:

$$\boxed{\,R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\sigma^2}{D}\right), \quad 0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2\,}$$

**Sanity checks:**
- $D = \sigma^2$: $R(\sigma^2) = 0$ ✓ (predict $\hat{X}=0$ always)
- $D \to 0^+$: $R(D) \to \infty$ ✓ (Gaussian source has infinite entropy, cannot be losslessly compressed at finite rate)
- $R(D)$ is convex ✓ (log function is concave in $D$, negative of that is convex)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Properties of R(D)

| Property | Statement | Why It Matters for Multimedia |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-negativity** | $R(D) \geq 0$ | Cannot have negative bits |
| **Monotonicity** | $R(D_1) \geq R(D_2)$ if $D_1 \leq D_2$ | Higher quality requires more bits |
| **Convexity** | $R(\theta D_1 + (1-\theta)D_2) \leq \theta R(D_1) + (1-\theta) R(D_2)$ | Time-sharing between two coders is no worse |
| **Continuity** | $R(D)$ is continuous on $[0, D_{\max}]$ | Smooth bit-allocation possible |
| **Inverse Relation** | $R(D)$ and $D(R)$ are strict inverses on the strict-decreasing part | Lets you choose rate *or* distortion |
| **Source Bound** | $R(0) = H(X)$ for discrete sources | Lossless compression limit |
| **$D_{\max}$ Value** | $D_{\max} = \min_{\hat{x}} E[d(X,\hat{x})]$ | Trivial coder reproduction |
| **Gaussian is Worst-Case** | Among all sources with variance $\sigma^2$, Gaussian maximizes $R(D)$ | Reference benchmark |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A memoryless binary source emits $X = 1$ with probability $p = 0.3$ and $X = 0$ with probability $0.7$. Use Hamming distortion. Compute the minimum bit-rate required to encode this source at average distortion $D = 0.1$.

**Solution:**

$$R(D) = H_b(p) - H_b(D)$$

Compute $H_b(0.3)$:

$$H_b(0.3) = -0.3 \log_2 0.3 - 0.7 \log_2 0.7 = 0.5211 + 0.3606 \approx 0.8813 \text{ bits}$$

Compute $H_b(0.1)$:

$$H_b(0.1) = -0.1 \log_2 0.1 - 0.9 \log_2 0.9 = 0.3322 + 0.1375 \approx 0.4690 \text{ bits}$$

Therefore:

$$R(0.1) = 0.8813 - 0.4690 \approx 0.4123 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Exam:** At distortion 0.1, the source can theoretically be compressed to roughly **0.41 bits per symbol** — far below the 1 bit/symbol of a naïve binary representation, and below the source entropy of 0.88 bits/symbol, *because* we have allowed a small amount of error.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Lossy Compression System Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical source–encoder–channel–decoder pipeline on which rate-distortion theory is built.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["SOURCE X"] --> encA["ENCODER fE"]
    encA --> idxA["INDEX M in 1 to 2 to the nR"]
    idxA --> chA["NOISELESS CHANNEL R bits per symbol"]
    chA --> decA["DECODER fD"]
    decA --> recA["RECONSTRUCTION X hat"]
    srcA -.-> dstA["DISTORTION dX X hat"]
    recA -.-> dstA
    recA --> swA["RECONSTRUCTED SOURCE OUTPUT"]
    dstA --> rdcA["RATE DISTORTION FUNCTION R OF D"]
    encA --> rdcA
    decA --> rdcA
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

1. **SOURCE X** — Discrete or continuous source producing symbols $X_1, X_2, X_3, \dots$ with known statistics.
2. **ENCODER fE** — Maps each source block $X^n$ to an index $M \in \{1, 2, \dots, 2^{nR}\}$.
3. **INDEX M** — Compressed representation containing at most $nR$ bits.
4. **NOISELESS CHANNEL** — Carries the index at rate $R$ bits/symbol.
5. **DECODER fD** — Maps the index $M$ back to a reconstruction $\hat{X}^n$.
6. **RECONSTRUCTION X̂** — Approximate version of the original source.
7. **DISTORTION d(X, X̂)** — Per-symbol cost accumulated into average distortion $D$.
8. **R(D) FUNCTION** — The optimal trade-off curve connecting the encoder/decoder choices to the resulting $(R, D)$ operating point.

---

## 4.2 Test-Channel Model of the Encoder–Decoder Pair

This schematic shows how the rate-distortion problem is reduced to designing a **test channel** $p(\hat{x}\mid x)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["X with distribution pX"] --> tchA["TEST CHANNEL p of X hat given X"]
    tchA --> outA["X hat with distribution pX hat"]
    inA --> miA["MUTUAL INFORMATION I of X and X hat"]
    outA --> miA
    inA --> dmA["DISTORTION dX X hat"]
    outA --> dmA
    dmA --> avgA["AVERAGE DISTORTION D"]
    miA --> rdfA["MINIMIZE TO GET R OF D"]
    avgA --> rdfA
    rdfA --> outR["OPTIMAL RATE DISTORTION FUNCTION R OF D"]
```

**Explanation:**
- The mutual information $I(X;\hat{X})$ becomes the **rate** $R$.
- The expected distortion $E[d(X,\hat{X})]$ becomes the **distortion** $D$.
- The minimization over $p(\hat{x}\mid x)$ traces out the **rate-distortion curve**.

---

## 4.3 Information-Theoretic Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["INFORMATION THEORY"]
    rootA --> srcA["SOURCE CODING"]
    rootA --> chA["CHANNEL CODING"]
    srcA --> lossA["LOSSLESS CODING"]
    srcA --> lossyA["LOSSY CODING"]
    lossA --> entA["ENTROPY HX and SHANNON-FANO"]
    lossyA --> rdtA["RATE DISTORTION THEORY R OF D"]
    rdtA --> sdisA["SHANNON LOWER BOUND"]
    rdtA --> gausA["GAUSSIAN R OF D = 0.5 log sigma squared over D"]
    rdtA --> binA["BINARY R OF D = Hb of p minus Hb of D"]
    rdtA --> appA["APPLICATIONS JPEG MP3 H.264 H.265 AV1"]
```

This hierarchy helps situate **rate-distortion theory** within the broader landscape of information theory, distinguishing it from:
- **Lossless source coding** (entropy, Huffman, arithmetic, Lempel–Ziv).
- **Channel coding** (capacity, error-correcting codes).
- **Rate-distortion theory** (lossy source coding — the focus of this module).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Rate-Distortion Function R(D). Explain the significance of $D_{\max}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Rate-Distortion Function** $R(D)$ is defined as the infimum of the mutual information $I(X; \hat{X})$ between the source output $X$ and the reconstruction $\hat{X}$, taken over all conditional distributions $p(\hat{x}\mid x)$ such that the expected distortion $E[d(X,\hat{X})] \leq D$:

$$R(D) = \min_{p(\hat{x}\mid x) : E[d]\leq D} I(X;\hat{X}) \quad \text{bits/symbol}$$

**Significance of $D_{\max}$:**
$D_{\max}$ is the maximum allowable distortion beyond which the rate is zero. It is computed as:

$$D_{\max} = \min_{\hat{x}} E\bigl[d(X,\hat{x})\bigr]$$

It is achieved by a **constant reconstruction** that ignores the source entirely (e.g., always outputting the symbol $\hat{x}^*$ that minimizes the expected distortion). $D_{\max}$ sets the right boundary of the meaningful domain of $R(D)$.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State and briefly justify the Shannon Lower Bound for a binary source with Hamming distortion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a binary memoryless source $X \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$ with Hamming distortion, the **Shannon Lower Bound (SLB)** states:

$$R(D) \geq H_b(p) - H_b(D), \quad 0 \leq D \leq \min(p, 1-p)$$

**Justification:** The bound follows from the chain of inequalities $I(X;\hat{X}) = H(X) - H(X\mid\hat{X}) \geq H(X) - H(X\mid \hat{X}, \text{best estimate})$, where conditioning on $\hat{X}$ reduces uncertainty about $X$ by at most the entropy of a Bernoulli($D$) random variable. The bound is **tight** for the binary Hamming case because the optimal test channel is symmetric, with crossover probability $D$ in both directions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**Question A (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the Rate-Distortion Function $R(D)$ for a memoryless **binary source** with **Hamming distortion**.

**Model Solution:**

We consider $X \in \{0,1\}$ with $P(X=1) = p$, and Hamming distortion $d(x,\hat{x}) = \mathbb{1}_{\{x \neq \hat{x}\}}$.

**Step 1:** Let the test channel have crossover probabilities $D_0 = p(\hat{X}=1\mid X=0)$ and $D_1 = p(\hat{X}=0\mid X=1)$. The average distortion is:

$$D = (1-p)D_0 + p D_1$$

**Step 2:** The conditional entropy of reconstruction:

$$H(\hat{X}\mid X) = (1-p)H_b(D_0) + p H_b(D_1)$$

**Step 3:** Output marginal probabilities:

$$P(\hat{X}=0) = (1-p)(1-D_0) + p D_1, \quad P(\hat{X}=1) = (1-p)D_0 + p(1-D_1)$$

**Step 4:** Hence $H(\hat{X}) = H_b\bigl((1-p)(1-D_0) + p D_1\bigr)$.

**Step 5:** Total mutual information:

$$I(X;\hat{X}) = H_b\bigl((1-p)(1-D_0) + p D_1\bigr) - (1-p)H_b(D_0) - p H_b(D_1)$$

**Step 6:** Minimize over $D_0, D_1$ subject to the distortion constraint. By symmetry, the optimum is $D_0 = D_1 = D$, giving $H(\hat{X}) = H_b(D)$ and $H(\hat{X}\mid X) = H_b(D)$. Thus:

$$\boxed{\,R(D) = H_b(p) - H_b(D)\,}$$

**Step 7 (Verification):**
- $D = 0$: $R(0) = H_b(p)$ ✓
- $D = \min(p,1-p)$: $R = 0$ ✓
- $R(D)$ convex and non-increasing ✓

**(7 marks — full marks awarded for the complete derivation.)**

---

**Question A (b) — 7 Marks:** A binary memoryless source has $p = 0.2$. Compute the minimum rate required at distortion $D = 0.1$ and comment on the result.

**Model Solution:**

Using the formula from part (a):

$$R(D) = H_b(p) - H_b(D) = H_b(0.2) - H_b(0.1)$$

Compute $H_b(0.2)$:

$$H_b(0.2) = -0.2 \log_2 0.2 - 0.8 \log_2 0.8 \approx 0.4644 + 0.2575 = 0.7219 \text{ bits}$$

Compute $H_b(0.1)$:

$$H_b(0.1) = -0.1 \log_2 0.1 - 0.9 \log_2 0.9 \approx 0.3322 + 0.1375 = 0.4690 \text{ bits}$$

Therefore:

$$R(0.1) = 0.7219 - 0.4690 = 0.2529 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Comment:** Even though the source entropy is about 0.72 bits/symbol, allowing 10% distortion reduces the required rate to ~0.25 bits/symbol — a saving of about **65%**. This quantifies the value of lossy compression in multimedia.

**(7 marks — full marks awarded for correct computation and meaningful comment.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**Question B (a) — 7 Marks:** Derive the Rate-Distortion Function for a **Gaussian source** with **mean-squared error** distortion.

**Model Solution:**

Let $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ and $d(x,\hat{x}) = (x-\hat{x})^2$.

**Step 1:** The optimization is:

$$R(D) = \min_{p(\hat{x}\mid x) : E[(X-\hat{X})^2] \leq D} I(X;\hat{X})$$

**Step 2:** The Shannon variational principle gives the optimal test channel as Gaussian:

$$\hat{X} = \alpha X + Z, \quad Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\beta), \quad Z \perp X$$

**Step 3:** Compute the distortion:

$$E[(X-\hat{X})^2] = (1-\alpha)^2\sigma^2 + \beta = D$$

**Step 4:** Compute the rate. Output variance:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{X}) = \alpha^2 \sigma^2 + \beta$$

$$I(X;\hat{X}) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{\alpha^2 \sigma^2}{\beta}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\alpha^2\sigma^2 + \beta}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\beta = D - (1-\alpha)^2 \sigma^2$ and minimize over $\alpha$. The minimum occurs at $\alpha = 1$ (the test channel should be unbiased) and $\beta = D$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\,R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{\sigma^2}{D}\right), \quad 0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2\,}$$

**Step 6 (Sanity checks):**
- $D = \sigma^2 \Rightarrow R = 0$ ✓
- $D \to 0 \Rightarrow R \to \infty$ ✓
- Convex in $D$ ✓

**(7 marks — full marks for the complete derivation.)**

---

**Question B (b) — 7 Marks:** A zero-mean Gaussian source has variance $\sigma^2 = 4$. Find the minimum rate (a) at distortion $D = 1$ and (b) at distortion $D = 0.5$. Comment on the rate increase.

**Model Solution:**

Using $R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$:

**Case (a):** $D = 1$

$$R(1) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{1}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 = 1 \text{ bit/symbol}$$

**Case (b):** $D = 0.5$

$$R(0.5) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{0.5}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(8) = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 = 1.5 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Comment:** Halving the distortion (from 1 to 0.5) costs an extra 0.5 bits/symbol — exactly the well-known **6 dB-per-bit rule** for Gaussian sources: each additional bit per symbol halves the MSE distortion. This rule governs the bit-allocation decisions in DCT-based image and audio codecs.

**(7 marks — full marks for correct computation and interpretation.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Rate-Distortion Questions (KTU Board Style)**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $R(D)$ and $D(R)$:** $R(D)$ is the *minimum rate* for a *given* distortion. $D(R)$ is its inverse — the *minimum distortion* for a *given* rate. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the distortion constraint:** Always state explicitly "subject to $E[d(X,\hat{X})] \leq D$" when writing the optimization. Without it, the answer is incomplete.
> 3. **Wrong base for $\log$:** KTU expects $\log_2$ (bits) unless otherwise specified. Using $\ln$ (nats) is acceptable **only if the unit is explicitly stated**.
> 4. **Missing sanity checks:** Examiners reward students who verify $R(0) = H(X)$, $R(D_{\max}) = 0$, and convexity. Skipping these forfeits partial credit.
> 5. **Confusing $D_0$ and $D_1$:** In the binary derivation, students often write $D$ instead of the two distinct crossover probabilities $D_0, D_1$ and lose marks for skipping Step 1 of the derivation.
> 6. **Forgetting $D_{\max} = \min(p, 1-p)$:** For a binary source, the maximum distortion is **not** 1, but $\min(p, 1-p)$, because we can always output the more likely symbol.
> 7. **Confusing the Shannon Lower Bound with the rate-distortion function:** The SLB is a *lower bound*; for binary Hamming sources it happens to be tight, but in general it is not. Examiners deduct marks for stating "R(D) = H(X) - H(D)" without specifying the binary-Hamming case.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Rate-Distortion Theory** = fundamental lossy-compression limit theory by Claude Shannon.
- **Rate** $R$ = bits/symbol needed; **Distortion** $D$ = average per-symbol error.
- **Definition:** $R(D) = \min I(X;\hat{X})$ subject to $E[d(X,\hat{X})] \leq D$.
- **Properties of $R(D)$:** **non-increasing**, **convex**, **continuous**, $R(0) = H(X)$ (discrete), $R(D_{\max}) = 0$.
- **$D_{\max}$** = distortion when the reconstruction is a constant (best constant choice).
- **Binary + Hamming:** $R(D) = H_b(p) - H_b(D)$, $0 \leq D \leq \min(p,1-p)$.
- **Gaussian + MSE:** $R(D) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2(\sigma^2/D)$, $0 \leq D \leq \sigma^2$.
- **Shannon Lower Bound (SLB):** $R(D) \geq H(X) - H(D)$ — tight for binary Hamming.
- **Shannon necessary condition** for optimum test channel: $p(\hat{x}\mid x) \propto p(\hat{x}) e^{s\,d(x,\hat{x})}$, with $s = -R'(D)$.
- **Channel analogy:** $R(D)$ for sources ↔ $C$ for channels — both are fundamental information-theoretic limits.
- **Operational equivalence:** $R^*(D) = R(D)$ as $n \to \infty$ (Shannon's lossy source-coding theorem).
- **Multimedia link:** All practical codecs (JPEG, MP3, H.264, AV1) approximate the R(D) curve of the underlying source.
- **6 dB-per-bit rule:** For Gaussian sources, each extra bit per symbol halves the MSE distortion.
- **Mutual information direction:** $I(X;\hat{X}) = H(X) - H(X\mid\hat{X})$ — always evaluate correctly before optimizing.
- **Convexity proof technique:** Use *time-sharing* argument: combining two coders with weights $\theta, 1-\theta$ cannot be worse than the convex combination of their rates.
- **Engineering takeaway:** Choose the operating point $(R, D)$ on the $R(D)$ curve to match **bandwidth** (network/storage) and **fidelity** (user perception) requirements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quantization** in multimedia is the process of constraining a continuous or large set of input sample values (e.g., analog audio amplitudes or pixel intensities in the spatial domain) to a relatively small, finite set of discrete output levels. Formally, it is a many-to-one mapping function $Q: \mathbb{R} \to \mathcal{Y}$, where $\mathbb{R}$ is the infinite-resolution input space and $\mathcal{Y} = \{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_L\}$ is the finite alphabet of $L$ reconstruction levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECNT527, Module 2):**
> *Quantization is the lossy compression step in which the precision of representation is reduced by mapping a range of analog sample values to a single digital code. It is the primary source of irreversible data loss in transform coding systems (e.g., JPEG, MPEG, MP3).*

In the context of multimedia compression, quantization is invariably a **lossy** operation. While the sampling theorem (in 1-D signals) and the Nyquist criterion deal with converting continuous-time to discrete-time signals, quantization deals with the conversion of **continuous-amplitude** to **discrete-amplitude** signals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling water in measuring cups. The water level can be at *any* height — 12.378 mL, 5.001 mL, 99.9999 mL. Now, suppose you only have **8 standardized cups** labeled 0 through 7 (representing $2^3 = 8$ levels). 

- Any water level between, say, 0 mL and 12.5 mL is *poured into cup 0* and you remember only "cup 0".
- Any level between 12.5 mL and 25 mL goes into cup 1, and so on.

You have **discarded the precise decimal values** (e.g., 5.001 mL becomes indistinguishable from 4.892 mL). The *error* between the true amount and the rounded amount is the **quantization error**. Fewer cups = more error = higher compression; more cups = less error = lower compression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Quantization needed in Multimedia?**
> In a raw digital image of 1920×1080 pixels with 24 bits per pixel, the file size is ~6.2 MB. After transform coding (DCT), most coefficients are small. **Quantization** zeroes out the perceptually-insignificant coefficients, allowing the entropy coder (Huffman/Arithmetic) to produce a tiny bitstream. Without quantization, multimedia files (MP3, JPEG, MP4) would be 10×–50× larger.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Exams

The following **bold** metrics are universally tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on this topic:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantization Step Size** | $\Delta$ | Volts (V) or intensity units |
| **Number of Quantization Levels** | $L$ | Dimensionless (integer) |
| **Bits per Sample** | $R$ | **bits/sample** |
| **Quantization Error** | $e_q$ | Same as input unit |
| **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio** | SQNR | **Decibels (dB)** |
| **Mean Squared Error** | MSE | Power units (W or V²) |

The fundamental relationship between levels and bits is:
$$L = 2^R \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R = \log_2 L$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Quantizers

Quantizers in multimedia are classified along two independent axes:

### A. By Dimensionality of Input Vector
1. **Scalar Quantization (SQ):** Operates on one sample at a time. $Q(x) = y_i$.
2. **Vector Quantization (VQ):** Operates on a block (vector) of samples simultaneously. $Q(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{y}_i$, where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^k$ and $\mathbf{y}_i$ is chosen from a codebook of size $N$.

### B. By Step-Size Regularity
1. **Uniform (Linear) Quantizer:** All intervals $\Delta_i$ are equal. Mid-rise and mid-tread are the two sub-types.
2. **Non-Uniform Quantizer:** Intervals vary (typically smaller where signal density is high). Examples: **μ-law (µ = 255)** and **A-law (A = 87.6)** companders used in PCM telephony.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mid-Rise vs. Mid-Tread (Frequently asked 3-mark question):**
> - **Mid-Rise:** Output levels are $y_i = \pm \Delta/2, \pm 3\Delta/2, \dots$ (origin is a *decision boundary*, not a level). Always even $L$.
> - **Mid-Tread:** Output levels are $y_i = 0, \pm \Delta, \pm 2\Delta, \dots$ (origin is a *reconstruction level*). Can be odd or even $L$.

## 2.2 The Quantization Process — Structured Logic

For a **uniform mid-tread quantizer** with input range $[-X_{max}, X_{max}]$ and $L$ levels:

1. **Determine step size:** $\Delta = \dfrac{2 X_{max}}{L}$.
2. **Define decision boundaries** $d_i = -X_{max} + i\Delta$, for $i = 0, 1, \dots, L$.
3. **Define reconstruction levels** $y_i = d_i + \Delta/2 = -X_{max} + (i + 0.5)\Delta$.
4. **Apply mapping rule:** $Q(x) = y_i$ if $d_i \le x < d_{i+1}$.
5. **Compute quantization error:** $e_q = x - Q(x)$, bounded by $-\Delta/2 \le e_q < \Delta/2$.

## 2.3 SQNR Derivation Insight (The 'Why' behind 6.02 dB/bit)

For a uniformly distributed input signal in $[-X_{max}, X_{max}]$:

- **Signal Power:** $P_s = \dfrac{X_{max}^2}{3}$ (variance of uniform distribution).
- **Quantization Noise Power:** $P_n = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ (variance of uniform error over $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$).
- **Ratio:** $\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{P_n} = \dfrac{X_{max}^2/3}{\Delta^2/12} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{X_{max}^2}{\Delta^2}$.

Since $\Delta = 2X_{max}/L$ and $L = 2^R$:

$$\text{SQNR} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{X_{max}^2}{4X_{max}^2/L^2} = L^2 = 2^{2R}$$

Converting to decibels:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(2^{2R}) = 20R \log_{10} 2 \approx 6.02 R \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **6.02 dB per bit** rule is a *board-exam favourite*. Each additional bit in a uniform quantizer improves SQNR by approximately **6 dB**, i.e., the noise power is halved.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = 2^R$ | Levels to bits | $R$ is integer bits/sample |
| 2 | $\Delta = \dfrac{2 X_{max}}{L}$ | Uniform step size | Mid-rise or mid-tread symmetric |
| 3 | $-\Delta/2 \le e_q < \Delta/2$ | Bounded error | Assumes no overload |
| 4 | $P_q = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Quantization noise power (uniform $e_q$) | Variance of uniform error |
| 5 | $P_s = \dfrac{X_{max}^2}{3}$ | Signal power (uniform $x$) | Variance of uniform signal |
| 6 | $\text{SQNR} = 2^{2R}$ | Linear SQNR | For uniform signal & uniform quantizer |
| 7 | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02R + C$ | In dB; $C$ depends on signal stats | $C = 0$ for uniform; $C \approx 1.76$ for full-scale sine |
| 8 | $r = \dfrac{R}{k}$ | Vector Quantizer rate | $k$ = vector dimension |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{1}{k} E[\vert \mathbf{x} - Q(\mathbf{x}) \vert^2]$ | VQ distortion | Per-sample MSE |
| 10 | Lloyd–Max Conditions | $\nabla D = 0 \Rightarrow y_i = E[x \mid x \in S_i]$ and $P(x=d_i) = \tfrac{1}{2}[P(x=d_i^-) + P(x=d_i^+)]$ | Necessary for optimality |

> [!TIP]
> **Pitfall to Avoid:** The 6.02 dB/bit rule **assumes uniform input distribution**. For a full-scale sine wave, the constant becomes $\approx 1.76$ dB, giving the popular formula $\text{SQNR} = 6.02R + 1.76$ dB. KTU examiners *love* testing this distinction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **JPEG Image Compression:** Uniform scalar quantization of 8×8 DCT coefficients, with a **luminance quantization table** that perceptually weights frequencies (more quantization at high frequencies the eye cannot resolve).
- **MP3 Audio:** Non-uniform quantization of MDCT coefficients inside a psychoacoustic model.
- **Speech Coding (G.711):** μ-law / A-law companding quantization at 64 kbps for PCM telephony.
- **Lloyd–Max Quantizer:** Optimal non-uniform quantizer for a known PDF; foundational in rate-distortion theory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantization Error Variance for Uniform Mid-Rise Quantizer

**Given:** Input $x$ uniformly distributed in $[-X_{max}, +X_{max}]$, $L$ levels, step size $\Delta = 2X_{max}/L$.

**To Prove:** $P_q = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$.

**Step 1 — Define the quantization error:**
The error for any sample in the $i$-th interval is $e = x - y_i$, where $y_i$ is the midpoint.

**Step 2 — Range of error in a uniform quantizer:**
By construction, $e \in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2)$.

**Step 3 — Probability density function of the error:**
For a fine quantizer (small $\Delta$) and smooth input PDF, the error $e$ is approximately **uniformly distributed** in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$. The PDF is:

$$f_E(e) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta}, \quad -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \le e < \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the variance (noise power):**
$$P_q = E[e^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \cdot f_E(e) \, de$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_q &= \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2 \, de \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \dfrac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \dfrac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \dfrac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} \right) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\Delta^3}{24} + \dfrac{\Delta^3}{24} \right) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta^3}{12} \\
&= \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** $P_q = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This 1/12 factor is the origin of the **1.76 dB** offset in the sine-wave formula: $10\log_{10}(12/2) \approx 1.76$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SQNR for a Full-Scale Sine Wave

**Given:** $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$, quantized with $R$ bits, no overload.

**Step 1 — Signal Power:** $P_s = \dfrac{A^2}{2}$ (mean square of a sinusoid).

**Step 2 — Quantization Noise Power:** $P_q = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12}$ with $\Delta = \dfrac{2A}{2^R}$.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= \dfrac{P_s}{P_q} = \dfrac{A^2/2}{\Delta^2/12} = \dfrac{12 A^2}{2 \Delta^2} = \dfrac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Delta = 2A \cdot 2^{-R}$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= \dfrac{6 A^2}{(2A \cdot 2^{-R})^2} = \dfrac{6 A^2}{4 A^2 \cdot 2^{-2R}} = \dfrac{6}{4} \cdot 2^{2R} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2R}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2R}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{3}{2}\right) + 20R \log_{10} 2 \\
&= 1.7609 + 6.0206 \, R \\
&\approx 1.76 + 6.02 R \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** For a full-scale sine wave, $\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02R + 1.76$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lloyd–Max Necessary Conditions for Optimality

**Problem:** Find reconstruction levels $y_i$ and decision boundaries $d_i$ that minimize the mean-squared error:
$$D = \int (x - Q(x))^2 f_X(x) \, dx = \sum_{i=1}^{L} \int_{d_{i-1}}^{d_i} (x - y_i)^2 f_X(x) \, dx$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate w.r.t. $y_i$ and set to 0 (Centroid Condition):**
$$\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial y_i} = -2 \int_{d_{i-1}}^{d_i} (x - y_i) f_X(x) \, dx = 0$$

Solving:
$$y_i = \dfrac{\int_{d_{i-1}}^{d_i} x \, f_X(x) \, dx}{\int_{d_{i-1}}^{d_i} f_X(x) \, dx} = E[X \mid X \in S_i]$$

**Interpretation:** $y_i$ is the **centroid** (conditional mean) of the input over its cell $S_i$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. $d_i$ and set to 0 (Nearest-Neighbor Condition):**
$$\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial d_i} = (d_i - y_i)^2 f_X(d_i) - (d_i - y_{i+1})^2 f_X(d_i) = 0$$

Equating the two squared distances:
$$(d_i - y_i)^2 = (d_i - y_{i+1})^2 \implies d_i = \dfrac{y_i + y_{i+1}}{2}$$

**Interpretation:** $d_i$ is the **midpoint** between adjacent reconstruction levels (nearest-neighbor rule).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Lloyd–Max is an *iterative* algorithm: alternate between (a) recomputing $y_i$ as centroids of current cells and (b) recomputing $d_i$ as midpoints, until convergence. It yields a *locally optimal* quantizer for a known PDF.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-mark problem)

> **Question:** An audio signal is uniformly distributed in $[-4, +4]$ V and is quantized using a uniform mid-rise quantizer with $L = 16$ levels. Calculate (a) the step size, (b) the quantization noise power, and (c) the SQNR in dB.

**Solution:**

**(a) Step size:**
$$\Delta = \dfrac{2 X_{max}}{L} = \dfrac{2 \times 4}{16} = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

**(b) Quantization noise power:**
$$P_q = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{0.5^2}{12} = \dfrac{0.25}{12} \approx 0.02083 \text{ V}^2$$

**(c) SQNR:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_q}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{max}^2/3}{\Delta^2/12}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4^2/3}{0.02083}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5.333}{0.02083}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(256) = 24.08 \text{ dB}$$

Cross-check using $R = \log_2 16 = 4$: $\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02 \times 4 = 24.08$ dB. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Scalar Quantizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode["Continuous Input x t"]
    sampler["Sampler x n"]
    quantizer{"Quantizer Q of x n"}
    codebook["Codebook of L levels y 1 ... y L"]
    outputNode["Discrete Output y i"]
    error["Quantization Error e q n"]

    inputNode --> sampler
    sampler --> quantizer
    codebook --> quantizer
    quantizer --> outputNode
    sampler -.-> error
    outputNode -.-> error
```

## 4.2 Mid-Rise vs. Mid-Tread Transfer Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Mid Rise Quantizer L is even origin is a boundary"]
        A1["x range negative Xmax to plus Xmax"]
        A2["y jumps from negative Delta by 2 to plus Delta by 2 at origin"]
    end
    subgraph B["Mid Tread Quantizer origin is a level"]
        B1["y has value 0 at x equals 0"]
        B2["y jumps by Delta on either side"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Vector Quantization Encoding-Decoding Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Source Vector x in R power k"]
    encBlock["Encoder Finds nearest codeword index i arg min distance"]
    codebook[("Shared Codebook y 1 ... y N")]
    ch["Channel or Storage index i transmitted"]
    decBlock["Decoder Looks up y i in codebook"]
    out["Reconstructed Vector y hat"]

    src --> encBlock
    codebook --> encBlock
    encBlock --> ch
    ch --> decBlock
    codebook --> decBlock
    decBlock --> out
```

## 4.4 Lloyd–Max Iterative Optimization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Step 1 Initialize decision boundaries d 0 d 1 ... d L"]
    s2["Step 2 Compute centroids y i equals E of x given x in cell i"]
    s3["Step 3 Update decision boundaries d i equals y i plus y i plus 1 divided by 2"]
    s4{"Step 4 Has D dropped below epsilon or max iterations"}
    s5["Step 5 Output optimal codebook and partition"]
    s6["Step 6 Iterate back to Step 2"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4
    s4 -- no --> s6
    s6 --> s2
    s4 -- yes --> s5
```

## 4.5 Rate–Distortion Trade-off Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Raxis["Rate R bits per sample axis"]
    Daxis["Distortion D axis"]
    p1["Point 1 Low R high D heavy quantization"]
    p2["Point 2 High R low D fine quantization"]
    curve["R D curve convex decreasing"]

    Raxis --- p1
    Raxis --- p2
    Daxis --- p1
    Daxis --- p2
    p1 --- curve
    p2 --- curve
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Scalar Quantization and Vector Quantization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Scalar Quantization | Vector Quantization |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Dimensionality** | One sample at a time (1-D) | Block of $k$ samples at a time ($k$-D) |
| **Mapping** | $Q: \mathbb{R} \to \mathcal{Y}$ | $Q: \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathcal{C}$ (codebook) |
| **Rate** | $R$ bits/sample | $r = \log_2 N / k$ bits/sample (often lower) |
| **Optimality** | Lloyd–Max for known PDF | Linde–Buzo–Gray (LBG) algorithm |
| **Complexity** | Low (table lookup) | High (nearest-neighbor search over codebook) |
| **Performance** | Sub-optimal at low rates | Always $\ge$ Scalar for same rate (Shannon's bound) |

*(1 mark for each of: definition, rate expression, complexity/performance note.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Companding" and explain μ-law companding with its significance in PCM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Companding = **COM**pressing + ex**PAND**ing. It is the process of non-uniformly quantizing a signal by first passing it through a *compressor* (which expands small amplitudes and compresses large ones), quantizing, transmitting, and then applying the inverse *expander* at the receiver.
- **μ-law formula (1 mark):** $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert / X_{max})}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$, with $\mu = 255$ for North American/Japanese PCM.
- **Significance (1 mark):** It improves SQNR for speech-like signals (which have a Laplacian PDF) without increasing bit-rate. Standardized in ITU-T **G.711** at 64 kbps for digital telephony.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **uniform scalar quantizer**. Derive the expression for **quantization noise power** and **SQNR** in dB for a uniformly distributed input signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Lloyd–Max quantizer** is to be designed for a Gaussian input with zero mean and variance $\sigma^2 = 4$, using $L = 4$ levels. Outline the **Lloyd–Max algorithm** and state the two necessary optimality conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Show sampler → quantizer (with decision logic + codebook) → output, plus error $e_q = x - Q(x)$.

**Derivation of $P_q = \Delta^2/12$ (3 marks):** Follow the steps in Section 3.1 above.

**Derivation of SQNR (3 marks):** For uniform input in $[-X_{max}, X_{max}]$:
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{X_{max}^2/3}{\Delta^2/12}\right) = 6.02 R \text{ dB}$$

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Lloyd–Max Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**
1. Initialize $L$ decision boundaries $d_0 < d_1 < \dots < d_L$.
2. Set $y_i = E[X \mid d_{i-1} \le X < d_i]$ (centroid condition).
3. Set $d_i = (y_i + y_{i+1}) / 2$ (nearest-neighbor condition).
4. Repeat 2–3 until convergence.

**Two Necessary Conditions (3 marks):**
- **Centroid Condition:** $y_i^* = E[X \mid X \in S_i]$, where $S_i = [d_{i-1}, d_i)$.
- **Nearest-Neighbor Condition:** $d_i^* = (y_i + y_{i+1}) / 2$.

For the given case ($L=4$, $\sigma^2=4 \Rightarrow \sigma=2$), symmetric Gaussian → codebook is $\{-1.51, -0.45, +0.45, +1.51\}$ (numerical values from Lloyd–Max tables).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare **Uniform** and **Non-Uniform Quantization**. Explain **μ-law and A-law companding** with their transfer characteristics. When is each preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **full-scale sinusoidal** signal $x(t) = 5 \sin(1000\pi t)$ V is quantized using an 8-bit uniform mid-rise quantizer with full-scale range $\pm 5$ V. Calculate (i) step size, (ii) quantization noise power, and (iii) SQNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Uniform | Non-Uniform |
|---|---|---|
| Step size | Constant $\Delta$ | Variable (finer near zero) |
| Best for | Uniform-distributed input | Speech/audio (Laplacian PDF) |
| Complexity | Low | Higher (lookup or log/exp) |

**μ-law (2 marks):** $F_\mu(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x\vert/X_{max})}{\ln(1+\mu)}$, $\mu = 255$. Compresses dynamic range; improves SNR for low-amplitude speech by ~24 dB.

**A-law (2 marks):** $F_A(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \dfrac{A \vert x \vert / X_{max}}{1 + \ln A}$ for small $x$, logarithmic for large $x$. $A = 87.6$ (European PCM).

**Preference (1 mark):** μ-law → North America/Japan, A-law → Europe. Both give ~13 bits of equivalent uniform resolution at 8 bits.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Step size (2 marks):** $\Delta = 2X_{max}/2^R = 10/256 = 0.03906$ V.

**(ii) Noise power (2 marks):** $P_q = \Delta^2/12 = (0.03906)^2/12 \approx 1.27 \times 10^{-4}$ V².

**(iii) SQNR (3 marks):** For full-scale sine, $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02R + 1.76 = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 49.92$ dB. $\approx \mathbf{49.9 \text{ dB}}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Quantization Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing 6.02 dB/bit with 6 dB/bit:** Examiners deduct 0.5 marks if you write "$\approx 6R$" without the 0.02. Always show $20 \log_{10} 2 \approx 6.02$.
> 2. **Wrong constant for sine wave:** For *uniform* input, $C=0$. For *full-scale sine*, $C=1.76$. Many students write 1.76 for uniform input — this is **wrong** and loses 1 full mark.
> 3. **Step-size formula sign error:** $\Delta = 2X_{max}/L$ requires the *peak-to-peak* range, not the peak. Students often write $\Delta = X_{max}/L$.
> 4. **Mid-rise vs. Mid-tread confusion:** In mid-rise, $L$ must be even and the origin is a *boundary*. The number of *positive* levels is $L/2$, not $(L-1)/2$.
> 5. **VQ rate mis-stated:** Vector Quantizer rate is $r = \log_2 N / k$ bits/sample. Many students forget to divide by vector length $k$.
> 6. **Lloyd–Max is *not* globally optimal:** It guarantees only a *local* minimum. The KTU answer key specifically expects the word "local" in the definition.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Quantization (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Quantization** = mapping infinite-resolution input to finite discrete levels; it is the **primary lossy step** in multimedia codecs.
- ✅ **Levels vs. bits:** $L = 2^R$; **step size:** $\Delta = 2X_{max}/L$.
- ✅ **Error bound:** $-\Delta/2 \le e_q < \Delta/2$ (uniform quantizer, no overload).
- ✅ **Noise power (uniform quantizer, fine-grain assumption):** $P_q = \Delta^2/12$.
- ✅ **SQNR (uniform input):** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02R$ dB.
- ✅ **SQNR (full-scale sine):** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02R + 1.76$ dB. **Remember the +1.76!**
- ✅ **Scalar vs. Vector Quantization:** Scalar = 1 sample; Vector = block of $k$ samples. VQ always $\ge$ SQ at the same bit-rate.
- ✅ **Uniform vs. Non-Uniform:** Use uniform for uniform-distributed input; use companded (μ-law / A-law) for speech/audio.
- ✅ **μ-law (μ = 255):** North American/Japanese PCM (ITU-T G.711).
- ✅ **A-law (A = 87.6):** European PCM.
- ✅ **Lloyd–Max Algorithm:** Iterative — alternate centroid (1) and nearest-neighbor (2) updates. Yields *local* minimum MSE.
- ✅ **Centroid condition:** $y_i^* = E[X \mid X \in S_i]$.
- ✅ **Nearest-neighbor condition:** $d_i^* = (y_i + y_{i+1})/2$.
- ✅ **Mid-rise:** origin is a boundary, $L$ even. **Mid-tread:** origin is a level.
- ✅ **JPEG use case:** Uniform scalar quantization of 8×8 DCT coefficients with a perceptually-weighted quantization table.
- ✅ **MP3 use case:** Non-uniform quantization of MDCT coefficients guided by a psychoacoustic model.
- ✅ **Rate–Distortion trade-off:** Increasing $R$ decreases $D$ along a convex, monotonically-decreasing $R(D)$ curve.
- ✅ **Six dB rule of thumb:** Every extra bit halves the quantization noise power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transform Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transform Coding # Transform Coding — Core Foundation ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Multimedia Technologies (PECNT527)**, **Transform Coding** is defined as a class of lossy data compression techniques in which a mathematical transform is applied to a block of source samples (pixels in an image, samples in audio, or blocks of frames in video) to convert them from a *spatial/temporal domain* representation into a *frequency/spectral domain* representation. The transformed coefficients are then independently quantized and entropy-coded for storage or transmission. The underlying principle is the **Energy Compaction Property** — most natural signals (images, audio, speech) have their information energy concentrated in a small number of low-frequency coefficients, while the high-frequency coefficients carry negligible perceptual information and can be coarsely quantized or discarded. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transform coding is a *core foundation topic* that underpins the JPEG image standard (DCT-based), MPEG/H.26x video standards, and MP3 audio. A clear understanding of the transform coder block diagram, the role of quantization, and the energy compaction property is mandatory for Part A and Part B questions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you have a noisy classroom of 60 students (the input data) and the teacher (the encoder) wants to write a *summary* of what was discussed in a notebook (the compressed file). Instead of trying to write down every word each student said, the teacher instead identifies: - **Low-frequency content** → The common, dominant themes repeated by almost everyone (e.g., "the exam is on Friday"). This is written down carefully in full detail. - **High-frequency content** → The random whispers, coughs, side-conversations between individual students. These are noted only briefly or ignored entirely because they don't contribute meaningfully to the overall message. The act of *listening, sorting, and prioritizing* is the **transform**. The act of *writing only the important stuff in detail* is the **quantization**. The act of *abbreviating the summary using common shorthand* is the **entropy coding**. When the student later reads the summary, they reconstruct (approximately) the original message — this is **decoding**. ## 1.3 Significance in Multimedia Engineering Transform coding is the *de-facto standard* paradigm for lossy compression across virtually all consumer multimedia: | Multimedia Domain | Transform Used | Standard | |---|---|---| | Still Images | 2-D DCT (8×8 blocks) | JPEG / JPEG 2000 (DWT) | | Video Coding | 2-D DCT (4×4 / 8×8 blocks) | MPEG-1/2/4, H.261/263, H.264/AVC, HEVC | | Audio Coding | MDCT (Modified DCT) | MP3, AAC, Ogg Vorbis | | Speech Coding | DCT / Wavelet | Various codecs | | Medical Imaging | DWT (Wavelets) | DICOM JPEG 2000 | > [!NOTE] > The key engineering insight: **transforms don't compress data by themselves.** They only *rearrange* the data so that subsequent quantization and entropy coding stages can compress it efficiently. The transform is a *preparation step*, not a compression step. --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Transform Coder A transform coding system is fundamentally a **symmetric pair** — an encoder and a decoder. The general block diagram consists of the following stages: ### Encoder Pipeline 1. **Source Input** — Original signal samples (e.g., 8×8 pixel block from an image). 2. **Decomposition into Blocks** — The image is partitioned into fixed-size blocks (commonly N = 8 for JPEG), each treated independently. 3. **Forward Transform** — A unitary linear transform **T** is applied: $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{T} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{T}^T$. This produces transform coefficients. 4. **Quantization** — Each coefficient $Y_k$ is mapped to a discrete level using a quantizer step size $\Delta_k$. This is the **only lossy stage**. 5. **Symbol Encoding / Entropy Coding** — The quantized symbols are converted to a bitstream using Huffman coding, Arithmetic coding, or run-length encoding. ### Decoder Pipeline (Inverse Operation) 1. **Entropy Decoding** — Recovers the quantized symbols from the bitstream. 2. **De-quantization** — Approximate inverse of quantization (lossy stage — only approximate recovery). 3. **Inverse Transform** — $\mathbf{\hat{X}} = \mathbf{T}^{-1} \mathbf{Y}_q \mathbf{T}^{-T}$ to reconstruct the spatial-domain samples. 4. **Reconstruction** — The blocks are re-assembled into the final image/signal. ## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation Let $\mathbf{X}$ be an $N \times N$ block of input samples. The **forward 2-D unitary transform** is: $$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{T} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{T}^T$$ The **inverse transform** reconstructs: $$\mathbf{\hat{X}} = \mathbf{T}^{-1} \mathbf{\hat{Y}} \mathbf{T}^{-T}$$ For unitary transforms, $\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \mathbf{T}^T$, so: $$\mathbf{\hat{X}} = \mathbf{T}^T \mathbf{\hat{Y}} \mathbf{T}$$ The reconstructed block differs from the original by a reconstruction error: $$\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{\hat{X}}$$ The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** per sample is the distortion metric: $$D = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} |X_{ij} - \hat{X}_{ij}|^2$$ ## 2.3 The Transform Selection Criteria The choice of transform is governed by four key properties: - **Energy Compaction:** Concentrate the signal energy in as few coefficients as possible. - **Decorrelation:** Make the resulting transform coefficients statistically independent (zero correlation). - **Computational Efficiency:** Fast algorithms (like the Fast DCT) must exist. - **Invertibility:** Perfect (lossless) reconstruction must be possible mathematically; quantization is the only lossy step. ## 2.4 Comparison of Common Transforms | Transform | Energy Compaction | Decorrelation | Complexity | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | **KLT (Karhunen-Loève)** | **Optimal (Theoretical best)** | **Perfect** | Very High (data-dependent basis) | Theoretical benchmark | | **DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform)** | Near-Optimal | Excellent | Low (fast algorithms exist) | JPEG, MPEG, H.26x | | **DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform)** | Poor for real signals | Moderate | Low | Audio (in modified form) | | **DWT (Discrete Wavelet Transform)** | Very Good (multi-resolution) | Good | Moderate | JPEG 2000, modern codecs | | **Walsh-Hadamard** | Poor | Poor | Very Low | Educational/early codecs | > [!NOTE] > **Why DCT is the practical winner:** The DCT has a basis very close to the KLT (the optimal transform) for typical natural images that exhibit high inter-pixel correlation. Critically, unlike the KLT, the DCT basis is *data-independent* and has $O(N \log N)$ fast algorithms — making it the engineering compromise between optimality and practicality. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Description / Unit | |---|---|---| | Forward 2-D Transform | $Y = T X T^T$ | Maps N×N spatial block to N×N transform block | | Inverse Transform | $\hat{X} = T^T \hat{Y} T$ | Reconstructs spatial block from quantized coeffs | | 1-D DCT (Type II) | $C(u) = \alpha(u) \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} f(x) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2N}\right]$ | $u = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ | | 2-D DCT (separable) | $F(u,v) = \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_{x}\sum_{y} f(x,y) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2N}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2y+1)v}{2N}\right]$ | Applied to N×N blocks | | Normalization factor | $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}}$ for $k=0$; $\alpha(k) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}$ for $k \neq 0$ | Ensures orthonormality | | DC Coefficient | $C(0,0)$ | Represents the block's average value | | AC Coefficients | $C(u,v)$ with $u+v > 0$ | Represent spatial frequencies | | Quantization | $Q(u,v) = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{F(u,v)}{Z(u,v)}\right]$ | $Z(u,v)$ is the quantizer matrix step | | Mean Squared Error | $D = \frac{1}{N^2}\sum\sum \vert X_{ij} - \hat{X}_{ij}\vert^2$ | Distortion metric | | Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{\text{Original Size (bits)}}{\text{Compressed Size (bits)}}$ | Unitless | | Energy Packing Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \sigma_k^2}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \sigma_k^2}$ for $M \ll N$ | Fraction of energy in $M$ largest coefficients | | Bit Rate | $R = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} b_k$ (bits per sample) | Total bits allocated per block | | Rate-Distortion Function | $R(D) = \sum_k \max\!\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\log_2 \frac{\sigma_k^2}{D \lambda}\right)$ | Optimal bit allocation per coefficient | ## 2.6 Rate-Distortion Theory and Bit Allocation A central engineering problem in transform coding is: **given a target bit budget $R$ or a target distortion $D$, how should we allocate bits among the transform coefficients?** The optimal solution (assuming Gaussian-distributed coefficients with variances $\sigma_k^2$) is the **water-filling principle** of bit allocation: $$b_k = \max\!\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\log_2 \frac{\sigma_k^2}{\lambda}\right)$$ where $\lambda$ is a Lagrange multiplier chosen to satisfy the total rate constraint $\sum_k b_k \leq R$. The intuition: coefficients with high variance get more bits; low-variance (high-frequency) coefficients may get *zero* bits and be discarded entirely. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **JPEG Image Compression:** DCT on 8×8 blocks → reduces typical 24-bit RGB image to 1–2 bits/pixel with visually imperceptible loss. - **Video Streaming (Netflix, YouTube):** HEVC/H.264 use hybrid transform coding (DCT + motion compensation) — the dominant data compression paradigm globally. - **Medical Imaging (DICOM):** Wavelet-based transform coding (JPEG 2000) for lossless archival of MRI/CT scans. - **Audio Streaming (Spotify, Apple Music):** AAC/MP3 use MDCT (Modified DCT) on overlapping audio frames. - **Satellite Imaging:** Transform coding enables transmission of multi-spectral earth observation data over bandwidth-limited downlinks. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: 1-D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT-II) The 1-D DCT of a sequence $f(x)$ of length $N$ is defined as: $$C(u) = \alpha(u) \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} f(x) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]$$ The inverse 1-D DCT (IDCT) is: $$f(x) = \sum_{u=0}^{N-1} \alpha(u) C(u) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]$$ with normalization: $$\alpha(u) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}}, & u = 0 \\ \sqrt{\frac{2}{N}}, & u = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1 \end{cases}$$ **Step 1 — Express the basis functions.** The DCT expands the signal as a sum of cosine basis functions. The $u$-th coefficient $C(u)$ measures the *projection* of the signal onto the cosine wave of frequency $u$. **Step 2 — Verify orthogonality.** The basis functions are: $$\phi_u(x) = \alpha(u) \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]$$ For the transform to be invertible, the basis must be orthogonal. We need: $$\sum_{x=0}^{N-1} \phi_u(x)\,\phi_v(x) = \delta_{uv}$$ This can be verified using the orthogonality identity: $$\sum_{x=0}^{N-1} \cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{2N}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)v\pi}{2N}\right] = \begin{cases} N, & u = v = 0 \\ \frac{N}{2}, & u = v \neq 0 \\ 0, & u \neq v \end{cases}$$ The normalization factor $\alpha(u)$ is precisely chosen to convert these into orthonormal basis vectors. **Step 3 — Express 2-D DCT as separable operation.** Since the 2-D DCT is *separable*, it can be applied as two successive 1-D DCTs: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Row DCT:}\quad G(x,v) &= \alpha(v) \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2y+1)v\pi}{2N}\right] \\[6pt] \text{Column DCT:}\quad F(u,v) &= \alpha(u) \sum_{x=0}^{N-1} G(x,v)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2x+1)u\pi}{2N}\right] \end{aligned}$$ This separability reduces the computational complexity from $O(N^4)$ to $O(N^3)$ for an $N \times N$ block — a critical engineering win for real-time codecs. ## 3.2 Derivation: The 8×8 DCT Example (Worked Numerical) To solidify understanding, let us compute the 2-D DCT of a simple $4 \times 4$ block: $$X = \begin{bmatrix} 52 & 55 & 61 & 66 \\ 63 & 59 & 55 & 90 \\ 90 & 78 & 64 & 65 \\ 80 & 76 & 71 & 83 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 1 — Compute the 1-D DCT matrix $T$.** For $N = 4$: $$T_{ij} = \alpha(i)\cos\!\left[\frac{(2j+1)i\pi}{8}\right]$$ Using $\alpha(0) = 1/2$ and $\alpha(i) = \sqrt{1/2}$ for $i > 0$: $$T \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.500 & 0.500 & 0.500 & 0.500 \\ 0.653 & 0.271 & -0.271 & -0.653 \\ 0.500 & -0.500 & -0.500 & 0.500 \\ 0.271 & -0.653 & 0.653 & -0.271 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $Y = T X$ (row transform).** Multiplying: $$T X = \begin{bmatrix} 145.5 & 134.0 & 125.5 & 152.0 \\ -14.0 & 1.0 & 13.0 & -23.0 \\ 16.0 & -3.0 & 7.0 & -10.0 \\ -10.0 & 14.0 & 7.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $Y = (TX) T^T$ (column transform).** Multiplying again: $$Y = \begin{bmatrix} 278.5 & -5.0 & 14.5 & -8.0 \\ -28.0 & 7.5 & -14.0 & 4.0 \\ 17.5 & -3.0 & 6.0 & -2.0 \\ -7.0 & 3.5 & 2.0 & -1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** Notice the **DC component** $Y(0,0) = 278.5$ dominates — it is the average intensity of the block times $N$. The other coefficients (especially the bottom-right corner) are tiny. This is the energy compaction property in action: most of the block's information is captured in just a handful of coefficients. **Step 5 — Quantization step.** Divide each coefficient by a step size (JPEG's standard luminance table scaled appropriately) and round: $$Q = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{Y}{Z}\right]$$ Many of the smaller coefficients will round to 0, which leads to massive compression during run-length encoding of the resulting sparse matrix. ## 3.3 Derivation: Quantization Error and Distortion Suppose a coefficient $Y$ has uniform quantization with step size $\Delta$. The quantizer $q(\cdot)$ maps $Y$ to the nearest multiple of $\Delta$: $$\hat{Y} = q(Y) = \Delta \cdot \text{round}\!\left[\frac{Y}{\Delta}\right]$$ The quantization error is $e = Y - \hat{Y}$, bounded by $|e| \leq \Delta/2$. The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** contribution from this coefficient, assuming uniform distribution of $Y$ over a quantization interval, is: $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_e^2 &= E[(Y - \hat{Y})^2] \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2\,de \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{e^3}{3}\bigg|_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2(\Delta/2)^3}{3} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\Delta^2}{12} \end{aligned}$$ This is the famous **$E[e^2] = \Delta^2 / 12$** result for uniform quantizers — the key relationship linking quantization step size to distortion. **Total block distortion** (assuming independent quantization errors): $$D_{\text{block}} = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{u=0}^{N-1} \sum_{v=0}^{N-1} \frac{\Delta_{uv}^2}{12}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Compaction Quantified The **Energy Packing Efficiency (EPE)** of a transform measures the fraction of total signal energy captured by the top $M$ coefficients out of $N$ total: $$\eta(M) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \sigma_k^2}{\sum_{k=1}^{N} \sigma_k^2}$$ For a Markov-1 image model with correlation coefficient $\rho$, the asymptotic EPE of the DCT (as $N \to \infty$) approaches the *ideal KLT* limit: $$\eta_{\text{ideal}}(M) \to 1 - \rho^{2M}$$ For $\rho = 0.95$ and $M = N/4$, the DCT retains over **99%** of the signal energy — explaining why low-pass quantization (zeroing high frequencies) is so effective. ## 3.5 Worked Example: Compression Ratio Calculation Consider a 512 × 512 grayscale image (8 bits/pixel) compressed using 8×8 JPEG with an average of 1.5 bits per DCT coefficient. **Step 1 — Original size:** $$\text{Size}_{\text{original}} = 512 \times 512 \times 8 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2 — Compressed size (using block statistics):** $$\text{Number of blocks} = \left(\frac{512}{8}\right)^2 = 4096$$ $$\text{Bits per block} = 64 \times 1.5 = 96 \text{ bits}$$ $$\text{Size}_{\text{compressed}} = 4096 \times 96 = 393{,}216 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3 — Compression ratio:** $$CR = \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{393{,}216} \approx 5.33 : 1$$ This is typical of medium-quality JPEG — visually near-lossless at this ratio. --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Transform Coding System ```mermaid flowchart LR src["SOURCE INPUT

Define Transform Coding. What is the role of a unitary transform in it? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Transform coding is a lossy compression technique that converts source samples from the spatial/temporal domain to a frequency/spectral domain using a mathematical transform, quantizes the resulting coefficients, and entropy-codes them for transmission. A **unitary transform** plays three critical roles: 1. **Decorrelation:** It maps correlated input samples into approximately uncorrelated transform coefficients. 2. **Energy Compaction:** It concentrates the signal's information energy into a small subset of low-frequency coefficients. 3. **Perfect Invertibility:** Because $\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \mathbf{T}^T$ for unitary matrices, the transform is mathematically lossless — quantization becomes the only source of error. *(3 marks — Definition 1, Role 1, Key property 1)* ### Q2. State and explain the Energy Compaction Property of DCT. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **Energy Compaction Property** states that the Discrete Cosine Transform concentrates the majority of the information energy of a highly correlated natural signal (image, audio) into a small number of low-frequency coefficients near the DC (top-left) corner of the transform block. High-frequency coefficients in the bottom-right corner typically carry very little energy and can be coarsely quantized or discarded with minimal perceptual impact. For a typical natural image with correlation coefficient $\rho \geq 0.9$, the top 10% of DCT coefficients retain over **90% of the total energy** — which is the mathematical basis for the high compression ratios of JPEG. *(3 marks — Statement 1, Explanation 1, Quantitative claim 1)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a transform coding system. Discuss the function of each block in detail. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The transform coding system consists of an **encoder** and a **decoder** separated by a transmission channel. **Encoder Blocks:** 1. **Source Input (X):** An $N \times N$ block of source samples is taken from the image/audio. For JPEG, $N = 8$. 2. **Decomposition into Blocks:** The full image is partitioned into non-overlapping blocks. This allows localized processing and reduces computational/memory requirements. 3. **Forward Transform (T):** The matrix transform $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{T} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{T}^T$ converts spatial-domain samples to frequency-domain coefficients.
