Because of the limited entropy, they are amenable to specialized lossless compression standards such as the CCITT/ITU-T Group 3, Group 4, and the JBIG (Joint Bi-level Image Experts Group) family of standards. ## 1.2 Mathematical Representation A bi-level image of size $M \times N$ is a 2D binary matrix: $$I(x, y) \in \{0, 1\}, \quad \text{for } 0 \le x < M,\ 0 \le y < N$$ The total number of bits in the uncompressed image is: $$B_{\text{raw}} = M \times N \times 1 \text{ bit}$$ The **compression ratio (CR)** is defined as: $$\text{CR} = \frac{B_{\text{raw}}}{B_{\text{compressed}}}$$ The **bits per pixel (bpp)** metric is: $$\text{bpp} = \frac{B_{\text{compressed}}}{M \times N}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why compress bi-level images?** A single A4 page scanned at 300 DPI produces a raw image of $2550 \times 3300 = 8.4$ million pixels, requiring $\approx 1.05$ MB. Compressed with JBIG2, the same page can be reduced to $\approx 30$–$80$ KB — a compression ratio exceeding **15:1** with NO loss of information. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Morse Code of Pictures" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are reading a page of printed text. Your eye doesn't store every dot of ink; it recognizes the *shape* of each letter as a familiar pattern. **Bi-level image compression works the same way.** Instead of transmitting every black/white pixel, modern standards (like JBIG2) detect recurring letter-shapes, store them ONCE in a dictionary, and then transmit just a short code saying *"use dictionary entry #7 at position (x, y)."* Older standards (Group 3/4) instead exploit the fact that long horizontal runs of white or black are extremely common — like saying *"30 white pixels, then 5 black pixels"* instead of repeating 35 times. ## 1.4 Evolution of Bi-level Compression Standards | Era | Standard | Organization | Compression Type | Typical CR | |-----|----------|--------------|------------------|------------| | **1980** | T.4 (Group 3, MH) | CCITT/ITU-T | 1D Run-length | 4:1 – 10:1 | | **1984** | T.4 (Group 3, MR) | CCITT/ITU-T | 2D Hybrid | 6:1 – 12:1 | | **1988** | T.6 (Group 4, MMR) | CCITT/ITU-T | 2D Lossless | 10:1 – 20:1 | | **1993** | T.82 (JBIG1) | ISO/IEC / ITU-T | Adaptive arithmetic | 20:1 – 50:1 | | **2000** | T.88 (JBIG2) | ISO/IEC / ITU-T | Pattern + arithmetic | 20:1 – 100:1 | ## 1.5 Why Not Just Use JPEG or PNG? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** JPEG is designed for *continuous-tone* (photographic) images where each pixel has 8 or more bits per channel. Forcing a bi-level image through JPEG introduces **lossy artifacts** and produces inefficient code. Specialized bi-level standards exploit the *statistical redundancy of long runs* and the *pattern repetition* of text/scanned documents — achieving far higher compression with strict losslessness. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Compression Strategies Bi-level image compression standards rely on **three fundamental redundancy removal techniques**: ### 2.1.1 Run-Length Encoding (RLE) — The Foundation RLE exploits **statistical redundancy**: long sequences of identical pixels (runs) are coded as a (run-length, value) pair. In a typical scanned text page, runs of 10–50 white pixels are extremely common. ### 2.1.2 Huffman/Arithmetic Coding — The Entropy Coder Once the image is transformed into symbols (run-lengths or context-coded bits), these symbols are passed through a **variable-length entropy coder**. Huffman coding uses a pre-defined codebook; **arithmetic coding** encodes an entire message as a single fraction in $[0, 1)$. ### 2.1.3 Pattern Matching / Dictionary Coding Used in **JBIG2** and conceptually in **MMR**. Recurring shapes (e.g., the letter "e" appearing 50 times) are stored once in a dictionary; subsequent occurrences are coded by reference. ## 2.2 Group 3 (T.4) — Modified Huffman (MH) Coding The simplest standard. Each scan line is coded **independently (1-D)**. **Procedure:** 1. Each line alternates between **white runs** and **black runs**. 2. Each run length is coded using a **predefined Huffman codebook** (static tables with 93 white codes + 93 black codes, ranging from 0 to 2560). 3. Special codes: - **EOFB (End of Facsimile Block):** marks the end of the page. - **Fill bits:** padded to byte boundaries. 4. A "**fill**" mechanism ensures no line exceeds the maximum transmission time of 5 seconds (for analog phone lines). > [!NOTE] > **Why 1-D?** MH was designed for slow analog phone lines where line errors would corrupt the entire 2-D reference. Coding each line independently limits error propagation. ## 2.3 Group 3 — Modified READ (MR) — 2-D Coding The **Relative Element Address Designate (READ)** scheme codes most lines in 2-D, using the previous line as a reference. Every group of `K` lines (typically `K = 2` or `K = 4`) starts with one **1-D (MH-coded) reference line**, followed by `K-1` **2-D coded lines**. This is the **hybrid** approach. ## 2.4 Group 4 (T.6) — Modified Modified READ (MMR) MMR is a **fully 2-D** version of READ with: - **No 1-D reference lines** (every line is coded relative to the previous one). - **No fill bits** required (used on digital lines). - **No end-of-line (EOL) codes** between lines — only one EOFB at the end. > [!IMPORTANT] > **MMR Trade-off:** Higher compression than MH/MR, but a single bit error can corrupt the entire remainder of the image. Acceptable for digital storage; problematic for noisy analog channels. ## 2.5 The 2-D READ Coding Modes (for MR and MMR) For each current scan line, three **pass modes** are possible: | Mode | Symbol | Description | Use Case | |------|--------|-------------|----------| | **Pass Mode** | `0001` | Skips over identical multi-pixel segments | Long runs of the same color | | **Horizontal Mode** | `001` | Encodes `a_1 a_2` run lengths explicitly | Short or irregular segments | | **Vertical Mode** | `0X` where `X \in {011,010,000,001,011,...}` (5 offsets) | Codes small offsets from reference positions | Small run-length shifts | The 5 vertical offsets are: $\{-3, -2, -1, 0, +1, +2, +3\}$ — coded with the prefix `0` and a 3-bit offset code `V(0)` through `V(3)`. ## 2.6 JBIG1 (T.82 / ISO 11544) JBIG1 is a **progressive, lossless** bi-level image standard using **context-based adaptive arithmetic coding (QM-Coder)**. **Key Innovations:** 1. **Context modeling:** A prediction is made for each pixel based on up to **10 neighboring pixels** (the **AT** — Adaptive Template — provides 1024 contexts in the default mode). 2. **Adaptive probability estimation:** An arithmetic coder (QM-Coder) maintains a probability estimate for each context and adapts it after every coded bit. 3. **Progressive build-up:** Optional hierarchical mode allows the image to be transmitted in multiple resolutions (pyramid coding), each layer doubling the resolution. > [!NOTE] > **Why JBIG beats Group 4:** JBIG typically achieves **1.1× to 1.5×** the compression of MMR for the same image while being fully lossless and error-resilient (since context modeling degrades gracefully on errors). ## 2.7 JBIG2 (T.88 / ISO 14492) — The Modern Standard JBIG2 is the **most powerful** bi-level compression standard, supporting both: - **Lossless mode** (numerically identical reconstruction). - **Lossy mode** (visually lossless, e.g., similar text glyphs merged). **Core Encoding Methods:** | Method | Symbol | Compression Principle | |--------|--------|----------------------| | **Generic Region** | GR | Context-based arithmetic coding (like JBIG1) | | **Symbol Region** | SR | **Pattern matching + dictionary substitution** | | **Halftone Region** | HT | Dictionary coding for halftone images | | **Unknown Region** | UR | No compression / raw | **The 4-Pass JBIG2 Encoder Architecture:** 1. **Page Segmentation** — divide the page into regions (text, halftone, generic). 2. **Pattern Extraction** — extract unique glyphs/shapes from symbol regions. 3. **Pattern Dictionary Construction** — cluster similar patterns; create a codebook. 4. **Coding** — encode each instance with a dictionary index + position. ## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes | |---|-------------------|------------|-------| | 1 | Raw image size | $B_{\text{raw}} = M \times N$ bits | 1 bit/pixel | | 2 | Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = B_{\text{raw}} / B_{\text{comp}}$ | Higher = better | | 3 | Bits per pixel (bpp) | $\text{bpp} = B_{\text{comp}} / (M \times N)$ | Lower = better | | 4 | Shannon Entropy | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits/symbol | Lower bound on bpp | | 5 | MH/MR run-length sum | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \ell_i = N$ | Total length of runs = line width | | 6 | JBIG1 contexts (default) | $C = 2^{10} = 1024$ | 10-bit context template | | 7 | MMR 2D coding modes | Pass + Horizontal + 5 Verticals | 7 mode codes | | 8 | MR K-factor | $K \in \{2, 4\}$ | Lines between MH references | | 9 | JBIG2 dictionary size | Limited by segment headers | Typically 64K – 4M entries | | 10 | Arithmetic coding output length | $L = \lceil -\log_2 P(\text{message}) \rceil$ bits | Approaching entropy | | 11 | Pass mode condition | $b_1 < a_1'$ | First black ≤ first black above | | 12 | Vertical mode offset range | $a_1 - a_1' \in \{-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3\}$ | 7 offsets total | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why RLE is Effective on Bi-level Images Consider a bi-level document image of size $M \times N$ where pixels are independent with probability $P(\text{white}) = p$ and $P(\text{black}) = 1 - p$. **Step 1: Bits per pixel without compression** $$H_0 = 1 \text{ bpp}$$ **Step 2: Expected number of bits per pixel using fixed-length run coding** If we use fixed-length run codes, with maximum run length $L_{\max}$, the entropy of run lengths is: $$H_R = -\sum_{\ell=1}^{L_{\max}} P(\ell) \log_2 P(\ell)$$ where the geometric distribution gives: $$P(\ell) = p^\ell (1-p)$$ **Step 3: For a run of length $\ell$ with Huffman coding** The average bits per pixel is: $$H_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\text{Average bits per run}}{\text{Average run length}} = \frac{H_R}{\bar{\ell}}$$ with the mean run length: $$\bar{\ell} = \sum_{\ell=1}^{\infty} \ell \cdot p^{\ell-1}(1-p) = \frac{1}{1-p}$$ **Step 4: Substituting** $$\text{bpp} = H_R \cdot (1-p)$$ For a typical text document with $p = 0.95$ (95% white): $$\bar{\ell}_{\text{white}} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ pixels/run}$$ This large mean run length is precisely why RLE-based methods (MH, MR, MMR) achieve substantial compression on text documents. ## 3.2 Worked Example: MH (Modified Huffman) Coding of a Single Line **Problem:** Encode the line `WWWWBBBBWWWWWWBBBBWWW` (line length 21) using MH. **Step 1: Identify run lengths** | Run | Color | Length | |-----|-------|--------| | 1 | White | 4 | | 2 | Black | 4 | | 3 | White | 6 | | 4 | Black | 4 | | 5 | White | 3 | **Step 2: Look up MH codewords** Using the standard MH codebook (from T.4): | Run Length | White Code | Black Code | Bits | |------------|------------|------------|------| | 4 | `1011` | `011` | 4, 3 | | 6 | `1110` | `1111` | 4, 4 | | 3 | `1000` | `10` | 4, 2 | **Step 3: Concatenate codewords (line always starts with white)** $$\text{Code} = \underbrace{1011}_{W=4} \ \underbrace{011}_{B=4} \ \underbrace{1110}_{W=6} \ \underbrace{011}_{B=4} \ \underbrace{1000}_{W=3}$$ **Step 4: Total bits and efficiency** $$B_{\text{comp}} = 4 + 3 + 4 + 3 + 4 = 18 \text{ bits}$$ $$B_{\text{raw}} = 21 \text{ bits}$$ $$\text{CR} = \frac{21}{18} = 1.17$$ > [!NOTE] > **Important Observation:** For a single short line, MH gives a marginal improvement. MH shines on **longer lines** with **longer runs** — a typical 1728-pixel fax line with many long runs achieves CRs of **4:1 to 10:1**. ## 3.3 Worked Example: JBIG2 Symbol Region Encoding **Problem:** A scanned page contains 500 instances of the letter "e". Each instance is a $12 \times 8 = 96$-pixel bitmap. Compute the compression using symbol region encoding. **Step 1: Raw cost (uncompressed)** $$B_{\text{raw}} = 500 \times 96 = 48{,}000 \text{ bits} = 6{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2: Symbol region cost** JBIG2 stores the glyph once in the dictionary and uses a (ref, x, y) tuple for each instance. Suppose: - **Dictionary entry for "e":** 96 bits (raw) + ~20 bits overhead = **116 bits** - **Per-instance reference:** x-coordinate (16 bits) + y-coordinate (16 bits) + dictionary index (typically 16–32 bits) = **~64 bits per instance** **Step 3: Total compressed cost** $$B_{\text{comp}} = 116 + 500 \times 64 = 32{,}116 \text{ bits} \approx 4{,}015 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4: Compression ratio** $$\text{CR} = \frac{48{,}000}{32{,}116} \approx 1.49$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **For pages with many repeated glyphs (typical A4 documents have 1500+ character instances), JBIG2 symbol region encoding achieves CRs of 20:1 to 50:1 — and with lossy text matching (visually similar glyphs merged), CRs of 50:1 to 100:1.** ## 3.4 Derivation: Pass Mode Decision Rule in READ Coding In the READ algorithm, when scanning the current line, let $a_1$ be the position of the first transition (white→black) on the current line that lies to the right of the previous transition $a_2$, and $a_1'$ the corresponding transition on the reference line. **Pass mode is selected** when: $$b_1 < a_1'$$ where $b_1$ is the first transition on the reference line to the right of $a_1'$. This means the **next black run on the reference line starts before the first black run on the current line** — the current line is "later" in starting its black run, so a pass mode is issued to advance the reference pointer past $b_2$. Mathematically, the **transmission efficiency** of 2-D READ over 1-D MH can be derived as: $$\eta_{2D} = \frac{H_{\text{2D}}}{H_{\text{1D}}} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7$$ This means **2-D READ typically uses 30–50% fewer bits than 1-D MH** for text-like images. ## 3.5 Context-Based Arithmetic Coding: The JBIG1 Model The probability that the current pixel $X$ is black, given the 10-pixel context $C$, is adaptively estimated as: $$P(X=1 \mid C) = \frac{N(1, C) + \alpha}{N(0, C) + N(1, C) + 2\alpha}$$ where: - $N(0, C)$ and $N(1, C)$ are the running counts of seen 0s and 1s in context $C$. - $\alpha$ is a smoothing constant (typically $\alpha = 0.5$ or $\alpha = 1.0$). The expected code length per pixel approaches the conditional entropy: $$H(X \mid C) = -\sum_{C} P(C) \left[ P(0\mid C) \log_2 P(0\mid C) + P(1\mid C) \log_2 P(1\mid C) \right]$$ which is always $\le H(X)$ (the unconditional entropy) — proving that **context modeling always provides gain** over memoryless entropy coding. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution Timeline of Bi-level Standards ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["1978
CCITT Group 1
Analog"] node2["1980
T.4 Group 3 MH
1D Huffman"] node3["1984
T.4 Group 3 MR
2D READ"] node4["1988
T.6 Group 4 MMR
Pure 2D"] node5["1993
T.82 JBIG1
Arithmetic"] node6["2000
T.88 JBIG2
Pattern Matching"] node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 node2 -.->|"CR 4-10x"| node2 node3 -.->|"CR 6-12x"| node3 node4 -.->|"CR 10-20x"| node4 node5 -.->|"CR 20-50x"| node5 node6 -.->|"CR 20-100x"| node6 ``` ## 4.2 JBIG1 Encoder Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB srcA[Input Bi-level Image] --> segA[Image Segmentation] segA --> tmplA[Adaptive Template Builder
10 Neighbour Pixels] tmplA --> ctxA[Context Lookup Table
1024 Entries] ctxA --> qmA[QM-Coder
Arithmetic Encoder] probA[Adaptive Probability
Estimator] --> qmA qmA --> outA[Compressed Bitstream] ctxA --> probA ``` ## 4.3 JBIG2 4-Pass Encoding Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB imgB[Input Bi-level Page] --> pass1[Pass 1: Page Segmentation] pass1 --> regA[Text Regions] pass1 --> regB[Halftone Regions] pass1 --> regC[Generic Regions] regA --> pass2[Pass 2: Connected Component Extraction] pass2 --> pass3[Pass 3: Pattern Clustering and Dictionary Build] pass3 --> dictB[Symbol Dictionary] regB --> dictB regC --> pass4[Pass 4: Generic Region Coding] dictB --> pass4 pass4 --> encB[Context Based Arithmetic Encoder] encB --> outB[Compressed JBIG2 Stream] ``` ## 4.4 READ 2-D Coding Mode Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB start[Start of Line Scan] --> q1{Is a1 black?} q1 -->|No| pass[Pass Mode Code 0001] q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is b1 less than a1 prime?} q2 -->|Yes| pass q2 -->|No| q3{Vertical offset within plus minus 3?} q3 -->|Yes| vert[Vertical Mode Code 0 V0..V3] q3 -->|No| hor[Horizontal Mode Code 001] hor --> run1[Encode a1a2 run lengths] vert --> nxt[Move to next transition] pass --> nxt run1 --> nxt nxt --> qend{End of line?} qend -->|No| q1 qend -->|Yes| eol[EOFB] ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Performance Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L1["Lossless Standards"] a1[MH Group 3] a2[MR Group 3] a3[MMR Group 4] a4[JBIG1] end subgraph L2["Lossy or Hybrid"] b1[JBIG2 Lossy Mode] b2[JBIG2 Lossless Mode] end L1 --> L2 a1 -.->|"CR 4-10x"| a1 a2 -.->|"CR 6-12x"| a2 a3 -.->|"CR 10-20x"| a3 a4 -.->|"CR 20-50x"| a4 b1 -.->|"CR 50-100x"| b1 b2 -.->|"CR 20-50x"| b2 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a bi-level image. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **Model Answer:** A bi-level image is a digital image in which each pixel is represented by a single bit, taking only one of two values: 0 (typically representing white) or 1 (typically representing black). Such images contain no gray levels or color information. Common examples include scanned text documents, fax pages, barcodes, engineering blueprints, and printed circuit diagrams. The mathematical representation is $I(x, y) \in \{0, 1\}$. Compression of bi-level images is critical because a single A4 page scanned at 300 DPI requires approximately 1.05 MB uncompressed, motivating the development of specialized standards such as Group 3, Group 4, JBIG1, and JBIG2. ### Question 2: List the main bi-level image compression standards and their typical compression ratios. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Model Answer:** The major bi-level image compression standards, in chronological order of development, are: | Standard | Year | Typical CR | |----------|------|------------| | Modified Huffman (MH), T.4 Group 3 | 1980 | 4:1 – 10:1 | | Modified READ (MR), T.4 Group 3 | 1984 | 6:1 – 12:1 | | Modified Modified READ (MMR), T.6 Group 4 | 1988 | 10:1 – 20:1 | | JBIG1, T.82 / ISO 11544 | 1993 | 20:1 – 50:1 | | JBIG2, T.88 / ISO 14492 | 2000 | 20:1 – 100:1 | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Modified Huffman (MH) coding scheme with an example. State its limitations. **Model Answer:** **Modified Huffman (MH) Coding — Group 3 (T.4):** MH is a **1-dimensional run-length coding scheme** standardized by CCITT/ITU-T in 1980. Each scan line is coded independently. The algorithm proceeds as follows: **Step 1 — Run Decomposition:** The current scan line is decomposed into alternating runs of white and black pixels. A line always starts with a white run (a leading white run of length 0 is allowed). **Step 2 — Codebook Lookup:** Each run length is replaced by a variable-length Huffman code. The standard defines **93 white codewords** and **93 black codewords** for run lengths 0 to 2560, plus a **"make-up"** code structure for long runs. **Step 3 — Concatenation:** Codewords are concatenated to form the bitstream. A special **End of Line (EOL)** code (`000000000001`) and an **End of Facsimile Block (EOFB)** code mark line and page boundaries. **Worked Example:** For the line `WWWWBBBBWWWWWWBBBBWWW` (line length 21): | Run | Color | Length | Code | |-----|-------|--------|------| | 1 | White | 4 | 1011 | | 2 | Black | 4 | 011 | | 3 | White | 6 | 1110 | | 4 | Black | 4 | 011 | | 5 | White | 3 | 1000 | Total: 4+3+4+3+4 = **18 bits** vs. **21 bits** raw. **Limitations of MH:** 1. **1-D only:** No inter-line correlation is exploited; vertical redundancy is completely ignored. 2. **No context modeling:** The same codebook is used regardless of local image statistics. 3. **No progressive transmission:** The image must be received in full resolution. 4. **EOL overhead:** Every line incurs 12 bits of EOL overhead, hurting CR on short lines. #### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the 2-D READ (Modified READ) coding scheme used in Group 3 and Group 4 standards. Compare with MH. **Model Answer:** **Modified READ (MR) and Modified Modified READ (MMR):** Both are **2-dimensional** schemes in which the current scan line is coded relative to the **reference line** (the previous coded line). This exploits vertical correlation between adjacent lines. **The Three Coding Modes:** For each transition in the current line, the encoder chooses one of three modes: 1. **Pass Mode (`0001`):** Issued when the first black pixel on the reference line is to the LEFT of the first black pixel on the current line. The reference pointer jumps past the corresponding black run. 2. **Horizontal Mode (`001`):** Issued when the offset between corresponding transitions exceeds $\pm 3$ pixels. The two run lengths $a_1 a_2$ are explicitly encoded using the standard MH codebook. 3. **Vertical Mode (`0V_k`):** The offset is small ($a_1 - a_1' \in \{-3, -2, -1, 0, +1, +2, +3\}$). The mode prefix `0` is followed by a 3-bit offset code. **Difference between MR (Group 3) and MMR (Group 4):** | Feature | MR (Group 3) | MMR (Group 4) | |---------|--------------|---------------| | Reference lines | Every Kth line (K=2 or 4) is MH-coded | ALL lines are 2-D coded | | EOL codes | Required | Not required | | Fill bits | Required | Not required | | Error propagation | Limited to K lines | Unlimited | | Typical use | Analog fax (T.30) | Digital storage, ISDN fax | **Comparison with MH:** $$\text{CR}_{\text{MMR}} \approx 2 \times \text{CR}_{\text{MH}}$$ MMR achieves 10:1 to 20:1 compression on text pages, compared to MH's 4:1 to 10:1. However, MMR's lack of resynchronization points makes it vulnerable to channel errors — a single bit error corrupts the rest of the image. ### Question B (14 Marks) #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the JBIG1 standard with reference to context-based arithmetic coding. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** **JBIG1 (T.82 / ISO 11544, 1993):** JBIG1 is a **progressive, lossless** bi-level image compression standard that significantly outperforms Group 3/4 by using **context-based adaptive arithmetic coding**. **The QM-Coder:** JBIG1 employs a simplified arithmetic coder called the **QM-Coder** (Q-Coder Modified), which encodes one bit at a time using the probability of that bit being 0 or 1. **The Adaptive Template:** For each pixel $X$ to be coded, a 10-bit context $C$ is constructed from previously coded neighboring pixels (typically the 10 pixels in the causal template — two above, two to the left, and others). This context indexes into a table of size $2^{10} = 1024$ probability estimates. **Adaptive Probability Estimation:** For each of the 1024 contexts, the coder maintains a count of observed 0s and 1s. The probability estimate for "1 given context C" is: $$P(1 \mid C) = \frac{N_1(C) + \alpha}{N_0(C) + N_1(C) + 2\alpha}$$ After each bit is coded, the corresponding counter is updated. This allows the coder to **adapt to local image statistics** — e.g., the probability of black in a "text" context vs. a "blank" context differs dramatically. **Progressive Mode (Optional):** JBIG1 supports **hierarchical (pyramid) coding**. The image is first reduced by 2× in each dimension, transmitted, then progressively refined by 2× layers. This enables the receiver to display a low-resolution preview quickly. **Performance:** JBIG1 achieves **1.1× to 1.5×** the compression of MMR on the same image, with no loss of information and graceful degradation on channel errors. #### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the JBIG2 standard in detail, including its encoding methods, region types, and 4-pass architecture. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Model Answer:** **JBIG2 (T.88 / ISO 14492, 2000):** JBIG2 is the **most advanced** bi-level compression standard, designed for both lossless and lossy coding of document images. **Region Types:** A JBIG2 page is segmented into four region types: 1. **Symbol Region (SR):** Text-like content with repeating glyphs. Encoded via pattern matching and dictionary substitution. 2. **Text Region:** Semantically labeled text (carries font, position, and dictionary index). 3. **Halftone Region (HT):** Halftone images of photographs. Encoded via dictionary of halftone patterns. 4. **Generic Region (GR):** Everything else (line art, etc.). Encoded like JBIG1. **The 4-Pass Encoding Architecture:** **Pass 1 — Page Segmentation:** The page is divided into the four region types. Standard algorithms analyze connected components and texture features. **Pass 2 — Connected Component Extraction:** For symbol regions, all connected black components are extracted. Each component becomes a candidate glyph. **Pass 3 — Pattern Dictionary Construction:** Similar glyphs are **clustered** (using techniques like k-means or agglomerative clustering on shape descriptors). A representative pattern is stored for each cluster. This is where lossy compression occurs — visually identical glyphs are merged. **Pass 4 — Coding:** Each glyph instance is coded as `(ref, x, y)`: a reference to its dictionary entry plus its position. The (ref, x, y) tuples are then entropy-coded using the same context-based arithmetic coder as JBIG1. **Compression Performance:** | Mode | Typical CR | Use Case | |------|------------|----------| | Lossless (Generic regions only) | 20:1 – 30:1 | Archival storage | | Lossless (Symbol + Generic) | 30:1 – 50:1 | Document archiving | | Lossy (visually similar glyphs merged) | 50:1 – 100:1 | Web/Mobile document delivery | **JBIG2 vs. JBIG1:** - JBIG2 supports **both** lossless and lossy modes; JBIG1 is lossless only. - JBIG2 uses **pattern matching** for text; JBIG1 uses generic pixel-wise coding. - JBIG2 allows **user-defined dictionaries**; JBIG1 has no such mechanism. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing MH and MMR:** Many students incorrectly state that Group 4 is "2D MH." MMR is based on the **READ algorithm**, not Huffman coding. State the three modes (Pass, Horizontal, Vertical) explicitly. > 2. **Misstating JBIG2's lossy mode:** JBIG2 lossy mode is **visually lossless for text** — it merges glyphs that look the same to humans, not arbitrary lossy compression. Do NOT equate it with JPEG-like lossy compression. > 3. **Forgetting the 4-pass architecture:** When asked about JBIG2 encoder, ALWAYS mention all four passes (Segmentation, Extraction, Dictionary Construction, Coding). > 4. **Forgetting that MH uses 1D coding only:** Many students incorrectly attribute inter-line compression to MH. MH codes each line independently. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:** - [x] A **bi-level image** uses 1 bit per pixel: $B_{\text{raw}} = M \times N$ bits. - [x] **MH (Modified Huffman)** is **1-D, run-length** coding using static Huffman codebooks (93 white + 93 black codes). - [x] **MR (Modified READ)** is a **hybrid 1D+2D** scheme; every Kth line is MH-coded, the rest are 2D-READ-coded. - [x] **MMR (Group 4)** is **pure 2D READ** with no EOL or fill bits; 10:1 to 20:1 CR. - [x] **READ 2D modes:** Pass, Horizontal, Vertical (with 7 offset codes). - [x] **JBIG1** uses **context-based adaptive arithmetic coding (QM-Coder)** with a **10-pixel / 1024-context** template; optional **progressive (pyramid)** mode. - [x] **JBIG2** supports **four region types**: Symbol (SR), Text, Halftone (HT), Generic (GR). - [x] **JBIG2 4-pass encoder:** Segmentation → Connected-Component Extraction → Dictionary Clustering → Coding. - [x] **JBIG2 lossy mode** merges visually similar glyphs in the dictionary — NOT a JPEG-like transform coder. - [x] **Compression Ratio:** $\text{CR} = B_{\text{raw}} / B_{\text{comp}}$; higher is better. - [x] **Standard progression:** MH (4–10×) → MR (6–12×) → MMR (10–20×) → JBIG1 (20–50×) → JBIG2 (20–100×). - [x] **JBIG2 is the modern standard for bi-level documents** (PDF's preferred codec for monochrome pages). - [x] **Trade-off:** Higher compression → higher error sensitivity (MH/MR are robust, MMR/JBIG2 are fragile). - [x] **Arithmetic coding length:** $L \approx -\log_2 P(\text{message})$ — approaches Shannon entropy. - [x] **JBIG1 vs. JBIG2:** JBIG1 is pixel-wise + arithmetic; JBIG2 adds pattern matching and dictionary substitution.

topics.title: Audio Compression Techniques - ADPCM in Speech Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Audio Compression Techniques - ADPCM in Speech Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Adaptive Differential Pulse Code Modulation (ADPCM)** is a waveform coding technique that compresses audio (especially speech) signals by combining two adaptive mechanisms — **Adaptive Prediction** and **Adaptive Quantization** — with **Differential Encoding (DPCM)**. It exploits the high temporal correlation between successive speech samples to reduce the bit rate while preserving perceptual quality. Standardized by the **ITU-T (formerly CCITT)** under recommendations **G.721, G.723, G.726, and G.727**, ADPCM encodes a **3.4 kHz bandwidth speech** at **32 kbps** (4 bits/sample) using **8 kHz sampling**, achieving a **4:1 compression ratio** over standard **64 kbps PCM (8-bit μ-law/A-law)**.

The signal processing chain in ADPCM operates on the principle that:
$$d(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$$

where the *difference* $d(n)$ between the actual sample $x(n)$ and its predicted value $\hat{x}(n)$ is small in magnitude and can be quantized with **fewer bits**. The quantizer step size $\Delta(n)$ and the predictor coefficients adapt to the local statistics of the speech signal.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is evaluated for its **mathematical formulation (predictor & quantizer adaptation)**, **block-diagram interpretation of encoder/decoder**, and **numerical analysis of adaptive step size (G.726 algorithm)**. Students should know the difference between **DPCM, APCM, and ADPCM** thoroughly.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: "The Smart Accountant"**
> Imagine you are tracking your **daily expenses** (this is the audio signal $x(n)$). Instead of recording every rupee spent (8-bit full PCM), a smart accountant records only the **difference from yesterday's spend** (this is the differential $d(n)$). Most days the difference is tiny (e.g., ₹20 less or more), so the accountant can record it in **a small notebook** (fewer bits).
> 
> But during Diwali week, expenses spike — so the accountant **switches to a bigger notebook** (larger step size $\Delta(n)$). When expenses return to normal, **the smaller notebook is used again**. This *smart switching* is the **adaptation mechanism** in ADPCM.

| Analogy Component | ADPCM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Daily expense | Audio sample $x(n)$ |
| Yesterday's spend | Predicted sample $\hat{x}(n)$ |
| Difference recorded | Differential $d(n)$ |
| Small vs. big notebook | Adaptive step size $\Delta(n)$ |
| Notebook switching logic | Quantizer adaptation algorithm |

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard ITU-T G.726 ADPCM Parameters**
> - **Sampling Frequency $f_s$** = **8 kHz**
> - **Bit Rate** = **32 kbps (4 bits per sample)**
> - **Speech Bandwidth** = **300 Hz to 3.4 kHz**
> - **Compression Ratio** = **2:1 (4 bits vs 8 bits of PCM)**
> - **Compression Savings** = **48 kbps (over 64 kbps μ-law PCM)**
> - **Quantizer Levels** = **16 (uniform within each segment)**
> - **Predictor Order** = **2 poles + 6 zeros (Pole-Zero predictor)**
> - **Standard $\log_2$ base** for step-size adaptation
> - **Audible bandwidth** = **20 Hz – 20 kHz** (full human hearing)
> - **Nyquist Rate** for speech = **2 × 3.4 kHz = 6.8 kHz** (< 8 kHz ✓)

---

### 1.4 Hierarchy: From PCM to ADPCM

Understanding the evolution of speech coding techniques is essential for KTU exams:

1. **PCM (Pulse Code Modulation)**: Quantizes each sample independently → high redundancy, no prediction.
2. **DPCM (Differential PCM)**: Quantizes the difference between actual and predicted sample → exploits sample-to-sample correlation.
3. **APCM (Adaptive PCM)**: Adapts the quantizer step size $\Delta(n)$ to signal variance → handles dynamic range.
4. **ADPCM (Adaptive DPCM)**: Adapts **both** the predictor coefficients $a_i(n)$ **and** the step size $\Delta(n)$ → best of DPCM and APCM combined.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept: Why is ADPCM Needed?

Speech signals exhibit two critical properties that ADPCM exploits:

- **Temporal Redundancy**: Adjacent speech samples are highly correlated (correlation coefficient $\rho \approx 0.85$ to $0.95$ for voiced speech). The difference signal has a much smaller variance than the original.
- **Non-Stationarity**: Speech is quasi-stationary (statistics change every **10–30 ms**). A fixed quantizer/predictor is suboptimal → adaptation is required.

> [!NOTE] **Variance Reduction Principle**
> If $x(n)$ has variance $\sigma_x^2$ and the predictor has gain $G_p$, the difference signal has variance:
> $$\sigma_d^2 = \sigma_x^2 \cdot (1 - G_p)$$
> The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) gain** of DPCM over PCM is:
> $$G_{SNR} = \frac{SNR_{DPCM}}{SNR_{PCM}} = \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_d^2} = \frac{1}{1-G_p}$$
> For $G_p = 0.9$, this yields an SNR gain of **10 dB**, equivalent to saving ~1.6 bits/sample.

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation of ADPCM

#### 2.2.1 The Predictor

The predicted value of the current sample is a weighted sum of past reconstructed samples:

$$\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{P} a_i(n) \cdot \tilde{x}(n-i)$$

where:
- $a_i(n)$ = time-varying predictor coefficients
- $\tilde{x}(n-i)$ = past reconstructed samples
- $P$ = predictor order

**G.726 Standard Predictor Structure** (Pole-Zero form):

$$\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} a_i(n)\,\tilde{x}(n-i) + \sum_{i=1}^{6} b_i(n)\,d_q(n-i)$$

- $a_i(n)$ = 2 pole coefficients (capture formant resonances)
- $b_i(n)$ = 6 zero coefficients (capture short-term correlation)

#### 2.2.2 The Differencer

The differential signal (prediction error):

$$d(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$$

#### 2.2.3 The Adaptive Quantizer

The differential is quantized with a step size that adapts:

$$d_q(n) = \Delta(n) \cdot Q\left[\frac{d(n)}{\Delta(n)}\right]$$

where $Q[\cdot]$ is a midtread non-uniform quantizer with **16 levels** (for 4-bit G.726) and $\Delta(n)$ is the **adaptive step size**.

#### 2.2.4 Step-Size Adaptation (G.726 Algorithm)

The step size follows a **multiplicative (logarithmic) adaptation rule**:

$$\Delta(n+1) = \Delta(n) \cdot M\bigl[\,I(n)\,\bigr]$$

where $I(n)$ is the quantizer index (0 to 15) and $M[I]$ is a multiplier table that:
- **Increases** step size when input magnitude is large
- **Decreases** step size when input magnitude is small
- Has a value close to 1 (e.g., **0.9 to 2.4**) to ensure stability

The locked state equilibrium condition:

$$\prod_{k=0}^{15} M[k] = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Multiplicative?**
> Multiplicative adaptation in the log-domain is equivalent to additive adaptation, which is computationally simpler and ensures **faster attack** (overshoot) than decay, matching human auditory perception.

#### 2.2.5 Predictor Adaptation (LMS Algorithm)

Coefficients are updated using the **LMS (Least Mean Squares) gradient rule**:

$$a_i(n+1) = (1 - \beta) \cdot a_i(n) + \alpha \cdot \text{sgn}[d_q(n)] \cdot \text{sgn}[\tilde{x}(n-i)]$$

- $\alpha$ = adaptation speed constant
- $\beta$ = leakage factor (prevents coefficient drift)
- $\text{sgn}[\cdot]$ = sign function (sign-LMS for simplicity)

This ensures the predictor "learns" the changing speech statistics.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exam-Ready Formula Table**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Physical Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential signal | $d(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$ | Prediction error to be quantized |
| 2 | Predicted sample | $\hat{x}(n) = \sum a_i(n)\,\tilde{x}(n-i)$ | Linear prediction of order $P$ |
| 3 | Quantized differential | $d_q(n) = \Delta(n) \cdot Q\bigl[d(n)/\Delta(n)\bigr]$ | Quantization with adaptive step |
| 4 | Reconstructed sample | $\tilde{x}(n) = \hat{x}(n) + d_q(n)$ | Decoder output (in encoder for prediction) |
| 5 | Step-size update | $\Delta(n+1) = \Delta(n) \cdot M[I(n)]$ | Log-domain adaptation |
| 6 | Stability condition | $\prod_{k=0}^{N-1} M[k] = 1$ | Multiplier table product = 1 |
| 7 | DPCM SNR gain | $G_{SNR} = 1/(1-G_p)$ | Improvement over PCM (in dB: $10\log_{10}$) |
| 8 | Bit rate formula | $R_b = f_s \cdot n$ bits/s | For $f_s=8$ kHz, $n=4$ bits: $R_b=32$ kbps |
| 9 | Compression ratio | $\text{CR} = n_{PCM}/n_{ADPCM}$ | For 8/4 = 2:1 |
| 10 | Log-gain equation | $\log_2 \Delta(n+1) = \log_2 \Delta(n) + \log_2 M[I(n)]$ | Equivalent additive form |
| 11 | Pole-zero predictor | $\hat{x}(n) = \sum a_i \tilde{x}(n-i) + \sum b_j d_q(n-j)$ | G.726 2-pole 6-zero form |
| 12 | LMS update | $a_i(n+1) = (1-\beta) a_i(n) + \alpha \,\text{sgn}[d_q]\,\text{sgn}[\tilde{x}]$ | Predictor adaptation |
| 13 | Quantization noise power | $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$ (uniform) | For $B$-bit uniform, $N=2^B$ levels |
| 14 | SNR of PCM | $SNR_{dB} \approx 6.02 B + 1.76$ | For uniform $B$-bit quantizer |
| 15 | Adaptive gain | $G_{APCM} = \text{E}[\Delta^2] / \text{E}[\Delta^2_{\text{fixed}}]$ | APCM advantage over fixed PCM |

> [!NOTE] **CRITICAL EXAM TIP**
> The G.726 standard **16-level quantizer** is divided into **8 positive** and **8 negative** levels with **non-uniform spacing** (concentrated near zero for fine resolution of small errors). Memorize the multiplier table values for full marks: typical values are in the range **[0.9, 2.4]** with the locked-state product = 1.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **PSTN Telephony**: ITU-T G.726 ADPCM at 32 kbps doubled the capacity of digital telephone trunks compared to 64 kbps PCM.
2. **VoIP Gateways**: Legacy interconnects between VoIP and circuit-switched networks still use ADPCM.
3. **Bluetooth SCO Audio**: Some Bluetooth voice channels use ADPCM-style coding for low-power headset communication.
4. **Satellite Voice Channels**: INMARSAT and other satellite systems use ADPCM for narrowband voice.
5. **Voicemail Storage**: Reduces storage footprint by 50% over PCM.
6. **Voice Recorders / Answering Machines**: Embedded devices with limited memory.
7. **DECT Cordless Phones**: 32 kbps ADPCM in the digital cordless standard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SNR Gain of DPCM over PCM

**Step 1: Define the prediction error variance.**

For a first-order linear predictor with coefficient $a_1$ applied to a stationary signal $x(n)$ with correlation $\rho$:

$$\hat{x}(n) = a_1 \cdot x(n-1)$$

The minimum mean-square error (MMSE) predictor coefficient is obtained by minimizing:

$$E[\,d^2(n)\,] = E\bigl[\,(x(n) - a_1 x(n-1))^2\,\bigr]$$

**Step 2: Differentiate w.r.t. $a_1$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial a_1} E[(x(n) - a_1 x(n-1))^2] = 0$$

$$-2 E[x(n) x(n-1)] + 2 a_1 E[x^2(n-1)] = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for $a_1$ using autocorrelation.**

$$a_1 = \frac{E[x(n) x(n-1)]}{E[x^2(n-1)]} = \frac{R(1)}{R(0)} = \rho$$

So the **optimal predictor coefficient equals the correlation coefficient** $\rho$.

**Step 4: Compute prediction error variance.**

$$\sigma_d^2 = E[d^2] = E[x^2] - 2a_1 E[x(n)x(n-1)] + a_1^2 E[x^2]$$

Substituting $a_1 = \rho$ and $R(0) = \sigma_x^2$, $R(1) = \rho\sigma_x^2$:

$$\sigma_d^2 = \sigma_x^2 - 2\rho^2\sigma_x^2 + \rho^2\sigma_x^2 = \sigma_x^2 (1 - \rho^2)$$

**Step 5: Express the predictor gain.**

$$G_p = 1 - \frac{\sigma_d^2}{\sigma_x^2} = \rho^2$$

**Step 6: SNR gain of DPCM over PCM.**

Since quantization noise is proportional to step size squared, and the step size scales with $\sigma_d$:

$$G_{SNR} = \frac{SNR_{DPCM}}{SNR_{PCM}} = \frac{1}{1 - G_p} = \frac{1}{1 - \rho^2}$$

For $\rho = 0.9$:

$$G_{SNR} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.81} = \frac{1}{0.19} \approx 5.26$$

In decibels:

$$G_{SNR,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(5.26) \approx 7.21 \text{ dB}$$

This corresponds to saving approximately **1.2 bits/sample** — enough to justify DPCM over PCM.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: G.726 Step-Size Multiplier Stability

**Step 1: Recall the adaptation rule.**

$$\Delta(n+1) = \Delta(n) \cdot M[I(n)]$$

**Step 2: Take the logarithm of both sides.**

$$\log_2 \Delta(n+1) = \log_2 \Delta(n) + \log_2 M[I(n)]$$

Let $y(n) = \log_2 \Delta(n)$ and $w[I] = \log_2 M[I]$:

$$y(n+1) = y(n) + w[I(n)]$$

**Step 3: Iterate over one full quantizer cycle.**

For a stationary input, the index $I(n)$ visits all 16 levels with equal probability $1/16$:

$$y(n+16) = y(n) + \sum_{k=0}^{15} w[k] = y(n) + \sum_{k=0}^{15} \log_2 M[k]$$

**Step 4: Apply the equilibrium (locked) condition.**

For a stationary input, we need $y(n+16) = y(n)$ so that step size does not drift:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{15} \log_2 M[k] = 0$$

This is equivalent to:

$$\log_2 \left(\prod_{k=0}^{15} M[k]\right) = 0 \implies \prod_{k=0}^{15} M[k] = 1$$

**Step 5: Verify with typical G.726 values (subset).**

Using the actual G.726 multiplier table (illustrative values, $\log_2$ scale):

| $I$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M[I]$ | 0.998 | 0.978 | 0.907 | 0.839 | 0.739 | 0.578 | 0.478 | 0.298 |

| $I$ | 8 | 9 | 10 | 11 | 12 | 13 | 14 | 15 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M[I]$ | 0.198 | 0.119 | 0.078 | 0.048 | 0.028 | 0.018 | 0.008 | 0.004 |

The product $\prod_{k=0}^{15} M[k] = 1$ exactly (by design of the standard).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Design Implication**
> The product condition prevents step-size drift during silence or steady tones, which would otherwise cause overflow or underflow in the reconstructed signal.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem (Typical 7-mark question):**
A speech signal sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz has adjacent sample correlation $\rho = 0.95$. An ADPCM encoder uses a 2nd-order predictor with quantization step $\Delta = 0.05$ V. Current sample $x(n) = 2.5$ V and the predicted value $\hat{x}(n) = 2.4$ V. Compute:
- (a) The differential signal $d(n)$.
- (b) The quantized differential $d_q(n)$ (assume the differential falls in the 6th positive quantizer level with effective quantized output $3\Delta$).
- (c) The reconstructed sample $\tilde{x}(n)$.
- (d) The bit rate and compression ratio compared to 8-bit PCM.

**Solution:**

**(a) Differential signal:**

$$d(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n) = 2.5 - 2.4 = 0.1 \text{ V}$$

**(b) Quantized differential:**

Given the differential is in the 6th positive level:

$$d_q(n) = 3 \cdot \Delta = 3 \times 0.05 = 0.15 \text{ V}$$

**(c) Reconstructed sample (encoder feedback):**

$$\tilde{x}(n) = \hat{x}(n) + d_q(n) = 2.4 + 0.15 = 2.55 \text{ V}$$

Note: The reconstruction is used for **future predictions** in the encoder so that the encoder and decoder stay synchronized (avoids drift).

**(d) Bit rate and compression ratio:**

For ADPCM with 4 bits per sample at 8 kHz:

$$R_{ADPCM} = 4 \times 8000 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

For 8-bit PCM:

$$R_{PCM} = 8 \times 8000 = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

Compression ratio:

$$\text{CR} = \frac{64 \text{ kbps}}{32 \text{ kbps}} = 2:1$$

Bit savings:

$$64 - 32 = 32 \text{ kbps (50% bandwidth reduction)}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DPCM vs APCM vs ADPCM

> [!IMPORTANT] **Highly Tested Table in KTU 2024**

| Parameter | DPCM (Differential PCM) | APCM (Adaptive PCM) | ADPCM (Adaptive DPCM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Predictor | **Fixed** coefficients | No predictor (direct) | **Adaptive** coefficients |
| Quantizer | **Fixed** step size $\Delta$ | **Adaptive** step size $\Delta(n)$ | **Adaptive** step size $\Delta(n)$ |
| Adapts to signal | **No** | **Yes** (quantizer only) | **Yes** (both) |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Bit rate (typical) | 32–48 kbps | 32–48 kbps | **16–32 kbps** |
| Voice quality | Good | Good | **Toll quality** |
| Standard | None widely used | None widely used | **G.721, G.723, G.726, G.727** |
| Predictor order | 1–2 | N/A | **2-pole + 6-zero** |
| Use case | Simple D/A | Dynamic range | **Telephony, VoIP** |
| SNR vs PCM gain | $\sim$ 6–10 dB | $\sim$ 4–6 dB | $\sim$ 14–18 dB |

> [!TIP] **Exam Strategy**
> When asked "Why is ADPCM better than DPCM?", always answer with the **dual adaptation** argument: DPCM uses a fixed predictor that fails when speech statistics change rapidly (e.g., vowels to consonants), while ADPCM tracks the changing spectral envelope via the pole-zero predictor adaptation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ADPCM Encoder Block Diagram (G.726 Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode["x of n: Input Audio Sample"] --> diffCalc["Differencer: Subtract Predictor Output"]
    diffCalc --> adaptQuant["Adaptive Quantizer: Q with step delta n"]
    adaptQuant --> outNode["I n: Quantizer Index 4 bit Output to Channel"]
    adaptQuant --> diffRecon["Inverse Quantizer: Multiply I n by delta n"]
    diffRecon --> sumNode["Adder: Combine with Prediction"]
    sumNode --> reconNode["x tilde of n: Reconstructed Sample"]
    reconNode --> predNode["Adaptive Predictor: 2 Pole 6 Zero Filter"]
    predNode --> predOut["x hat of n: Predicted Value"]
    predOut --> diffCalc
    predOut --> sumNode
    adaptQuant --> stepNode["Step Size Adapter: delta n+1 equals delta n times M I n"]
    stepNode --> adaptQuant
    predOut --> coeffNode["Coefficient Adapter: LMS Update on a i and b j"]
    coeffNode --> predNode
    outNode --> chLine["Transmission Channel 32 kbps"]
```

---

### 4.2 ADPCM Decoder Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    chIn["I n: Received 4 bit Codeword from Channel"] --> invQuant["Inverse Adaptive Quantizer: dq n equals delta n times Q inverse"]
    invQuant --> sumNode2["Adder: dq n plus x hat n"]
    sumNode2 --> outRecon["x tilde of n: Reconstructed Speech Output"]
    outRecon --> predDec["Adaptive Predictor: Pole Zero with LMS Update"]
    predDec --> predOutDec["x hat of n: Predicted Sample"]
    predOutDec --> sumNode2
    predOutDec --> predDec
    invQuant --> stepDec["Step Size Adapter: delta n+1 from M I n Table"]
    stepDec --> invQuant
    invQuant --> coeffDec["Coefficient Adapter: Update a i and b j"]
    coeffDec --> predDec
    outRecon --> outDAC["DAC and Lowpass Filter: Analog Speech"]
```

---

### 4.3 Signal Flow: PCM to ADPCM Evolution (Conceptual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pcmBlock["PCM: Sample, Quantize 8 bit, Encode per sample independently"]
    dpcmBlock["DPCM: Add Linear Predictor, Quantize difference only"]
    apcmBlock["APCM: Make Quantizer Step Size Adaptive to Signal Variance"]
    adpcmBlock["ADPCM: Combine Adaptive Predictor plus Adaptive Quantizer"]
    pcmBlock --> dpcmBlock
    dpcmBlock --> apcmBlock
    apcmBlock --> adpcmBlock
    pcmBlock --> rate1["Bit Rate: 64 kbps"]
    dpcmBlock --> rate2["Bit Rate: 48 kbps"]
    apcmBlock --> rate3["Bit Rate: 48 kbps"]
    adpcmBlock --> rate4["Bit Rate: 32 kbps at toll quality"]
```

---

### 4.4 Adaptive Quantizer Internal Structure (G.726 16-Level Non-Uniform)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    diffIn["d n: Differential Input"] --> normStep["Normalize: d n divided by delta n"]
    normStep --> midQuant["Midtread 16 Level Quantizer: 8 Positive plus 8 Negative"]
    midQuant --> indexOut["I n: 4 bit Index 0 to 15"]
    indexOut --> multTable["Multiplier Table M I: 16 Log Domain Values"]
    multTable --> logUpdate["Log Update: y n+1 equals y n plus log2 M I"]
    logUpdate --> expStep["Exponentiate: delta n+1 equals 2 power y n+1"]
    expStep --> normStep
    indexOut --> outChan["Transmit Index to Decoder"]
```

---

### 4.5 G.726 Codec System-Level Architecture (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mic["Microphone"] --> lpIn["Anti Alias LPF: 3.4 kHz Cutoff"]
    lpIn --> adc["ADC: 8 kHz Sampling"]
    adc --> adpcmEnc["ADPCM Encoder: Adaptive Prediction plus Quantization"]
    adpcmEnc --> chan["Digital Channel: 32 kbps"]
    chan --> adpcmDec["ADPCM Decoder: Inverse Quantization plus Prediction"]
    adpcmDec --> dacOut["DAC: 8 kHz Reconstruction"]
    dacOut --> lpOut["Reconstruction LPF: 3.4 kHz Smoothing"]
    lpOut --> spk["Loudspeaker Output"]
    adpcmEnc -.feedback.-> adpcmEnc
    adpcmDec -.feedback.-> adpcmDec
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> The dashed feedback lines in the diagrams represent the **local decoder inside the encoder**. This is essential: the encoder must replicate the decoder's state exactly to keep predictions in sync, otherwise encoder and decoder would drift apart over time, especially during long utterances. This is a **classic G.726 design feature** that examiners love to ask about.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Note**
> Part A carries **2 marks** for the answer and **1 mark** for clarity/keywords. Keep answers **crisp (4–6 lines)** with one defining equation.

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between DPCM and ADPCM. Mention the role of adaptation in ADPCM.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Aspect | DPCM | ADPCM |
|---|---|---|
| Predictor | Fixed coefficients $a_i$ | Adaptive coefficients $a_i(n)$ |
| Quantizer | Fixed step size $\Delta$ | Adaptive step size $\Delta(n)$ |
| Bit rate | ~48 kbps | ~32 kbps (G.726) |
| Adaptation | None | Both predictor and quantizer |

**Role of Adaptation:**
In ADPCM, two adaptive mechanisms operate:
- **Adaptive Quantization**: The step size $\Delta(n+1) = \Delta(n) \cdot M[I(n)]$ is updated based on the quantized output, allowing the quantizer to handle the wide dynamic range of speech.
- **Adaptive Prediction**: The pole-zero predictor coefficients are updated via LMS to track the changing spectral envelope of speech.

This dual adaptation yields toll-quality speech at 32 kbps, half the bit rate of PCM, making ADPCM suitable for **telephony (G.726), DECT, and VoIP gateways**.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Explain the concept of adaptive quantization in ADPCM with the help of the step-size adaptation equation.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

**Adaptive Quantization** is a technique where the quantizer step size $\Delta(n)$ is dynamically varied to match the local amplitude statistics of the difference signal $d(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$.

**Core Equation:**

$$\Delta(n+1) = \Delta(n) \cdot M[I(n)]$$

where $M[I(n)]$ is a **16-entry multiplier table** indexed by the quantizer output $I(n)$.

**Working Principle:**
- When $\vert d(n) \vert$ is **large** (e.g., onset of a vowel or plosive), the index $I(n)$ is large, and $M[I(n)] > 1$ → step size **grows** to avoid clipping (overload distortion).
- When $\vert d(n) \vert$ is **small** (e.g., silence or steady vowel), the index $I(n)$ is small, and $M[I(n)] < 1$ → step size **shrinks** to reduce granular noise.
- **Stability constraint**: $\prod_{k=0}^{15} M[k] = 1$ prevents drift during stationary input.

In logarithmic form: $\log_2 \Delta(n+1) = \log_2 \Delta(n) + \log_2 M[I(n)]$, which is a simple accumulator.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**
> Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. KTU provides internal choice; here we present **two full 14-mark alternatives** with complete model solutions.

---

### **Part B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, ESE Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the block diagram of an ADPCM encoder. Discuss the role of the inverse quantizer in the encoder feedback path.

**(b) [7 Marks)** For a speech signal sampled at 8 kHz, an ADPCM encoder uses a 2nd-order predictor. The correlation between adjacent samples is $\rho = 0.92$. Compute the SNR gain of DPCM over PCM. If the standard PCM bit rate is 64 kbps, what is the bit rate after ADPCM compression and the resulting bandwidth savings?

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A:**

##### **Part (a) — ADPCM Encoder Block Diagram [7 marks]**

The ADPCM encoder consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Differencer** (1 mark): Computes $d(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$, the prediction error.
2. **Adaptive Quantizer** (1 mark): Quantizes $d(n)$ using a 4-bit (16-level) non-uniform midtread quantizer with step size $\Delta(n)$ to produce the index $I(n)$ transmitted to the channel.
3. **Inverse Quantizer (Local Decoder)** (2 marks): Multiplies $I(n)$ by $\Delta(n)$ to recover the quantized differential $d_q(n)$. This is **inside the encoder** to keep the encoder's state identical to the decoder's state.
4. **Adder (Reconstruction)** (1 mark): Combines $\hat{x}(n)$ and $d_q(n)$ to produce the reconstructed sample $\tilde{x}(n) = \hat{x}(n) + d_q(n)$.
5. **Adaptive Predictor (Pole-Zero)** (1 mark): Generates the predicted sample $\hat{x}(n)$ from past reconstructed samples and past quantized differentials.
6. **Adaptation Logic** (1 mark): Step-size adapter uses the multiplier table; coefficient adapter uses the LMS rule.

**Role of the Inverse Quantizer (the local decoder) [2 of the 7 marks]:**

The inverse quantizer ensures that the **encoder's predictor sees the same input as the decoder's predictor**. Without this feedback path:
- The encoder would predict from the **original** $x(n)$ (perfect prediction).
- The decoder would reconstruct from the **quantized** $\tilde{x}(n)$ (lossy prediction).
- Over time, encoder and decoder outputs would **drift apart**, especially after many samples, causing accumulated error and audible artifacts.

By including the inverse quantizer in the encoder, both sides operate on the **same reconstructed history** $\tilde{x}(n-1), \tilde{x}(n-2), \ldots$, ensuring **synchronization** throughout the transmission.

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Predictor gain from correlation (1 mark):**

For a 1st-order predictor with $a_1 = \rho$:

$$G_p = \rho^2 = (0.92)^2 = 0.8464$$

**Step 2: DPCM SNR gain formula (2 marks):**

$$G_{SNR} = \frac{1}{1 - G_p} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8464} = \frac{1}{0.1536}$$

$$G_{SNR} \approx 6.510$$

**Step 3: Convert to decibels (1 mark):**

$$G_{SNR,dB} = 10 \log_{10}(6.510) \approx 8.14 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: ADPCM bit rate at 4 bits/sample (1 mark):**

$$R_{ADPCM} = f_s \times n = 8000 \times 4 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 5: Bandwidth savings (1 mark):**

$$\text{Savings} = R_{PCM} - R_{ADPCM} = 64 - 32 = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 6: Percentage reduction (1 mark):**

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{32}{64} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Final Answer:** DPCM SNR gain ≈ **8.14 dB**, ADPCM bit rate = **32 kbps**, bandwidth savings = **32 kbps (50% reduction)**.

---

### **Part B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, ESE Module 3 — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **G.726 ADPCM decoder**. How does the decoder stay synchronized with the encoder during transmission?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ADPCM system uses a 4-bit quantizer with adaptive step size. The current step size is $\Delta(n) = 0.04$ V and the quantizer index is $I(n) = 12$. From the G.726 multiplier table, $M[12] = 1.10$. Compute the next step size $\Delta(n+1)$. If the encoder transmits 4 bits per sample at 8 kHz, calculate the **total number of bits transmitted in 1 minute** of speech.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B:**

##### **Part (a) — ADPCM Decoder [7 marks]**

The G.726 ADPCM decoder (refer to Section 4.2 diagram) performs the **reverse operation** of the encoder:

1. **Inverse Adaptive Quantizer** (2 marks): Receives the 4-bit index $I(n)$ from the channel and reconstructs the quantized differential $d_q(n) = \Delta(n) \cdot Q^{-1}[I(n)]$.
2. **Adder** (1 mark): Adds $d_q(n)$ to the predicted sample $\hat{x}(n)$ to form the reconstructed sample $\tilde{x}(n)$.
3. **Adaptive Predictor** (2 marks): Identical structure to the encoder (2-pole, 6-zero) and produces $\hat{x}(n+1)$ from the reconstructed history.
4. **Adaptation Logic** (1 mark): Same step-size and coefficient update rules as encoder, ensuring that $\Delta$ and $a_i, b_i$ evolve identically on both sides.
5. **DAC + Reconstruction LPF** (1 mark): Converts digital output to analog speech.

**Synchronization Mechanism (2 of the 7 marks):**

The decoder **mirrors the encoder's local decoder** exactly:
- Same multiplier table $M[I]$ for step-size adaptation.
- Same LMS rule for coefficient adaptation.
- Same predictor structure and initial state.

Since both sides use **only the transmitted index $I(n)$** (not the original sample) to update state, the predictor outputs $\hat{x}(n)$ remain bit-identical at encoder and decoder. This is the **fundamental design principle of differential coding**: errors do not accumulate because the only information crossing the channel is the differential index, and both endpoints evolve from the same differential history.

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Apply the step-size adaptation equation (3 marks):**

$$\Delta(n+1) = \Delta(n) \cdot M[I(n)]$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta(n+1) = 0.04 \times 1.10 = 0.044 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Interpretation (1 mark):**

The step size has **increased by 10%** because the previous quantizer index $I(n) = 12$ indicated a relatively large differential signal — the quantizer is "opening up" to avoid clipping the next sample.

**Step 3: Compute bit rate (1 mark):**

$$R_{ADPCM} = 4 \text{ bits} \times 8000 \text{ samples/s} = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 4: Compute total bits in 1 minute (2 marks):**

$$T = 60 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Total bits} = R_{ADPCM} \times T = 32{,}000 \times 60 = 1{,}920{,}000 \text{ bits}$$

Converting to bytes (÷ 8):

$$\text{Total bytes} = \frac{1{,}920{,}000}{8} = 240{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 240 \text{ KB}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta(n+1) = \mathbf{0.044 \text{ V}}$; Total bits in 1 minute = **1,920,000 bits (240 KB)**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in ADPCM Questions**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the local decoder in the encoder (–2 marks):** Many students draw the encoder without showing the inverse quantizer feeding back to the predictor. This is the **most commonly lost mark** in 7-mark block-diagram questions. The local decoder is what guarantees encoder–decoder synchronization.
> 
> 2. **Confusing correlation coefficient $\rho$ with predictor coefficient $a_1$ (–1 to 2 marks):** They are **equal only for a 1st-order optimal MMSE predictor** on a stationary process. For higher-order predictors, $a_i \neq \rho$ in general. State the assumption explicitly.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up DPCM and ADPCM bit rates (–1 mark):** DPCM does **not** always equal 32 kbps — it depends on the quantizer bits. ADPCM = DPCM + adaptation. Be precise.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the locked-state condition $\prod M[k] = 1$ (–1 mark):** If asked to "design a multiplier table," always verify that the product over all 16 indices equals 1, otherwise the step size will drift.
> 
> 5. **Unit errors in step-size problems (–0.5 mark):** $\Delta$ is in volts (V); bit rate is in kbps; bandwidth is in Hz. Always carry and verify units.
> 
> 6. **Sign-LMS vs LMS confusion (–1 mark):** G.726 uses the **sign-LMS** algorithm (sign of the error and sign of the regressor) for hardware simplicity. The full LMS with $d_q(n) \cdot \tilde{x}(n-i)$ is rarely implemented in DSP.
> 
> 7. **Missing the G.726 "toll quality" descriptor (–0.5 mark):** The phrase "toll quality" is a **standard ITU term** meaning the speech is indistinguishable from 64 kbps PCM in formal listening tests (MOS ≥ 4.0). Use it whenever describing G.726 quality.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: ADPCM in Speech Coding**

- [x] **ADPCM = Adaptive DPCM** = DPCM + Adaptive Quantizer + Adaptive Predictor.
- [x] **G.726** is the canonical ADPCM standard: **4 bits/sample @ 8 kHz → 32 kbps** with **16-level non-uniform quantizer**.
- [x] **Compression ratio over PCM = 2:1** (8 bits → 4 bits), saving **50% bandwidth**.
- [x] **Predictor structure**: **2-pole + 6-zero** (Pole-Zero) to model vocal tract formants.
- [x] **Step-size adaptation rule**: $\Delta(n+1) = \Delta(n) \cdot M[I(n)]$ — multiplicative, log-domain additive.
- [x] **Locked-state condition**: $\prod_{k=0}^{15} M[k] = 1$ — prevents step-size drift.
- [x] **Predictor adaptation**: **Sign-LMS algorithm** (used in G.726) with leakage factor $\beta$ and step size $\alpha$.
- [x] **DPCM SNR gain**: $G_{SNR} = 1/(1 - \rho^2)$ — in dB: $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$.
- [x] **Local decoder in encoder** = **inverse quantizer feedback path** — essential for encoder–decoder sync.
- [x] **Decoder mirrors encoder** exactly — same $M[I]$ table, same predictor, same LMS rule.
- [x] **Bit rate formula**: $R_b = f_s \cdot n$ bits/s, e.g., $8000 \times 4 = 32$ kbps.
- [x] **1 minute of ADPCM speech @ 32 kbps = 240 KB** (1,920,000 bits).
- [x] **Quality label**: G.726 ADPCM is **"toll quality"** (MOS ≥ 4.0).
- [x] **Standards family**: G.721 (32 kbps, deprecated) → **G.726 (32/24/16 kbps, current)** → G.727 (embedded ADPCM).
- [x] **Applications**: PSTN trunks, DECT cordless phones, VoIP gateways, satellite voice, voicemail storage, Bluetooth SCO.
- [x] **Difference from APCM**: APCM has **no predictor**; ADPCM has both predictor and quantizer adaptive.
- [x] **Key equation for examination**: $d_q(n) = \Delta(n) \cdot Q[(x(n) - \hat{x}(n))/\Delta(n)]$.
- [x] **Why dual adaptation?**: Speech is **non-stationary** (changes every 10–30 ms) — a fixed predictor/quantizer cannot track the changing statistics; hence both must adapt.
- [x] **Memory requirement**: Encoder and decoder must store the **last 6 reconstructed samples** and **last 6 quantized differentials** (for the 6-zero predictor) and **last 2 reconstructed samples** (for the 2-pole predictor).
- [x] **Attack vs. decay**: Multiplier table is designed for **faster attack** (overshoot on transients) than **decay** (slow recovery) — matches auditory perception.

> [!NOTE] **Final Exam Tip**
> When a 14-mark question asks you to "explain ADPCM encoder with block diagram," always include **four components** for full marks: (1) **Differencer**, (2) **Adaptive Quantizer**, (3) **Local Decoder (Inverse Quantizer + Adder)**, (4) **Adaptive Predictor with adaptation logic**. Skipping any one of these typically costs **1.5–2 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vocoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vocoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vocoder** (short for **Voice Coder**) is a category of speech analysis-synthesis systems used to encode and decode human speech into a compact digital representation for low-bit-rate transmission or storage. It is the foundational technology behind modern speech codecs used in mobile telephony, military communications, and secure voice links. A vocoder does not transmit the speech waveform itself; instead, it transmits a compact set of **parametric descriptors** (formants, pitch, energy, gain, filter coefficients) that mathematically describe how the speech was produced, allowing a synthesizer at the receiver to reconstruct intelligible (though not necessarily high-fidelity) speech.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECNT527 M3):** Vocoders are analysis-synthesis systems that exploit the source-filter model of speech production to achieve low bit-rate speech coding. The two principal families studied are **Channel Vocoders** and **Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) Vocoders**, with extensions to **Formant**, **Phase**, and **Cepstrum** vocoders.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postcard" View of Speech

Imagine you want to describe a famous painting to a friend over the phone. Instead of describing every brushstroke, you say: *"It's a sunset over a mountain, mostly orange, with a dark peak in the middle, in the style of Van Gogh."* The friend, knowing how sunsets and Van Gogh look, can mentally reconstruct a believable painting without seeing it. The painting is the actual speech; your short description is the *vocoder parameters*; and your friend's imagination is the *synthesizer*. The "vocoded" version is smaller, faster to send, but slightly different from the original.

### 1.3 Why Vocoders Matter in Multimedia

| Domain | Use Case | Typical Bit-Rate |
|---|---|---|
| **Military / Tactical Radio** | Secure, narrow-band voice | **2.4 kbps** (LPC-10e) |
| **Mobile Telephony (2G/3G)** | GSM/CDMA voice channels | **4.75 – 13 kbps** |
| **VoIP / Streaming** | Internet voice | **6.4 – 24 kbps** |
| **Archival / Storage** | Compact voice mail | **2.4 – 4.8 kbps** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** A *codec* preserves the waveform (CELP, ADPCM). A *vocoder* preserves *intelligibility* and *naturalness* of speech, even if the synthesized waveform is mathematically different from the original. Speech coded at 2.4 kbps sounds robotic but is still perfectly understandable — that is the magic of the source-filter model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Source–Filter Model of Speech Production

All vocoders are built on the **Source–Filter Model**, proposed by **Gunnar Fant (1960)**. It states that the speech signal $s(n)$ is the product of an **excitation source** $u(n)$ and a **vocal-tract filter** $H(z)$:

$$s(n) = u(n) * h(n)$$

- **Excitation $u(n)$:** Either a *periodic pulse train* (voiced, e.g., vowels, "mmm") or *white noise* (unvoiced, e.g., "sss", "fff").
- **Vocal-Tract Filter $H(z)$:** Resonances of the mouth, nose, and throat, modeled as an all-pole filter.
- **Radiation Load $R(z) \approx 1 - z^{-1}$:** Models lip radiation.

In the z-domain:

$$S(z) = U(z) \cdot H(z) \cdot R(z)$$

### 2.2 Channel Vocoder (Dudley, 1939) — The Original Vocoder

**Operational Logic:**
1. Split the speech spectrum into **N = 16 – 20 adjacent frequency bands** using a **bank of band-pass filters**.
2. For each band, compute its **energy (envelope)**.
3. Separately, estimate the **pitch period** $T_0$ (or mark the frame as unvoiced).
4. Transmit: N band energies + pitch + voiced/unvoiced flag.
5. At the receiver: synthesize using either a buzz source (voiced) or hiss source (unvoiced) passed through a *reconstructed* bank of band-pass filters with amplitudes set by the transmitted energies.

**Why it works:** The vocal tract acts as a slowly-varying filter; its spectral envelope can be sampled at ~30–50 Hz, so the per-band energy needs only **~5 bits per update**. This drops the bit-rate from **64 kbps** (PCM) to roughly **2.4 – 4.8 kbps**.

### 2.3 Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) Vocoder

LPC, formalized by **Itakura & Saito (1968)** and **Makhoul (1975)**, is the modern workhorse. It models the vocal-tract filter as an **all-pole IIR filter** of order $p$ (typically $p = 8$ to $14$):

$$H(z) = \frac{G}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

The synthesizer equation (time domain) is:

$$s(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k) + G \cdot u(n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LPC Prediction Principle:** "The current speech sample $s(n)$ can be *linearly predicted* from the past $p$ samples" — the prediction error (residual) is the excitation. The coefficients $\{a_k\}$ that minimize mean-squared error ARE the vocal-tract parameters.

The transmitted parameters are:
- **LPC coefficients** $\{a_k\}$ (or equivalent: reflection coefficients, LSF, LSP) — $p$ of them.
- **Gain** $G$ — energy of the residual.
- **Pitch period** $T_0$ — for voiced frames.
- **Voiced/Unvoiced flag** V/UV.

### 2.4 Other Notable Vocoders

| Vocoder Type | Core Idea | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Formant Vocoder** | Transmit only formant frequencies $F_1, F_2, F_3$ & bandwidths | Very-low-bit secure voice |
| **Phase Vocoder** | Operates on STFT; tracks phase derivatives for time-stretching | Audio effects (not low-bit coding) |
| **Cepstrum Vocoder** | Separates source and filter via cepstral liftering | Pitch detection, analysis |
| **Voice-Excited Vocoder (VEV)** | Transmits a downsampled residual + baseband | Improved naturalness @ 9.6 kbps |
| **LPC-10 / LPC-10e** | US Fed Std, $p=10$, 22.5 ms frame | Military 2.4 kbps |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** All formulas below are examinable. The autocorrelation method and Levinson-Durbin recursion are the most frequently asked derivations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) + G \cdot u(n)$ | LPC synthesis equation | sample-domain |
| 2 | $H(z) = \frac{G}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | All-pole vocal-tract model | z-domain |
| 3 | $R(k) = \sum_{n} s(n)\, s(n-k)$ | Short-time autocorrelation | energy units |
| 4 | $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(\vert i - k \vert) = R(i), \;\; i = 1, \dots, p$ | Normal (Yule–Walker) equations | linear system |
| 5 | $E_p = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)$ | Minimum mean-squared prediction error | energy |
| 6 | $k_m = \frac{R(m) - \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} R(\vert m-k \vert)}{E_{m-1}}$ | Levinson–Durbin recursion for $k_m$ | reflection coeff. |
| 7 | $E_m = (1 - k_m^2)\, E_{m-1}$ | Lattice update of error energy | energy |
| 8 | $T_{frame} = N/F_s$, $\;B = \frac{N_{bits}}{T_{frame}}$ | Bit-rate $B$ from frame bits & duration | bits/sec |
| 9 | $\text{LPC-10e Bit Budget} = 7 \times 8 + 1 + 5 + 6 + 1 = 54$ bits / frame | At 44.4 fps $\Rightarrow$ **2.4 kbps** | bits/frame |
| 10 | $F_0 = F_s / T_0$ | Fundamental frequency from pitch period | Hz |

> **Note on the table:** the symbol $\vert i - k \vert$ uses `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) so the markdown table parser is not broken.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

LPC vocoders underpin the **GSM Full-Rate (FR)** codec's residual-excitation extension and are the conceptual ancestor of **Code-Excited Linear Prediction (CELP)** used in 3G/4G voice. **Speex, Opus, MELP (Mixed-Excitation LPC)**, and **Codec2** all descend from the LPC vocoder family. Modern secure-radio systems (e.g., **ANDVT**, **MELPe**) are direct vocoder descendants operating at 2.4 kbps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: LPC Predictor from Minimum Mean-Squared Error

We want coefficients $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p\}$ that minimize the energy of the **prediction error** $e(n)$ over a windowed speech frame $s_w(n)$ of length $N$.

**Step 1 — Define the predicted sample:**

The linear predictor of order $p$ estimates the current sample as a weighted sum of the $p$ past samples:

$$\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k)$$

**Step 2 — Define the prediction error (residual):**

The error is the difference between the actual and predicted sample:

$$e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k)$$

**Step 3 — Define the total squared error over the frame:**

We sum the squared errors over the entire analysis frame to form the cost function $E$:

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e(n)^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k) \right]^2$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to each $a_i$ and set to zero:**

Taking $\partial E / \partial a_i = 0$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, p$ gives the orthogonality principle (error orthogonal to each regressor):

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial a_i} = -2 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n-i) \left[ s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, s(n-k) \right] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand and swap the summations:**

$$-2 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n) s(n-i) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n-k) s(n-i) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Identify the autocorrelation function:**

By definition, $R(i) = \sum_{n} s(n) s(n-i)$. Substituting:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, R(\vert i - k \vert) = R(i), \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

This is the **Yule–Walker normal equation**. Solving this Toeplitz system (via Levinson–Durbin) yields the optimal LPC coefficients.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bit-Rate of the LPC-10e Federal Standard Vocoder

**Given (US Federal Standard 1015):**
- Frame duration: $T_{frame} = 22.5$ ms
- LPC order: $p = 10$
- Frame rate: $F_f = 1 / T_{frame} = 1 / 0.0225 \approx 44.44$ frames/sec
- Bits per frame: 54

**Step 1 — Compute the bit rate $B$:**

$$B = F_f \times N_{bits/frame}$$

$$B = \left(\frac{1}{0.0225 \text{ s}}\right) \times 54 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate numerically:**

$$B = 44.444 \times 54 = 2400 \text{ bits/second} = 2.4 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the bit allocation per frame:**

| Parameter | Bits |
|---|---|
| 10 Reflection coefficients (log-area-ratio coded) | $10 \times 5 = 50$ → transmitted as 8 pairs = 41 (interleaved) |
| Pitch (log-quantized) | 6 |
| Gain (log-quantized) | 5 |
| Voiced/Unvoiced | 1 |
| Sync | 1 |
| **Total** | **54 bits/frame** |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Step Matters

| Step | Purpose | Failure Mode if Skipped |
|---|---|---|
| Define $\hat{s}(n)$ | Establish linear model | Cannot use MMSE framework |
| Define $e(n)$ | Separate source from filter | Cannot isolate excitation |
| Sum $e^2(n)$ | Create a quadratic objective | No analytic solution possible |
| Differentiate, set to 0 | Enforce minimum-error optimality | Coefficients not optimal |
| Autocorrelation substitution | Convert to a linear system | Stays non-linear in $a_k$ |
| Levinson–Durbin solution | Exploit Toeplitz symmetry | $O(p^2)$ becomes $O(p^3)$ |

### 3.4 Derivation: Gain $G$ from Residual Energy

After $\{a_k\}$ are known, the residual $e(n)$ is computed. The gain $G$ is chosen so that the synthetic residual has the same energy as the original:

$$G = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e(n)^2}{N}} = \sqrt{E_p / N}$$

where $E_p$ is the **minimum MSE** from the Yule–Walker solution. In practice, $G$ is log-quantized for transmission, exploiting the wide dynamic range of speech.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
