## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a vocoder. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a channel vocoder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **vocoder** is a speech analysis-synthesis system that compresses speech by transmitting only its *parametric descriptors* (spectral envelope, pitch, gain) instead of the raw waveform, exploiting the **source-filter model** of speech production.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 above. The encoder passes speech through a **bank of band-pass filters**, computes the **energy in each band**, estimates the **pitch** and **voiced/unvoiced (V/UV) flag**, and transmits these as a low-bit-rate stream. The **decoder** uses a buzz source (voiced) or hiss source (unvoiced) passed through a matching **filter bank** whose gains are set by the transmitted energies to reconstruct speech.

**Working (2 marks):** Typical bit-rate: **2.4 – 4.8 kbps**; bands: 16–20; update rate: 30–50 Hz. It works because the vocal-tract envelope is *slowly varying* compared to the waveform itself.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three advantages and two limitations of LPC vocoders.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Very low bit-rate** (down to **2.4 kbps** for LPC-10e) — ~25× lower than 64 kbps PCM.
2. **Accurate parametric model** of vocal tract via all-pole filter of order $p \approx 10$.
3. **Analytically tractable** — coefficients obtained by solving the Yule–Walker equations with the Levinson–Durbin recursion in $O(p^2)$ time.

**Limitations:**
1. **Synthetic, robotic quality** — only an *approximate* model of speech; no fine spectral detail.
2. **Sensitive to pitch errors** — wrong pitch detection on a voiced frame produces buzzy artefacts.
3. *(Bonus)* Requires **V/UV decision** which fails on mixed-excitation sounds (e.g., voiced fricatives).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (ESE, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) vocoder** system. Describe the role of the **LPC analyzer** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Yule–Walker normal equations** for finding the LPC coefficients $\{a_k\}$ using the **principle of minimum mean-squared prediction error**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LPC Vocoder — Block Diagram & Analyzer Role (7 marks)**

Refer to the **Section 4.3** block diagram. Key points to write:

1. **Pre-processing:** The speech signal is **digitized** at $F_s$ (e.g., 8 kHz), blocked into frames of length $N$ (e.g., 22.5 ms = 180 samples), and **windowed** with a Hamming window to reduce spectral leakage. *(1 mark)*

2. **Autocorrelation computation:** For each frame, compute $R(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} s_w(n)\, s_w(n+k)$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, p$. *(1 mark)*

3. **Levinson–Durbin recursion:** Solve the Toeplitz system efficiently to get $\{a_k\}$ and the residual energy $E_p$. *(1 mark)*

4. **Residual & gain:** Compute $e(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$. The gain $G = \sqrt{E_p / N}$ is log-quantized. *(1 mark)*

5. **Pitch detection & V/UV:** Apply an algorithm (autocorrelation of $e(n)$ or waveform) to estimate the fundamental period $T_0$, and classify the frame as voiced or unvoiced. *(1 mark)*

6. **Quantization & transmission:** Convert all parameters to bits (54 bits/frame for LPC-10e) and send at **2.4 kbps**. The decoder performs the inverse operations. *(1 mark)*

7. **Synthesis equation:** $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \hat{s}(n-k) + G \cdot u(n)$ with $u(n)$ being a pulse train (voiced) or white noise (unvoiced). *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Derivation of Yule–Walker Equations (7 marks)**

Step-by-step derivation as in **Section 3.1** above. Allocation of marks:

| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Define predictor $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$ | 1 |
| Define error $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ | 1 |
| Define total error $E = \sum e^2(n)$ | 1 |
| Differentiate $E$ w.r.t. $a_i$ and set to 0 | 1 |
| Use orthogonality principle to derive $\sum a_k R(\vert i-k \vert) = R(i)$ | 2 |
| Final Yule–Walker equation in matrix form | 1 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Channel Vocoder** system in detail. Compare the channel vocoder with the **LPC vocoder** under the headings: (i) parametric representation, (ii) bit-rate, (iii) speech quality, and (iv) computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **LPC-10e vocoder** uses frames of duration 22.5 ms and transmits 54 bits per frame. Compute the bit-rate in kbps. If the system is upgraded to a **Cepstrum-based residual vocoder** that doubles the bits/frame but halves the frame duration, what is the new bit-rate? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Channel Vocoder & Comparison (7 marks)**

**Channel Vocoder Explanation (4 marks):** See Section 2.2. The speech is split into 16–20 band-pass filter outputs, each envelope-detected and quantized. The **pitch** and **V/UV** flag are transmitted separately. At the decoder, a buzz/hiss source is filtered by a matching band-pass bank whose gains equal the received energies. Bit-rate: **2.4 – 4.8 kbps**.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Channel Vocoder | LPC Vocoder |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Parametric representation | Spectral energy in $N$ bands | $p$ LPC coefficients + gain + pitch + V/UV |
| (ii) Bit-rate | **2.4 – 4.8 kbps** | **2.4 kbps (LPC-10e)** |
| (iii) Speech quality | Robotic, band-limited | More natural, smoother formants |
| (iv) Complexity | $O(N \cdot L)$ filters | $O(p^2)$ Levinson–Durbin |

---

**(b) Bit-Rate Calculation (7 marks)**

**Part 1 — LPC-10e bit-rate (3 marks):**

$$T_{frame} = 22.5 \text{ ms} = 0.0225 \text{ s}$$

$$B_1 = \frac{54 \text{ bits}}{0.0225 \text{ s}} = 2400 \text{ bits/s} = \boxed{2.4 \text{ kbps}}$$

**Part 2 — Upgraded vocoder (4 marks):**

New bits per frame: $N_{bits}' = 2 \times 54 = 108$ bits
New frame duration: $T_{frame}' = 0.0225 / 2 = 0.01125$ s

$$B_2 = \frac{108}{0.01125} = 9600 \text{ bits/s} = \boxed{9.6 \text{ kbps}}$$

> The bit-rate **quadruples** because doubling bits and halving duration compound multiplicatively. This matches the **Voice-Excited Vocoder (VEV)** which operates at 9.6 kbps by transmitting baseband residual.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing vocoder with codec** — saying "vocoder preserves the waveform" is *wrong*; that describes CELP. Vocoders sacrifice waveform fidelity for parametric efficiency.
> 2. **Forgetting the radiation model $R(z)$** in the source-filter model — KTU examiners explicitly test for completeness of the speech production equation.
> 3. **Sign errors in LPC synthesis equation** — the standard form is $s(n) = \sum a_k s(n-k) + G \cdot u(n)$, NOT $s(n) = \sum a_k s(n-k) - G \cdot u(n)$. Both forms appear in textbooks; stick to the *one* taught in class.
> 4. **Skipping orthogonality in the derivation** — setting $\partial E / \partial a_i = 0$ is **mandatory** for full marks on the Yule–Walker derivation.
> 5. **Bit-rate miscalculation** — students often forget to convert **ms → s** and write $22.5 \times 54 = 1215$ "kbps" (units disaster). Always check units.
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — the **band-pass filter bank** and **pitch detector** must be explicitly named. Unlabelled arrows earn zero.
> 7. **V/UV flag confusion** — the flag is *one bit* per frame, not a continuous value.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vocoder = Voice Coder** — analysis-synthesis system, NOT a waveform codec. **(High-yield)**
- Built on **Source–Filter model** by Gunnar Fant: $S(z) = U(z) \cdot H(z) \cdot R(z)$.
- **Channel vocoder:** splits spectrum into **16–20 bands**, transmits band energies + pitch + V/UV. Bit-rate **2.4 – 4.8 kbps**.
- **LPC vocoder:** models vocal tract as an **all-pole filter of order $p$**; transmits $\{a_k\}$, $G$, $T_0$, V/UV.
- **LPC-10e:** 10th-order, **22.5 ms** frames, **54 bits/frame**, **2.4 kbps** — Federal Standard 1015. **(Numerically important)**
- **Yule–Walker equations** arise from minimizing mean-squared prediction error: $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(\vert i-k \vert) = R(i)$.
- **Levinson–Durbin recursion** solves Yule–Walker in $O(p^2)$ time using reflection coefficients $k_m$ with the update $E_m = (1-k_m^2) E_{m-1}$.
- **LPC synthesis:** $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \hat{s}(n-k) + G \cdot u(n)$, where $u(n)$ is a pulse train (voiced) or white noise (unvoiced).
- **Bit-rate formula:** $B = N_{bits/frame} / T_{frame}$.
- **Formant vocoders** track $F_1, F_2, F_3$ only — even lower bit-rate, less natural.
- **Phase vocoder** is an STFT-based tool, mainly for **time-stretching and pitch-shifting**, not low-bit-rate coding.
- **Vocoded speech** sounds **robotic** at low rates — naturalness is recovered by **VEV (9.6 kbps)** or by **mixed-excitation** models (MELPe).
- Modern descendants: **CELP, ACELP, Speex, Opus, Codec2** — all built on the LPC foundation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Video Compression Techniques - Introduction to Video Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Video Compression** (also called *Video Coding* or *Video Encoding*) is the process of reducing the amount of data required to represent a digital video stream by exploiting statistical, spatial, temporal, and psycho-visual redundancies, while preserving a level of perceptual quality acceptable for the intended application. The reconstructed video after decompression is allowed to differ from the original (lossy) or be identical (lossless), depending on the coding strategy.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, video compression is positioned as a sub-discipline of **Multimedia Data Compression** that specifically addresses the temporal dimension (successive frames) in addition to the spatial dimension handled by still-image compression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Basic Video Compression Techniques" in Module 3 covers the *introduction, taxonomy of redundancies, intra/inter-frame prediction philosophy, and the generic encoder–decoder (codec) pipeline* — NOT the detailed internals of any specific standard (those are covered later in the module or in advanced courses).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Flipbook Telegraph"

Imagine you are sending a **flipbook animation** of a person waving their hand across 30 pages per second. The naïve way is to photograph every single page and post all 30 photographs. But notice:

- **Most of the picture is the same** (background, clothing) → this is **spatial redundancy**.
- **The hand barely moves between pages** → if the receiver already knows "page 1", you only need to say *"move hand 2 cm to the right"* → this is **temporal redundancy**.
- **The eye cannot detect differences finer than a certain shade of red** → you don't need to send every exact colour → this is **psycho-visual redundancy**.

A video codec is essentially a **clever telegraph operator** who, instead of sending 30 full pictures every second, sends *one full picture plus tiny "change instructions"* — slashing the data dramatically while the human viewer sees the same waving hand.

## 1.3 Why is Video Compression Necessary? — The Raw Data Problem

A standard definition (SD) uncompressed digital video at Common Intermediate Format (CIF) of **352 × 288** pixels, **30 frames per second (fps)**, **4:2:0 chroma sub-sampling**, and **8 bits per colour sample** produces:

- Luma samples per frame: 352 × 288 = **101,376**
- Chroma samples per frame (4:2:0): 2 × (176 × 144) = **50,688**
- Total samples per frame: **152,064 bytes**
- Data rate at 30 fps: 152,064 × 30 ≈ **4.56 MB/s ≈ 36.5 Mbps**

For **HD 1920 × 1080 @ 30 fps**, the raw rate exceeds **1.5 Gbps** — completely impractical for storage or streaming over typical broadband (where **broadband** is defined by KTU/ITU as anything above 256 kbps). Hence **video compression is not optional; it is mandatory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Memorise:** A single uncompressed 1080p frame at 8-bit RGB ≈ **6.22 MB**. One minute of such video = **~11 GB**. Compression ratios of **50:1 to 200:1** are routinely achieved by modern codecs while remaining visually indistinguishable from the original.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Redundancies Exploited

| **Redundancy Type** | **Also Called** | **What is Repeated?** | **Where Exploited** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spatial | Intra-frame | Pixels similar to their neighbours within the same frame | DCT/wavelet transform, intra prediction |
| Temporal | Inter-frame | Objects repeated across consecutive frames | Motion estimation & compensation |
| Spectral | Cross-channel | Colour planes correlated with each other | Colour space conversion (RGB→YCbCr) |
| Psycho-visual | Perceptual | Imperceptible details to the human eye | Quantisation, sub-band coding |
| Statistical / Coding | Entropy | Frequent symbol patterns | Huffman, Arithmetic, CABAC coding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Questions on *"list the types of redundancies exploited in video compression"* appear almost every cycle. Memorise the table above verbatim.

## 1.5 Lossy vs Lossless Compression

**Lossless compression** allows perfect reconstruction of the original video (e.g., for medical/archival footage). Achievable ratios are modest, typically **1.5:1 to 3:1**.

**Lossy compression** sacrifices some fidelity for dramatically higher ratios (**20:1 to 1000:1**). All consumer video standards (MPEG, H.26x) are lossy. The trick is to discard only the information the human visual system (HVS) cannot perceive — the science of **perceptual coding**.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Video Codec Architecture

Every modern video compression standard (MPEG-1/2/4, H.261/263/264/265) is structurally built on the same **hybrid predictive + transform codec** model. Understanding this skeleton is essential before studying any specific standard.

### 2.1.1 Encoder Side (Logical Pipeline)

1. **Frame Reordering / Group of Pictures (GOP) structuring** — frames classified as **I, P, B**.
2. **Pre-processing & colour conversion** — RGB → YCbCr (4:2:0 sub-sampling).
3. **Partitioning** — frame divided into **macroblocks** (16 × 16 luma + 8 × 8 chroma) or **coding tree units** in newer standards.
4. **Prediction stage**:
   - Intra prediction (within frame) OR
   - Inter prediction (motion-compensated from reference frames).
5. **Residual computation** — Difference between original block and prediction.
6. **Transform** — Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) or similar.
7. **Quantisation** — lossy stage that throws away perceptually-irrelevant bits.
8. **Entropy coding** — Huffman / Arithmetic / CABAC, producing the final bitstream.
9. **Inverse quantisation & inverse transform** — locally reconstructs reference for next prediction.
10. **Deblocking / in-loop filtering** — smooths coding artefacts before storing as reference.

### 2.1.2 Decoder Side

The decoder performs steps 8 → 7 → 6 → 5 → 4 in reverse. **Crucially: motion vectors, prediction modes, and transform coefficients are all transmitted in the bitstream**, so the decoder can reconstruct identically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hybrid?** "Hybrid" because the codec combines two paradigms: (a) **predictive coding in the spatial/temporal domain** (removes redundancy) and (b) **transform coding in the frequency domain** (packs energy compactly for efficient quantisation).

## 2.2 Frame Types — I, P, B

| **Frame Type** | **Full Name** | **Reference?** | **Predicts?** | **Typical Size** | **Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I-frame** | Intra-coded | None needed | Nothing | Largest | Random access points, scene changes |
| **P-frame** | Predictive | Past I or P | One direction (forward) | Medium | Removes temporal redundancy |
| **B-frame** | Bi-predictive | Past & Future | Both directions | Smallest | Highest compression |

A **Group of Pictures (GOP)** is the repeating structure of I, P, B frames between two I-frames, commonly written as **M N** where **M** is the I-to-I distance and **N** is the I-to-P distance. Example: `IBBPBBPBBPBB` is a **GOP 12,3**.

## 2.3 Motion Estimation and Compensation — The Heart of Inter-Coding

Since adjacent frames differ mainly due to object motion, the encoder searches each **macroblock** of the current frame inside the **previous frame** (or future frame for B-frames) for the best matching block. The displacement is called the **Motion Vector (MV)**.

- **Block Matching Algorithms** (KTU scope): *Exhaustive Full Search, Three-Step Search (TSS), Diamond Search, Hexagonal Search*.
- **Search range**: typically ±16 to ±128 pixels depending on resolution and standard.
- **Block sizes**: 16 × 16 (legacy), 8 × 8, and down to 4 × 4 (H.264+) for fine detail.

The transmitted residual = current block − motion-compensated prediction. The decoder receives the MV, fetches the reference block, adds the residual, and reconstructs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse *motion estimation* (encoder-side search, computationally expensive) with *motion compensation* (decoder-side block reconstruction). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In all formulas below, absolute value is written as `\vert x \vert` (LaTeX form), never with the literal pipe symbol `|x|`, to prevent markdown table breakage.

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raw frame size (4:2:0) | $F = W \cdot H + 2 \cdot \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot \dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{3}{2} WH$ | bytes (8-bit) | W, H = width, height in pixels |
| 2 | Uncompressed bit rate | $R = F \cdot f_{ps} \cdot 8$ | bits per second (bps) | $f_{ps}$ = frames per second |
| 3 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{R_{original}}{R_{compressed}}$ | dimensionless | $> 1$ means compression achieved |
| 4 | Bits per Pixel (bpp) | $bpp = \dfrac{R}{W \cdot H \cdot f_{ps}}$ | bits/pixel | Typical: 0.1–0.5 for compressed video |
| 5 | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\vert I(i,j) - \hat I(i,j) \vert^{2}$ | — | Pixel-domain fidelity metric |
| 6 | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^{2}}{MSE}\right)$ | decibels (dB) | $L$ = peak sample value (e.g., 255) |
| 7 | SAD (matching cost) | $SAD(u,v) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\vert C_{ij} - R_{i+u,\,j+v}\vert$ | — | Used in block motion search |
| 8 | Entropy (Shannon) | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_{i} \log_{2} p_{i}$ | bits/symbol | Lower bound on lossless coding length |
| 9 | Coding efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{H}{L_{avg}}$ | 0 to 1 | $L_{avg}$ = average codeword length |
| 10 | Quantisation step (uniform) | $q_{step} = \dfrac{\Delta}{Q_{p}}$ | — | $Q_{p}$ = quantisation parameter |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Video compression is the **enabling technology** for:

- **OTT Streaming** (Netflix, YouTube, Hotstar) — H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC, AV1.
- **Video conferencing** (Zoom, Teams, Meet) — H.264 SVC, VP9.
- **Broadcast television** — MPEG-2 (DVB), H.264 (DVB-T2/ATSC), H.265 (ATSC 3.0).
- **Surveillance** — H.265+ with smart region-of-interest coding.
- **Medical imaging** — JPEG 2000 (lossless) for DICOM archival.
- **UAV/drone links** — H.265 low-latency profile.
- **VR/AR** — MV-HEVC and VVC for multi-view spherical video.

## 2.6 Classification of Video Compression Standards

| **Era** | **Standards Family** | **Typical Use** | **Typical Bitrate (SD)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Early 1990s | H.261, MPEG-1 | ISDN video, VCD | 1–2 Mbps |
| Mid 1990s | MPEG-2, H.262 | DVD, DVB, ATSC | 4–8 Mbps |
| Late 1990s | H.263, MPEG-4 Part 2 | 3G video, internet | 0.5–1 Mbps |
| 2003 | H.264 / AVC | Blu-ray, HD streaming | 1–3 Mbps |
| 2013 | H.265 / HEVC | 4K streaming | 0.5–1.5 Mbps |
| 2020+ | VVC, AV1, EVC | 8K, screen content | 0.3–1 Mbps |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Raw Bit-Rate of Uncompressed Video (4:2:0)

**Goal:** Prove that CIF (352 × 288) video at 30 fps produces ≈ 36.5 Mbps.

**Step 1 — Luma samples per frame.** For 4:2:0 sub-sampling, the luma plane (Y) is at full resolution:

$$Y_{samples} = W \times H = 352 \times 288 = 101{,}376$$

**Step 2 — Chroma samples per frame.** In 4:2:0, both chroma planes (Cb and Cr) are sub-sampled by 2 in each dimension:

$$Chroma_{samples} = 2 \times \left(\dfrac{W}{2} \times \dfrac{H}{2}\right) = 2 \times (176 \times 144) = 50{,}688$$

**Step 3 — Total samples per frame.** Add luma and chroma samples:

$$F_{samples} = Y_{samples} + Chroma_{samples} = 101{,}376 + 50{,}688 = 152{,}064$$

**Step 4 — Bytes per frame at 8 bits/sample.** Since each sample is **1 byte**:

$$F_{bytes} = \dfrac{F_{samples} \times 8}{8} = 152{,}064 \text{ bytes} \approx 148.5 \text{ KiB}$$

**Step 5 — Bytes per second at 30 fps.** Multiply by frame rate:

$$D_{rate\_B} = F_{bytes} \times f_{ps} = 152{,}064 \times 30 = 4{,}561{,}920 \text{ bytes/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to bits per second.** Multiply by 8:

$$R_{raw} = 4{,}561{,}920 \times 8 = 36{,}495{,}360 \text{ bps} \approx 36.5 \text{ Mbps}$$

This confirms the intuition that even SD video exceeds 30 Mbps raw, and HD/4K easily crosses Gbps thresholds. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compression Ratio and Bits-per-Pixel for H.264 CIF

**Given:** CIF video (352 × 288 @ 30 fps) compressed to **1 Mbps** using H.264/AVC. Find the **CR** and the **bpp**.

**Step 1 — Raw rate (re-used from §3.1).** 

$$R_{raw} = 36.5 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 2 — Compression Ratio.** 

$$CR = \dfrac{R_{raw}}{R_{compressed}} = \dfrac{36.5 \text{ Mbps}}{1 \text{ Mbps}} = 36.5$$

**Step 3 — Total pixels per second.**

$$P_{per\_sec} = W \times H \times f_{ps} = 352 \times 288 \times 30 = 3{,}041{,}280 \text{ pixels/s}$$

**Step 4 — Bits per pixel.**

$$bpp = \dfrac{R_{compressed}}{P_{per\_sec}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{3{,}041{,}280} \approx 0.329 \text{ bits/pixel}$$

So H.264 delivers **~0.33 bpp** for CIF — a remarkable compression compared to the **12 bpp** of uncompressed 4:4:4 video. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PSNR Calculation from a Hypothetical Residual

**Given:** An 8-bit luma frame ($L = 255$). The encoder-decoder system introduces a residual error with $MSE = 45$. Compute the **PSNR in dB**.

**Step 1 — Recall the PSNR formula:**

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^{2}}{MSE}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $L = 255$, $MSE = 45$:**

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{45}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{65{,}025}{45}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the ratio:**

$$\dfrac{65{,}025}{45} = 1{,}445.0$$

**Step 4 — Apply $\log_{10}$:**

$$10 \log_{10}(1445) \approx 10 \times 3.1599 = 31.60 \text{ dB}$$

**Interpretation:** PSNR ≈ 31.6 dB is generally regarded as **"good" quality** for consumer video (broadcast TV typically achieves 35–45 dB). Values below 25 dB become visibly objectionable. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Block Matching Algorithm Comparison

| **Algorithm** | **Search Pattern** | **Steps to Converge (worst case)** | **Computational Complexity** | **Quality** | **KTU Exam Relevance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full Search (FS) | Brute-force, all positions in window | 1 | $O((2w+1)^{2})$ | Optimal (minimum SAD) | Often asked: "Why is FS rarely used in hardware?" |
| Three-Step Search (TSS) | 3 step sizes: 4 → 2 → 1 | 3 iterations × 8 pts ≈ 25 evaluations | $O(\log_{2} w)$ | Near-optimal for slow motion | Very high — usually asked |
| New Three-Step Search (NTSS) | TSS + centre-biased initial check | Similar to TSS, fewer evals | $O(\log_{2} w)$ | Better for centred-biased MVs | Less frequent |
| Diamond Search (DS) | Large Diamond Search (LDSP) → Small Diamond (SDSP) | Variable | $O(\log_{2} w)$ | Excellent speed-quality trade-off | Common in H.264 literature |
| Hexagonal Search (HEXBS) | Hexagonal pattern refinement | Variable | $O(\log_{2} w)$ | Slightly faster than DS | Less common at KTU level |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Lossless Coding Methods

| **Method** | **Symbol Unit** | **Code Property** | **Strength** | **Weakness** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Run-Length Coding (RLE) | Run of identical symbols | Variable-length | Trivial, fast | Inefficient for random data |
| Huffman | Single symbols | Prefix-free, optimal for given distribution | Simple decoder | Needs symbol statistics; tree overhead |
| Adaptive Huffman | Single symbols | Updates tree as data streams | One-pass, tracks changing stats | More complex |
| Arithmetic Coding | Sequence of symbols | Near-entropy bound (fractional bits/symbol) | Highest compression | Slower, patented (until 2017 for some variants) |
| CABAC (Context-Adaptive Binary Arithmetic Coding) | Binarised symbols with adaptive context | Context-modelled, near-optimal | Used in H.264/HEVC/VVC | Most complex |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: Group of Pictures (GOP) Size Calculation

**Given:** A video stream uses a GOP pattern `IBBPBBPBBPBB` with $f_{ps} = 25$ and 30 GOPs per minute. Compute the **average bit allocation ratio** assuming I:P:B size ratio is **8 : 4 : 1**.

**Step 1 — Count frames in one GOP.** From the pattern, 1 I + 3 P + 8 B = **12 frames** per GOP. So this is a **GOP-12**.

**Step 2 — Assign weights.** Using the 8:4:1 size ratio, normalised:

$$I_{weight} = 8, \quad P_{weight} = 4, \quad B_{weight} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Total weighted bits per GOP (per "frame-equivalent"):**

$$W_{GOP} = 1(8) + 3(4) + 8(1) = 8 + 12 + 8 = 28$$

**Step 4 — Fraction of total bits per frame type:**

$$\%_I = \dfrac{8}{28} = 28.6\%, \quad \%_P = \dfrac{12}{28} = 42.9\%, \quad \%_B = \dfrac{8}{28} = 28.6\%$$

**Step 5 — Average frame size factor (relative to B as unit 1):**

$$\bar S = \dfrac{28}{12} \approx 2.33 \text{ "B-units" per frame}$$

This explains why increasing the number of **B-frames** dramatically reduces average bitrate. $\blacksquare$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Hybrid Video Encoder–Decoder Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNodeA[Input Frame F_n] --> splitNodeA{Split into\nMacroblocks}
    splitNodeA --> modeSelectNodeA{Prediction\nMode}

    modeSelectNodeA -->|Intra| intraPredNodeA[Intra Prediction\nI-block]
    modeSelectNodeA -->|Inter| interPredNodeA[Inter Prediction\nMotion Estimation\n+ Compensation]

    intraPredNodeA --> predOutNodeA[Predicted Block P_n]
    interPredNodeA --> predOutNodeA

    inputNodeA --> subtractorNodeA[(-)]
    predOutNodeA --> subtractorNodeA
    subtractorNodeA --> residualNodeA[Residual R_n]

    residualNodeA --> dctNodeA[DCT\nTransform T]
    dctNodeA --> quantNodeA[Quantiser Q]
    quantNodeA --> entropyNodeA[Entropy Encoder\nHuffman / CABAC]
    entropyNodeA --> bitstreamNodeA[Output Bitstream]

    quantNodeA --> invQuantNodeA[Inverse Q]
    invQuantNodeA --> invDctNodeA[Inverse DCT]
    invDctNodeA --> addNodeA[(+)]
    predOutNodeA --> addNodeA
    addNodeA --> loopFilterNodeA[De-blocking Filter]
    loopFilterNodeA --> refStoreNodeA[Reference Frame Store]
    refStoreNodeA -.-> interPredNodeA
```

## 4.2 Decoder Block Diagram (Companion to Encoder)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bitstreamInNode1[Received Bitstream] --> entropyDecNode1[Entropy Decoder\nHuffman / CABAC]
    entropyDecNode1 --> invQNode1[Inverse Quantiser]
    invQNode1 --> invTNode1[Inverse DCT]

    entropyDecNode1 --> mvParseNode1[Parse Motion Vectors]
    mvParseNode1 --> mcNode1[Motion Compensation\nusing Reference]
    mcNode1 --> predOutNode1[Predicted Block P_n]

    invTNode1 --> resNode1[Residual R_n]
    resNode1 --> adderNode1[(+)]
    predOutNode1 --> adderNode1
    adderNode1 --> loopFiltNode1[De-blocking Filter]
    loopFiltNode1 --> outFrameNode1[Output Frame F_n']
    loopFiltNode1 --> refNode1[Reference Store]
    refNode1 -.-> mcNode1
```

## 4.3 Group of Pictures (GOP) Structure with Frame Dependencies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    i1Node[I-frame\nFrame 0] --> p1Node[P-frame\nFrame 3]
    i1Node --> b1Node[B-frame\nFrame 1]
    i1Node --> b2Node[B-frame\nFrame 2]
    p1Node --> b3Node[B-frame\nFrame 4]
    p1Node --> b4Node[B-frame\nFrame 5]
    p1Node --> p2Node[P-frame\nFrame 6]
    b1Node -.-> p1Node
    b2Node -.-> p1Node
    b3Node -.-> p2Node
    b4Node -.-> p2Node
    p2Node --> i2Node[I-frame\nFrame 9]
```

## 4.4 Taxonomy of Redundancies — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode[Video Compression Redundancies] --> spatNode[Spatial Redundancy\nwithin a frame]
    rootNode --> tempNode[Temporal Redundancy\nacross frames]
    rootNode --> specNode[Spectral Redundancy\nacross colour channels]
    rootNode --> psyNode[Psycho-visual Redundancy\nHVS limitations]
    rootNode --> statNode[Statistical Redundancy\nsymbol distribution]

    spatNode --> toolA[Intra Prediction\nDCT Transform]
    tempNode --> toolB[Motion Estimation\nMotion Compensation]
    specNode --> toolC[RGB to YCbCr\nChroma Sub-sampling]
    psyNode --> toolD[Quantisation\nPerceptual Weighting]
    statNode --> toolE[Huffman\nArithmetic CABAC]
```

## 4.5 Block Motion Estimation — Search Window Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    currFrameNode[Current Frame F_n] --> mbNode[Target Macroblock\nat position x,y]
    prevFrameNode[Previous Frame F_n-1] --> searchWinNode[Search Window\ncentre x,y\nrange +- p]
    searchWinNode --> candNodes[All candidate positions\nwithin +- p]
    candNodes --> sadComputeNode[SAD Computation\nfor each candidate]
    sadComputeNode --> bestMatchNode[Best Match Position\n= Reference + MV]
    mbNode --> sadComputeNode
    bestMatchNode --> residualOutNode[Residual = Current - Best Match\nMV sent to bitstream]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 
**"List and briefly explain the different types of redundancies exploited in video compression."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Video compression removes five classes of redundancy:

1. **Spatial redundancy** — neighbouring pixels within a frame are highly correlated (e.g., smooth blue sky).
2. **Temporal redundancy** — successive frames are nearly identical except for moving objects (e.g., newsreader with static backdrop).
3. **Spectral redundancy** — the R, G, B colour planes carry correlated information; converting to YCbCr and sub-sampling chroma removes this.
4. **Psycho-visual redundancy** — the human eye is insensitive to certain high-frequency details and colour shifts, so quantisation can discard them invisibly.
5. **Statistical redundancy** — some symbol patterns (e.g., zero-valued DCT coefficients) occur far more often; entropy coding compresses them efficiently.

*(1 mark for correct list of types, 1 mark for one-line meaning each, 1 mark for any one example.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between I-frame, P-frame and B-frame in video compression."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **I-frame** | **P-frame** | **B-frame** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full form | Intra-coded | Predictive | Bi-predictive |
| Reference | None — self-contained | Previous I/P frame | Both past & future I/P frames |
| Compression | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Role | Random access point | Removes temporal redundancy forward | Removes redundancy in both directions |
| Typical size | Largest (~3× P) | Medium | Smallest (~1/3 P) |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for at least one engineering implication such as "I-frames enable seek/seek-ability".)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hybrid predictive-transform video encoder**. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital video sequence has resolution **640 × 480**, **4:2:0** sub-sampling, **25 fps**, and **8 bits/sample**. Compute the **uncompressed bit-rate** and find the **compression ratio** if the compressed bitstream is **1.5 Mbps**. Also calculate the **PSNR** if the mean-squared error between original and reconstructed luma frame is **80**. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Hybrid Encoder Block Diagram (7 marks):**

*(Full marks for diagram; partial for explanation. Diagram shown in §4.1 above.)*

**Block-wise role (≈ 1 mark each for 5 key blocks + 2 marks for interconnection):**

1. **Input & partitioning** — The frame $F_n$ is divided into macroblocks of 16 × 16 luma + 8 × 8 chroma (or coding tree units in H.265).
2. **Prediction unit** — Operates in two modes:
   - *Intra*: predicts current block from already-decoded neighbouring blocks in same frame.
   - *Inter*: searches reference frame(s) using motion estimation to find best matching block; motion vector $\vec{v}$ is transmitted.
3. **Subtractor** — Computes residual $R_n = F_n - P_n$ where $P_n$ is the prediction.
4. **Transform (DCT)** — Converts spatial-domain residual into frequency-domain coefficients, compacting energy into low-frequency bins.
5. **Quantiser (Q)** — Divides each coefficient by a quantisation step; this is the **primary lossy stage**. Coarse quantisation → higher compression, lower quality.
6. **Entropy encoder** — Losslessly encodes quantised coefficients + side information (MVs, modes) using Huffman/Arithmetic/CABAC into the output bitstream.
7. **Reconstruction loop** — Performs inverse Q and inverse DCT, adds back the prediction, applies a deblocking filter, and stores the result as reference for future inter-prediction.

**Engineering insight:** Without the reconstruction loop, the encoder and decoder would drift apart, because the decoder only has compressed data — it cannot see the original. Hence the encoder must simulate the decoder locally.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Luma and chroma samples per frame:**

$$Y = 640 \times 480 = 307{,}200$$
$$Chroma = 2 \times 320 \times 240 = 153{,}600$$
$$F_{samples} = 307{,}200 + 153{,}600 = 460{,}800$$

**Step 2 — Bytes per frame (8 bits = 1 byte):**

$$F_{bytes} = 460{,}800 \text{ bytes} \approx 450 \text{ KiB}$$

**Step 3 — Uncompressed bit-rate:**

$$R_{raw} = 460{,}800 \times 25 \times 8 = 92{,}160{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 92.16 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — Compression Ratio:**

$$CR = \dfrac{92.16}{1.5} = 61.44 : 1$$

**Step 5 — PSNR with $L = 255$, $MSE = 80$:**

$$PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{80}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(812.81) = 10 \times 2.9101 = 29.10 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:** Raw bit-rate ≈ 92.16 Mbps, CR = 61.44, PSNR ≈ 29.1 dB. $\blacksquare$

---

### Q-B. [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**(a)** Explain the concept of **motion estimation and motion compensation** in inter-frame video coding. Describe the **block-matching algorithm** with a diagram and compare the **Full Search** and **Three-Step Search** methods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **PSNR**, **MSE**, **Compression Ratio** and **bpp**. A CIF video (352 × 288, 30 fps) when compressed to 800 kbps gives a reconstructed frame whose pixel-wise error with respect to the original has an MSE of 60. Calculate the **PSNR** and the **bits-per-pixel**. Comment on the visual quality. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Motion Estimation and Compensation (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** Inter-frame video coding exploits temporal redundancy. Since consecutive frames are highly similar except for moving objects, the encoder does **not** re-transmit the full frame; instead, it finds, for each block of the current frame, the best matching block in a previously decoded reference frame. The displacement vector is called the **Motion Vector (MV)**, and the process of finding it is **motion estimation (ME)**. The decoder then **motion-compensates (MC)** by shifting the reference block by MV and adding the residual.

**Block-matching algorithm description (3 marks):**
1. Partition current frame into non-overlapping macroblocks (16 × 16).
2. For each macroblock, search a ±$p$ pixel window in the reference frame.
3. Compute matching cost (typically **SAD**) at every candidate position.
4. Select position with minimum SAD → that displacement is the MV.
5. Compute residual = current − motion-compensated reference.
6. Send MV + residual to bitstream.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer §4.5.

**Comparison Full Search vs Three-Step Search (1 mark):**

| **Criterion** | **Full Search** | **Three-Step Search** |
|---|---|---|
| Search points | $(2p+1)^{2}$ (exhaustive) | $\approx 25$ for $p=7$ |
| Optimality | Globally optimal | Near-optimal (sub-optimal for large motions) |
| Hardware cost | Very high | Low |
| Real-time use | Rare | Yes, common in MPEG-2/H.263 encoders |

**Part (b) — Definitions and Numerical (7 marks):**

**Definitions (≈ 1 mark each):**

- **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** average of squared pixel-wise differences. $MSE = \frac{1}{MN}\sum \sum \vert I(i,j) - \hat I(i,j)\vert^{2}$.
- **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** log-scale fidelity metric in dB. $PSNR = 10\log_{10}(L^{2}/MSE)$.
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** ratio of original to compressed bit-rates. $CR = R_{orig}/R_{comp}$.
- **bpp (bits per pixel):** average bits spent on each pixel. $bpp = R/(W \cdot H \cdot f_{ps})$.

**Numerical (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — PSNR with $L=255$, $MSE=60$:**

$$PSNR = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^{2}}{60}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1083.75) = 10 \times 3.0348 = 30.35 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — bpp at 800 kbps, 352 × 288 @ 30 fps:**

$$bpp = \dfrac{800{,}000}{352 \times 288 \times 30} = \dfrac{800{,}000}{3{,}041{,}280} \approx 0.263 \text{ bits/pixel}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on quality (1 mark):**

PSNR ≈ 30.35 dB falls in the **"acceptable for consumer applications"** range (above 30 dB generally means no visible artefacts to casual viewers; broadcast quality usually ≥ 38 dB). At 0.263 bpp, the compression is approximately **138:1** over 12-bpp raw — typical for streaming-grade CIF video.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting chroma sub-sampling factor** — when computing raw bit-rate for 4:2:0, students often use $3 \times W \times H$ (4:4:4) instead of $1.5 \times W \times H$, leading to a wrong answer by a factor of 2. **Always re-state sub-sampling format explicitly.**
> 2. **Confusing encoder vs decoder** — the decoder does **not** have a motion estimator. It only uses the MVs that the encoder transmitted. Many students incorrectly draw an ME block inside the decoder.
> 3. **Misquoting PSNR formula** — using `255 * 255` only works for 8-bit. For 10-bit video, $L = 1023$. Examiners love testing this.
> 4. **Confusing Compression Ratio with bpp** — these are **inversely related**, not equal. CR is unitless; bpp is bits/pixel.
> 5. **Writing motion estimation without stating "encoder-side only"** — losing 1 mark is routine.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] Video compression **must** exploit both *spatial* and *temporal* redundancy — image-only codecs are insufficient.
- [x] The **hybrid predictive-transform** architecture is the universal skeleton of every modern codec (H.26x, MPEG-x, AV1, VVC).
- [x] **I-frames** are the only self-contained frames; **P-frames** use one reference; **B-frames** use two.
- [x] A **GOP** is the I-frame-to-I-frame sequence; shorter GOP → better random access, longer GOP → better compression.
- [x] **Motion Estimation** = encoder-side search (expensive). **Motion Compensation** = decoder-side reconstruction (cheap).
- [x] **SAD** is the dominant block-matching cost metric; full search gives optimal MVs but is rarely used in practice.
- [x] The **raw bit-rate formula** for 4:2:0, 8-bit: $R = \frac{3}{2} W H f_{ps} \times 8$ bps.
- [x] **PSNR ≥ 30 dB** = acceptable consumer quality; **≥ 38 dB** = broadcast-grade.
- [x] **Quantisation** is the *primary* lossy step; entropy coding is **lossless**.
- [x] **Y/Cb/Cr** colour space + 4:2:0 sub-sampling exploits spectral redundancy before any spatial coding.
- [x] Compression standards timeline: **H.261 → MPEG-1 → MPEG-2/H.262 → H.263/MPEG-4 → H.264/AVC → H.265/HEVC → VVC/AV1**.
- [x] Every modern codec relies on a **decoder-mimicking reconstruction loop** in the encoder to prevent encoder-decoder drift.
- [x] For KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions: focus on definitions, taxonomy, and *I/P/B-frame differences*.
- [x] For KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions: master the **encoder block diagram** and the **numerical raw-bitrate / PSNR / CR problems**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Video Compression Based on Motion Compensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Video Compression Based on Motion Compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: VIDEO COMPRESSION BASED ON MOTION COMPENSATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motion Compensation (MC)** is a temporal redundancy reduction technique used in inter-frame video coding, where each frame is partitioned into **macroblocks** (typically 16×16 pixels), and the best-matching block is searched from a previously decoded reference frame within a defined search window. The spatial displacement that produces this best match is encoded as a **Motion Vector (MV)**. The difference between the predicted block and the actual current block — called the **Prediction Error** or **Residual Frame** — is then transformed, quantized, and entropy-coded.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, motion compensation is the **prediction engine** of any modern hybrid video codec (H.261, H.263, MPEG-1/2/4, H.264/AVC, H.265/HEVC). It exploits the **temporal correlation** between successive frames so that only the residual difference and a handful of motion vectors need to be transmitted instead of the entire frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlight)**
> - **I-frame (Intra-coded frame):** Self-contained, no motion compensation.
> - **P-frame (Predictive frame):** Uses forward motion compensation from previous I/P frame.
> - **B-frame (Bi-directional frame):** Uses motion compensation from both past and future reference frames.
> - **GOP (Group of Pictures):** Sequence of frames between two I-frames.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Window" Intuition

Imagine you are watching a movie on a screen. The camera pans slowly to the right. To you, most of the image hasn't *actually changed* — only the *position* of every object has shifted slightly to the left.

> 🎬 **Analogy:** Think of each video frame as a **photograph in an old flip-book**. If you superimpose two consecutive photographs and slide one slightly, most pixels overlap perfectly. The tiny "non-overlapping" patches are the only *real* new information.

Motion compensation does exactly this:
1. It **slides** a previous frame over the current frame in tiny steps.
2. For every 16×16 block, it finds the *slide distance* (the motion vector) that best aligns with the current frame.
3. It transmits only the *slide distance* and the *residual difference* — a tiny amount of data.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this so powerful?** A typical raw YUV 4:2:0 frame at 720×576 resolution is ~622 KB. With motion compensation, the **residual frame** for that same scene may be only **5–20 KB**, achieving a compression ratio of **30:1 to 120:1** with virtually no visible quality loss.

---

## 2. Spatial vs. Temporal Redundancy — Why Motion Compensation Exists

| Redundancy Type | Exploited By | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial** | DCT, Wavelet, Fractal | Correlation between neighbouring pixels *within* a single frame |
| **Temporal** | **Motion Compensation** | Correlation between pixels *across* consecutive frames |
| **Statistical** | Huffman, Arithmetic, VLC | Non-uniform symbol probabilities in coded data |
| **Psychovisual** | Quantization, chroma subsampling | Human eye insensitivity to certain frequency/colour details |

> [!NOTE]
> Motion compensation **only handles temporal redundancy**. To build a complete video codec, it must be combined with a spatial transform (DCT), quantization, and entropy coding — forming the **hybrid coding loop** standardized in MPEG/H.26x families.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 3. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 The Motion Compensation Pipeline — Operational Breakdown

1. **Frame Partitioning**
   - The current frame is divided into non-overlapping **macroblocks** (default size = **16×16** pixels in H.261/MPEG-1; H.264 supports 16×8, 8×16, 8×8, 8×4, 4×8, 4×4).
   - Each macroblock is independently processed.

2. **Search Window Definition**
   - A **search region** of size $(2p+1) \times (2p+1)$ is defined around the macroblock's co-located position in the reference frame.
   - $p$ is the **search parameter** (e.g., $p = 7$ in MPEG-1).
   - For a 16×16 block and $p = 7$, search window = 30×30 pixels = **900 candidate positions**.

3. **Block Matching (Motion Estimation)**
   - For each candidate displacement $(u, v)$ in the search window, compute a **distortion measure** between the current macroblock and the candidate reference block.
   - The $(u, v)$ that **minimizes** the distortion is selected as the **Motion Vector** $\vec{MV} = (u^*, v^*)$.

4. **Motion-Compensated Prediction**
   - The reference block at position $(u^*, v^*)$ is used as the **prediction** of the current block.

5. **Residual Computation**
   - The **prediction error** is computed pixel-by-pixel: $E(x,y) = I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x+u^*, y+v^*)$.

6. **Encoding of MV + Residual**
   - Motion vectors are **DPCM-coded** (differential), then variable-length coded.
   - The residual is **DCT-transformed**, **quantized**, and **entropy-coded**.

7. **Decoder-Side Reconstruction**
   - The decoder uses the received MV to fetch the reference block, adds the decoded residual, and reconstructs the frame.

### 🔁 Closed-Loop Prediction (Why it Matters in KTU Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reference frame used for prediction is the **reconstructed (decoded) frame**, NOT the original. This prevents the **drift error** that would otherwise accumulate between encoder and decoder.

$$
I_{t}^{recon}(x,y) = I_{t-1}^{recon}(x+u^*, y+v^*) + E_{decoded}(x,y)
$$

where $E_{decoded}$ is the residual after quantization and inverse-quantization.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SAD** (Sum of Absolute Differences) | $\text{SAD}(u,v) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \vert I_t(i,j) - I_{t-1}(i+u, j+v) \vert$ | pixels-value (0–255) |
| 2 | **MAD** (Mean Absolute Difference) | $\text{MAD}(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{N^2}\,\text{SAD}(u,v)$ | pixels-value |
| 3 | **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $\text{MSE}(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{N^2}\sum\sum \left[I_t - I_{t-1}\right]^2$ | (pixels-value)² |
| 4 | **PSNR** (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) | $\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | **decibels (dB)**; ≥ 30 dB is "good" |
| 5 | Motion Vector Selection | $(u^*, v^*) = \arg\min_{(u,v)\in W} \text{SAD}(u,v)$ | pixels |
| 6 | Search Window Cardinality | $N_W = (2p+1)^2$ | integer candidates |
| 7 | Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Original size}}{\text{Compressed size}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Bitrate | $R = \dfrac{\text{Total bits}}{\text{Duration (sec)}}$ | **bits per second (bps)** |
| 9 | Residual Energy | $E_{res} = \sum\sum \vert E(x,y) \vert^2$ | (pixels-value)² |
| 10 | Block Matching Complexity (Full Search) | $O\!\left(N^2 \cdot (2p+1)^2\right)$ per macroblock | operations |

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Streaming platforms (Netflix, YouTube):** H.264/AVC and H.265/HEVC codecs use motion compensation to deliver 1080p video at **3–6 Mbps**.
- **Video conferencing (Zoom, Teams):** H.264 SVC with motion compensation handles low-bandwidth real-time streams.
- **Surveillance & CCTV:** Motion-compensated temporal filtering (MCTF) is used in scalable codecs for archival.
- **Medical imaging & telemedicine:** Motion-compensated prediction enables transmission of ultrasound video at low bitrates.
- **Autonomous vehicles:** Motion vectors are reused in scene-flow estimation for object tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 4. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

### 🔬 Worked Example 1: Computing SAD and Selecting Motion Vector (Full Search)

> **Problem:** A 4×4 current macroblock $I_t$ is given below. The reference frame $I_{t-1}$ (16×16) is also given. Using **SAD** as the matching criterion, find the motion vector with search parameter $p = 1$ (so candidates are $(u,v) \in \{-1, 0, 1\}^2$). Show all 9 SAD computations.

**Current macroblock $I_t$ (4×4):**
$$
I_t = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 110 & 120 & 130 \\ 90 & 95 & 100 & 105 \\ 80 & 85 & 90 & 95 \\ 70 & 75 & 80 & 85 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Reference region around co-located position (rows 1–6, cols 1–6 of $I_{t-1}$):**

| 102 | 108 | 118 | 128 | 138 | 148 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 88  | 94  | 100 | 106 | 110 | 115 |
| 82  | 86  | 92  | 96  | 100 | 105 |
| 68  | 74  | 82  | 86  | 92  | 98  |
| 60  | 64  | 72  | 78  | 82  | 88  |
| 55  | 58  | 64  | 70  | 76  | 80  |

The co-located block (offset = 0,0) corresponds to the **top-left 4×4** of the reference region. Candidate shifts are at $(u,v) \in \{-1,0,1\}$.

**Step 1: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (0,0)$**

The candidate reference block is the top-left 4×4 of $I_{t-1}$:
$$
R_{(0,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 102 & 108 & 118 & 128 \\ 88 & 94 & 100 & 106 \\ 82 & 86 & 92 & 96 \\ 68 & 74 & 82 & 86 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now compute the absolute differences:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAD}(0,0) &= \sum_{i=0}^{3}\sum_{j=0}^{3}\vert I_t(i,j) - R_{(0,0)}(i,j) \vert \\
&= \vert 100-102\vert + \vert 110-108\vert + \vert 120-118\vert + \vert 130-128\vert \\
&\quad + \vert 90-88\vert + \vert 95-94\vert + \vert 100-100\vert + \vert 105-106\vert \\
&\quad + \vert 80-82\vert + \vert 85-86\vert + \vert 90-92\vert + \vert 95-96\vert \\
&\quad + \vert 70-68\vert + \vert 75-74\vert + \vert 80-82\vert + \vert 85-86\vert \\
&= 2+2+2+2+2+1+0+1+2+1+2+1+2+1+2+1 = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (-1,0)$** (reference block shifted right by 1)

$$
R_{(-1,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 108 & 118 & 128 & 138 \\ 94 & 100 & 106 & 110 \\ 86 & 92 & 96 & 100 \\ 74 & 82 & 86 & 92 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAD}(-1,0) &= 8+8+8+8+4+5+6+5+6+7+6+5+4+7+6+7 = 98
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (1,0)$** (reference block shifted left by 1)

$$
R_{(1,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 88 & 94 & 100 & 106 \\ 82 & 86 & 92 & 96 \\ 68 & 74 & 82 & 86 \\ 60 & 64 & 72 & 78 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAD}(1,0) &= 12+16+20+24+8+9+8+9+12+11+8+9+10+11+8+7 = 182
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (0,-1)$** (reference block shifted down by 1, take rows 2–5)

$$
R_{(0,-1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 88 & 94 & 100 & 106 \\ 82 & 86 & 92 & 96 \\ 68 & 74 & 82 & 86 \\ 60 & 64 & 72 & 78 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAD}(0,-1) &= 12+16+20+24+8+9+8+9+12+11+8+9+10+11+8+7 = 182
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (0,1)$** (reference block shifted up by 1)

$$
R_{(0,1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 102 & 108 & 118 & 128 \\ 88 & 94 & 100 & 106 \\ 82 & 86 & 92 & 96 \\ 68 & 74 & 82 & 86 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Wait — for $v=+1$, the reference block uses rows 0–3 of the visible region. Since the reference region shown starts at row 0 already, $v=+1$ would need to access a row above which is **out of bounds**. With proper boundary padding (replicate the edge row), the row above the region is $[102, 108, 118, 128, 138, 148]$. Taking cols 0–3: $\begin{bmatrix} 102 & 108 & 118 & 128 \end{bmatrix}$ — this is **identical to $R_{(0,0)}$**. So:

$$
\text{SAD}(0,1) = 24
$$

**Step 6: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (-1,-1)$** (shift right 1, shift down 1)

$$
R_{(-1,-1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 94 & 100 & 106 & 110 \\ 86 & 92 & 96 & 100 \\ 74 & 82 & 86 & 92 \\ 64 & 72 & 78 & 82 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAD}(-1,-1) &= 6+10+14+20+4+3+4+5+6+3+4+3+6+3+2+3 = 96
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (1,1)$** (shift left 1, shift up 1)

$$
R_{(1,1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 102 & 108 & 118 & 128 \\ 88 & 94 & 100 & 106 \\ 82 & 86 & 92 & 96 \\ 68 & 74 & 82 & 86 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{SAD}(1,1) = 24
$$

**Step 8: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (-1,1)$** (shift right 1, shift up 1)

$$
R_{(-1,1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 108 & 118 & 128 & 138 \\ 94 & 100 & 106 & 110 \\ 86 & 92 & 96 & 100 \\ 74 & 82 & 86 & 92 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{SAD}(-1,1) = 98
$$

**Step 9: Compute SAD at $(u,v) = (1,-1)$** (shift left 1, shift down 1)

$$
R_{(1,-1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 82 & 86 & 92 & 96 \\ 68 & 74 & 82 & 86 \\ 60 & 64 & 72 & 78 \\ 55 & 58 & 64 & 70 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAD}(1,-1) &= 18+24+28+34+22+21+18+19+20+21+18+17+15+17+16+15 = 323
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aggregation Table of SAD values:**

| $(u,v)$ | SAD |
|---|---|
| **(0,0)** | **24** ✅ |
| (-1,0) | 98 |
| (1,0) | 182 |
| (0,-1) | 182 |
| (0,1) | 24 |
| (-1,-1) | 96 |
| (1,1) | 24 |
| (-1,1) | 98 |
| (1,-1) | 323 |

**Step 10: Select Minimum**

$$
(u^*, v^*) = \arg\min \text{SAD} = (0,0),\ (0,1),\ (1,1)
$$

Conventionally the encoder picks the **first minimum encountered** (in raster scan order) — so the **selected motion vector is $\vec{MV} = (0, 0)$** with $\text{SAD}_{min} = 24$.

**Step 11: Compute the Residual Block** $E = I_t - R_{(0,0)}$
$$
E = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\ -2 & -1 & -2 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This residual has much smaller dynamic range than the original — perfect for compression via DCT + quantization.

---

### 🔬 Worked Example 2: Deriving Compression Ratio with Motion Compensation

> **Problem:** A CIF-format video (352×288 pixels) at 30 fps is encoded in RGB 8-bit. Compute the uncompressed bitrate, and then estimate the bitrate after motion compensation if the average residual block uses 4 bits/pixel and each macroblock has 1 motion vector of 16 bits.

**Step 1: Uncompressed data rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame size} &= 352 \times 288 \times 3 \text{ bytes} = 304{,}128 \text{ bytes/frame} \\
\text{Bytes/sec} &= 304{,}128 \times 30 = 9{,}123{,}840 \text{ bytes/s} \\
\text{Bitrate} &= 9{,}123{,}840 \times 8 = 72{,}990{,}720 \text{ bps} \approx 73 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Macroblock count per frame**

$$
\text{Macroblocks/frame} = \frac{352}{16} \times \frac{288}{16} = 22 \times 18 = 396
$$

**Step 3: Bits per macroblock after MC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Residual bits} &= 16 \times 16 \times 3 \times 4 = 3072 \text{ bits} \quad (\text{4 bits/pixel × 3 channels}) \\
\text{MV bits} &= 16 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total/MB} &= 3072 + 16 = 3088 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Bitrate after MC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits/frame} &= 396 \times 3088 = 1{,}222{,}848 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Bitrate} &= 1{,}222{,}848 \times 30 = 36{,}685{,}440 \text{ bps} \approx 36.7 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compression Ratio**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{72{,}990{,}720}{36{,}685{,}440} \approx 1.99 \approx 2:1
$$

> [!TIP]
> This 2:1 ratio comes **only** from motion compensation. Combined with DCT + quantization + entropy coding, the final CR is typically **50:1 to 200:1** for broadcast-quality video.

---

### 🔬 Worked Example 3: PSNR Computation After MC Reconstruction

> **Problem:** A reconstructed block $R$ and original block $O$ of size 4×4 are given. Compute PSNR.

$$
O = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 110 & 120 & 130 \\ 90 & 95 & 100 & 105 \\ 80 & 85 & 90 & 95 \\ 70 & 75 & 80 & 85 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
R = \begin{bmatrix} 102 & 108 & 118 & 128 \\ 88 & 94 & 100 & 106 \\ 82 & 86 & 92 & 96 \\ 68 & 74 & 82 & 86 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1: Compute pixel-wise error**
$$
E = O - R = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & -1 \\ -2 & -1 & -2 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute squared errors**
$$
E^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 4 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 4 & 1 & 4 & 1 \\ 4 & 1 & 4 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the squared errors**
$$
\Sigma = 4+4+4+4+4+1+0+1+4+1+4+1+4+1+4+1 = 42
$$

**Step 4: MSE**
$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{42}{16} = 2.625
$$

**Step 5: PSNR**
$$
\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{2.625}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(24{,}768) \approx 43.94 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A PSNR of ~44 dB indicates **excellent** visual quality — well above the 30 dB acceptability threshold.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 5. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🎨 Figure 1: Motion Compensation Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef enc fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#1a4480,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dec fill:#ffe7c2,stroke:#a0522d,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef data fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef proc fill:#f4d4e6,stroke:#8b1f5c,color:#000,stroke-width:1px

    curFrame[/Current Frame I_t/]:::data
    refFrame[/Reference Frame I_t-1 recon/]:::data
    partCur[Frame Partitioner 16x16 macroblocks]:::proc
    me[Motion Estimation Block Matching Algorithm]:::proc
    sad[SAD / MAD Distortion Calculator]:::proc
    mvSel[(u* v*) Motion Vector Selector]:::proc
    pred[Predicted Block from Reference]:::proc
    sub[Subtractor Residual = I_t minus Prediction]:::proc
    dct[DCT Transformer]:::proc
    q[Quantizer]:::proc
    vlc[Entropy Encoder VLC]:::proc
    bitstream[/Compressed Bitstream/]:::data
    channel((Transmission Channel))
    recv[/Received Bitstream/]:::data
    ivlc[Entropy Decoder]:::dec
    iq[Inverse Quantizer]:::dec
    idct[Inverse DCT]:::dec
    add[Adder Reconstruction]:::dec
    recFrame[/Reconstructed Frame I_t/]:::data
    mem[(Frame Memory Buffer for next GOP)]:::data

    curFrame --> partCur
    partCur --> me
    refFrame --> me
    me --> sad
    sad --> mvSel
    mvSel --> pred
    pred --> sub
    curFrame --> sub
    sub --> dct --> q --> vlc --> bitstream
    bitstream --> channel --> recv --> ivlc --> iq --> idct --> add
    pred --> add
    add --> recFrame
    recFrame --> mem
    mem -.next frame reference.-> refFrame
    mvSel -.transmitted MV.-> bitstream
```

### 🎨 Figure 2: Block Matching Search Window Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef win fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
