## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Cloud Assisted Media Sharing. List any three advantages it offers over traditional client-server media delivery.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 for definition + 2 for advantages):**

Cloud Assisted Media Sharing (CAMS) is a multimedia delivery model in which media content is uploaded, transcoded, stored, and distributed to end-users via remote cloud computing resources, leveraging scalability, redundancy, and geographic distribution to enable device-agnostic access.

**Three Advantages:**
1. **Elastic Scalability** — Cloud resources auto-scale during traffic spikes (e.g., viral videos), unlike fixed-capacity servers.
2. **High Availability** — Replicated storage across multiple zones ensures **99.9–99.99 % uptime**.
3. **Reduced Latency** — CDN edge caching places content near users, reducing round-trip time from ~200 ms (transcontinental) to <20 ms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is an ABR (Adaptive Bitrate) ladder? Why is it essential for cloud-assisted video sharing?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An ABR ladder is a set of pre-encoded versions of the same media asset at different combinations of **bitrate** and **resolution** (e.g., 400 kbps@240p, 2500 kbps@720p, 5000 kbps@1080p).

**Essential because:**
1. It enables **client-side adaptation** — the player dynamically switches rungs based on real-time network conditions.
2. It prevents **rebuffering events** which severely degrade Mean Opinion Score (MOS).
3. It optimizes **bandwidth utilization** by avoiding over-delivery of high-bitrate streams to bandwidth-constrained mobile users.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer architecture** of a Cloud Assisted Media Sharing system. Describe the role of each layer in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cloud media service stores 4K videos at 25 Mbps (HEVC encoded). The platform serves 50,000 concurrent viewers across 12 CDN PoPs during a sports event. Each PoP has a 10 Gbps uplink to the internet. Compute:
   (i) Per-stream bandwidth with 10 % overhead.
   (ii) Total bandwidth required at the origin.
   (iii) Whether the current PoP configuration is sufficient; if not, how many additional PoPs are needed. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five-Layer Architecture (7 Marks — Allocate 1 mark per layer + 2 for the diagram):**

| Layer | Function | Key Technologies |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Ingestion** | Accepts uploads from user devices via secure APIs | HTTPS, RTMP, WebRTC, OAuth 2.0 |
| **2. Processing** | Transcodes, watermarks, generates thumbnails, runs AI tagging | FFmpeg, TensorFlow, OpenCV |
| **3. Storage** | Persists media objects with replication and tiering | S3, HDFS, erasure coding (Reed-Solomon) |
| **4. Distribution** | Caches and serves media from geographic edge nodes | CDN (CloudFront, Akamai), Anycast |
| **5. Consumption** | Delivers adaptive streams to heterogeneous end devices | DASH (.mpd), HLS (.m3u8), MSE/EME |

**Marking Rubric (KTU Examiner Standard):**
- Diagram with all 5 layers labeled → **2 marks**
- One-line role of each layer → **5 × 1 = 5 marks**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks — allocate 3 + 2 + 2 across sub-parts):**

**(i) Per-stream bandwidth with overhead (3 Marks):**

$$
B = R_b \times (1 + \alpha) = 25 \text{ Mbps} \times 1.10 = 27.5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**(ii) Total bandwidth at origin (2 Marks):**

$$
B_{agg} = 50{,}000 \times 27.5 \text{ Mbps} = 1{,}375{,}000 \text{ Mbps} = 1375 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**(iii) PoP sufficiency check (2 Marks):**

Total PoP capacity:

$$
C_{total} = 12 \times 10 \text{ Gbps} = 120 \text{ Gbps}
$$

Since $C_{total} = 120 \text{ Gbps} \ll B_{agg} = 1375 \text{ Gbps}$, the current configuration is **grossly insufficient**.

Assuming each PoP can absorb **90 %** of its theoretical capacity due to cache hits:

$$
C_{effective} = 120 \times 0.90 = 108 \text{ Gbps}
$$

Required number of PoPs:

$$
N_{PoP}^{req} = \left\lceil \dfrac{B_{agg}}{C_{PoP}^{eff}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{1375}{9} \right\rceil = \lceil 152.78 \rceil = 153 \text{ PoPs}
$$

Additional PoPs needed:

$$
\Delta N = 153 - 12 = 141 \text{ additional PoPs}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Area):**
> 1. **Forgetting overhead ($\alpha$):** Many students compute $25 \text{ Mbps} \times 50{,}000 = 1250 \text{ Gbps}$ — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $1024$ vs $1000$:** When converting MB↔GB or Mbps↔Gbps, consistently use $1 \text{ GB} = 1024 \text{ MB}$ for storage and $1 \text{ Gbps} = 1000 \text{ Mbps}$ for network. Mixing these loses marks.
> 3. **Ignoring cache-hit efficiency:** A PoP rated 10 Gbps does not serve 10 Gbps of unique traffic; realistic efficiency is 80–95 %.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the role of **Content Delivery Networks (CDN)** in Cloud Assisted Media Sharing. Discuss **cache-hit ratio** and **pull vs. push** caching strategies with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the end-to-end latency for uploading a 2 GB video file from a client in Trivandrum to a cloud server in Singapore (distance ≈ 3,000 km), given an upload link of 50 Mbps, queueing delay of 30 ms, and processing delay of 250 ms. Use $v_{fiber} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CDN Role and Caching Strategies (7 Marks):**

**Role of CDN (4 marks):**
- Reduces **origin server load** by serving repeat requests from edge caches.
- Decreases **latency** by serving content from geographically proximal PoPs.
- Improves **availability** during DDoS attacks by distributing the attack surface.
- Enables **HTTP/3 (QUIC)** for faster TLS handshakes on edge connections.

**Pull vs. Push Caching (3 marks):**

| Strategy | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Pull (Lazy)** | Edge fetches from origin **only when a user requests** it | Long-tail content (e.g., old videos) |
| **Push (Eager)** | Origin **preloads** content to edges before user requests | Predicted viral content, scheduled live events (e.g., IPL final) |

**Cache Hit Ratio Formula:**

$$
CHR = \dfrac{H_{cache}}{H_{cache} + H_{miss}}
$$

A CHR of 80 % means 4 out of 5 requests never touch the origin — directly reducing origin bandwidth costs.

---

**(b) End-to-End Latency Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Propagation Delay (2 Marks):**

$$
T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_{fiber}} = \dfrac{3{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.015 \text{ s} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission Delay (3 Marks):**

$$
T_{trans} = \dfrac{L_{file}}{R_{link}} = \dfrac{2 \times 8 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ bits}}{50 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}}
$$

$$
T_{trans} = \dfrac{17{,}179{,}869{,}184}{50{,}000{,}000} \approx 343.6 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Latency (2 Marks):**

$$
L = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}
$$

$$
L = 15 \text{ ms} + 343{,}600 \text{ ms} + 30 \text{ ms} + 250 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
L \approx 343{,}895 \text{ ms} \approx 343.9 \text{ s} \approx 5 \text{ min } 43.9 \text{ s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Converting 2 GB correctly:** $2 \text{ GB} = 2 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8$ **bits** = 17.18 × $10^9$ bits. Students often write $2 \times 10^9$ — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit consistency:** Link rate is in **bits per second (bps)**; file size must be in **bits**, not bytes. A 2 GB file is 16 Gbit, not 2 Gbit.
> 3. **MS vs. S mixing:** Always convert $T_{prop}$ and $T_{queue}$ to the same unit as $T_{trans}$ (seconds) before summing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Cloud Assisted Media Sharing**

- [x] **CAMS = Cloud + Media Processing + CDN Delivery** — three pillars, not one technology.
- [x] **Five-Layer Architecture:** Ingest → Process → Store → Distribute → Consume.
- [x] **ABR Ladder** is mandatory for adaptive streaming over variable networks.
- [x] **Storage Cost** = $\text{Size} \times \text{Tier Price} \times \text{Time}$; tiering is the primary cost lever.
- [x] **CDN Edge Caching** reduces origin load by 60–90 %; CHR is the key performance metric.
- [x] **Latency Formula:** $L = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$ — remember all four components.
- [x] **Propagation speed in fiber** = $2 \times 10^8$ m/s (NOT $3 \times 10^8$ m/s — that's vacuum).
- [x] **HLS (.m3u8) for Apple ecosystem; DASH (.mpd) for cross-platform** — both are industry standards.
- [x] **DRM (Widevine, FairPlay, PlayReady)** protects premium content at the consumption layer.
- [x] **Standard SLA Tiers:** 99.9 % (Three-Nines) ≈ 8.76 hours downtime/year; 99.99 % ≈ 52.6 minutes/year.
- [x] **Common exam traps:** unit mismatches (bytes vs bits), omitting overhead factor $\alpha$, confusing Mbps with MBps.
- [x] **Mermaid diagram rule:** Always include edge label annotations (e.g., `-->`, `-.->`) to denote data flow direction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computation Offloading for Multimedia Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Computation Offloading for Multimedia Services # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Computation Offloading (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Computation Offloading* is a distributed computing paradigm in which resource-constrained client devices (smartphones, IoT nodes, wearables) delegate the execution of compute-intensive, memory-intensive, or energy-intensive multimedia tasks — such as video transcoding, image rendering, speech recognition, 3-D reconstruction, and augmented reality (AR) processing — to a more powerful surrogate computing infrastructure (cloud servers, edge nodes, or fog nodes) across a wireless network, in order to alleviate local hardware limitations, reduce energy consumption, decrease response latency, and extend battery lifetime. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course: PECNT527), computation offloading is treated as a **core enabler of Mobile Cloud Computing (MCC)** and **Mobile Edge Computing (MEC)** for multimedia workloads. The Course Outcome (CO) mapping typically ties this topic to **CO4: Apply offloading techniques to optimize multimedia service delivery**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen Takeaway" Model** > > Imagine you are hosting a dinner party at home, but your kitchen is tiny (analogous to a **smartphone's limited CPU/RAM/battery**). Cooking an elaborate 5-course meal (analogous to **4K video transcoding + neural style transfer + encryption**) in that kitchen would: > - Take far too long (high **latency**), > - Overheat the stove (high **thermal throttling**), > - And exhaust all your gas (rapid **battery drain**). > > Instead, you order the heavy cooking from a professional restaurant kitchen (analogous to a **cloud data center** or **MEC edge server**), which has industrial burners, helpers, and parallel stations. You just **pick up the finished plates** when ready. > > The decision of *what to cook at home vs. what to order outside* is precisely the **Offloading Decision Problem** — the brain of multimedia offloading systems. ## 1.3 Why Offload? — The Three Pillars > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Pillars Justifying Computation Offloading (KTU High-Yield):** > > 1. **Energy Efficiency (E):** Wireless transmission can be *cheaper in joules* than local computation for heavy multimedia tasks. > 2. **Latency Reduction (T):** Parallel server-grade hardware executes faster than a single mobile SoC. > 3. **Capability Augmentation (C):** Mobile devices gain access to GPU clusters, AI accelerators, and big storage they physically cannot host. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bold Constants) | Term | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Mobile device CPU frequency | $f_m$ | **0.5 – 2.5 GHz** | | Server CPU frequency | $f_s$ | **2.0 – 4.0 GHz** | | Wireless uplink data rate | $r_u$ | **1 – 100 Mbps** (4G/5G) | | Wireless downlink data rate | $r_d$ | **10 – 1000 Mbps** | | Per-cycle energy coefficient | $\kappa$ | $\approx 10^{-27}$ to $10^{-25}$ | | Network round-trip time | $RTT$ | **5 – 80 ms** | | Battery capacity | $Q$ | **3000 – 5000 mAh** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Offloading Decision Framework The offloading decision answers a fundamental binary question: *Should task $i$ be executed locally on the mobile device, or remotely on the surrogate server?* > [!NOTE] > **KTU Conceptual Anchor:** This is governed by a multi-objective optimization function balancing **Energy**, **Latency**, **Monetary Cost**, and **QoE (Quality of Experience)**. ### 2.1.1 Decision Logic (Bulleted Step Breakdown) - **Step 1 — Task Profiling:** The offloading framework first measures the task's computational complexity $w_i$ (in CPU cycles/bit) and its data payload size $d_i$ (in bytes). - **Step 2 — Network Sensing:** It measures current bandwidth $\beta$, uplink/downlink rates $r_u, r_d$, and latency $RTT$. - **Step 3 — Cost Computation:** It computes the *local cost* (energy + time on device) and the *remote cost* (transmission energy + time + server execution time + transmission back). - **Step 4 — Comparison:** If $\text{Cost}_{\text{remote}} < \text{Cost}_{\text{local}}$, the task is offloaded. Otherwise, it executes locally. - **Step 5 — Granularity Selection:** The system may offload *the whole task* (full offloading) or only *a sub-module* (partial offloading / function-level partitioning). ## 2.2 Energy Consumption Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Local Execution Energy ($E_{\text{local}}$):** The energy consumed by the mobile CPU to execute task $i$ locally. $$E_{\text{local}} = \kappa \cdot w_i \cdot f_m^2$$ Where: - $\kappa$ = effective switched capacitance of the mobile processor (typically $\sim 10^{-26}$). - $w_i$ = workload in CPU cycles required for task $i$. - $f_m$ = CPU clock frequency of the mobile device. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remote Execution Energy ($E_{\text{remote}}$):** Sum of transmission energy + negligible server energy (charged to the cloud, not the mobile). $$E_{\text{remote}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot \frac{d_i}{r_u} + P_{\text{rx}} \cdot \frac{d_i'}{r_d}$$ Where: - $P_{\text{tx}}, P_{\text{rx}}$ = transmission and reception power (in **Watts**). - $d_i$ = uplink data size; $d_i'$ = downlink result size. - $r_u, r_d$ = uplink/downlink data rates. ## 2.3 Latency (Execution Time) Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Local Latency:** $$T_{\text{local}} = \frac{w_i}{f_m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Remote Latency:** $$T_{\text{remote}} = \underbrace{\frac{d_i}{r_u}}_{\text{uplink}} + \underbrace{\frac{RTT}{2}}_{\text{propagation}} + \underbrace{\frac{w_i}{f_s}}_{\text{server exec}} + \underbrace{\frac{d_i'}{r_d}}_{\text{downlink}}$$ ## 2.4 The Offloading Decision Rule > [!IMPORTANT] > **Offload IF AND ONLY IF:** $$\Delta E = E_{\text{local}} - E_{\text{remote}} > 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \Delta T = T_{\text{local}} - T_{\text{remote}} > 0$$ A task is offloaded only if it saves **both** energy **and** time. Otherwise, it is executed locally. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{\text{local}} = \kappa \cdot w_i \cdot f_m^2$ | Energy to compute locally | Joules | | 2 | $E_{\text{remote}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}} + P_{\text{rx}} \cdot t_{\text{rx}}$ | Energy to transmit for offload | Joules | | 3 | $T_{\text{local}} = w_i \,/\, f_m$ | Local execution time | seconds | | 4 | $T_{\text{remote}} = d_i/r_u + \text{RTT}/2 + w_i/f_s + d_i'/r_d$ | Remote execution time | seconds | | 5 | $\Delta E = E_{\text{local}} - E_{\text{remote}}$ | Energy saving | Joules | | 6 | $\Delta T = T_{\text{local}} - T_{\text{remote}}$ | Time saving | seconds | | 7 | $\text{Offload Decision} = (\Delta E > 0) \wedge (\Delta T > 0)$ | Decision rule | Boolean | | 8 | $\text{Speedup} = T_{\text{local}} / T_{\text{remote}}$ | Offloading gain | dimensionless | | 9 | $\text{EnergyRatio} = E_{\text{remote}} / E_{\text{local}}$ | Efficiency metric | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{QoE} = f(\text{PSNR}, \text{latency}, \text{jitter})$ | User experience score | MOS (1-5) | > [!NOTE] > **Critical LaTeX Safety Note:** All absolute value bars in tables use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Video Streaming (Netflix, YouTube):** Server-side **transcoding** adapts bitrate to the user's device and network in real time — a canonical offloaded multimedia task. - **AR/VR (Pokémon GO, Apple Vision Pro):** Heavy 6-DoF SLAM and 3-D rendering offloaded to **MEC servers** to preserve mobile battery. - **Voice Assistants (Siri, Google Assistant):** Speech-to-text NLP models are too large for on-device execution; offloaded to cloud ASR engines. - **IoT Surveillance:** Camera nodes offload **motion detection + object recognition** to fog nodes. - **Healthcare:** Wearable ECG monitors offload **arrhythmia detection** to edge AI for rapid diagnosis. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Offloading Decision for a Video Transcoding Task > **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):** > A smartphone must transcode a 30-second 1080p video clip. The local energy cost to perform the full transcoding is measured at **12 J**. The transcoded result is 2 MB, and the source is 50 MB. The uplink bandwidth is **10 Mbps**, downlink is **50 Mbps**, $P_{\text{tx}} = 1.0$ W, $P_{\text{rx}} = 0.8$ W, and the cloud server can complete the transcoding in **2.0 s** with negligible additional network latency. Should the task be offloaded based on energy alone? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Calculate the uplink transmission time. $$t_{\text{tx}} = \frac{d_i}{r_u} = \frac{50 \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = \frac{400 \times 10^6}{10^7} = 40 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the downlink transmission time. $$t_{\text{rx}} = \frac{d_i'}{r_d} = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^6} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^7} = 0.32 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the uplink transmission energy. $$E_{\text{tx}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}} = 1.0 \text{ W} \times 40 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ J}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate the downlink reception energy. $$E_{\text{rx}} = P_{\text{rx}} \cdot t_{\text{rx}} = 0.8 \text{ W} \times 0.32 \text{ s} = 0.256 \text{ J}$$ **Step 5:** Sum the remote-side energy consumption. $$E_{\text{remote}} = E_{\text{tx}} + E_{\text{rx}} = 40 + 0.256 = 40.256 \text{ J}$$ **Step 6:** Compare with local energy. $$\Delta E = E_{\text{local}} - E_{\text{remote}} = 12 - 40.256 = -28.256 \text{ J}$$ **Step 7:** Interpret the result. Since $\Delta E < 0$, the mobile device would *consume more energy* by offloading (transmission dominates). **Conclusion: Do NOT offload for this 50 MB workload over a 10 Mbps link** — local execution is energetically preferable. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why modern offloading frameworks prefer **partial offloading** — only the metadata or feature vectors (e.g., I-frames, keypoints) are sent, not the full raw video. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Latency-Optimal Partitioning Factor > **Problem Statement:** > Consider a multimedia pipeline (e.g., a CNN-based image classifier) split into two stages. The first stage runs locally (feature extraction), the second runs on the server (classification). The local CPU has $f_m = 1.5$ GHz, server has $f_s = 3.0$ GHz. Total workload $W = 9 \times 10^9$ cycles. Uplink bandwidth $r_u = 20$ Mbps, intermediate data $d = 5$ MB. Find the optimal partition fraction $\alpha$ (fraction executed locally) that minimizes total latency. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Express the workload split. $$W_{\text{local}} = \alpha W, \quad W_{\text{server}} = (1 - \alpha) W$$ **Step 2:** Express the total latency as a function of $\alpha$. $$T(\alpha) = \underbrace{\frac{\alpha W}{f_m}}_{\text{local exec}} + \underbrace{\frac{d}{r_u}}_{\text{transmission}} + \underbrace{\frac{(1-\alpha)W}{f_s}}_{\text{server exec}}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute numerical values. $$T(\alpha) = \frac{\alpha \cdot 9 \times 10^9}{1.5 \times 10^9} + \frac{5 \times 8 \times 10^6}{20 \times 10^6} + \frac{(1-\alpha) \cdot 9 \times 10^9}{3.0 \times 10^9}$$ $$T(\alpha) = 6\alpha + 2 + 3(1 - \alpha)$$ **Step 4:** Simplify algebraically. $$T(\alpha) = 6\alpha + 2 + 3 - 3\alpha = 3\alpha + 5$$ **Step 5:** Differentiate with respect to $\alpha$. $$\frac{dT}{d\alpha} = 3$$ **Step 6:** Interpret the derivative. Since the derivative is a *positive constant*, $T(\alpha)$ is monotonically increasing in $\alpha$. Therefore, the minimum latency is achieved at $\alpha = 0$ (fully remote). **Step 7:** Compute the minimum latency. $$T_{\min} = T(0) = 3(0) + 5 = 5 \text{ s}$$ **Step 8:** Compare with the all-local case. $$T(\alpha = 1) = 3(1) + 5 = 8 \text{ s}$$ **Step 9:** Final conclusion. $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T(\alpha = 1)}{T(\alpha = 0)} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6\times$$ > The pipeline should be **fully offloaded** to the server, achieving a **1.6× speedup**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Offloading Decision Cases | Scenario | Workload Type | Data Size | Network | Decision | Justification | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 4K Video Transcoding | Very Large (>50 MB) | Weak (3G) | **Local** | Transmission energy dominates | | 2 | Speech Recognition (audio) | Small (<100 KB) | Strong (5G) | **Remote** | Cloud NLP is far more capable | | 3 | AR Object Tracking | Medium (1-5 MB) | MEC available | **Edge** | Latency-sensitive, partial offload | | 4 | Image Filter (Instagram) | Small (<5 MB) | Strong (Wi-Fi) | **Remote** | Heavy GPU filters on cloud | | 5 | IoT Motion Detection | Tiny (<10 KB) | Cellular | **Edge** | Privacy + latency critical | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Offloading System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Multimedia Application
User Interface Layer] --> B[Task Profiler
Workload Analyzer] B --> C{Offloading Decision Engine
Multi-Objective Optimizer} C -- Offload Decision = YES --> D[Network Interface Module
Wi-Fi / 5G / LTE] C -- Offload Decision = NO --> E[Local Execution Engine
Mobile CPU and GPU] D --> F[Edge Server MEC Node
Low Latency Processing] D --> G[Remote Cloud Data Center
High Performance Computing] F --> H[Result Aggregator
QoE Estimator] G --> H E --> H H --> I[Adaptive Feedback Loop
Network and Energy Monitor] I --> C H --> J[Multimedia Output
Display and Audio] classDef userLayer fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000 classDef decisionLayer fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef networkLayer fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000 classDef computeLayer fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef outputLayer fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000 class A,J userLayer class B,C,I decisionLayer class D networkLayer class E,F,G,H computeLayer ``` ## 4.2 Offloading Decision Tree (Hierarchical) ```mermaid flowchart TB Start[New Multimedia Task Arrives] --> Q1{Latency Sensitive?
e.g. AR VR Real Time} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Nearby MEC Node Available?} Q1 -- NO --> Q3[Proceed to Cloud Offload] Q2 -- YES --> P1[Offload to MEC Edge] Q2 -- NO --> Q4[Execute Locally] Q3 --> Q5{Energy Cost Lower Remotely?} Q5 -- YES --> P2[Offload to Cloud] Q5 -- NO --> Q6[Execute Locally] P1 --> Result[Return Processed Result] P2 --> Result Q4 --> Result Q6 --> Result ``` ## 4.3 End-to-End Data Flow: Offloaded Video Transcoding ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Smartphone participant N as Network Layer 5G participant E as Edge MEC Server participant C as Cloud Data Center participant Q as QoE Monitor U->>U: Step 1 Capture Raw 4K Video 50 MB U->>U: Step 2 Task Profiler computes workload U->>N: Step 3 Transmit compressed video segment 5 MB N->>E: Step 4 Route to nearest MEC node E->>E: Step 5 GPU accelerated transcoding E-->>N: Step 6 Return transcoded 1080p stream N-->>U: Step 7 Deliver transcoded video U->>Q: Step 8 Report playback metrics Q-->>U: Step 9 Feedback to offloading engine ``` ## 4.4 Comparison: Cloud vs Edge vs Local ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Local_Device A1[Smartphone CPU]:::local A2[Limited Battery]:::local A3[Constrained Storage]:::local end subgraph Edge_Node B1[MEC GPU Server]:::edge B2[Low Latency 5 to 20 ms]:::edge B3[Regional Coverage]:::edge end subgraph Cloud_Data_Center C1[Massive GPU Cluster]:::cloud C2[High Latency 50 to 200 ms]:::cloud C3[Global Scale]:::cloud end classDef local fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 classDef edge fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef cloud fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 A1 -.Offload Decision.-> B1 A1 -.Further Offload.-> C1 B1 -.Fallback.-> C1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The `:::className` syntax in the above Mermaid blocks is used to apply visual styling to nodes. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `C3`) to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword safeguard rule. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** *Define computation offloading. List any two multimedia applications that benefit from it.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (2 Marks):** Computation offloading is the process of migrating compute-intensive or data-intensive tasks from a resource-constrained mobile device to a more powerful remote server (cloud or edge), accessed via a wireless network, in order to save energy, reduce latency, and overcome device hardware limitations. - **Applications (1 Mark — any two):** 1. Video transcoding for adaptive bitrate streaming. 2. Speech-to-text processing for voice assistants. 3. AR/VR scene rendering for mobile games. 4. Image recognition for camera-based AI. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** *Distinguish between full offloading and partial offloading with an example.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Full Offloading (1.5 Marks):** The entire application/task is migrated to the remote server. Example: Uploading a full 4K video to the cloud for server-side rendering. - **Partial Offloading (1.5 Marks):** Only a sub-module or sub-function of the application is offloaded; the rest executes locally. Example: In a CNN, only the fully-connected classifier layers run on the cloud, while convolutional feature extraction runs on the device. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a mobile computation offloading system. List the key components and their functions. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A smartphone is running a 1080p video filter that requires $W = 4.5 \times 10^9$ CPU cycles. The local CPU runs at $f_m = 1.0$ GHz with energy coefficient $\kappa = 5 \times 10^{-26}$. Compute: (i) The local execution time. (ii) The local energy consumption. (iii) The offloading decision if transmission energy is 2 J and server execution time is 1.5 s. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Architecture Explanation [7 Marks]:** The mobile computation offloading architecture consists of the following key components (as shown in Section 4.1): 1. **Task Profiler / Workload Analyzer (1 Mark):** Profiles the multimedia task to determine its computational complexity ($w_i$ in cycles), data size ($d_i$ in bytes), and deadline constraints. 2. **Offloading Decision Engine (2 Marks):** The core decision-making module. It uses optimization algorithms (e.g., Lyapunov optimization, Markov Decision Process, or heuristic rules) to decide *whether* and *what* to offload. Inputs: network conditions, energy budget, QoE requirements. 3. **Network Interface Module (1 Mark):** Manages wireless connectivity (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G). Monitors real-time bandwidth, latency, and signal strength. 4. **Local Execution Engine (1 Mark):** Executes the parts of the task that remain on the device using the mobile CPU/GPU. 5. **Remote Execution Surrogate (1 Mark):** The cloud data center or MEC edge server that processes the offloaded task using high-performance hardware (GPUs, TPUs). 6. **Result Aggregator and QoE Estimator (1 Mark):** Receives the result, merges it with local computation, estimates Quality of Experience, and feeds back to the decision engine for adaptive learning. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:** **Given:** - $W = 4.5 \times 10^9$ cycles - $f_m = 1.0 \text{ GHz} = 10^9$ Hz - $\kappa = 5 \times 10^{-26}$ - Transmission energy $E_{\text{tx}} = 2$ J - Server execution time $T_s = 1.5$ s **Step 1 — Local Execution Time (2 Marks):** $$T_{\text{local}} = \frac{W}{f_m} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^9}{1.0 \times 10^9} = 4.5 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2 — Local Energy Consumption (2 Marks):** $$E_{\text{local}} = \kappa \cdot W \cdot f_m^2 = (5 \times 10^{-26}) \cdot (4.5 \times 10^9) \cdot (10^9)^2$$ $$E_{\text{local}} = 5 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-26+9+18} = 22.5 \times 10^{1} = 225 \text{ J}$$ **Step 3 — Offloading Decision (3 Marks):** Compute remote cost (assuming negligible network latency and reception energy for simplicity): $$E_{\text{remote}} \approx E_{\text{tx}} = 2 \text{ J}$$ $$T_{\text{remote}} \approx T_s = 1.5 \text{ s}$$ Compute savings: $$\Delta E = E_{\text{local}} - E_{\text{remote}} = 225 - 2 = 223 \text{ J} \;\; (\text{> 0}) \;\;\checkmark$$ $$\Delta T = T_{\text{local}} - T_{\text{remote}} = 4.5 - 1.5 = 3.0 \text{ s} \;\; (\text{> 0}) \;\;\checkmark$$ **Conclusion:** Since both $\Delta E > 0$ and $\Delta T > 0$, the task **SHOULD BE OFFLOADED** to the remote server. The energy saving is **223 J** and the time saving is **3.0 s**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the Mobile Cloud Computing (MCC) and Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) paradigms for multimedia services. Compare their latency, energy, and use cases. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A multimedia application needs to choose between offloading to a cloud server (latency = 100 ms, energy = 0.5 J) and an MEC edge server (latency = 10 ms, energy = 1.2 J). Local execution takes 800 ms and consumes 4 J. The user assigns equal priority to energy and latency. Using a weighted score function $S = 0.5 \cdot (\Delta E / E_{\text{local}}) + 0.5 \cdot (\Delta T / T_{\text{local}})$, recommend the best offloading destination. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — MCC vs MEC [7 Marks]:** | Parameter | Mobile Cloud Computing (MCC) | Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) | |---|---|---| | **Definition (2 Marks)** | Centralized cloud data center accessed via the Internet (e.g., AWS, Azure). | Distributed computing resources at the network edge (e.g., base station, gNB). | | **Latency (1 Mark)** | High: **50 – 200 ms** | Low: **5 – 20 ms** | | **Energy (1 Mark)** | High transmission cost (long route) | Low transmission cost (short hop) | | **Best for (1 Mark)** | Heavy batch processing: video analytics, ML training. | Real-time: AR/VR, autonomous driving, IoT. | | **Architecture (1 Mark)** | Multi-hop, Internet-routed. | Single-hop, RAN-adjacent. | | **Scalability (1 Mark)** | Virtually unlimited. | Limited by edge node capacity. | --- **Part (b) — Weighted Scoring Solution [7 Marks]:** **Given:** $T_{\text{local}} = 0.8$ s, $E_{\text{local}} = 4$ J. **Step 1 — Option 1: Cloud Offloading (2 Marks):** $$\Delta E_{\text{cloud}} = 4 - 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ J}$$ $$\Delta T_{\text{cloud}} = 0.8 - 0.1 = 0.7 \text{ s}$$ $$S_{\text{cloud}} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{3.5}{4} + 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.7}{0.8} = 0.5 \cdot 0.875 + 0.5 \cdot 0.875 = 0.875$$ **Step 2 — Option 2: MEC Edge Offloading (2 Marks):** $$\Delta E_{\text{edge}} = 4 - 1.2 = 2.8 \text{ J}$$ $$\Delta T_{\text{edge}} = 0.8 - 0.01 = 0.79 \text{ s}$$ $$S_{\text{edge}} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{2.8}{4} + 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.79}{0.8} = 0.5 \cdot 0.7 + 0.5 \cdot 0.9875 = 0.8438$$ **Step 3 — Comparison and Recommendation (3 Marks):** Since $S_{\text{cloud}} = 0.875 > S_{\text{edge}} = 0.8438$, the **cloud server is the preferred offloading destination** for this scenario. > **Note:** If the priority weights were latency-heavy (e.g., 0.8 weight on latency), the **MEC edge** would win because of its near-zero latency. The choice is **weight-sensitive**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Examiner Pitfalls):** > > 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks if the unit (J, s, Mbps) is missing. > 2. **Mixing $f_m$ (mobile) and $f_s$ (server) in the energy formula.** The $\kappa \cdot W \cdot f^2$ model applies to the *local* device; do not apply it to the cloud. > 3. **Reporting only $\Delta E$ without $\Delta T$.** A complete offloading decision requires BOTH energy AND time saving. Reporting only one loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Confusing full offloading with partial offloading.** A diagram showing *which module* runs where is mandatory for full marks in part (a). > 5. **Ignoring downlink energy.** Always include $E_{\text{rx}}$ when the result is non-trivial in size. > 6. **Writing Python code in non-CS theory papers.** PECNT527 is theory-oriented; algorithmic pseudocode in *structured English* is preferred. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):** > > - **Definition:** Computation offloading = delegating multimedia tasks from mobile to remote/edge server. > - **Three Pillars:** Energy saving, latency reduction, capability augmentation. > - **Two Paradigms:** MCC (high latency, high compute) vs MEC (low latency, regional). > - **Two Granularities:** Full offloading vs partial offloading (partition-based). > - **Local Energy Formula:** $E_{\text{local}} = \kappa \cdot w_i \cdot f_m^2$. > - **Remote Energy Formula:** $E_{\text{remote}} = P_{\text{tx}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}} + P_{\text{rx}} \cdot t_{\text{rx}}$. > - **Local Time Formula:** $T_{\text{local}} = w_i \,/\, f_m$. > - **Remote Time Formula:** $T_{\text{remote}} = d_i/r_u + \text{RTT}/2 + w_i/f_s + d_i'/r_d$. > - **Decision Rule:** Offload IF $\Delta E > 0$ AND $\Delta T > 0$. > - **Key Applications:** Video transcoding, AR/VR, voice assistants, IoT analytics. > - **QoE Metrics:** PSNR, latency, jitter, frame rate. > - **Security Concern:** Encrypted offloading, trusted execution environments. > - **Mermaid Safeguard:** Node IDs must be alphanumeric; never use reserved words as node names. > - **LaTeX Safeguard:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of $\mid$ inside Markdown tables.

topics.title: Interactive Cloud Gaming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT527 - MULTIMEDIA TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interactive Cloud Gaming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Interactive Cloud Gaming** (also called **Game Streaming**, **Gaming-on-Demand**, or **Cloud-based Rendering**) is a distributed multimedia computing paradigm in which the entire computational pipeline of a video game — including physics simulation, AI logic, scene rendering, and audio synthesis — is executed on a remote high-performance cloud server. The server captures the rendered frames as raw pixel buffers, encodes them into a compressed video stream using hardware-accelerated codecs, and transmits them over an IP network to a thin client device. The client decodes the video, presents it on the local display, samples user input events (keyboard, mouse, gamepad, touch, motion), and ships those events upstream back to the server in a tight, sub-frame control loop.

The system is governed by an end-to-end **Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget** (typically bounded at **≤ 80 ms** for competitive-grade responsiveness and **≤ 150 ms** for casual gameplay) and a sustained downstream **video bitrate** ranging from **10 Mbps** (720p/30fps) to **50 Mbps** (4K/120fps HDR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Interactive Cloud Gaming sits at the convergence of *distributed systems*, *real-time multimedia streaming*, *video compression*, and *QoS-aware networking*. It is the canonical use-case that stresses all layers of the multimedia stack simultaneously.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Remote Puppet Theatre"

Imagine a master puppeteer sitting inside a powerful, well-lit theatre (the **cloud GPU server**) operating an exquisitely detailed marionette (the **game world**). The audience (the **player**) sits in a small room across town watching a live TV feed of the puppet show.

- The puppeteer moves the marionette's strings in response to letters you mail him describing what you want the puppet to do (your **input packets**, sent *upstream*).
- A film crew in the theatre records the marionette in ultra-high definition and broadcasts the live feed to your TV (the **encoded video stream*, sent *downstream*).
- The mail delivery time + filming delay + broadcast delay = the **latency** you feel between your command and seeing the result.

You never own the puppet. You only ever *see* and *command* it. The "puppet" can be infinitely more powerful than anything that fits in your living room — exactly the value proposition of cloud gaming.

---

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics (Industry Standard)

| Metric | Typical Value | Hard Limit for Playability |
|---|---|---|
| **Motion-to-Photon Latency** | 50–100 ms | **< 150 ms** |
| **Frame Rate (FPS)** | 60 fps / 120 fps | ≥ 30 fps minimum |
| **Video Bitrate** | 15–45 Mbps | ≥ 5 Mbps |
| **Resolution** | 1080p / 4K | ≥ 720p |
| **Codec** | H.264 / H.265 / AV1 | — |
| **Network RTT** | 20–60 ms | < 80 ms |
| **Input Sampling Rate** | 1000 Hz | 125 Hz minimum |

> [!NOTE]
> The single most unforgiving constraint in cloud gaming is **latency**, not bandwidth. Unlike Netflix-style VoD (where 2–5 seconds of pre-buffering is acceptable), interactive gaming tolerates almost **zero buffering**. This is what makes cloud gaming a fundamentally harder problem than conventional video streaming.

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & Industry Milestones

- **2009** — OnLive (pioneer, defunct 2015) demonstrated viability at CES.
- **2014** — Sony acquires Gaikai → becomes **PlayStation Now**.
- **2017** — NVIDIA **GeForce NOW** enters public beta.
- **2019** — **Google Stadia** launches (discontinued January 2023 — a key lesson on infrastructure economics).
- **2020** — **Xbox Cloud Gaming (xCloud)** integrated into Game Pass Ultimate.
- **2023–present** — **Boosteroid**, **Amazon Luna**, **GeForce NOW RTX 4080 SuperPODs**, **Shadow PC** dominate the live market.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reference Architecture — Five Logical Planes

A production cloud-gaming stack decomposes cleanly into five logical planes. Each plane is owned by different engineering teams in real deployments.

### Plane 1 — Game Execution Plane (Cloud Server)
- Runs the unmodified game binary on a virtualized **GPU instance** (e.g., AWS G5/G4dn, Azure NV-series, custom RTX 4080/4090 blades).
- Frame rendering is intercepted by a custom **graphics API hook** (DirectX 11/12, Vulkan, OpenGL) that captures each presented framebuffer before it is discarded by the swap-chain.
- Physics, AI, game logic: executed at native tick-rate (typically 60 Hz simulation, 120 Hz render).

### Plane 2 — Capture & Encode Plane
- Captured framebuffer is uploaded to a **hardware H.264/HEVC/AV1 encoder** (NVENC, NVENC AV1, VCN, QuickSync, or MediaFoundation).
- Encoding latency must stay **sub-frame** (≤ 4 ms for a 60 fps stream) to avoid pipeline stall.
- Bitrate is dynamically adjusted by an **adaptive bitrate (ABR) controller** observing network RTT, jitter, and packet loss.

### Plane 3 — Transport Plane (Network)
- **Downstream (video):** Uses UDP-based protocols — **WebRTC**, **SRT (Secure Reliable Transport)**, or proprietary UDP variants.
- **Upstream (input):** Tiny UDP datagrams (~16–64 bytes), transmitted at controller polling frequency.
- **QoS marking:** DSCP EF (Expedited Forwarding) for downstream video; AF31 for upstream input.
- **Edge POPs (Points-of-Presence):** Geographically distributed so that RTT is bounded by speed-of-light, not by transcontinental hops.

### Plane 4 — Client Decode & Render Plane
- Thin client (Chromebook, smart TV, mobile, low-spec PC) uses **hardware decode** (VPU) to decompress the video stream.
- The decoded frame is **direct-rendered to the scanout buffer** (zero-copy GPU path) to bypass compositor-induced latency.
- Display at native panel refresh rate (60/120/144 Hz) using **variable refresh rate (VRR)** / FreeSync / G-Sync to absorb jitter.

### Plane 5 — Input Acquisition Plane
- Polls local HID (Human Interface Device) at **1 kHz**.
- Batches samples into UDP packets sent every **2–8 ms** (configurable).
- Performs **client-side prediction** for analog sticks (extrapolation) to mask uplink RTT.

---

## 2.2 The Latency Budget — Where Every Millisecond Goes

The end-to-end **Motion-to-Photon Latency** ($L_{total}$) is the sum of nine independent, sequential pipeline stages:

$$
L_{total} = L_{input} + L_{net,up} + L_{queue} + L_{server} + L_{render} + L_{encode} + L_{net,down} + L_{decode} + L_{display}
$$

| Symbol | Stage | Typical (ms) | Worst-case (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{input}$ | HID sampling + USB/HID stack | **1** | 4 |
| $L_{net,up}$ | Uplink network RTT / 2 | **20** | 50 |
| $L_{queue}$ | Server input queue + scheduler jitter | **2** | 8 |
| $L_{server}$ | Game logic tick | **5** | 12 |
| $L_{render}$ | GPU render (1 frame at 60 fps) | **16.67** | 33.3 |
| $L_{encode}$ | HW encoder (NVENC) | **2** | 5 |
| $L_{net,down}$ | Downlink network RTT / 2 | **20** | 50 |
| $L_{decode}$ | HW decoder (VPU) | **2** | 5 |
| $L_{display}$ | Scanout + panel response | **8** | 16 |
| **$L_{total}$** | **Sum** | **≈ 77 ms** | **≈ 183 ms** |

> [!NOTE]
> At 60 fps, one frame is exactly **16.67 ms** long. Any optimization that "saves" more than one frame's worth of time is *noticeable to humans* (Just-Noticeable-Difference threshold ≈ 13–16 ms in the visual cortex).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Critical markdown safeguard:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` inside all table cells to prevent markdown table-parser breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frame Time | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{\text{FPS}}$ | seconds | $T_f = 16.67$ ms @ 60 fps |
| 2 | Bitrate (uncompressed) | $R = W \times H \times \text{FPS} \times b_{depth}$ | bps | e.g., $1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \times 24$ |
| 3 | Bitrate (compressed) | $R_c = R \times \dfrac{1}{\text{CR}}$ | bps | CR = Compression Ratio |
| 4 | Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{R}{R_c}$ | unitless | HW encoders: 100–500× |
| 5 | Motion-to-Photon Latency | $L_{m2p} = L_{total}$ | ms | Budget: **< 100 ms** for FPS |
| 6 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = R_c \times \text{RTT}$ | bits | Determines optimal queue depth |
| 7 | Required Throughput | $\text{Thr} \geq R_c \times (1 + \epsilon)$ | bps | $\epsilon$ = protocol overhead (≈ 10 %) |
| 8 | Display Refresh Period | $T_{disp} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}}$ | seconds | $T_{disp} = 8.33$ ms @ 120 Hz |
| 9 | Encoder Latency Floor | $L_{enc} \geq \dfrac{1}{\text{Encode Throughput}}$ | ms | NVENC sub-frame |
| 10 | PSNR (Quality Metric) | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{MAX}_I^{2}}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | dB | > 40 dB = visually lossless |
| 11 | SSIM (Structural Similarity) | $\text{SSIM}(x,y) = \dfrac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + c_2)}{(\mu_x^{2} + \mu_y^{2} + c_1)(\sigma_x^{2} + \sigma_y^{2} + c_2)}$ | [0,1] | > 0.95 = high quality |
| 12 | Server Capacity (CCU) | $N_{users} = \dfrac{\text{GPU RAM}_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{per user}}}$ | users | $V$ ≈ 2–6 GB VRAM/user |

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

The cloud-gaming workload is **uniquely hostile** to cloud infrastructure because it violates three classical assumptions of distributed systems:

1. **Zero tolerance for buffering** — unlike VoD, there is no pre-fetch window. The ABR algorithm must operate at *sub-second* granularity.
2. **Symmetric low-latency bidirectional traffic** — the uplink (input) is hundreds of times smaller than the downlink (video), but *both* must arrive within the same frame budget.
3. **GPU-sharing is non-trivial** — multiple users cannot share a single GPU as easily as they share CPU/RAM (memory-bandwidth-bound, frame-timing-deterministic). This is why NVIDIA's vGPU and AMD's MxGPU with **SR-IOV** are essential primitives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Latency Budget Audit for a Hypothetical Session

**Scenario:** A KTU exam question asks you to audit whether a proposed cloud-gaming configuration meets the **80 ms motion-to-photon** target.

**Given Pipeline:**
- HID sampling rate = 500 Hz → sampling interval = 2 ms
- Uplink WAN RTT = 30 ms → one-way ≈ 15 ms
- Server input queue wait = 1.5 ms
- Game tick @ 60 Hz = 16.67 ms
- Hardware encoder = 3 ms
- Downlink WAN RTT = 40 ms → one-way ≈ 20 ms
- Client HW decoder = 2 ms
- Display panel = 144 Hz → $T_{disp} = 6.94$ ms
- Assume scanout at frame midpoint → effective display wait = $T_{disp} / 2$

**Step 1 — Identify all nine latency terms.**

$$
L_{input} = 2~\text{ms},\quad L_{net,up} = 15~\text{ms},\quad L_{queue} = 1.5~\text{ms}
$$
$$
L_{server} = 16.67~\text{ms},\quad L_{render} \subset L_{server} = 16.67~\text{ms}
$$
$$
L_{encode} = 3~\text{ms},\quad L_{net,down} = 20~\text{ms},\quad L_{decode} = 2~\text{ms}
$$
$$
L_{display} = \dfrac{T_{disp}}{2} = \dfrac{6.94}{2} = 3.47~\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the additive latency model.**

$$
L_{total} = L_{input} + L_{net,up} + L_{queue} + L_{server} + L_{encode} + L_{net,down} + L_{decode} + L_{display}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
L_{total} = 2 + 15 + 1.5 + 16.67 + 3 + 20 + 2 + 3.47
$$

**Step 4 — Sum left-to-right with explicit carry.**

$$
L_{total} = (2 + 15) + (1.5 + 16.67) + (3 + 20) + (2 + 3.47)
$$

$$
L_{total} = 17 + 18.17 + 23 + 5.47 = 63.64~\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to budget.**

$$
L_{total} = 63.64~\text{ms} < 80~\text{ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Configuration is ACCEPTABLE}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the safety margin.**

$$
\text{Margin} = 80 - 63.64 = 16.36~\text{ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{20.45\% safety margin}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly forget to *halve* the network RTT (RTT is round-trip; one-way is RTT / 2) and forget to *halve* the display period (display latency is typically the *average* wait, which is half the refresh period). Both errors inflate the apparent latency by tens of milliseconds.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Bandwidth Calculation for a 4K HDR Stream

**Problem:** Compute the compressed downstream bitrate for a 4K (3840 × 2160), 60 fps, 10-bit HDR stream assuming an H.265 (HEVC) encoder achieves a compression ratio of 250:1.

**Step 1 — Compute uncompressed raw bitrate.**

$$
R = W \times H \times \text{FPS} \times b_{depth} \times \text{channels}
$$

$$
R = 3840 \times 2160 \times 60 \times 10 \times 3
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply incrementally.**

$$
R = (3840 \times 2160) \times (60 \times 10 \times 3)
$$

$$
3840 \times 2160 = 8{,}294{,}400~\text{pixels/frame}
$$

$$
60 \times 10 \times 3 = 1800~\text{bits/pixel/second}
$$

$$
R = 8{,}294{,}400 \times 1800 = 14{,}929{,}920{,}000~\text{bps}
$$

$$
R \approx 14.93~\text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply compression ratio.**

$$
R_c = \dfrac{R}{\text{CR}} = \dfrac{14.93~\text{Gbps}}{250} = 59.7~\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Add protocol overhead (≈ 10 %).**

$$
R_{final} = 59.7 \times 1.10 = 65.67~\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$
\boxed{R_{final} \approx 65.67~\text{Mbps}}
$$

This matches the real-world **GeForce NOW 4K HDR** preset of 50–75 Mbps.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Server Capacity & GPU Multiplexing

**Problem:** An Azure NV-series blade has 1 × A100 GPU with **80 GB VRAM** and a measured **180 fps rasterization throughput** at 1080p. Each cloud-gaming user requires **2.5 GB VRAM** and must sustain **60 fps**. How many concurrent users can the blade host, assuming FPS is the binding constraint?

**Step 1 — Compute VRAM-bound capacity.**

$$
N_{VRAM} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{80}{2.5} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 32 \rfloor = 32~\text{users}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FPS-bound capacity.**

$$
N_{FPS} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{180}{60} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 3 \rfloor = 3~\text{users}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the binding (minimum) constraint.**

$$
N_{blade} = \min(N_{VRAM},\, N_{FPS}) = \min(32,\, 3) = \mathbf{3}~\text{users}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** This is *exactly* why a single A100 cannot replace a gaming PC. Modern cloud-gaming providers use **dedicated consumer GPUs** (RTX 4080 / 4090) in single-tenant or 1+1 configurations, not datacenter GPUs, because rasterization throughput per dollar is dramatically higher on gaming silicon.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cloud Gaming vs. Other Streaming Workloads

| Property | Cloud Gaming | Video-on-Demand (Netflix) | Live Sports Stream | Video Conferencing (Zoom) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Latency tolerance | < 100 ms | 2–5 s (buffered) | 3–10 s | < 400 ms |
| Bitrate (typical) | 15–45 Mbps | 5–25 Mbps | 5–15 Mbps | 1–4 Mbps |
| Directionality | Bidirectional | One-way | One-way | Bidirectional |
| Encoding deadline | Hard (sub-frame) | Soft (offline) | Soft (offline/real-time) | Hard (real-time) |
| Codec | H.264/HEVC/AV1 | HEVC/AV1 | HEVC/AV1 | H.264/AV1 |
| Buffering acceptable? | **No** | Yes | Yes | Small (jitter buffer) |
| Failure mode | Input lag / disorientation | Re-buffer | Stale scoreboard | Audio cut-out |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Cloud Gaming Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph clientZone["CLIENT ZONE - Player Premises"]
        hid["HID Layer\nKeyboard / Mouse / Gamepad"]
        predictor["Input Predictor\nExtrapolation Engine"]
        decoder["HW Video Decoder\nVPU / NVDEC"]
        scanout["Direct Scanout\nVRR Display"]
        hid --> predictor
        decoder --> scanout
    end

    subgraph edgeZone["EDGE POP - Metro POP"]
        edgeRT["Edge Relay\nDSCP EF Forwarder"]
    end

    subgraph cloudZone["CLOUD REGION - GPU Data Center"]
        scheduler["Game Session Scheduler\nOrchestrator"]
        vm["Game VM\nCPU + Game Logic"]
        gpu["GPU Slice\nRendering"]
        capture["Framebuffer Capture\nDXGI / Vulkan Hook"]
        encoder["HW Encoder\nNVENC AV1 / HEVC"]
        abrc["Adaptive Bitrate Controller\nCongestion Observer"]

        scheduler --> vm
        vm --> gpu
        gpu --> capture
        capture --> encoder
        abrc -.-> encoder
    end

    predictor -- "UDP input\n16 B / 8 ms" --> edgeRT
    edgeRT -- "WAN uplink\nRTT 20 ms" --> scheduler
    encoder -- "UDP video\nRTP / WebRTC" --> edgeRT
    edgeRT -- "WAN downlink\nRTT 20 ms" --> decoder
```

## 4.2 Latency Pipeline Waterfall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Player Presses Button\nt = 0 ms"] --> B["HID Sample\n+1 ms"]
    B --> C["Client Encodes Input\n+1 ms"]
    C --> D["WAN Uplink\n+20 ms"]
    D --> E["Server Queue + Decode\n+3 ms"]
    E --> F["Game Logic Tick\n+16.67 ms"]
    F --> G["GPU Render\n+0 ms (parallel)"]
    G --> H["HW Encode\n+3 ms"]
    H --> I["WAN Downlink\n+20 ms"]
    I --> J["Client Decode\n+2 ms"]
    J --> K["Scanout to Panel\n+3 ms"]
    K --> L["Photon Emitted\n+3 ms"]
    L --> M["Player Sees Result\nL_total = 69.67 ms"]
```

## 4.3 Adaptive Bitrate Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    obs["Network Observer\nRTT, Jitter, Loss"] --> q["QoS Estimator\nEWMA Filter"]
    q --> dec{"Decision\nEngine"}
    dec -- "Loss < 0.5%\nRTT stable" --> up["Raise Bitrate\n+15% step"]
    dec -- "Loss 0.5% - 2%" --> hold["Hold Bitrate\nno change"]
    dec -- "Loss > 2%\nRTT spike" --> down["Lower Bitrate\n-25% step"]
    up --> enc["Reconfigure Encoder"]
    hold --> enc
    down --> enc
    enc --> obs
```

## 4.4 GPU Multiplexing Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gpu["Physical GPU A100 / RTX 4090"]
    gpu --> sriov["SR-IOV / vGPU"]
    sriov --> vf1["VF1\nUser A - 1080p60"]
    sriov --> vf2["VF2\nUser B - 1080p60"]
    sriov --> vf3["VF3\nUser C - 720p60"]
    gpu --> passthru["PCIe Passthrough\n1 User 1 GPU"]
    passthru --> vip["VF4\nVIP User - 4K120"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Part A]`
**Define Interactive Cloud Gaming. State TWO reasons why it imposes stricter latency requirements than conventional video streaming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Interactive Cloud Gaming is a real-time multimedia streaming paradigm in which a video game is executed on a remote cloud GPU server, and the rendered output is encoded as a video stream and transmitted to a thin client, while user input events are sent back upstream. *(1 mark)*

Two reasons for stricter latency requirements: *(2 marks)*
1. **Bidirectional interactivity** — unlike VoD, the system forms a closed control loop. A delay in input transport directly desynchronizes the player's actions from on-screen feedback, breaking the sense of agency.
2. **State-dependent content** — each frame is determined by the player's previous input. There is no pre-rendered buffer of "future" frames available to absorb delay, so latency cannot be masked by buffering.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Part A]`
**List any THREE components of the motion-to-photon latency pipeline in a cloud-gaming system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Network round-trip time** (uplink + downlink WAN latency)
2. **Server-side processing latency** (game tick + render + HW encoding)
3. **Client-side display latency** (HW decoding + panel scanout + photon emission)

*(Acceptable alternatives: HID sampling latency, input queue wait, GPU rasterization latency.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4, Part B]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the reference architecture of an Interactive Cloud Gaming system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of EACH of the five planes.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cloud gaming service renders at 1080p × 60 fps with 8-bit color depth. The H.264 encoder achieves a compression ratio of 150:1. Compute the compressed downstream bitrate and the required TCP-friendly throughput assuming 10 % protocol overhead. State if the stream is feasible on a 25 Mbps connection.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — Architecture

The reference architecture consists of **five logical planes**:

1. **Game Execution Plane** *(1 mark)* — runs the game binary on a virtualized GPU instance in the cloud data center. Owns all CPU/GPU compute and the game's persistent state.

2. **Capture & Encode Plane** *(1 mark)* — intercepts the rendered framebuffer at swap-chain present time, uploads it to a hardware encoder (NVENC/VCN), and produces an H.264/HEVC/AV1 bitstream. Performs ABR bitrate selection.

3. **Transport Plane** *(1 mark)* — distributes the encoded video downstream over UDP-based protocols (WebRTC, SRT) and ingests tiny upstream input datagrams. Includes edge POPs to minimize RTT.

4. **Client Decode & Render Plane** *(1 mark)* — uses hardware VPU to decode incoming video and direct-renders the decoded framebuffer to the local scanout, bypassing compositor latency.

5. **Input Acquisition Plane** *(2 marks)* — polls local HID devices at high frequency (1 kHz), applies client-side prediction/extrapolation for analog inputs, and ships batched UDP datagrams to the server every few milliseconds.

*(Bonus 1 mark for a clean, labeled block diagram — see SECTION 4.1.)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — Bandwidth Computation

**Step 1 — Compute uncompressed bitrate.** *(2 marks)*

$$
R = W \times H \times \text{FPS} \times b_{depth} \times \text{channels}
$$

$$
R = 1920 \times 1080 \times 60 \times 8 \times 3
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and simplify.** *(2 marks)*

$$
R = (1920 \times 1080) \times (60 \times 8 \times 3)
$$

$$
R = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 1440 = 2{,}985{,}984{,}000~\text{bps}
$$

$$
R \approx 2.986~\text{Gbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply compression ratio.** *(1 mark)*

$$
R_c = \dfrac{R}{\text{CR}} = \dfrac{2.986~\text{Gbps}}{150} \approx 19.91~\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Add protocol overhead.** *(1 mark)*

$$
R_{final} = 19.91 \times 1.10 \approx 21.90~\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Feasibility conclusion.** *(1 mark)*

$$
R_{final} = 21.90~\text{Mbps} < 25~\text{Mbps connection} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{FEASIBLE with 12.4\% headroom}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Marks Lost Here Every Year:**
> - **Forgetting the third color channel** — RGB requires × 3, not just resolution × fps × depth. This single omission inflates the answer by 3×.
> - **Confusing bps and Bps** — the question asks for **bits per second**, not bytes. 1 Byte = 8 bits.
> - **Ignoring protocol overhead** — UDP/RTP/RTCP headers consume 5–15 %. Examiners explicitly look for this *engineering realism*.
> - **No unit conversion** — leaving the answer in `bps` instead of `Mbps` costs half a mark for readability.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Part B]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the latency budget of an Interactive Cloud Gaming system. List ANY SIX pipeline stages with typical values and identify which TWO stages contribute the largest share to total latency.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the role of (i) Adaptive Bitrate (ABR) streaming, (ii) Edge computing (Edge POPs), and (iii) Hardware-accelerated codecs in mitigating cloud-gaming latency. For each, state one limitation.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — Latency Budget

The **motion-to-photon latency** is the end-to-end delay from a player input to the corresponding photon being emitted by the display. *(1 mark)*

**Six pipeline stages with typical values** *(4 marks — 0.5 mark each for stage + value, 0.5 mark for listing):*

| # | Stage | Typical Latency |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | HID sampling | **1–4 ms** |
| 2 | Network RTT (uplink + downlink) | **20–60 ms** |
| 3 | Server-side game tick + queue | **5–20 ms** |
| 4 | GPU rasterization | **16.67 ms** (at 60 fps) |
| 5 | Hardware encode | **2–5 ms** |
| 6 | Hardware decode + display scanout | **8–16 ms** |

**Two largest contributors:** *(2 marks)*

1. **Network RTT (round-trip WAN latency)** — typically 20–60 ms one-way, often the *single largest* contributor in real deployments, and the only term bounded by physics (speed of light in fiber ≈ 200,000 km/s).
2. **Server-side game tick + rasterization** — at 60 fps this single stage consumes 16.67 ms, and any sub-optimal frame timing cascades into encoder backlog.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — Mitigation Techniques

**(i) Adaptive Bitrate (ABR) Streaming** *(2 marks + 1 mark limitation)*

ABR dynamically adjusts the encoder bitrate in response to real-time network measurements (RTT, jitter, packet loss). A typical controller uses an EWMA-filtered congestion estimator that steps the bitrate **up** by ~15 % when the link is stable and **down** by ~25 % when loss exceeds 2 %. This prevents catastrophic re-buffer events.

*Limitation:* ABR cannot reduce *latency* itself — only *quality*. It also causes visible resolution/quantization changes that can be distracting in fast-paced scenes.

**(ii) Edge Computing / Edge POPs** *(2 marks + 1 mark limitation)*

Edge POPs are small data centers geographically dispersed (one per metro area) that act as low-latency relays between the user and the central GPU region. By terminating the WAN connection at the closest POP, the network RTT is reduced from ~60 ms (transcontinental) to ~10–20 ms (intra-metro).

*Limitation:* Edge POPs are extremely CAPEX-intensive. Maintaining GPU-capable edge infrastructure at global scale is economically infeasible, so most providers keep GPU compute in central regions and use edge POPs only as *stateless relays* (which limits further RTT reduction).

**(iii) Hardware-Accelerated Codecs (NVENC, NVDEC, VCN, QuickSync)** *(1 mark + 1 mark limitation)*

Dedicated silicon (separate from the render pipeline) compresses frames in **2–5 ms** versus 30–80 ms for software codecs. This is essential for sub-frame encoding deadlines.

*Limitation:* Hardware encoders offer limited control over perceptual quality (no deep rate-distortion optimization), and resolution/format support can lag behind software reference encoders (x265, SVT-AV1) by 12–18 months.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common-Deduction Notice:**
> - Stating that **ABR reduces latency** — *wrong*. ABR trades **quality for stability**; it never reduces delay.
> - Confusing **Edge POP with Edge Computing** — POPs are typically *stateless relay caches*, not GPU compute nodes.
> - Calling the codec "the bottleneck" — modern hardware codecs are rarely the bottleneck; *network* and *rasterization* are.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- **Cloud Gaming = remote rendering + video stream + bidirectional input loop.** Not the same as VoD, not the same as Remote Desktop.
- **Latency, not bandwidth, is the binding constraint.** Target $L_{m2p} < 100$ ms for FPS-class games; < 150 ms for casual.
- **Master the 9-stage latency formula** — every stage is independent and additive. Forgetting to halve RTT or display period is the most common error.
- **Codec choices**: H.264 (universal, low-latency), HEVC (50 % better at same quality, royalty-encumbered), AV1 (royalty-free, higher encode latency).
- **Transport protocols**: UDP-based always. WebRTC for browser clients, SRT for broadcast-grade, proprietary UDP for native clients. **Never TCP for video** (TCP's retransmission violates real-time deadline).
- **Edge POPs ≠ Edge GPU compute.** Most architectures keep GPUs in central regions and use edge POPs as relay caches.
- **GPU multiplexing is hard.** Modern providers use **1 user : 1 GPU** (PCIe passthrough) or **2–3 users : 1 GPU** (SR-IOV vGPU) for cost recovery — never more.
- **Quality metrics**: PSNR (dB, > 40 = visually lossless) and SSIM ([0,1], > 0.95 = high quality) are the standard objective measures for cloud-gaming streams.
- **Frame budget at 60 fps = 16.67 ms**, at 120 fps = 8.33 ms — every sub-system must fit inside one frame budget or the pipeline stalls.
- **Cloud gaming ≠ game subscription**. You can own *access to a virtual machine* (Shadow PC) or *access to a specific game library* (GeForce NOW, Xbox Cloud Gaming). The licensing model differs from the technology.
- **Future directions to mention in long-form answers**: 5G URLLC sub-1 ms radio, multi-view streaming for VR/AR cloud gaming, AI-based frame interpolation (NVIDIA DLSS 3 Frame Generation, AMD Fluid Motion Frames), neural codecs (JPEG AI, H.266/VVC), and server-side ray-traced rendering with neural denoising.
